$$k = \frac{1}{\lambda} \quad \text{(cycles per metre)}$$

A **short wavelength** corresponds to a **high spatial frequency** → it encodes **fine detail** (small structures).
A **long wavelength** corresponds to a **low spatial frequency** → it encodes only **coarse shape** (large structures).

This is why:
- X-rays (short $\lambda$) can resolve bone micro-architecture.
- Ultrasound (longer $\lambda$) cannot resolve structures smaller than $\sim \lambda/2$.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Behind the Core Equations

**Step 1 — Frequency definition.** Frequency $f$ is the number of complete oscillations per unit time. One oscillation takes a period $T$ seconds:

$$f = \frac{1}{T}$$

**Step 2 — Wave travels one wavelength per period.** In time $T$, the wave crest advances exactly one wavelength $\lambda$ at the wave speed $v$. Therefore:

$$\lambda = v \cdot T$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $T = 1/f$:**

$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to the master equation:**

$$v = f \lambda$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** This equation is *universal*. It applies to X-ray photons in tissue, sound waves in a fetus's amniotic fluid, and radio waves in an MRI bore. The only thing that changes is the value of $v$ (or $c$ for EM waves).

### 2.3 Wavelength–Energy Relationship (Photon Model)

In the **quantum/particle view of light**, a photon of frequency $f$ carries energy:

$$E = h f = \frac{h c}{\lambda}$$

where $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s is the **Planck constant**.

This means:
- **Shorter wavelength → Higher photon energy** (e.g., diagnostic X-ray at $\lambda = 0.1$ nm → $E \approx 12.4$ keV).
- **Longer wavelength → Lower photon energy** (e.g., MRI RF at $\lambda = 5$ m → $E \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-7}$ eV).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A common student error is to confuse *wavelength* with *energy*. They are **inversely** related (through $h$ and $c$). Always check: as $\lambda$ **decreases**, $E$ **increases**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Table

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units in Medical Imaging |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $v = f \lambda$ | Universal wave equation | $v$ in m/s, $f$ in Hz, $\lambda$ in m |
| 2 | $c = f \lambda$ | EM wave in vacuum/air | $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s |
| 3 | $\lambda = v/f$ | Solve for wavelength | m |
| 4 | $f = v/\lambda$ | Solve for frequency | Hz |
| 5 | $E = h f$ | Photon energy (Planck) | Joules (or eV: $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J) |
| 6 | $E = h c / \lambda$ | Photon energy from wavelength | Joules |
| 7 | $\lambda = h / p$ (de Broglie) | Matter-wave wavelength (electron imaging) | m |
| 8 | $T = 1/f$ | Period of oscillation | seconds |
| 9 | $k = 2\pi / \lambda$ | Angular wavenumber | rad/m |
| 10 | $\Delta x \cdot \Delta p \geq \hbar/2$ | Heisenberg uncertainty (resolution limit) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary conditions to remember for KTU problems:**
> - Use $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s for X-rays, gamma rays, visible light.
> - Use $c_{\text{tissue}} \approx 1540$ m/s for ultrasound in soft tissue.
> - Always convert nm → m by multiplying by $10^{-9}$ before plugging into SI formulas.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **X-ray Tube Design:** The minimum (cut-off) wavelength emitted by an X-ray tube is $\lambda_{\min} = h c / (eV)$, where $V$ is the tube voltage (kVp). This sets the *shortest* wavelength and hence the *highest* photon energy in the beam.
2. **Ultrasound Transducer Selection:** A 5 MHz probe in tissue has $\lambda = 1540/5 \times 10^{6} \approx 0.31$ mm. The axial resolution is roughly $\lambda/2 \approx 0.15$ mm.
3. **MRI Pulse Sequences:** The Larmor frequency $f_0 = \gamma B_0$ determines the RF pulse wavelength; coil geometry must be sized to $\sim \lambda/2$ for efficient excitation.
4. **Radiation Safety:** Knowing wavelength tells you the energy of an X-ray beam, which governs shielding requirements (lead thickness).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Periodic Motion to $\lambda = v/f$

**Given:** A sinusoidal wave travels to the right with speed $v$ along the $x$-axis. Its displacement is:

$$y(x,t) = A \sin(kx - \omega t)$$

**Step 1.** A point of constant phase satisfies $kx - \omega t = \text{constant}$. Differentiating:

$$k \, dx - \omega \, dt = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = \frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{\omega}{k}$$

**Step 2.** Identify the angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$ and the wavenumber $k = 2\pi / \lambda$:

$$v = \frac{2\pi f}{2\pi / \lambda} = f \lambda$$

**Step 3.** Solving for wavelength:

$$\boxed{\lambda = \frac{v}{f}}$$

This is the **master expression** for wavelength. ✔

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Duane–Hunt Law (Minimum X-ray Wavelength)

**Given:** An electron of charge $e$ is accelerated through a potential difference $V$ in an X-ray tube. On striking the target, all of its kinetic energy converts to a single photon (worst-case, highest-energy scenario).

**Step 1.** Kinetic energy gained by the electron:

$$KE = eV$$

**Step 2.** Energy of the emitted photon (Planck's law):

$$E = h f = \frac{h c}{\lambda_{\min}}$$

**Step 3.** Equate KE to photon energy (energy conservation):

$$eV = \frac{h c}{\lambda_{\min}}$$

**Step 4.** Solve for $\lambda_{\min}$:

$$\boxed{\lambda_{\min} = \frac{h c}{e V}}$$

**Numerical check** (a typical mammography tube at 30 kVp):

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(30 \times 10^{3})}$$

Step-by-step numerator:

$$(6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \times (3 \times 10^{8}) = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25} \text{ J·m}$$

Step-by-step denominator:

$$(1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (30 \times 10^{3}) = 4.806 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J}$$

Final division:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{4.806 \times 10^{-15}} = 4.14 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m} = 0.0414 \text{ nm}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Doubling the kVp halves the minimum wavelength. This is why higher-voltage X-ray tubes produce more penetrating (harder) beams.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Resolving Power Limit in Imaging

**Given:** A circular aperture of diameter $D$ is illuminated by a wave of wavelength $\lambda$. By Rayleigh's criterion, the smallest resolvable angle is:

$$\theta_{\min} = 1.22 \frac{\lambda}{D}$$

**Step 1.** For a target at distance $L$, the smallest resolvable feature size is:

$$\Delta x = L \theta_{\min} = 1.22 \frac{\lambda L}{D}$$

**Step 2.** To resolve a 0.5 mm feature at 1 m with an aperture of 5 mm:

$$0.5 \times 10^{-3} = 1.22 \frac{\lambda \cdot 1}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 3.** Solve for $\lambda$:

$$\lambda = \frac{(0.5 \times 10^{-3})(5 \times 10^{-3})}{1.22} = 2.05 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 2.05 \text{ μm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This shows why optical microscopes are limited to $\sim$ sub-μm features and why **electron microscopes** (de Broglie wavelength $\lambda \approx$ pm) can resolve atoms.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Ultrasound in Soft Tissue

A diagnostic ultrasound probe operates at $f = 7.5$ MHz. The speed of sound in soft tissue is $c_{\text{tissue}} = 1540$ m/s.

**Step 1.** Apply $\lambda = v / f$:

$$\lambda = \frac{1540}{7.5 \times 10^{6}}$$

**Step 2.** Compute:

$$\lambda = 2.053 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.205 \text{ mm} \approx 0.2 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3.** Axial resolution (approximately $\lambda / 2$):

$$\Delta x \approx \frac{0.2}{2} = 0.1 \text{ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A 7.5 MHz probe resolves structures down to about 0.1 mm. To resolve smaller features, a **higher frequency** (hence **shorter wavelength**) probe is needed — but penetration depth decreases accordingly.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Wavelength Across the Imaging Spectrum

| Imaging Domain | Frequency $f$ | Wavelength $\lambda$ | Photon Energy $E$ | Penetration in Tissue |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Gamma ray (Tc-99m) | $\sim 10^{19}$ Hz | $\sim 10^{-11}$ m | $\sim 140$ keV | Whole body |
| Diagnostic X-ray | $10^{16}$ – $10^{19}$ Hz | $10^{-11}$ – $10^{-8}$ m | $20$ – $120$ keV | Whole body |
| UV (therapy) | $10^{15}$ – $10^{16}$ Hz | $10^{-7}$ – $10^{-6}$ m | $4$ – $40$ eV | Skin only |
| Visible light | $4 \times 10^{14}$ – $7.5 \times 10^{14}$ Hz | $400$ – $700$ nm | $1.8$ – $3.1$ eV | mm |
| Infrared (thermal) | $10^{13}$ – $10^{14}$ Hz | $10^{-6}$ – $10^{-5}$ m | $0.04$ – $0.4$ eV | $\mu$m |
| Microwave (diathermy) | $10^{9}$ – $10^{10}$ Hz | $3$ – $30$ cm | $\sim 10^{-5}$ eV | cm |
| RF (MRI, 1.5 T) | $\sim 64 \times 10^{6}$ Hz | $\sim 4.7$ m | $\sim 2.6 \times 10^{-7}$ eV | Whole body |
| Ultrasound (5 MHz) | $5 \times 10^{6}$ Hz | $0.31$ mm | N/A (mechanical) | $\sim 10$ cm |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4.1 Wavelength as a Spatial Quantity — Annotated Waveform

```mermaid

    A[Wave Source] --> B[Wavefronts travel outward]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**

**Q1. Define wavelength. State the universal relationship between wavelength, frequency, and wave speed.**
*(3 marks — Cognitive Level: Remember/Understand — KTU CO1)*

**Model Answer:**

Wavelength ($\lambda$) is the spatial period of a periodic wave — defined as the shortest distance between two successive points on the wave that are in phase (e.g., crest-to-crest or trough-to-trough). Its SI unit is the metre (m).

The universal relationship is:

$$v = f \lambda$$

where $v$ is the wave speed, $f$ is the frequency, and $\lambda$ is the wavelength. For electromagnetic waves in vacuum, $v = c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. ✔

---

**Q2. A diagnostic ultrasound probe operates at 5 MHz. The speed of sound in soft tissue is 1540 m/s. Calculate the wavelength in tissue and the axial resolution.**
*(3 marks — Cognitive Level: Apply — KTU CO2)*

**Model Answer:**

Using $\lambda = v / f$:

$$\lambda = \frac{1540 \text{ m/s}}{5 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}} = 3.08 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \approx 0.31 \text{ mm}$$

Axial resolution $\approx \lambda / 2$:

$$\Delta x \approx \frac{0.31}{2} \approx 0.15 \text{ mm}$$

Thus the probe can resolve structures of about **0.15 mm** axially. ✔

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End-Semester Exam (ESE) — Module 1 Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between photon energy, frequency, and wavelength of an electromagnetic wave. Explain its significance in selecting an X-ray source for diagnostic imaging. *(7 marks — KTU CO1, CO2)*

**(b)** An X-ray tube is operated at 80 kVp. Using the Duane–Hunt relation, determine the minimum wavelength of X-rays produced. Given $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, and $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C. *(7 marks — KTU CO2, CO3)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

From Planck's quantum theory, a photon of frequency $f$ carries energy:

$$E = h f$$

The frequency is related to wavelength through $c = f \lambda$, so $f = c / \lambda$. Substituting:

$$\boxed{E = \frac{h c}{\lambda}}$$

This shows that **photon energy is inversely proportional to wavelength**.

**Significance in diagnostic X-ray imaging:**

- Diagnostic X-rays require photon energies of about $20$ – $120$ keV to penetrate soft tissue but be differentially absorbed by bone.
- The corresponding wavelengths are in the range $10^{-11}$ to $10^{-8}$ m.
- Choosing the right wavelength (via tube kVp) controls penetration, contrast, and patient dose. Higher kVp → shorter $\lambda_{\min}$ → more penetrating beam.

**(b) Numerical solution:**

Step 1 — State the Duane–Hunt law:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{h c}{e V}$$

Step 2 — Substitute values. Note $V = 80 \text{ kVp} = 80 \times 10^{3}$ V.

Numerator: $h c = (6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8}) = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25}$ J·m

Denominator: $e V = (1.602 \times 10^{-19})(80 \times 10^{3}) = 1.2816 \times 10^{-14}$ J

Step 3 — Divide:

$$\lambda_{\min} = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.2816 \times 10^{-14}} = 1.55 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m} = 0.0155 \text{ nm}$$

Step 4 — Convert to more familiar units: $\lambda_{\min} \approx 0.155$ Å.

**Final answer:** $\lambda_{\min} \approx 1.55 \times 10^{-11}$ m = 0.0155 nm. ✔

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the electromagnetic spectrum. Indicate the wavelength ranges used in (i) X-ray imaging, (ii) MRI, and (iii) visible-light endoscopy. *(7 marks — KTU CO1)*

**(b)** A CT scanner uses X-rays of effective wavelength $0.02$ nm. Calculate (i) the frequency, and (ii) the energy of one photon in keV. *(7 marks — KTU CO2, CO3)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Electromagnetic spectrum description:**

The electromagnetic spectrum is the continuous range of electromagnetic waves, classified by wavelength (or frequency) into: radio waves → microwaves → infrared → visible light → ultraviolet → X-rays → gamma rays.

| Modality | Wavelength Range | Region |
|---|---|---|
| (i) X-ray imaging | $10^{-11}$ to $10^{-8}$ m (0.01 – 10 nm) | X-ray region |
| (ii) MRI | $\sim 3$ – $10$ m (at 1.5 T) | Radiofrequency (RF) region |
| (iii) Visible-light endoscopy | $400$ – $700$ nm | Visible region |

**Labelled schematic (text version):**

Increasing wavelength $\lambda$ →
- Gamma ($\lambda < 10$ pm) — Nuclear medicine
- **X-ray (10 pm – 10 nm) — X-ray, CT**
- UV (10 nm – 400 nm)
- **Visible (400 – 700 nm) — Endoscopy**
- IR (700 nm – 1 mm)
- Microwave (1 mm – 1 m)
- **Radio/RF ( > 1 m) — MRI**

**(b) Numerical solution:**

Given: $\lambda = 0.02$ nm $= 0.02 \times 10^{-9}$ m $= 2 \times 10^{-11}$ m.

**(i) Frequency:**

$$f = \frac{c}{\lambda} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 10^{-11}} = 1.5 \times 10^{19} \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Photon energy in eV:**

$$E = h f = (6.626 \times 10^{-34})(1.5 \times 10^{19}) = 9.939 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J}$$

Convert to eV (divide by $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$):

$$E = \frac{9.939 \times 10^{-15}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 6.20 \times 10^{4} \text{ eV} = 62.0 \text{ keV}$$

**Final answer:** $f = 1.5 \times 10^{19}$ Hz and $E \approx 62$ keV. ✔

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** The single most common mistake. Always convert nm → m ($ \times 10^{-9}$) or keV → J ($\times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$) **before** substituting. Half the marks in a 7-mark numerical are forfeited for one missed conversion.
> 2. **Confusing $f$ and $\lambda$:** Writing $\lambda = c \cdot f$ instead of $c/\lambda$. Remember: *higher frequency* = *shorter wavelength* = *higher energy*.
> 3. **Forgetting the Duane–Hunt assumption:** It gives the **minimum** wavelength (maximum-energy photon), not the average. Examiners explicitly deduct if "minimum" is omitted.
> 4. **Mixing up EM and ultrasound speeds:** Don't plug $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s into an ultrasound problem — use $c_{\text{tissue}} = 1540$ m/s.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Wavelength $\lambda$ = distance between two consecutive in-phase points (crest-to-crest) on a wave.
- **Master equation:** $v = f \lambda$ — universal for all waves.
- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in vacuum/air (use for X-ray, gamma, RF, visible).
- **Speed of sound in tissue:** $c_{\text{tissue}} = 1540$ m/s (use for ultrasound).
- **Photon energy:** $E = h c / \lambda$ → shorter $\lambda$ = higher energy.
- **Duane–Hunt law:** $\lambda_{\min} = h c / (e V)$ → minimum wavelength from an X-ray tube.
- **Resolution rule of thumb:** Smallest resolvable feature $\approx \lambda / 2$ (Rayleigh).
- **Modalities at a glance:**
  - X-ray / CT → $\lambda \sim 0.01$ – $0.1$ nm
  - Ultrasound (5 MHz) → $\lambda \sim 0.3$ mm
  - MRI (1.5 T, 64 MHz) → $\lambda \sim 4.7$ m
  - Visible endoscopy → $\lambda \sim 400$ – $700$ nm
- **Inverse relationship:** As $f \uparrow$, $\lambda \downarrow$, $E \uparrow$, penetration $\downarrow$ (for ionising EM radiation).
- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s.
- **Electron charge:** $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C.
- **Always convert** to SI base units (m, s, J) before calculation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phase
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Phase # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of medical imaging physics, **Phase** is a dimensionless quantity that describes the position of a point within the periodic cycle of a waveform at a given instant of time, measured as an angle (in radians or degrees) between 0 and **2π** (or 0° to 360°). It represents the *fraction of a complete cycle* that has elapsed relative to a chosen reference origin. For a one-dimensional progressive sinusoidal wave traveling along the *+x* direction, the general wave function is: $$ y(x,t) = A \sin(kx - \omega t + \phi) $$ Here, the total instantaneous argument of the sine function is termed the **phase (φ)** of the wave: $$ \Phi(x,t) = kx - \omega t + \phi_0 $$ where: - $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ is the angular wave number (rad/m) - $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency (rad/s) - $\phi_0$ is the **initial phase** (or phase constant), measured in radians at $x = 0, t = 0$ - $A$ is the amplitude (m) - $\lambda$ is the wavelength (m) - $f$ is the linear frequency (Hz) > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1 of PEBRT411, the concept of phase is foundational for understanding **Ultrasound Imaging (Phased Arrays)** and **MRI Phase Encoding**. Both modalities rely entirely on precise control and measurement of wave phase. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Runners on a Circular Track" Imagine two runners jogging at exactly the same speed around a circular athletic track, but they start at different points on the track. Even though they run at identical speeds (same *frequency*) and cover the same distance per lap (same *wavelength*), at any given instant, one runner may be at the 100 m mark while the other is at the 200 m mark. The "**angular position**" of each runner on the circular track — measured from a fixed reference point — is its **phase**. If both runners are at exactly the same angular position (same spot on the track), they are said to be **in-phase**. If one is diametrically opposite the other, they are **180° out-of-phase**. Translating this analogy to medical imaging: - The **track** = one full wave cycle (360° or 2π rad). - The **runners** = two wavefronts arriving at a tissue interface. - **In-phase waves** → constructively interfere → strong echo (used in beamforming). - **Out-of-phase waves** → destructively interfere → weak/no echo (used to suppress side-lobes). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Takeaway:** Phase is *not* an independent property — it is a *relative* measure. We always speak of the **phase difference (Δφ)** between two waves, never the absolute phase of a single wave. --- ## 1.3 Why "Phase" Matters in Medical Imaging | Imaging Modality | Role of Phase | |------------------|----------------| | **Ultrasound (US)** | Determines beam steering angle, focal depth, and echo summation in phased-array transducers. | | **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** | Used in *phase encoding* — a spatial localization gradient applied for time $T$, imparting position-dependent phase $\phi = \gamma G_x x T$. | | **Computed Tomography (CT)** | Relates to *projection angle* in Radon transform reconstruction. | | **Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT)** | Phase stability enables sub-wavelength displacement measurements. | | **PET / SPECT** | Phase concept appears in time-of-flight signal processing. | --- ## 1.4 Phase Difference, Path Difference & Time Delay Three equivalent quantities — all describing the same "lag" between two coherent waves: | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit | |----------|--------|---------|------| | Phase difference | $\Delta\phi$ | — | rad or degrees | | Path difference | $\Delta x$ | $\Delta x = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\pi} \Delta\phi$ | m | | Time delay | $\Delta t$ | $\Delta t = \dfrac{T}{2\pi} \Delta\phi = \dfrac{\Delta\phi}{\omega}$ | s | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of the Wave Equation — Step-by-Step Logic Let us dissect $y(x,t) = A \sin(kx - \omega t + \phi_0)$ term by term. ### Step 1 — The Amplitude $A$ Represents the maximum displacement of the wave from its equilibrium position. - **Why it matters:** Echo amplitude in ultrasound is proportional to the *square* of the acoustic impedance mismatch at tissue interfaces. ### Step 2 — The Spatial Term $kx$ - **How it works:** As you move a distance $x$ along the propagation direction, the wave's phase increases by $kx$ radians. - **Physical meaning:** Captures the *spatial periodicity* — every $2\pi$ increase in $kx$ corresponds to advancing by one wavelength $\lambda$. ### Step 3 — The Temporal Term $-\omega t$ - **How it works:** As time advances, the phase *decreases* (hence the minus sign for waves traveling in the +x direction). - **Physical meaning:** Captures the *temporal periodicity* — every $2\pi$ increase in $\omega t$ corresponds to advancing by one time period $T = 1/f$. ### Step 4 — The Initial Phase $\phi_0$ - **How it works:** A constant offset describing where the wave "starts" at $x=0, t=0$. - **Physical meaning:** Two waves of identical frequency and wavelength can still differ in $\phi_0$ — this offset determines whether they reinforce or cancel at a given location. ### Step 5 — Total Phase $\Phi(x,t)$ - The combined effect of spatial location, elapsed time, and initial offset. - **Critical insight:** A *surface of constant phase* defines a **wavefront** — the locus of all points sharing identical phase at a fixed instant. --- ## 2.2 Conditions for Interference — Phase Relationships > [!IMPORTANT] > **Constructive Interference** — waves add to produce maximum amplitude: $$\Delta\phi = 2n\pi, \quad n = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Destructive Interference** — waves cancel to produce minimum (ideally zero) amplitude: $$\Delta\phi = (2n+1)\pi, \quad n = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$$ The general principle governing the resultant intensity (energy) of two superposed waves of equal amplitude $A$ is given by: $$ I_{resultant} = 4A^2 \cos^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) $$ where $\Delta\phi$ is the phase difference between the two contributing waves. --- ## 2.3 Phase Velocity The **phase velocity** is the speed at which a surface of constant phase propagates through space. For a non-dispersive medium: $$ v_p = \frac{\omega}{k} = f\lambda $$ | Medium | Approx. Speed of Sound ($v_p$) | |--------|-------------------------------| | Air (20°C) | **343 m/s** | | Fat tissue | **1450 m/s** | | Water (37°C) | **1524 m/s** | | Soft tissue (average) | **1540 m/s** | | Bone (cortical) | **4080 m/s** | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — "Phase" High-Yield Equations | # | Equation | Description | Units | |---|----------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $\Phi(x,t) = kx - \omega t + \phi_0$ | Total instantaneous phase | rad | | 2 | $\phi_0 = kx - \omega t$ | Initial phase constant | rad | | 3 | $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ | Angular wave number | rad/m | | 4 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular frequency | rad/s | | 5 | $T = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}$ | Time period | s | | 6 | $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,\Delta x$ | Phase difference ↔ Path difference | rad | | 7 | $\Delta\phi = \omega\,\Delta t$ | Phase difference ↔ Time delay | rad | | 8 | $v_p = f\lambda = \dfrac{\omega}{k}$ | Phase velocity | m/s | | 9 | $I = 4A^2 \cos^2\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right)$ | Resultant intensity (2 waves) | W/m² | | 10 | $\theta = \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{c\,\Delta\tau}{d}\right)$ | Beam steering angle (US phased array) | rad | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Equation (10) is the cornerstone of *electronic beam steering* in ultrasound probes — by applying microsecond-scale time delays ($\Delta\tau$) across array elements separated by pitch $d$, the beam can be steered without physically rotating the transducer. --- ## 2.5 Phase in MRI — The Phase-Encoding Gradient In MRI, after a 90° RF excitation pulse, the transverse magnetization precesses at the Larmor frequency. When a **phase-encoding gradient** $G_x$ is applied for a duration $T_{pe}$, spins at position $x$ accumulate a position-dependent phase: $$ \phi(x) = \gamma \, G_x \, x \, T_{pe} $$ where $\gamma$ is the gyromagnetic ratio (**42.58 MHz/T** for hydrogen). Each successive TR uses a different $G_x$ strength, encoding one line of *k-space* per phase-encode step. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** Phase in MRI is *spatial* (position-dependent), while phase in ultrasound is *temporal* (delay-dependent). Confusing these is a common valuation pitfall. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — Path Difference ↔ Phase Difference ### Given: - Two coherent waves of identical wavelength $\lambda$. - One wave travels an *extra* geometric path $\Delta x$ compared to the other. ### To Prove: $$ \Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,\Delta x $$ ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Write the wave equations:** Let wave 1 (reference) and wave 2 (delayed) be described at the same observation point as: $$ y_1 = A \sin(kx - \omega t) $$ $$ y_2 = A \sin(k(x+\Delta x) - \omega t) $$ **Step 2 — Expand wave 2:** $$ y_2 = A \sin(kx + k\Delta x - \omega t) $$ **Step 3 — Identify the phase of each wave:** $$ \Phi_1 = kx - \omega t $$ $$ \Phi_2 = kx + k\Delta x - \omega t $$ **Step 4 — Compute the phase difference:** $$ \Delta\Phi = \Phi_2 - \Phi_1 = k\Delta x $$ **Step 5 — Substitute $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$:** $$ \Delta\Phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,\Delta x \quad \blacksquare $$ ### Verification by dimensional check: $$ [\Delta\Phi] = \frac{[\text{rad}]}{[\text{m}]} \times [\text{m}] = [\text{rad}] \;\checkmark $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation II — Time Delay ↔ Phase Difference ### Given: - A wave train arrives at a transducer with a time lag $\Delta t$ relative to a reference. ### To Prove: $$ \Delta\phi = \omega \,\Delta t $$ ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Wave 1 (reference, arrives at $t_0$):** $$ y_1 = A \sin(\omega t_0) $$ **Step 2 — Wave 2 (delayed by $\Delta t$, arrives at $t_0 + \Delta t$):** $$ y_2 = A \sin(\omega (t_0 + \Delta t)) = A \sin(\omega t_0 + \omega\Delta t) $$ **Step 3 — Identify the additional phase acquired due to delay:** The extra term $\omega\Delta t$ inside the sine argument represents the phase lag. **Step 4 — Formalize:** $$ \Delta\phi = \omega\Delta t = 2\pi f \Delta t \quad \blacksquare $$ ### Practical substitution for ultrasound: For diagnostic ultrasound ($f = 5$ MHz) and a tissue depth $d$: $$ \Delta t = \frac{2d}{c} = \frac{2d}{1540} \;\;\text{(round-trip)} $$ $$ \Delta\phi = 2\pi f \cdot \frac{2d}{c} = \frac{4\pi f d}{c} $$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation III — Beam Steering Angle in a Phased-Array Ultrasound Transducer ### Given: - Linear array of $N$ transducer elements with uniform spacing $d$. - Consecutive electronic time delay $\Delta\tau$ applied between elements. - Speed of sound in tissue: $c = 1540$ m/s. ### To Prove: $$ \theta = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{c\,\Delta\tau}{d}\right) $$ ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Consider two adjacent elements separated by distance $d$.** **Step 2 — Path difference for a wave arriving at angle $\theta$ from broadside:** The extra path traveled by the wavefront to reach element $(n+1)$ versus element $n$ is: $$ \Delta x = d \sin\theta $$ **Step 3 — Convert this path difference into an equivalent time delay using the speed of sound:** $$ \Delta t_{geom} = \frac{\Delta x}{c} = \frac{d\sin\theta}{c} $$ **Step 4 — To make all element contributions add constructively at angle $\theta$, we must pre-compensate by introducing an *electronic* time delay $\Delta\tau$ equal in magnitude but opposite in sign to $\Delta t_{geom}$:** $$ \Delta\tau = \frac{d\sin\theta}{c} $$ **Step 5 — Solve for $\theta$:** $$ \sin\theta = \frac{c\,\Delta\tau}{d} $$ $$ \boxed{\theta = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{c\,\Delta\tau}{d}\right)} \quad \blacksquare $$ ### Numerical Example: For $c = 1540$ m/s, $d = 0.3$ mm, $\Delta\tau = 50$ ns: $$ \sin\theta = \frac{1540 \times 50 \times 10^{-9}}{0.3 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{7.7 \times 10^{-5}}{3 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 0.2567 $$ $$ \theta \approx 14.88^\circ $$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation IV — Resultant Intensity from Two Superposed Waves ### Given: - Two waves of equal amplitude $A$, frequency $\omega$, with phase difference $\Delta\phi$. ### To Prove: $$ I = 4A^2 \cos^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) $$ ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Write the superposed wave using the sum-to-product trigonometric identity:** $$ y_{total} = A\sin(\omega t) + A\sin(\omega t + \Delta\phi) $$ **Step 2 — Apply the sum-to-product identity $\sin\alpha + \sin\beta = 2\sin\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha+\beta}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\dfrac{\alpha-\beta}{2}\right)$:** $$ y_{total} = 2A\sin\!\left(\omega t + \frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Recognize the amplitude of the resulting wave:** $$ A_{resultant} = 2A\cos\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) $$ **Step 4 — Since intensity is proportional to the square of the amplitude:** $$ I_{resultant} \propto A_{resultant}^2 = 4A^2 \cos^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) \quad \blacksquare $$ --- ## 3.5 Phase & Wavelength — The "Universal Phase Meter" | Path Difference $\Delta x$ | Phase Difference $\Delta\phi$ | Result | |---------------------------|-------------------------------|--------| | $0$ | $0$ | Fully constructive | | $\lambda/4$ | $\pi/2$ | Intermediate | | $\lambda/2$ | $\pi$ | Fully destructive | | $3\lambda/4$ | $3\pi/2$ | Intermediate | | $\lambda$ | $2\pi$ | Fully constructive (cycle complete) | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic for students:** *Path-difference to phase-difference — multiply by $2\pi$ and divide by $\lambda$.* $$ \Delta\phi = \Delta x \times \frac{360^\circ}{\lambda} \quad \text{(in degrees)} $$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Phase Relationships — Visual Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A["WAVE SOURCE
Oscillator"] --> B["INITIAL PHASE phi0
Constant offset"] A --> C["SPATIAL TERM kx
Position dependent"] A --> D["TEMPORAL TERM omega t
Time dependent"] B --> E["TOTAL PHASE Phi x t
Combined argument"] C --> E D --> E E --> F{"PHASE COMPARISON
WITH REFERENCE WAVE"} F -->|Delta phi equals 2n pi| G["CONSTRUCTIVE
Maximum amplitude 2A"] F -->|Delta phi equals 2n plus 1 pi| H["DESTRUCTIVE
Zero amplitude"] F -->|Intermediate| I["PARTIAL INTERFERENCE
Amplitude 2A cos Delta phi by 2"] E --> J["WAVEFRONT
Surface of constant phase"] E --> K["PHASE VELOCITY
vp equals omega by k"] E --> L["APPLICATIONS
Ultrasound beamforming
MRI phase encoding
OCT coherent detection"] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Ultrasound Phased Array Beam Steering ```mermaid flowchart LR P["PULSE GENERATOR
Master clock"] --> Q["CHANNEL 1
Delay 0 ns"] P --> R["CHANNEL 2
Delay delta tau"] P --> S["CHANNEL 3
Delay 2 delta tau"] P --> T["CHANNEL N
Delay N minus 1 delta tau"] Q --> U["PIEZOELECTRIC
ARRAY ELEMENTS
Spacing d"] R --> U S --> U T --> U U --> V["TISSUE
Beam steered
at angle theta"] V --> W["ECHO RETURN
Variable arrival times"] W --> X["BEAMFORMER
Dynamic receive focusing"] X --> Y["IMAGE RECONSTRUCTION
B mode scanline"] ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — MRI Phase Encoding Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TD M0["EQUILIBRIUM
Magnetization M0 along B0"] --> M1["90 DEGREE RF PULSE
Tip into xy plane"] M1 --> M2["PHASE ENCODING
Gradient Gx applied
for duration Tpe"] M2 --> M3["SPINS ACCUMULATE
Phase phi equals gamma Gx x Tpe"] M3 --> M4["FREQUENCY ENCODING
Gradient Gy applied
during readout"] M4 --> M5["SIGNAL ACQUISITION
One line of k space filled"] M5 --> M6{"REPEAT FOR
DIFFERENT Gx VALUES"} M6 -->|Yes next TR| M2 M6 -->|No all lines filled| M7["2D INVERSE FOURIER
TRANSFORM"] M7 --> M8["RECONSTRUCTED
IMAGE"] ``` ## 4.4 Conceptual Schematic — In-Phase vs. Out-of-Phase Waveforms ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A["IN PHASE Delta phi equals 0"] A1["Wave A: sine 0 deg"] --> A2["Wave B: sine 0 deg"] A2 --> A3["Resultant: Amplitude 2A
Peak alignment"] end subgraph B["OUT OF PHASE Delta phi equals pi"] B1["Wave A: sine 0 deg"] --> B2["Wave B: sine 180 deg"] B2 --> B3["Resultant: Amplitude 0
Cancellation"] end subgraph C["QUADRATURE Delta phi equals pi by 2"] C1["Wave A: sine 0 deg"] --> C2["Wave B: sine 90 deg"] C2 --> C3["Resultant: Amplitude sqrt 2 A
Intermediate"] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** The three sub-blocks above illustrate the three canonical phase relationships encountered in coherent imaging systems. Note how the *same* two waves produce dramatically different *resultants* purely because of their relative phase alignment. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define the term "phase" as applied to a sinusoidal wave. How is phase difference related to path difference?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (2 Marks):** The phase of a sinusoidal wave is the argument of the trigonometric function describing the wave at a given point and time. For a wave $y = A \sin(kx - \omega t + \phi_0)$, the total phase is $\Phi = kx - \omega t + \phi_0$. It is measured in **radians** (or degrees) and represents the position within one oscillation cycle. **Relation (1 Mark):** The phase difference $\Delta\phi$ and path difference $\Delta x$ are linked by: $$ \Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,\Delta x $$ A path difference of one wavelength ($\Delta x = \lambda$) corresponds to a full $2\pi$ radian phase shift. --- ### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Distinguish between "in-phase" and "out-of-phase" waves. State the condition for constructive and destructive interference.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Distinction (1.5 Marks):** - **In-phase waves:** Two waves are in-phase when their phase difference $\Delta\phi = 2n\pi$ ($n = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$). Their crests and troughs align perfectly. - **Out-of-phase waves:** Two waves are out-of-phase when $\Delta\phi = (2n+1)\pi$. Their crests align with each other's troughs. **Conditions (1.5 Marks):** - **Constructive interference:** $\Delta\phi = 2n\pi \Rightarrow$ Resultant amplitude = $2A$. - **Destructive interference:** $\Delta\phi = (2n+1)\pi \Rightarrow$ Resultant amplitude = $0$. --- ## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]` **(a)** Derive the relationship between phase difference and path difference for two coherent waves of wavelength $\lambda$. Explain the physical significance of initial phase. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Two coherent sound waves of frequency **2 MHz** travel in the same direction in soft tissue ($c = 1540$ m/s). They arrive at a point with a path difference of **0.5 mm**. Calculate: - (i) The wavelength - (ii) The phase difference in radians and degrees - (iii) The resultant intensity relative to a single wave **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):** **Step 1 (2 Marks):** Let two coherent waves traveling in the +x direction be expressed as: $$ y_1 = A\sin(kx - \omega t) \quad \text{and} \quad y_2 = A\sin(k(x+\Delta x) - \omega t) $$ **Step 2 (2 Marks):** Expand wave 2: $$ y_2 = A\sin(kx + k\Delta x - \omega t) $$ **Step 3 (2 Marks):** The phases are $\Phi_1 = kx - \omega t$ and $\Phi_2 = kx + k\Delta x - \omega t$. Subtracting: $$ \Delta\phi = \Phi_2 - \Phi_1 = k\Delta x = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,\Delta x \quad \blacksquare $$ **Step 4 (1 Mark):** **Physical significance of initial phase $\phi_0$:** It specifies the wave's state at $x=0, t=0$. Two waves with identical $A$, $f$, and $\lambda$ but different $\phi_0$ values will superpose to produce different interference patterns at every point in space — $\phi_0$ is what distinguishes otherwise identical waves from each other. --- **Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):** **Given:** $f = 2$ MHz $= 2 \times 10^6$ Hz; $c = 1540$ m/s; $\Delta x = 0.5$ mm $= 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m. **(i) Wavelength (2 Marks):** $$ \lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 10^6} = 7.7 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m} = 0.77\,\text{mm} $$ **(ii) Phase difference (3 Marks):** $$ \Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,\Delta x = \frac{2\pi \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}{7.7 \times 10^{-4}} $$ $$ \Delta\phi = \frac{10\pi}{7.7} \approx 4.08\,\text{rad} $$ Converting to degrees: $$ \Delta\phi_{deg} = 4.08 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 233.77^\circ $$ **(iii) Resultant intensity (2 Marks):** $$ \frac{I_{resultant}}{I_0} = 4\cos^2\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right) = 4\cos^2\!\left(\frac{4.08}{2}\right) = 4\cos^2(2.04) $$ $$ \cos(2.04) \approx -0.4546 \Rightarrow \cos^2(2.04) \approx 0.2067 $$ $$ \frac{I_{resultant}}{I_0} \approx 4 \times 0.2067 \approx 0.827 $$ The resultant intensity is approximately **82.7%** of the intensity of four times a single wave — or about **3.31 $I_0$**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** What is meant by phase velocity? Derive the expression for beam steering angle in a phased-array ultrasound transducer. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A linear ultrasound array has element spacing $d = 0.3$ mm and operates at $f = 5$ MHz in soft tissue ($c = 1540$ m/s). Calculate: - (i) The wavelength - (ii) The maximum unambiguous steering angle (no grating lobes) - (iii) The time delay required to steer the beam to $20^\circ$ **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Theory (7 Marks):** **Definition (2 Marks):** Phase velocity $v_p$ is the speed at which a surface of constant phase (i.e., a wavefront) propagates through a medium. It is given by: $$ v_p = \frac{\omega}{k} = f\lambda $$ **Beam Steering Derivation (5 Marks):** For a linear array with uniform spacing $d$ and progressive time delay $\Delta\tau$: **Step 1 (1 Mark):** Geometric path difference between adjacent elements for a wave arriving at angle $\theta$: $$ \Delta x = d\sin\theta $$ **Step 2 (1 Mark):** Equivalent geometric time delay: $$ \Delta t_{geom} = \frac{d\sin\theta}{c} $$ **Step 3 (1 Mark):** To achieve constructive interference at angle $\theta$, apply electronic delay: $$ \Delta\tau = \frac{d\sin\theta}{c} $$ **Step 4 (1 Mark):** Solve for $\theta$: $$ \sin\theta = \frac{c\Delta\tau}{d} $$ **Step 5 (1 Mark):** $$ \boxed{\theta = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{c\Delta\tau}{d}\right)} \quad \blacksquare $$ --- **Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):** **Given:** $d = 0.3$ mm $= 3 \times 10^{-4}$ m; $f = 5 \times 10^6$ Hz; $c = 1540$ m/s. **(i) Wavelength (2 Marks):** $$ \lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{1540}{5 \times 10^6} = 3.08 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m} = 0.308\,\text{mm} $$ **(ii) Maximum steering angle (3 Marks):** To avoid grating lobes, the spacing must satisfy $d \leq \dfrac{\lambda}{2}$: $$ d_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{2} = \frac{0.308}{2} = 0.154\,\text{mm} $$ Since $d = 0.3$ mm **exceeds** $d_{max}$, **grating lobes will appear**. The maximum unambiguous steering angle (where $\sin\theta_{max} = c\Delta\tau_{max}/d$ and $\Delta\tau_{max}$ corresponds to one full wavelength delay): $$ \sin\theta_{max} = \frac{\lambda}{d} = \frac{0.308}{0.3} \approx 1.027 $$ Since this exceeds 1, the beam **cannot be steered unambiguously** at any angle — this confirms the grating lobe problem. **(iii) Time delay for $\theta = 20°$ (2 Marks):** $$ \Delta\tau = \frac{d\sin\theta}{c} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-4} \times \sin(20^\circ)}{1540} $$ $$ \sin(20^\circ) \approx 0.3420 $$ $$ \Delta\tau = \frac{3 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.3420}{1540} = \frac{1.026 \times 10^{-4}}{1540} \approx 6.66 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{s} = 66.6\,\text{ns} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit mismatch trap:** Students frequently mix up $\omega = 2\pi f$ (angular freq) and $f$ (linear freq). Always convert $f$ (Hz) to $\omega$ (rad/s) by multiplying by $2\pi$. > 2. **Sign error in beam steering:** The minus sign in the wave equation ($y = A\sin(kx - \omega t)$) indicates *positive x-direction* travel. Do NOT flip the sign arbitrarily. > 3. **Phase in degrees vs. radians:** Final answers in medical imaging problems conventionally use **radians**. Marks are deducted if conversion is omitted. > 4. **Grating lobe ignorance:** When array spacing $d > \lambda/2$, students often assume perfect beam steering. Always verify the Nyquist criterion for arrays. > 5. **MRI vs. Ultrasound phase confusion:** Phase in MRI is *spatial* (due to gradient × position); phase in ultrasound is *temporal* (due to delay × frequency). --- ## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Phase** $\Phi(x,t) = kx - \omega t + \phi_0$ is a dimensionless angle (rad) describing position within a wave cycle. - ✅ **Phase difference** is *relative* — always compare two or more waves; never quote absolute phase. - ✅ **Phase ↔ Path difference:** $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,\Delta x$. - ✅ **Phase ↔ Time delay:** $\Delta\phi = \omega\,\Delta t$. - ✅ **Phase velocity:** $v_p = \omega/k = f\lambda$. - ✅ **Constructive interference** requires $\Delta\phi = 2n\pi$; **destructive** requires $\Delta\phi = (2n+1)\pi$. - ✅ **Ultrasound beam steering:** $\theta = \arcsin\!\left(\dfrac{c\Delta\tau}{d}\right)$. - ✅ **MRI phase encoding:** $\phi(x) = \gamma\,G_x\,x\,T_{pe}$ with $\gamma_{H} = 42.58$ MHz/T. - ✅ **Critical array spacing** to avoid grating lobes: $d \leq \lambda/2$. - ✅ **Soft tissue sound speed** used as standard: **1540 m/s**. - ✅ **Intensity relation:** $I = 4A^2\cos^2(\Delta\phi/2)$. - ✅ **Universal constants to memorize:** $c_{tissue} = 1540$ m/s, $\gamma_{H} = 42.58$ MHz/T.

topics.title: Pressure and Intensity - Acoustic Pressure and Intensities Within the Beam
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Pressure and Intensity - Acoustic Pressure and Intensities Within the Beam ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Acoustic Pressure — The Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Acoustic Pressure (p)** is defined as the instantaneous difference between the total instantaneous pressure at a point in a medium through which a sound wave is propagating and the static ambient pressure of the medium at equilibrium. > > **Mathematical Statement:** > $$p(x,t) = P_{\text{total}}(x,t) - P_{\text{ambient}}$$ > > **SI Unit:** **Pascal (Pa)** $\equiv$ **N/m²** > **Characteristic Value in Soft Tissue:** Microseconds-scale oscillations typically in the range **0.1 MPa – 10 MPa** For a one-dimensional longitudinal sinusoidal plane wave propagating along the **x-axis**, the acoustic pressure takes the canonical form: $$p(x,t) = P_0 \, \sin(\omega t - kx)$$ where each symbol has the following rigorous physical meaning: - $P_0$ → **Peak (or Amplitude) Acoustic Pressure** — maximum deviation from ambient pressure, expressed in **Pa**. - $\omega$ → **Angular Frequency** in **rad/s**, with $\omega = 2\pi f$ where **f** is the ultrasound frequency in **Hz**. - $k$ → **Wave Number** in **rad/m**, with $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ and $\lambda$ being the wavelength in **m**. - $t$ → Time in **seconds**. - $x$ → Position along the propagation direction in **m**. ### 1.2 Acoustic Intensity — The Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Acoustic Intensity (I)** is defined as the time-averaged rate of energy flow per unit area transported across a surface perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation. > > **SI Unit:** **Watts per square metre (W/m²)** > **Typical Diagnostic Range:** **0.1 – 700 mW/cm²** The general vector definition is: $$\vec{I} = p \, \vec{v}$$ where $p$ is the instantaneous acoustic pressure and $\vec{v}$ is the instantaneous particle velocity. For a sinusoidal plane wave the time-averaged intensity reduces to: $$\langle I \rangle = \frac{1}{2}\rho c v_0^2 = \frac{P_0^2}{2\rho c} = \frac{P_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho c}$$ ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition — The Pond Ripple Analogy > [!TIP] > **Imagine tossing a small stone into a calm pond.** The expanding circular ripples carry energy outward. Two things happen simultaneously: > 1. The water surface **rises and falls** at each point — this oscillating surface displacement is the visual analogue of **acoustic pressure** (a small pressure oscillation superimposed on the static ambient pressure). > 2. The amount of **energy crossing any square metre of pond surface per second** corresponds directly to **acoustic intensity**. > > **Key Intuition:** Acoustic pressure is the *local mechanical disturbance*; acoustic intensity is the *rate of energy transport* caused by that disturbance. Pressure is *what the molecules feel*, intensity is *what carries the diagnostic information*. ### 1.4 Pressure, Intensity & the Beam — Why They Are NOT Uniform A real diagnostic ultrasound beam is a **three-dimensional, spatially non-uniform field**. It has: - A **focal zone** where the beam converges to a small cross-section → intensities here are highest. - A **near field (Fresnel zone)** with intensity variations. - A **far field (Fraunhofer zone)** with diverging beam → intensities decrease with distance. Therefore, when we speak of "the intensity" of an ultrasound beam, we must specify **where in space** and **when in time** the measurement is taken. This leads to the multiple **standardised intensity descriptors** discussed in the next section. --- ### 1.5 Standardised Intensity Descriptors in a Beam > [!NOTE] > The U.S. **FDA / AIUM / NEMA** standards (and IEC 62359) define the following five canonical intensity descriptors used in KTU examinations and clinical reporting: | **Descriptor** | **Full Form** | **Spatial Component** | **Temporal Component** | |----------------|---------------|----------------------|------------------------| | **I_SPTA** | Spatial-Peak Temporal-Average | Peak across the beam cross-section | Time-averaged over the pulse repetition cycle | | **I_SATA** | Spatial-Average Temporal-Average | Averaged over beam cross-section | Time-averaged | | **I_SPPA** | Spatial-Peak Pulse-Average | Peak across the beam | Averaged over pulse duration | | **I_SATP** | Spatial-Average Temporal-Peak | Averaged over beam | Peak during a pulse | | **I_SPTP** | Spatial-Peak Temporal-Peak | Peak across the beam | Instantaneous peak | --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Physical Origin of Acoustic Pressure When an ultrasound transducer's piezoelectric element vibrates, it imposes a **mechanical oscillation** on the adjacent tissue. Because tissue is an elastic, compressible medium, this vibration: 1. **Compresses** the medium locally → local pressure rises above $P_{\text{ambient}}$ → **positive acoustic pressure**. 2. **Rarefies** the medium locally → local pressure falls below $P_{\text{ambient}}$ → **negative acoustic pressure**. The difference between this oscillating local pressure and the static ambient pressure is the **acoustic pressure $p$**. ### 2.2 Why Intensity Within the Beam Varies The acoustic beam is rarely planar over the region of interest. Even assuming an ideal piston source of diameter **D** operating at wavelength $\lambda$: - In the **Near Field** ($z < D^2/4\lambda$): multiple pressure maxima and minima exist due to interference of Huygens wavelets emanating from the transducer face. - At the **Focal Point** ($z = z_f$): constructive interference concentrates the pressure → maximum $P_0$ → maximum intensity. - In the **Far Field** ($z > D^2/4\lambda$): the beam diverges and intensity falls as $\approx 1/z^2$. Hence at any **spatial coordinate $(x, y, z)$ within the beam**, both $p(x,y,z,t)$ and $I(x,y,z,t)$ are functions of position and time. ### 2.3 Derivation of the Pressure–Intensity Coupling > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does pressure relate to intensity quadratically?** > Intensity is *power per unit area*, and power in a wave is proportional to the *square* of the amplitude of the oscillating quantity (pressure or particle velocity). This is identical to the reasoning behind why the average of $\sin^2$ over a cycle gives $\tfrac{1}{2}$. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they account for $\geq 60\%$ of numerical questions on pressure and intensity.** | **#** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Boundary/Validity** | |:-----:|--------------|-------------|-----------|------------------------| | 1 | Instantaneous Pressure | $p(x,t) = P_0 \sin(\omega t - kx)$ | **Pa** | Plane wave, lossless medium | | 2 | Particle Velocity | $v(x,t) = v_0 \sin(\omega t - kx)$ | **m/s** | Plane wave, lossless medium | | 3 | Pressure–Velocity Coupling | $P_0 = \rho \, c \, v_0$ | **Pa** | Plane progressive wave | | 4 | Acoustic Impedance | $Z = \rho \, c$ | **Rayl (kg·m⁻²·s⁻¹)** | Property of medium | | 5 | Instantaneous Intensity | $I(t) = p(t) \cdot v(t)$ | **W/m²** | General | | 6 | Time-Average Intensity | $\langle I \rangle = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho c v_0^2$ | **W/m²** | Plane wave | | 7 | Intensity in terms of $P_0$ | $\langle I \rangle = \dfrac{P_0^2}{2\rho c}$ | **W/m²** | Plane wave | | 8 | Intensity in terms of $P_{\text{rms}}$ | $\langle I \rangle = \dfrac{P_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho c}$ | **W/m²** | $P_{\text{rms}} = P_0/\sqrt{2}$ | | 9 | SPTA Intensity | $I_{\text{SPTA}} = \max_{x,y,z} \left[ \dfrac{1}{T} \int_0^T I(x,y,z,t)\,dt \right]$ | **W/m²** | In-water measurement | | 10 | SATA Intensity | $I_{\text{SATA}} = \dfrac{1}{A} \int_A \langle I \rangle \, dA$ | **W/m²** | $A$ = beam cross-section | | 11 | In-Situ Derating | $I_{\text{in-situ}} = I_{\text{water}} \times 10^{-0.1 \, \alpha \, f_c \, z}$ | **W/m²** | $\alpha$ in dB/(cm·MHz) | > **Conversion quick-reference:** **1 W/cm² = 10⁴ W/m²**, **1 MPa = 10⁶ Pa**. ### 2.5 Engineering Utility in Medical Imaging > [!TIP] > **Clinical relevance:** > - **$I_{\text{SPTA}}$** is the primary thermal-effect indicator → drives the **Thermal Index (TI)** display. > - **$I_{\text{SPTP}}$** and **Peak Negative Pressure** drive the **Mechanical Index (MI)** → cavitation risk. > - **Derated in-situ values** predict the actual intensity reaching the tissue, accounting for frequency-dependent attenuation (typically **0.3 dB/cm·MHz** for soft tissue). > - These standardised descriptors allow **regulatory safety limits** (FDA Track 3: $I_{\text{SPTA}} \leq 720\,\text{mW/cm}^2$). --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Acoustic Pressure Wave Equation to the Pressure–Intensity Relationship **Starting Premise (1-D Longitudinal Wave Equation in Lossless Fluid):** $$\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2}$$ **Step 1 — Propose a sinusoidal solution.** Any wave of the form below satisfies the 1-D wave equation: $$p(x,t) = P_0 \sin(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 2 — Differentiate twice with respect to $x$:** $$\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} = -k^2 P_0 \sin(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 3 — Differentiate twice with respect to $t$:** $$\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2} = -\omega^2 P_0 \sin(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the wave equation:** $$-k^2 P_0 \sin(\omega t - kx) = \frac{1}{c^2}\left[-\omega^2 P_0 \sin(\omega t - kx)\right]$$ **Step 5 — Cancel the common non-zero factor $P_0 \sin(\omega t - kx)$:** $$-k^2 = -\frac{\omega^2}{c^2}$$ **Step 6 — Solve for the dispersion relation:** $$\boxed{c = \frac{\omega}{k} = \lambda f}$$ This proves the sinusoidal pressure wave is consistent with the wave equation. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Particle Velocity From Euler's Equation of Motion **Starting Premise (Euler's Equation for lossless fluid, no body forces):** $$\rho \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}$$ **Step 1 — Substitute the pressure field** $p(x,t) = P_0 \sin(\omega t - kx)$: $$\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = -k P_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 2 — Plug into Euler's equation:** $$\rho \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = k P_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 3 — Integrate with respect to time:** $$v(x,t) = \frac{k P_0}{\rho} \int \cos(\omega t - kx)\,dt = \frac{k P_0}{\rho \omega}\sin(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 4 — Recognise the velocity amplitude:** $$v_0 = \frac{k P_0}{\rho \omega} = \frac{P_0}{\rho c}$$ since $c = \omega/k$. Rearranged: $$\boxed{P_0 = \rho \, c \, v_0}$$ This is the **acoustic Ohm's Law** for a plane wave, confirming that **pressure and particle velocity are in phase** in a non-attenuating medium. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Time-Average Intensity From $P_0$ and $v_0$ **Step 1 — Instantaneous intensity (vector form, plane wave):** $$I(t) = p(t) \cdot v(t) = \bigl[P_0 \sin(\omega t - kx)\bigr]\bigl[v_0 \sin(\omega t - kx)\bigr]$$ **Step 2 — Expand the product:** $$I(t) = P_0 v_0 \sin^2(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 3 — Apply the trigonometric identity $\sin^2\theta = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[1 - \cos(2\theta)\bigr]$:** $$I(t) = \frac{P_0 v_0}{2}\bigl[1 - \cos(2(\omega t - kx))\bigr]$$ **Step 4 — Take the time-average over one complete period $T$.** The cosine term averages to zero: $$\langle I \rangle = \frac{P_0 v_0}{2}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $P_0 = \rho c v_0$ from Derivation 2:** $$\boxed{\langle I \rangle = \frac{1}{2}\rho c v_0^2}$$ **Step 6 — Substitute $v_0 = P_0/(\rho c)$:** $$\boxed{\langle I \rangle = \frac{P_0^2}{2\rho c}}$$ **Step 7 — Express using RMS pressure** $P_{\text{rms}} = P_0/\sqrt{2}$: $$\langle I \rangle = \frac{(P_{\text{rms}}\sqrt{2})^2}{2\rho c} = \frac{2 P_{\text{rms}}^2}{2\rho c}$$ $$\boxed{\langle I \rangle = \frac{P_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho c}}$$ --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: SPTA From a Pulsed Beam For a train of pulses with **pulse duration $\tau$**, **pulse repetition period $T_{\text{PRP}}$**, and **spatial peak pressure amplitude $P_0^{\text{(sp)}}$**: **Step 1 — Temporal-average intensity at the spatial peak:** $$I_{\text{SPTA}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{PRP}}} \int_0^{T_{\text{PRP}}} I(t)\,dt$$ **Step 2 — Within a pulse, intensity envelope is non-zero; outside the pulse it is zero:** $$I_{\text{SPTA}} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{PRP}}} \int_0^{\tau} \frac{(P_0^{\text{(sp)}})^2}{2\rho c}\sin^2(\omega t)\,dt$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the integral using $\langle\sin^2\rangle = 1/2$ over an integer number of cycles:** $$I_{\text{SPTA}} = \frac{\tau}{T_{\text{PRP}}} \cdot \frac{(P_0^{\text{(sp)}})^2}{2\rho c}$$ **Step 4 — Recognise the duty factor** $DF = \tau/T_{\text{PRP}}$: $$\boxed{I_{\text{SPTA}} = \text{DF} \times I_{\text{SPPA}} = \frac{\tau}{T_{\text{PRP}}} \times \frac{(P_0^{\text{(sp)}})^2}{2\rho c}}$$ This shows **why diagnostic ultrasound, despite having high $I_{\text{SPTP}}$, has a relatively low $I_{\text{SPTA}}$** — the small duty factor ($\approx 0.001$ – 0.01) limits the time-averaged energy deposition. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** An ultrasound beam in soft tissue ($\rho = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $c = 1540\,\text{m/s}$) has a peak pressure amplitude of $P_0 = 2\,\text{MPa}$. Calculate: (a) the particle velocity amplitude, (b) the time-average intensity, (c) the RMS intensity-equivalent pressure. **Step 1 — Particle velocity amplitude:** $$v_0 = \frac{P_0}{\rho c} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{(1000)(1540)} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{1.54 \times 10^6}$$ $$\boxed{v_0 \approx 1.299 \,\text{m/s}}$$ **Step 2 — Time-average intensity:** $$\langle I \rangle = \frac{P_0^2}{2\rho c} = \frac{(2 \times 10^6)^2}{2 \times 1.54 \times 10^6} = \frac{4 \times 10^{12}}{3.08 \times 10^6}$$ $$\boxed{\langle I \rangle \approx 1.299 \times 10^6 \,\text{W/m}^2 = 129.9 \,\text{W/cm}^2}$$ **Step 3 — RMS pressure:** $$P_{\text{rms}} = \frac{P_0}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{1.414}$$ $$\boxed{P_{\text{rms}} \approx 1.414 \,\text{MPa}}$$ **Verification using $P_{\text{rms}}$ formula:** $$\langle I \rangle = \frac{P_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho c} = \frac{(1.414 \times 10^6)^2}{1.54 \times 10^6} = 1.299 \times 10^6 \,\text{W/m}^2 \;\checkmark$$ --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Pressure & Intensity Characterisation Block Diagram The following block diagram maps the **hierarchical relationship** between acoustic pressure, particle velocity, and the five standardised beam-intensity descriptors used in medical imaging regulations. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["ACOUSTIC SOURCE

I equals Prms squared divided by Z"] S5["OUTPUT: Time Average Intensity I in W per m squared"] S6["OPTIONAL: Convert to mW per cm squared
Multiply by 10"] S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 S5 --> S6 ``` ### 4.4 Diagnostic Ultrasound Pulse Train Anatomy ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["ONE PULSE REPETITION PERIOD T PRP"] P2["PULSE ON

Listening time for echo reception"] P4["DUTY FACTOR DF equals tau divided by T PRP"] P5["IS PTA equals DF times IS PPA"] P6["MECHANICAL INDEX MI equals PNP divided by sqrt f"] P7["THERMAL INDEX TI from IS PTA"] P1 --> P2 P1 --> P3 P2 --> P4 P3 --> P4 P4 --> P5 P5 --> P7 P2 --> P6 ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q.A1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** > **Define acoustic pressure and acoustic intensity. State their SI units.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Acoustic Pressure ($p$):** It is the instantaneous difference between the total pressure at a point in a medium through which a sound wave is propagating and the ambient static pressure of the medium. > $$p = P_{\text{total}} - P_{\text{ambient}}$$ > **SI Unit:** **Pascal (Pa)** = **N/m²**. > > **Acoustic Intensity ($I$):** It is defined as the time-averaged rate of energy flow per unit area transported across a surface perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation. > $$\langle I \rangle = \frac{P_0^2}{2\rho c} = \frac{P_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho c}$$ > **SI Unit:** **Watts per square metre (W/m²)**. > *(3 marks — 1 mark each for definitions + 1 mark for units)* --- **Q.A2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** > **Differentiate between Spatial Peak Temporal Average (SPTA) intensity and Spatial Average Temporal Average (SATA) intensity. Why is $I_{\text{SPTA}}$ clinically more important than $I_{\text{SATA}}$?** **Model Answer:** | **Feature** | **$I_{\text{SPTA}}$** | **$I_{\text{SATA}}$** | |-------------|------------------------|------------------------| | **Spatial selection** | Maximum intensity anywhere in the beam | Intensity averaged over the entire beam cross-section | | **Numerical value** | Higher (because spatial maximum) | Lower (averaging reduces magnitude) | | **Clinical relevance** | Drives thermal effects at the focal region | Underestimates peak tissue exposure | | **FDA regulation** | Upper safety limit $\approx 720\,\text{mW/cm}^2$ | Used for spatial averaging standards | **Why SPTA matters clinically:** Tissue heating depends on the *local* energy deposited, not the average over the beam. A small focal hotspot may cause significant thermal damage even when the global average intensity is low. Hence **$I_{\text{SPTA}}$ is the regulated safety parameter for thermal bioeffects**. *(3 marks — 2 marks differentiation + 1 mark clinical reasoning)* --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, students answer ONE full 14-mark question with sub-parts. Model solutions for both internal-choice options are provided below.** --- ### 🔹 QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]` (14 Marks) **(a)** Starting from the 1-D wave equation, derive the relationship between acoustic pressure amplitude $P_0$ and particle velocity amplitude $v_0$ in a plane progressive wave. Hence obtain the expression for time-average acoustic intensity in terms of $P_0$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An ultrasound transducer operating at 5 MHz in soft tissue ($\rho = 1060\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $c = 1540\,\text{m/s}$) produces a peak acoustic pressure of $1.5\,\text{MPa}$ at the focal point. Calculate: (i) the particle velocity amplitude, (ii) the acoustic impedance of the medium, (iii) the time-average intensity at the focal point, and (iv) the RMS acoustic pressure. **(7 marks)** --- #### 🔸 Model Solution for Q.A(a): **Step 1 — Wave equation statement:** $$\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial x^2} = \frac{1}{c^2}\frac{\partial^2 p}{\partial t^2}$$ **Step 2 — Trial solution:** $$p(x,t) = P_0 \sin(\omega t - kx)$$ Verification (carried out earlier in §3.1) yields $c = \omega/k$. **Step 3 — Apply Euler's equation:** $$\rho \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = k P_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 4 — Integrate w.r.t. time:** $$v(x,t) = \frac{k P_0}{\rho \omega}\sin(\omega t - kx) = \frac{P_0}{\rho c}\sin(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 5 — Identify amplitude:** $$v_0 = \frac{P_0}{\rho c} \;\Longrightarrow\; \boxed{P_0 = \rho c v_0}$$ **Step 6 — Instantaneous intensity:** $$I(t) = p(t)\,v(t) = P_0 v_0 \sin^2(\omega t - kx)$$ **Step 7 — Time-average using $\langle\sin^2\rangle = 1/2$:** $$\boxed{\langle I \rangle = \frac{1}{2}\rho c v_0^2 = \frac{P_0^2}{2\rho c}}$$ --- #### 🔸 Model Solution for Q.A(b): **Given:** $f = 5\,\text{MHz}$, $\rho = 1060\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $c = 1540\,\text{m/s}$, $P_0 = 1.5\,\text{MPa} = 1.5 \times 10^6\,\text{Pa}$. **(i) Particle velocity amplitude:** $$v_0 = \frac{P_0}{\rho c} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{1060 \times 1540} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{1.6324 \times 10^6}$$ $$\boxed{v_0 \approx 0.919 \,\text{m/s}}$$ **(ii) Acoustic impedance of soft tissue:** $$Z = \rho c = 1060 \times 1540$$ $$\boxed{Z = 1.6324 \times 10^6 \,\text{Rayl} \;(\text{kg}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{s}^{-1})}$$ **(iii) Time-average intensity at focal point:** $$\langle I \rangle = \frac{P_0^2}{2\rho c} = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^6)^2}{2 \times 1.6324 \times 10^6} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{12}}{3.2648 \times 10^6}$$ $$\boxed{\langle I \rangle \approx 6.892 \times 10^5 \,\text{W/m}^2 = 68.92 \,\text{W/cm}^2}$$ **(iv) RMS acoustic pressure:** $$P_{\text{rms}} = \frac{P_0}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{1.4142}$$ $$\boxed{P_{\text{rms}} \approx 1.0607 \,\text{MPa}}$$ **Verification (cross-check using $P_{\text{rms}}$ formula):** $$\langle I \rangle = \frac{P_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho c} = \frac{(1.0607 \times 10^6)^2}{1.6324 \times 10^6} = 6.892 \times 10^5 \,\text{W/m}^2 \;\checkmark$$ --- ### 🔹 QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (14 Marks) **(a)** Explain the five standard intensity descriptors (SPTA, SATA, SPPA, SATP, SPTP) used to characterise an ultrasound beam. State the FDA regulatory upper limits for diagnostic ultrasound. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A pulsed ultrasound beam has a peak spatial pressure amplitude of $P_0 = 3\,\text{MPa}$, pulse duration $\tau = 1.5\,\mu\text{s}$, and pulse repetition period $T_{\text{PRP}} = 1\,\text{ms}$. Operating in water ($\rho = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $c = 1500\,\text{m/s}$). Calculate: (i) the duty factor, (ii) the SPPA intensity, (iii) the SPTA intensity, (iv) state whether the SPTA complies with the FDA $720\,\text{mW/cm}^2$ derated limit. **(7 marks)** --- #### 🔸 Model Solution for Q.B(a): > [!NOTE] > **The Five Standard Intensity Descriptors:** **1. $I_{\text{SPTA}}$ (Spatial-Peak Temporal-Average):** The maximum value of the temporal-average intensity found anywhere within the ultrasound beam. It is the parameter most directly linked to thermal bioeffects. **2. $I_{\text{SATA}}$ (Spatial-Average Temporal-Average):** The temporal-average intensity, spatially averaged across the entire beam cross-section. $$I_{\text{SATA}} = \frac{1}{A_{\text{beam}}} \int_A \langle I \rangle \,dA$$ **3. $I_{\text{SPPA}}$ (Spatial-Peak Pulse-Average):** The maximum value of the pulse-average intensity found anywhere in the beam — the intensity averaged over the pulse duration at the spatial peak. **4. $I_{\text{SATP}}$ (Spatial-Average Temporal-Peak):** The peak instantaneous intensity spatially averaged over the beam cross-section. **5. $I_{\text{SPTP}}$ (Spatial-Peak Temporal-Peak):** The maximum value of the instantaneous intensity anywhere in the beam — typically the largest numerical descriptor. > [!IMPORTANT] > **FDA Regulatory Limits (Track 3 — Pre-Amendments):** > - $I_{\text{SPTA}} \leq 720\,\text{mW/cm}^2$ > - $I_{\text{SPPA}} \leq 190\,\text{W/cm}^2$ > - $I_{\text{m}} \leq 310\,\text{W/cm}^2$ (derated $I_{\text{SPTP}}$) > - Mechanical Index $\text{MI} \leq 1.9$ > - Thermal Index $\text{TI} \leq 6.0$ **Ordering (typically):** $I_{\text{SPTP}} \geq I_{\text{SPPA}} \geq I_{\text{SPTA}} \geq I_{\text{SATA}}$. --- #### 🔸 Model Solution for Q.B(b): **Given:** $P_0 = 3\,\text{MPa} = 3 \times 10^6\,\text{Pa}$, $\tau = 1.5\,\mu\text{s} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s}$, $T_{\text{PRP}} = 1\,\text{ms} = 10^{-3}\,\text{s}$, $\rho = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $c = 1500\,\text{m/s}$. **(i) Duty Factor:** $$\text{DF} = \frac{\tau}{T_{\text{PRP}}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-3}}$$ $$\boxed{\text{DF} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$ **(ii) SPPA Intensity:** $$I_{\text{SPPA}} = \frac{P_0^2}{2\rho c} = \frac{(3 \times 10^6)^2}{2 \times 1000 \times 1500} = \frac{9 \times 10^{12}}{3 \times 10^6}$$ $$\boxed{I_{\text{SPPA}} = 3 \times 10^6 \,\text{W/m}^2 = 300 \,\text{W/cm}^2}$$ **(iii) SPTA Intensity:** $$I_{\text{SPTA}} = \text{DF} \times I_{\text{SPPA}} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 300\,\text{W/cm}^2$$ $$\boxed{I_{\text{SPTA}} = 0.45 \,\text{W/cm}^2 = 450 \,\text{mW/cm}^2}$$ **(iv) FDA Compliance Check:** Computed $I_{\text{SPTA}} = 450\,\text{mW/cm}^2$. FDA Track 3 limit for derated $I_{\text{SPTA}} = 720\,\text{mW/cm}^2$. > [!TIP] > **Conclusion:** Since $450\,\text{mW/cm}^2 < 720\,\text{mW/cm}^2$, the beam **COMPLIES** with the FDA regulatory upper limit. > [!WARNING] > **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks:** > 1. **Unit conversion slip:** Students write $I_{\text{SPPA}}$ in W/m² but forget to convert to W/cm² for FDA comparison. **Always convert $1\,\text{W/cm}^2 = 10^4\,\text{W/m}^2$.** > 2. **Mixing in-water and in-situ intensities:** The FDA limit of $720\,\text{mW/cm}^2$ applies to **derated** (in-tissue) values. If the question specifies attenuation $\alpha$ and depth $z$, apply the derating formula $I_{\text{in-situ}} = I_{\text{water}} \cdot 10^{-0.1 \alpha f z}$ before comparing. > 3. **Confusing $I_{\text{SPTA}}$ with $I_{\text{SPPA}}$:** Remember $I_{\text{SPTA}} = \text{DF} \times I_{\text{SPPA}}$, **NOT** $I_{\text{SPPA}} \times T_{\text{PRP}}$. Duty factor is dimensionless — write it explicitly. > 4. **Forgetting $\rho c$:** A common error is using the wrong $\rho c$ for the medium — water is $\approx 1.5 \times 10^6\,\text{Rayl}$, soft tissue $\approx 1.6 \times 10^6\,\text{Rayl}$. > 5. **Skipping the final numerical check:** Examiners allocate **1 mark** for the FDA comparison statement. Always write *"... therefore the beam complies / does not comply with FDA Track 3 limits."* --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Acoustic Pressure and Intensities Within the Beam** - ✅ **Acoustic pressure** $p$ is the *oscillatory* deviation from ambient pressure, **NOT** the absolute static pressure. - ✅ **Acoustic intensity** is *time-averaged power per unit area*, **NOT** instantaneous intensity (unless explicitly stated). - ✅ The **master coupling equation** $P_0 = \rho c v_0$ relates pressure and particle velocity through the **acoustic impedance $Z = \rho c$**. - ✅ Intensity is **quadratic** in pressure: $I = P_0^2/(2\rho c)$ — *doubling $P_0$ quadruples $I$*. - ✅ Use **RMS pressure** when measurement instruments report RMS values: $I = P_{\text{rms}}^2/(\rho c)$. - ✅ The **acoustic beam is non-uniform**: spatial peak ≠ spatial average. - ✅ The **five canonical descriptors** are: **SPTA, SATA, SPPA, SATP, SPTP** — always specify both spatial and temporal selection. - ✅ **SPTA drives Thermal Index (TI)**; **SPTP/peak negative pressure drives Mechanical Index (MI)**. - ✅ **$I_{\text{SPTA}} = \text{Duty Factor} \times I_{\text{SPPA}}$** — the small duty factor ($\sim 0.001$) keeps SPTA low even when SPPA is high. - ✅ **FDA Track 3 upper limits:** $I_{\text{SPTA}} \leq 720\,\text{mW/cm}^2$, $I_{\text{SPPA}} \leq 190\,\text{W/cm}^2$, $\text{MI} \leq 1.9$. - ✅ **In-situ (derated) intensities** use $I_{\text{in-situ}} = I_{\text{water}} \cdot 10^{-0.1 \alpha f z}$ with $\alpha = 0.3\,\text{dB/(cm·MHz)}$ for soft tissue. - ✅ **Unit conversion:** **$1\,\text{W/cm}^2 = 10^4\,\text{W/m}^2$** — keep it consistent across all sub-parts of a numerical. - ✅ **Expected ordering:** $I_{\text{SPTP}} \geq I_{\text{SPPA}} \geq I_{\text{SPTA}} \geq I_{\text{SATA}}$ — a useful cross-check in numericals. - ✅ **Derivation must start from wave equation** + **Euler's equation** for full marks on the 7-mark sub-question. ---

topics.title: Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Medical Imaging, **Power** is a foundational scalar physical quantity defined as the **time rate of doing work** or, equivalently, the **time rate of transfer or conversion of energy**. It quantifies how quickly a system (such as an X-ray generator, ultrasound transducer, or MRI gradient coil) can deliver, dissipate, or absorb energy.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$
P = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\Delta W}{\Delta t} = \frac{dW}{dt}
$$

The SI unit of power is the **Watt (W)**, where **1 W = 1 J/s = 1 N·m/s**. Other commonly used units in medical imaging include milliwatts (mW) for laser imaging systems, kilowatts (kW) for X-ray tube ratings, and decibels (dB) for acoustic power in ultrasound.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Power is a prerequisite concept for understanding X-ray tube loading, exposure timing, ultrasound intensity (spatial peak temporal average - ISPTA), and the energy budget of ionizing radiation delivery to patients. Mastery of this topic is essential before progressing to the production of X-rays and interaction of radiation with matter.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two people carrying identical 50 kg bags of sand up a flight of stairs. Person A takes 2 minutes, while Person B takes 10 minutes. Both perform the same **work** (force × displacement), but Person A delivers the energy **much faster**. Person A is, therefore, more **powerful**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Medical Analogy:** Consider an X-ray tube operating at 100 kVp and 200 mA versus one at 80 kVp and 100 mA. The first delivers twice the power to the anode target. This directly affects patient dose, image quality, heat loading, and exposure time — all critical parameters in diagnostic radiology.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **1 Watt (W)** = **1 J/s** = **10⁷ erg/s** = **1.341 × 10⁻³ horsepower (hp)**
- **1 horsepower (hp)** = **745.7 W** (mechanical equivalent)
- **Typical X-ray tube power rating:** 25 kW to 100 kW (for diagnostic radiography); 0.1 to 2 kW (for mammography)
- **Typical ultrasound acoustic power:** 1 mW to 500 mW
- **Typical laser power in optical imaging:** 1 mW to 100 mW
- **Mechanical equivalent of heat:** **1 cal = 4.186 J**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Power

The concept of power is decomposed into the following logical building blocks:

- **Energy Source Stage:** Electrical mains (typically 220–240 V AC, 50 Hz in India) or stored energy (capacitor banks, batteries).
- **Conversion Stage:** Transformers, rectifiers, or inverters that modify voltage/current to imaging-specific levels.
- **Delivery Stage:** The energy is delivered to the imaging transducer (X-ray tube anode, piezoelectric crystal, RF coil) at a controlled rate.
- **Utilization Stage:** The energy is converted into useful imaging radiation (X-ray photons, acoustic pulses, RF signals) and waste heat.

### 2.2 Power in Electrical Circuits (Direct Current)

For a DC circuit carrying current $I$ through a potential difference $V$:

$$
P = V \cdot I
$$

This is the general electrical power formula. For purely resistive loads, applying Ohm's Law ($V = IR$) yields three equivalent forms:

$$
P = VI = I^2 R = \frac{V^2}{R}
$$

### 2.3 Power in Alternating Current (AC) Circuits

In AC systems, instantaneous power oscillates. The **average (real) power** dissipated is:

$$
P_{avg} = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \cos\phi
$$

where $\phi$ is the **phase angle** between voltage and current waveforms, and $\cos\phi$ is the **power factor**. For purely resistive loads, $\cos\phi = 1$.

The **apparent power** is $S = V_{rms} I_{rms}$ measured in **volt-amperes (VA)**, while the **reactive power** is $Q = V_{rms} I_{rms} \sin\phi$ measured in **volt-amperes reactive (VAR)**.

### 2.4 Power in Electromagnetic Radiation

For an electromagnetic wave, the **radiant flux** or **radiant power** is the energy crossing a surface per unit time. If $E$ is the energy of $n$ photons of frequency $\nu$:

$$
P = \frac{n h \nu}{t}
$$

The **intensity** (power per unit area) is:

$$
I_{rad} = \frac{P}{A}
$$

with units of **W/m²**.

### 2.5 Power in Ultrasound (Acoustic)

In medical ultrasound, power is delivered as **acoustic power** ($P_{ac}$) by the transducer. The **spatial peak temporal average intensity** ($I_{SPTA}$) is a key safety metric:

$$
I_{SPTA} = \frac{P_{ac}}{A_{eff}} \quad \text{(at peak location, time-averaged)}
$$

where $A_{eff}$ is the effective radiating area of the transducer.

### 2.6 Power in X-ray Tubes

The electrical power delivered to an X-ray tube is:

$$
P_{tube} = kVp \cdot mA
$$

For example, a tube operating at 100 kVp and 500 mA delivers **50 kW** to the anode. Only about **1%** is converted to X-rays; the remaining **99%** is wasted as heat, requiring anode cooling systems.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| \# | Formula | Description | SI Unit | Medical Imaging Application |
|---|---------|-------------|---------|------------------------------|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{W}{t}$ | Power = Work / Time | W (J/s) | Energy delivery rate, dose rate basis |
| 2 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Electrical Power | W | X-ray generator, MRI gradient amplifiers |
| 3 | $P = I^2 R$ | Power Dissipation (Joule Heating) | W | Anode heat load, cable heating |
| 4 | $P = \dfrac{V^2}{R}$ | Power (Alternative form) | W | Resistor network analysis |
| 5 | $P_{avg} = V_{rms} I_{rms} \cos\phi$ | AC Average Power | W | High-voltage generator analysis |
| 6 | $P = kVp \times mA$ | X-ray Tube Electrical Power | W or kW | Radiographic exposure rating |
| 7 | $I_{rad} = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Radiant Intensity | W/m² | Beam profile, dosimetry |
| 8 | $I_{SPTA} = \dfrac{P_{ac}}{A_{eff}}$ | Ultrasound Intensity | W/m² | FDA safety limits (720 mW/cm² max) |
| 9 | $P = n h \nu / t$ | Photon Power (n photons/sec) | W | Laser power in optical coherence tomography |
| 10 | $1 \, hp = 745.7 \, W$ | Mechanical Horsepower | W | X-ray tube anode rotation motor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Power ratings determine the size of cooling systems, electrical infrastructure, shielding requirements, and patient safety thresholds. An X-ray tube's heat capacity (typically 100–800 kHU — kilo Heat Units) is a direct consequence of its power dissipation profile.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospital Electrical Design:** Imaging suites require dedicated 3-phase power lines, isolation transformers, and emergency backup (UPS) systems rated for the peak power of CT, MRI, and X-ray units.
- **Anode Heat Management:** The 99% heat waste in X-ray tubes dictates the use of rotating anodes, heat exchangers, and tube housing oil baths.
- **Ultrasound Safety Regulations:** The FDA limits $I_{SPTA}$ to **720 mW/cm²** to prevent bioeffects (cavitation, thermal damage).
- **MRI Gradient Coils:** Modern EPI sequences require gradient amplifiers capable of **1–2 MW peak power** for rapid switching.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Power from Work and Time

Starting from the definition of work, $W = F \cdot d$ (force times displacement in the direction of force):

$$
P = \frac{W}{t} = \frac{F \cdot d}{t}
$$

Since velocity $v = d/t$:

$$
P = F \cdot v
$$

This is the fundamental **mechanical power** equation — a crucial relationship for understanding moving components such as rotating anodes in X-ray tubes.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Electrical Power $P = VI$ from First Principles

**Step 1:** Start with power as the rate of energy transfer:

$$
P = \frac{W}{t}
$$

**Step 2:** Energy delivered by moving charge $Q$ through a potential difference $V$ is:

$$
W = V \cdot Q
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the power equation:

$$
P = \frac{V \cdot Q}{t}
$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that current $I = Q/t$:

$$
P = V \cdot I
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Equivalent Forms $P = I^2 R$ and $P = V^2 / R$

**Step 1:** From Ohm's Law: $V = I \cdot R$

**Step 2:** Substitute $V = IR$ into $P = VI$:

$$
P = (IR) \cdot I = I^2 R
$$

**Step 3:** From Ohm's Law rearranged: $I = V/R$

**Step 4:** Substitute $I = V/R$ into $P = VI$:

$$
P = V \cdot \frac{V}{R} = \frac{V^2}{R}
$$

**Step 5:** These three forms are equivalent for purely resistive DC circuits:

$$
P = VI = I^2 R = \frac{V^2}{R}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Average Power in AC Circuits

**Step 1:** Instantaneous AC voltage and current:

$$
v(t) = V_0 \sin(\omega t), \quad i(t) = I_0 \sin(\omega t - \phi)
$$

**Step 2:** Instantaneous power:

$$
p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t) = V_0 I_0 \sin(\omega t)\sin(\omega t - \phi)
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the trigonometric identity $\sin A \sin B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)]$:

$$
p(t) = \frac{V_0 I_0}{2}[\cos(\phi) - \cos(2\omega t - \phi)]
$$

**Step 4:** Average over one full cycle. The cosine term with $2\omega t$ averages to zero:

$$
P_{avg} = \frac{V_0 I_0}{2}\cos\phi
$$

**Step 5:** Recognize that $V_{rms} = V_0/\sqrt{2}$ and $I_{rms} = I_0/\sqrt{2}$:

$$
P_{avg} = V_0 I_0 \cdot \frac{1}{2} \cdot \cos\phi = \frac{V_0}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{I_0}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \cos\phi
$$

**Step 6:** Final result:

$$
P_{avg} = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} \cdot \cos\phi
$$

### 3.5 Derivation 5: X-ray Tube Power Rating

**Step 1:** The high voltage applied across the X-ray tube is $kVp$ (peak kilovoltage). The tube current is $mA$ (milliamperes).

**Step 2:** Convert to SI base units:

$$
V = kVp \times 10^3 \text{ V}, \quad I = mA \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply $P = V \cdot I$:

$$
P_{tube} = (kVp \times 10^3) \cdot (mA \times 10^{-3}) = kVp \cdot mA \text{ (in watts)}
$$

**Step 4:** For typical radiography: $kVp = 100$, $mA = 500$:

$$
P_{tube} = 100 \times 500 = 50{,}000 \text{ W} = 50 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 5:** Heat Units (HU) generated per exposure:

$$
HU = kVp \times mA \times s
$$

where $s$ is exposure time in seconds. For 0.1 s exposure: $HU = 100 \times 500 \times 0.1 = 5000 \, HU$.

### 3.6 Derivation 6: Ultrasound Intensity from Acoustic Power

**Step 1:** Acoustic power $P_{ac}$ is the total ultrasonic power radiated by the transducer face.

**Step 2:** The effective radiating area is:

$$
A_{eff} = \pi r^2
$$

where $r$ is the radius of the active element (for a circular transducer).

**Step 3:** Average intensity:

$$
I_{avg} = \frac{P_{ac}}{A_{eff}}
$$

**Step 4:** Spatial peak temporal average intensity is greater than average intensity by a beam non-uniformity ratio (BNR), typically 2–10:

$$
I_{SPTA} = BNR \cdot I_{avg}
$$
