| RGB to YIQ Q-channel | $Q = 0.211R - 0.523G + 0.312B$ | Magenta-green axis |
| RGB to HSI Hue | $H = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{0.5[(R-G)+(R-B)]}{\sqrt{(R-G)^2 + (R-B)(G-B)}}\right)$ | Radians or degrees |
| RGB to HSI Saturation | $S = 1 - \frac{3 \cdot \min(R,G,B)}{R+G+B}$ | [0,1] range |
| RGB to HSI Intensity | $I = (R + G + B) / 3$ | [0,1] range |
| Colour Difference $\Delta E$ | $\Delta E = \sqrt{(L_1-L_2)^2 + (a_1-a_2)^2 + (b_1-b_2)^2}$ | CIE Lab perceptual metric |
| Dominant Wavelength | Solved graphically on CIE diagram | Projection from white point |
| Gamut Boundary | Convex hull of three primaries on CIE diagram | Defines reproducible colour set |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility**
> - **Surgical endoscopy systems** (Olympus, Karl Storz, Stryker) capture RGB streams and apply HSI-based tissue classification algorithms in real time.
> - **Digital pathology scanners** (Hamamatsu NanoZoomer, Aperio) use sRGB and CIE Lab spaces to ensure cross-site colour reproducibility for cancer grading.
> - **Colour calibration phantoms** (e.g., the Macbeth ColorChecker) are imaged routinely in radiology quality assurance to verify display chain integrity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Normalized RGB to HSI Conversion

**Problem Setup:** Given an arbitrary RGB triplet with each component normalized in [0, 1], derive the Hue (H), Saturation (S), and Intensity (I) components.

**Step 1 — Intensity (I) Definition**
Intensity is defined as the arithmetic mean of the three RGB channels, representing the achromatic brightness:

$$I = \frac{R + G + B}{3}$$

This follows directly from the property that grey-level intensity carries no chromatic information, and equal contributions of R, G, B yield neutral grey.

**Step 2 — Saturation (S) Definition**
Saturation quantifies the degree to which a colour departs from the neutral grey of equal intensity. A purely white pixel (R = G = B) has zero saturation, while a fully saturated colour has one or two channels at zero:

$$S = 1 - \frac{3 \cdot \min(R, G, B)}{R + G + B}$$

**Derivation Logic:** Let $m = \min(R, G, B)$ and $\Sigma = R + G + B$. For a pure grey pixel, $R = G = B$, so $m = \Sigma / 3$, and $S = 1 - 3(\Sigma/3)/\Sigma = 0$. For a pure primary (e.g., R=1, G=0, B=0), $m = 0$, so $S = 1$. This satisfies both boundary conditions.

**Step 3 — Hue (H) Definition**
The Hue represents the dominant colour attribute on the colour wheel. It is derived from the angle of the chromaticity vector in the (R, G, B) colour space:

$$H = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{0.5 \cdot [(R - G) + (R - B)]}{\sqrt{(R - G)^2 + (R - B)(G - B)}}\right)$$

**Derivation Logic:** The numerator represents twice the projection of the colour vector onto the red axis in an equilateral RGB colour space. The denominator is the magnitude of the chromaticity deviation vector. The arccosine yields an angle in [0°, 180°] for the RG sector; for GB and BR sectors, a correction rule applies:

$$H = \begin{cases} \theta & \text{if } B \le G \\ 360° - \theta & \text{if } B > G \end{cases}$$

where $\theta$ is the arccosine result converted to degrees.

**Step 4 — Verification with a Worked Example**

Let $R = 0.6$, $G = 0.4$, $B = 0.2$.

**Step 4a — Intensity:**

$$I = \frac{0.6 + 0.4 + 0.2}{3} = \frac{1.2}{3} = 0.4$$

**Step 4b — Saturation:**

$$S = 1 - \frac{3 \times \min(0.6, 0.4, 0.2)}{0.6 + 0.4 + 0.2} = 1 - \frac{3 \times 0.2}{1.2} = 1 - 0.5 = 0.5$$

**Step 4c — Hue (numerator and denominator):**

$$\text{Num} = 0.5 \times [(0.6 - 0.4) + (0.6 - 0.2)] = 0.5 \times [0.2 + 0.4] = 0.5 \times 0.6 = 0.3$$

$$\text{Den} = \sqrt{(0.6 - 0.4)^2 + (0.6 - 0.2)(0.4 - 0.2)} = \sqrt{(0.2)^2 + (0.4)(0.2)} = \sqrt{0.04 + 0.08} = \sqrt{0.12} \approx 0.3464$$

$$\theta = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{0.3}{0.3464}\right) = \cos^{-1}(0.8660) = 30°$$

Since $B = 0.2 \le G = 0.4$, the result is final: $H = 30°$ (orange-red sector).

**Step 5 — Final HSI Value:** $H = 30°$, $S = 0.5$, $I = 0.4$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: CIE Tristimulus Value Computation

**Problem Setup:** Compute the CIE 1931 tristimulus values for a given spectral power distribution.

**Step 1 — Define the Spectral Variables**
- $S(\lambda)$ = spectral power distribution of the illuminant (e.g., D65 standard daylight)
- $\bar{x}(\lambda), \bar{y}(\lambda), \bar{z}(\lambda)$ = CIE 1931 standard observer colour-matching functions (tabulated every 5 nm or 10 nm)

**Step 2 — Compute the Luminance Denominator Integral**

$$D = \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda) \bar{y}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$

This integral represents the total photopic (luminance) response of the standard observer to the illuminant.

**Step 3 — Compute the Normalization Constant**

$$k = \frac{100}{D}$$

The factor of 100 ensures that the Y tristimulus value equals exactly 100 for a perfect white surface reflecting 100% of the illuminant.

**Step 4 — Compute X, Y, Z Tristimulus Values**

$$X = k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda) \bar{x}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$
$$Y = k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda) \bar{y}(\lambda) \, d\lambda = 100$$
$$Z = k \int_{380}^{780} S(\lambda) \bar{z}(\lambda) \, d\lambda$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Chromaticity Coordinates**

$$x = \frac{X}{X + Y + Z}, \quad y = \frac{Y}{X + Y + Z}, \quad z = \frac{Z}{X + Y + Z}$$

**Step 6 — Verification:** Confirm that $x + y + z = 1$. For D65 illuminant, the canonical CIE 1931 chromaticity coordinates are:

$$x_{D65} = 0.3127, \quad y_{D65} = 0.3290$$

This can be used as a validation check.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: RGB ↔ YIQ Forward and Inverse Transform

**Step 1 — Forward Transformation (RGB → YIQ)**

The YIQ matrix is derived from the NTSC colour television broadcast standard. The forward transform is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} Y \\ I \\ Q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.299 & 0.587 & 0.114 \\ 0.596 & -0.274 & -0.322 \\ 0.211 & -0.523 & 0.312 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} R \\ G \\ B \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Inverse Transformation (YIQ → RGB)**

Inverting the 3×3 matrix yields the recovery equations:

$$R = 1.000Y + 0.956I + 0.621Q$$
$$G = 1.000Y - 0.272I - 0.647Q$$
$$B = 1.000Y - 1.106I + 1.703Q$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example**

Given $R = 200, G = 100, B = 50$ (out of 255), normalize and convert:

**Step 3a — Normalize to [0, 1]:**

$$R_n = 200/255 \approx 0.784, \quad G_n = 100/255 \approx 0.392, \quad B_n = 50/255 \approx 0.196$$

**Step 3b — Compute Y:**

$$Y = 0.299 \times 0.784 + 0.587 \times 0.392 + 0.114 \times 0.196 = 0.2344 + 0.2301 + 0.0223 \approx 0.4868$$

**Step 3c — Compute I:**

$$I = 0.596 \times 0.784 - 0.274 \times 0.392 - 0.322 \times 0.196 = 0.4673 - 0.1074 - 0.0631 \approx 0.2968$$

**Step 3d — Compute Q:**

$$Q = 0.211 \times 0.784 - 0.523 \times 0.392 + 0.312 \times 0.196 = 0.1654 - 0.2050 + 0.0612 \approx 0.0216$$

**Step 3e — Verification using inverse transform:**

$$R' = 1.000(0.4868) + 0.956(0.2968) + 0.621(0.0216) \approx 0.4868 + 0.2837 + 0.0134 \approx 0.784$$

This recovers $R_n = 0.784$ ✓ (round-trip verified).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Colour Models

| Model | Type | Channels | Strength | Weakness | Medical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **RGB** | Additive | R, G, B | Hardware-native, intuitive | Non-perceptual (unequal spacing) | Camera/display pipeline |
| **CMY(K)** | Subtractive | C, M, Y, (K) | Accurate for printing | Dark gamut, ink waste | Surgical atlas printing |
| **HSI** | Perceptual | H, S, I | Decouples brightness/chromaticity | Singular at $S=0$ | Histopathology segmentation |
| **HSV** | Perceptual | H, S, V | Intuitive for colour pickers | Non-uniform hue spacing | Skin lesion dermatoscopy |
| **YIQ** | Broadcast | Y, I, Q | Backward-compatible B&W | Legacy system | NTSC medical video archives |
| **YCbCr** | Digital video | Y, Cb, Cr | Compression-friendly | Not perceptually uniform | DICOM video streams |
| **CIE XYZ** | Reference | X, Y, Z | Device-independent | Not intuitive | Calibration standards |
| **CIE Lab** | Perceptual | L*, a*, b* | Approximately uniform | Complex computation | $\Delta E$ colour difference QA |

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the trichromatic theory of colour vision. List the three types of cone photoreceptors and their peak spectral sensitivities.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The trichromatic theory, proposed by Thomas Young in 1802 and later refined by Hermann von Helmholtz, states that the human eye perceives all visible colours through the combined responses of three distinct types of cone photoreceptors located in the retina. Each cone type contains a different photopigment (opsin) with peak sensitivity at a different region of the visible spectrum:

1. **L-cones (Long wavelength):** Peak sensitivity at **~564 nm** — responsible for the perception of **red** light.
2. **M-cones (Medium wavelength):** Peak sensitivity at **~534 nm** — responsible for the perception of **green** light.
3. **S-cones (Short wavelength):** Peak sensitivity at **~420 nm** — responsible for the perception of **blue** light.

The relative stimulation of these three cone types, integrated by the visual cortex, allows the human visual system to discriminate approximately **10 million distinct colours** under photopic (well-lit) conditions. The trichromatic theory is the physiological basis for the RGB colour model used in all digital colour imaging systems.

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram. What are chromaticity coordinates?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram** is a 2D horseshoe-shaped plot developed by the Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage (CIE) that represents all colours perceivable by the standard human observer. It is constructed from the three CIE tristimulus values (X, Y, Z) by normalizing them by their sum:

$$x = \frac{X}{X + Y + Z}, \quad y = \frac{Y}{X + Y + Z}, \quad z = \frac{Z}{X + Y + Z}$$

Since $x + y + z = 1$, only two chromaticity coordinates (**x, y**) are independent and sufficient to uniquely specify a colour's chromaticity (independent of luminance). The boundary of the horseshoe represents the pure spectral colours (monochromatic wavelengths from 380 nm to 780 nm), while the straight line connecting the extremes is the **"line of purples"** representing non-spectral magenta hues. Any real device or illuminant can be plotted as a point (x, y) within the diagram, and the convex hull of three primary points defines the device's **colour gamut**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain the fundamental difference between **additive (RGB)** and **subtractive (CMY)** colour models. Derive the mathematical relationship between the two models. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **HSI colour model** in detail. Derive the expressions for Hue, Saturation, and Intensity from the normalized RGB values. Compute the HSI values for the pixel $R = 0.5$, $G = 0.5$, $B = 0.0$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The **RGB model** is an **additive** system used in devices that emit light (monitors, TVs, digital cameras). Colours are produced by adding red, green, and blue light components. White is the sum of all three primaries at maximum intensity, while black is the absence of all three.

The **CMY model** is a **subtractive** system used in devices that reflect light (printed media, hardcopy outputs). Colours are produced by subtracting (absorbing) complementary wavelengths from white incident light. Cyan ink absorbs red, magenta absorbs green, and yellow absorbs blue.

**Mathematical Relationship Derivation:**

In the subtractive model, an ink of colour $C$ placed on white paper removes the red component, leaving the green and blue components reflected:

$$C = 1 - R, \quad M = 1 - G, \quad Y = 1 - B$$

The inverse relationship (recovering RGB from CMY):

$$R = 1 - C, \quad G = 1 - M, \quad B = 1 - Y$$

In **matrix form:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} C \\ M \\ Y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} - \begin{bmatrix} R \\ G \\ B \end{bmatrix}$$

**Why the CMYK extension exists:** Real CMY inks are imperfect — overlapping all three produces a muddy dark brown rather than true black. Hence a separate **K (Key/Black)** channel is added to improve contrast and reduce ink consumption:

$$K = \min(C, M, Y)$$
$$C' = C - K, \quad M' = M - K, \quad Y' = Y - K$$

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

The **HSI (Hue, Saturation, Intensity)** model separates the achromatic brightness component (I) from the two chromatic components (H, S), aligning with human perceptual dimensions.

**Hue (H):** Represents the dominant wavelength/pure colour, measured in degrees on the colour wheel (0° = Red, 60° = Yellow, 120° = Green, 180° = Cyan, 240° = Blue, 300° = Magenta).

**Saturation (S):** Represents the purity of the colour, ranging from 0 (neutral grey) to 1 (fully saturated).

**Intensity (I):** Represents the overall brightness, ranging from 0 (black) to 1 (white).

**Derivation (refer to Section 3.1 above for full derivation logic):**

$$I = \frac{R + G + B}{3}, \quad S = 1 - \frac{3 \cdot \min(R, G, B)}{R + G + B}$$

$$H = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{0.5[(R - G) + (R - B)]}{\sqrt{(R - G)^2 + (R - B)(G - B)}}\right)$$

**Worked Computation for R = 0.5, G = 0.5, B = 0.0:**

**Step 1 — Intensity:**

$$I = \frac{0.5 + 0.5 + 0.0}{3} = \frac{1.0}{3} \approx 0.3333$$

**Step 2 — Saturation:**

$$S = 1 - \frac{3 \times \min(0.5, 0.5, 0.0)}{0.5 + 0.5 + 0.0} = 1 - \frac{3 \times 0.0}{1.0} = 1 - 0 = 1.0$$

**Step 3 — Hue:**

$$\text{Num} = 0.5 \times [(0.5 - 0.5) + (0.5 - 0.0)] = 0.5 \times [0 + 0.5] = 0.25$$

$$\text{Den} = \sqrt{(0.5 - 0.5)^2 + (0.5 - 0.0)(0.5 - 0.0)} = \sqrt{0 + 0.25} = 0.5$$

$$\theta = \cos^{-1}\left(\frac{0.25}{0.5}\right) = \cos^{-1}(0.5) = 60°$$

Since $B = 0.0 \le G = 0.5$, $H = 60°$ (pure yellow, as expected for a yellow pixel).

**Final HSI:** $H = 60°$, $S = 1.0$, $I = 0.333$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram** in detail. Explain how tristimulus values X, Y, Z and chromaticity coordinates x, y are computed from a given spectral power distribution. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **YIQ colour model**. Derive the forward transformation matrix from RGB to YIQ. Justify why the Y channel uses the coefficients 0.299, 0.587, and 0.114. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The **CIE 1931 chromaticity diagram** is the universal reference standard for representing colour in a device-independent manner. It was developed by the CIE based on colour-matching experiments with a standard 2° field of view observer. (Refer to Section 2.3 and Section 3.2 of these notes for the full mathematical framework.)

**Tristimulus Computation Procedure:**

1. **Obtain the spectral power distribution $S(\lambda)$** of the illuminant (e.g., D65, A, F11) sampled at regular wavelength intervals (typically 5 nm or 10 nm) over the visible range 380–780 nm.

2. **Multiply by the CIE standard observer functions $\bar{x}(\lambda), \bar{y}(\lambda), \bar{z}(\lambda)$** and integrate over wavelength:

$$X = k \int S(\lambda) \bar{x}(\lambda) d\lambda, \quad Y = k \int S(\lambda) \bar{y}(\lambda) d\lambda, \quad Z = k \int S(\lambda) \bar{z}(\lambda) d\lambda$$

3. **Normalize** using $k$ such that $Y = 100$ for a perfect white diffuser.

4. **Compute chromaticity coordinates:**

$$x = X/(X+Y+Z), \quad y = Y/(X+Y+Z)$$

The resulting point (x, y) is plotted on the chromaticity diagram. The horseshoe boundary represents monochromatic spectral colours, and the interior contains all real-world (non-monochromatic) perceivable colours.

**Applications in Medical Imaging:**
- Defining the **sRGB colour space** used in medical displays
- Specifying **gamut** of endoscopic cameras
- Calibrating **light sources** for surgical microscopy
- Quantifying **colour difference** ($\Delta E$) between imaging sessions

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

The **YIQ model** was developed for the **NTSC (National Television System Committee)** colour television broadcasting standard. It separates the image into a **luminance** component (Y) and two **chrominance** components (I and Q), exploiting the fact that the human visual system is more sensitive to brightness variations than to colour variations.

**Forward Transformation Matrix:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} Y \\ I \\ Q \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.299 & 0.587 & 0.114 \\ 0.596 & -0.274 & -0.322 \\ 0.211 & -0.523 & 0.312 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} R \\ G \\ B \end{bmatrix}$$

**Justification of the Y coefficients (0.299, 0.587, 0.114):**

The coefficients of the Y channel correspond to the **luminance contribution** of each primary colour to the perceived brightness, weighted by the **photopic luminosity function** $V(\lambda)$ of the human eye:

- The **green channel** has the highest coefficient (0.587) because the human eye's photopic sensitivity peaks near 555 nm — which lies in the green region of the spectrum. The eye is therefore most sensitive to variations in green light.
- The **red channel** has the second-highest coefficient (0.299) because red wavelengths (~620 nm) still contribute substantially to luminance perception.
- The **blue channel** has the lowest coefficient (0.114) because the human eye is relatively insensitive to short wavelengths (~470 nm), and because $V(\lambda)$ is significantly smaller in the blue region.

The three coefficients sum to **0.299 + 0.587 + 0.114 = 1.000**, ensuring that a pure white pixel (R = G = B = 1) yields Y = 1 (maximum luminance).

**Justification of the I and Q axes:**
- The **I axis (In-phase)** is aligned with the orange-cyan opponent channel of human colour vision and is encoded with wider bandwidth (~1.3 MHz).
- The **Q axis (Quadrature)** is aligned with the magenta-green opponent channel and is encoded with narrower bandwidth (~0.4 MHz).

This unequal bandwidth allocation leverages the human visual system's reduced sensitivity to fine chromatic detail, allowing efficient NTSC transmission.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the normalization factor in CIE XYZ** — Students often write the integrals without the $k$ factor, losing 1–2 marks. Always include $k = 100 / \int S(\lambda) \bar{y}(\lambda) d\lambda$ in the final formula.
>
> 2. **Confusing additive vs subtractive primaries** — RGB adds light; CMY subtracts light. Mixing up the direction of the formula $C = 1 - R$ is a common error.
>
> 3. **Hue singularity at low saturation** — The $\cos^{-1}$ formula becomes numerically unstable when $S \approx 0$. Examiners may deduct marks if the answer assumes arbitrary RGB inputs without acknowledging this edge case.
>
> 4. **YIQ coefficient sign errors** — The I and Q rows contain **negative** coefficients for some channels. Forgetting the negative signs in the matrix is a recurring mistake.
>
> 5. **Unit confusion in $\Delta E$** — The colour difference is **dimensionless** when computed in CIE Lab space. Writing it with "units" is incorrect.
>
> 6. **Skipping the worked numerical example** — For 7-mark sub-questions, a numerical computation is almost always required. A derivation without a worked example typically caps marks at 4–5.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - [ ] **Trichromatic theory** — 3 cone types: L (~564 nm), M (~534 nm), S (~420 nm)
> - [ ] **Visible spectrum** — 380 nm to 780 nm
> - [ ] **RGB** — Additive, native to displays, 8-bit per channel = 16.7 M colours
> - [ ] **CMY/CMYK** — Subtractive, for printing, $C=1-R$, $M=1-G$, $Y=1-B$
> - [ ] **HSI/HSV** — Perceptual, decouples intensity from chromaticity
> - [ ] **YIQ** — NTSC broadcast, $Y$ = luminance, I/Q = chrominance
> - [ ] **CIE 1931** — Standard observer, horseshoe chromaticity diagram
> - [ ] **Tristimulus values** — $X$, $Y$, $Z$ from integrals with $\bar{x}, \bar{y}, \bar{z}$ functions
> - [ ] **Chromaticity coordinates** — $x = X/(X+Y+Z)$, $y = Y/(X+Y+Z)$
> - [ ] **CIE Lab** — Perceptually uniform, used for $\Delta E$ colour difference
> - [ ] **Gamut** — Convex hull of primaries on CIE diagram
> - [ ] **Bayer filter** — RGGB mosaic in digital camera sensors
> - [ ] **Pseudo-colour** — Assigning artificial colour to greyscale images (CT, MRI)
> - [ ] **False colour** — Mapping invisible spectra (NIR, IR) to visible colour
> - [ ] **D65 illuminant** — Standard daylight, $x = 0.3127$, $y = 0.3290$
> - [ ] **Y coefficients** — 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B (sum = 1, weighted by $V(\lambda)$)
> - [ ] **HSI hue formula** — $\cos^{-1}$ of normalized RGB difference ratio
> - [ ] **Hue rule** — If $B > G$, use $H = 360° - \theta$ correction
> - [ ] **Medical applications** — Endoscopy, fundus photography, dermatoscopy, histopathology, fluorescence-guided surgery
> - [ ] **Colour calibration** — Macbeth ColorChecker, DICOM Part 14 GSDF
> - [ ] **Clinical value** — Pseudo-colour in CT/MRI boosts diagnostic confidence in tissue differentiation

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advanced Techniques for Flow and Tissue Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advanced Techniques for Flow and Tissue Motion** in medical imaging refers to a specialized suite of diagnostic methodologies engineered to detect, quantify, and visualize the movement of biological fluids (primarily **blood**) and the mechanical displacement of soft tissues in real time. These techniques exploit physical phenomena such as the **Doppler Effect**, **phase shifts in magnetic resonance signals**, and **echo time-of-flight variations** to render non-invasive hemodynamic and biomechanical information that is invisible to conventional static B-mode imaging.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBRT411)**, this topic sits at the intersection of ultrasound physics, signal processing, and clinical instrumentation. The principal modalities include:

- **Doppler Ultrasound** (Continuous Wave, Pulsed Wave, Color Flow, Power Doppler)
- **Phase Contrast Magnetic Resonance Imaging (PC-MRI)**
- **Time-of-Flight Magnetic Resonance Angiography (TOF-MRA)**
- **Ultrasound Elastography** (Strain and Shear Wave imaging)
- **Motion Compensation Strategies** (Cardiac gating, Navigator echoes, Breath-holding)

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Ambulance Siren Analogy:** Imagine standing on a sidewalk when an ambulance approaches you with its siren wailing. As the vehicle moves **toward** you, the pitch of the siren sounds **higher** (compressed sound waves, shorter wavelength). As it moves **away**, the pitch drops **lower** (stretched waves, longer wavelength). The ambulance driver, however, hears a constant pitch because they are moving *with* the sound source.
>
> **Medical Doppler imaging works on the exact same principle.** Moving red blood cells act like the ambulance. Stationary tissues act like the sidewalk observer. The ultrasound machine listens to the *shifted* echoes bouncing off red blood cells. A shift toward a higher frequency means blood flowing **toward** the transducer; a shift toward a lower frequency means blood flowing **away** from it. The magnitude of the shift tells us **how fast** the blood is moving.

## 1.3 Core Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Speed of sound in soft tissue:** $c \approx 1540 \, \text{m/s}$
- **Resting frequency range of diagnostic transducers:** $f_0 = 2 \, \text{MHz} \text{ to } 15 \, \text{MHz}$
- **Doppler shift frequency range in clinical practice:** $\Delta f \approx 20 \, \text{Hz} \text{ to } 20 \, \text{kHz}$ (audible range)
- **Normal peak systolic blood velocity (carotid artery):** $0.5 \text{ to } 1.2 \, \text{m/s}$
- **Magnetic field strengths for PC-MRI:** $B_0 = 1.5 \, \text{T} \text{ to } 3.0 \, \text{T}$
- **Gyromagnetic ratio of hydrogen:** $\gamma = 42.58 \, \text{MHz/T}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For the PEBRT411 course, primary emphasis is laid on **Doppler ultrasound physics**, the **Doppler shift equation**, the architectural differences between **CW and PW Doppler systems**, and the operational principles of **Color Flow Imaging**. Phase Contrast MRI is generally touched upon as a comparative advanced modality.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Doppler Shift — Physical Foundation

When an ultrasound wave of frequency $f_0$ is transmitted into tissue and strikes a moving target (such as a red blood cell), the reflected echo returns at a slightly different frequency. The difference between transmitted and received frequencies is called the **Doppler shift frequency**, denoted $\Delta f$.

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. **Transmission Phase:** The piezoelectric transducer emits a sinusoidal pressure wave of frequency $f_0$ into tissue at the speed of sound $c$.
2. **Scattering Phase:** The wave encounters moving scatterers (RBCs) traveling at velocity $v$ relative to the transducer.
3. **Two Shifts Occur:**
   - The moving RBC first acts as a *moving receiver*, perceiving an incoming frequency $f_1$ that is shifted from $f_0$.
   - The RBC then acts as a *moving source*, re-emitting the wave at a further shifted frequency $f_2$ that travels back to the transducer.
4. **Detection Phase:** The stationary transducer receives $f_2$. The total observed Doppler shift is $\Delta f = f_2 - f_0$.

## 2.2 Continuous Wave (CW) Doppler

> [!NOTE]
> **CW Doppler uses two independent crystals** — one continuously transmitting and one continuously receiving. It can measure *any* velocity without aliasing, but **cannot provide range information** (it interrogates the entire beam path).

- **Strengths:** No aliasing, no velocity limit, excellent for measuring very high velocities (e.g., stenotic jets).
- **Weakness:** Range ambiguity — signals from all depths along the beam are summed.

## 2.3 Pulsed Wave (PW) Doppler

> [!NOTE]
> **PW Doppler uses a single crystal** that alternates between transmit and receive modes, gating the reception to a precise time window corresponding to a specific depth. It provides **range resolution** but is subject to the **Nyquist limit**, causing aliasing if velocities exceed the limit.

- **Pulse Repetition Frequency (PRF):** The number of pulses emitted per second.
- **Nyquist Limit:** $v_{\max} = \dfrac{c \cdot PRF}{4 f_0 \cos\theta}$
- **Depth Constraint:** $d_{\max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}$

## 2.4 Color Flow Imaging (CFI)

Color Flow Imaging is essentially **multi-gate PW Doppler** performed across hundreds of sample volumes within a 2D region. Each sample volume is interrogated for Doppler shift, and the **mean velocity, variance, and direction** are encoded as a color overlay on the B-mode image.

| Direction / Property | Conventional Color Encoding |
| :--- | :--- |
| Flow **toward** transducer | **Red** |
| Flow **away** from transducer | **Blue** |
| Turbulence (high variance) | **Green** (BART — Blue Away, Red Toward) |

## 2.5 Power Doppler (Energy Mode)

Unlike color Doppler which displays the *mean* frequency shift, power Doppler displays the **integrated power (amplitude) of the Doppler signal**. It is **angle-independent** (insensitive to the Doppler angle), highly sensitive to slow flow, but discards directional and velocity information.

## 2.6 Phase Contrast MRI (PC-MRI)

PC-MRI uses **bipolar gradient pulses** to encode the velocity of moving spins into the **phase** of the MR signal. Stationary spins accrue no net phase, while moving spins accrue a phase proportional to their velocity. Two acquisitions are made (with and without velocity encoding), and their phase difference yields a velocity map.

**Velocity Encoding (VENC):** The operator-chosen maximum velocity. $\text{VENC} = \dfrac{\pi}{\gamma G_v \tau^2}$ where $G_v$ is the bipolar gradient amplitude and $\tau$ is the lobe duration.

## 2.7 Ultrasound Elastography

Elastography maps the **stiffness of tissue** by measuring displacement in response to mechanical excitation (manual compression or acoustic radiation force). It produces an **elastogram** where stiff tissues (e.g., malignant tumors) appear differently than soft tissues.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the master reference for solving all KTU numerical and theoretical questions on this topic. Memorize each row.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variable Definitions | Clinical Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Doppler Shift Equation | $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 f_0 \, v \cos\theta}{c}$ | $f_0$ = transmitted freq, $v$ = velocity, $\theta$ = angle, $c$ = sound speed | Compute blood velocity from measured shift |
| 2 | Velocity from Shift | $v = \dfrac{\Delta f \, c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}$ | Rearranged Doppler equation | Direct clinical velocity readout |
| 3 | Nyquist Velocity Limit | $v_{\max} = \dfrac{c \cdot PRF}{4 f_0 \cos\theta}$ | PRF = pulse repetition freq | Max measurable velocity (PW) |
| 4 | Maximum Depth (PW) | $d_{\max} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}$ | $c$ = sound speed | Range of PW Doppler |
| 5 | Doppler Angle | $\theta = \arccos\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta f \, c}{2 f_0 \, v}\right)$ | Inverse form | Vessel angle estimation |
| 6 | Aliasing Frequency | $f_{\text{alias}} = \dfrac{PRF}{2}$ | Nyquist boundary | Detect and correct aliasing |
| 7 | PC-MRI Phase Shift | $\phi = \gamma \, G_v \, v \, T \, \Delta T$ | $\gamma$ = gyromagnetic ratio, $T$ = duration, $\Delta T$ = separation | Velocity encoding in MRI |
| 8 | VENC Definition | $VENC = \dfrac{\pi}{\gamma \, G_v \, \tau^2}$ | $\tau$ = gradient lobe width | Set velocity range in PC-MRI |
| 9 | Wavelength in Tissue | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f_0}$ | Acoustic wavelength | Resolution calculation |
| 10 | Acoustic Power (Power Doppler) | $P_D \propto \int \vert S(f) \vert^2 \, df$ | Integrated power spectrum | Angle-insensitive flow detection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Beyond cardiology and obstetrics, Doppler flowmetry is used industrially in **flow meters**, **non-destructive testing**, and **fluid dynamics research**. PC-MRI is the gold standard for quantifying CSF flow in hydrocephalus and blood flow in the aorta.

# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Doppler Shift Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Goal:** Derive the expression $\Delta f = \dfrac{2 f_0 \, v \cos\theta}{c}$ from first principles, showing every algebraic and physical step. This is the single most heavily-tested formula in the PEBRT411 syllabus.

### Step 1 — Define the Scene
A transducer (stationary source) emits an ultrasound wave of frequency $f_0$. A red blood cell (the moving target) travels with velocity $v$ at an angle $\theta$ relative to the beam axis. The speed of sound in tissue is $c$.

### Step 2 — First Frequency Shift (Moving Receiver)
A moving receiver perceives a frequency $f_1$ different from $f_0$. Using the classical Doppler formula for a stationary source and moving observer:

$$
f_1 = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c} \right)
$$

The sign convention: **+** if the RBC moves **toward** the source (reducing the relative distance), **−** if away. We assume motion toward the transducer, hence the plus sign.

### Step 3 — Second Frequency Shift (Moving Source)
The RBC now re-radiates the wave at frequency $f_1$ while moving. The stationary transducer acts as a stationary observer perceiving a frequency $f_2$:

$$
f_2 = f_1 \left( \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)
$$

The minus sign in the denominator reflects the RBC moving **toward** the receiver during re-emission.

### Step 4 — Substitute $f_1$ into the Expression for $f_2$

$$
f_2 = f_0 \left( \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c} \right) \left( \frac{c}{c - v \cos\theta} \right)
$$

Cancel the $c$ in numerator and denominator of the adjacent fraction:

$$
f_2 = f_0 \, \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Doppler Shift $\Delta f$

By definition:

$$
\Delta f = f_2 - f_0
$$

Substitute:

$$
\Delta f = f_0 \, \frac{c + v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta} - f_0
$$

Factor $f_0$:

$$
\Delta f = f_0 \left[ \frac{c + v \cos\theta - (c - v \cos\theta)}{c - v \cos\theta} \right]
$$

Simplify the numerator (combine like terms, $c$ cancels with $-c$):

$$
\Delta f = f_0 \left[ \frac{2 v \cos\theta}{c - v \cos\theta} \right]
$$

### Step 6 — Apply the Medical Approximation
In diagnostic ultrasound, blood velocity $v$ is on the order of $\leq 1 \, \text{m/s}$, while $c \approx 1540 \, \text{m/s}$. Therefore $v \cos\theta \ll c$, and the denominator can be approximated:

$$
c - v \cos\theta \approx c
$$

### Step 7 — Final Simplified Doppler Equation

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta f = \frac{2 f_0 \, v \cos\theta}{c}\;}
$$

This is the **fundamental Doppler equation** used in every clinical Doppler system.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Velocity Calculation

**Problem:** A 5 MHz transducer is used to interrogate blood flow in the carotid artery. The vessel runs at an angle of $\theta = 60^\circ$ to the beam. The measured Doppler shift is $\Delta f = 2.5 \, \text{kHz}$. Compute the blood velocity. Take $c = 1540 \, \text{m/s}$.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Write the rearranged Doppler equation:**

$$
v = \frac{\Delta f \, c}{2 f_0 \cos\theta}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values** (use SI units: $\Delta f = 2500 \, \text{Hz}$, $f_0 = 5 \times 10^6 \, \text{Hz}$):

$$
v = \frac{(2500)(1540)}{2 \times (5 \times 10^6) \times \cos 60^\circ}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $\cos 60^\circ$:**

$$
\cos 60^\circ = 0.5
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the cosine value:**

$$
v = \frac{(2500)(1540)}{2 \times (5 \times 10^6) \times 0.5}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the denominator:**

$$
2 \times 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.5 = 5 \times 10^6
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the numerator:**

$$
2500 \times 1540 = 3{,}850{,}000
$$

**Step 7 — Divide:**

$$
v = \frac{3{,}850{,}000}{5{,}000{,}000} = 0.77 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**Answer:** The peak blood velocity in the carotid artery is $v = 0.77 \, \text{m/s}$, which lies within the normal physiological range (typically $0.5 \text{ to } 1.2 \, \text{m/s}$).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Nyquist Limit and Aliasing

**Problem:** A PW Doppler system operates at $f_0 = 4 \, \text{MHz}$ with a PRF of $8 \, \text{kHz}$. The beam-to-flow angle is $\theta = 0^\circ$. Compute (a) the Nyquist velocity limit, and (b) the maximum unambiguous depth.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Nyquist velocity limit:**

$$
v_{\max} = \frac{c \cdot PRF}{4 f_0 \cos\theta}
$$

$$
v_{\max} = \frac{1540 \times 8000}{4 \times 4 \times 10^6 \times \cos 0^\circ}
$$

$$
v_{\max} = \frac{12{,}320{,}000}{16 \times 10^6} = 0.77 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**Part (b) — Maximum unambiguous depth:**

$$
d_{\max} = \frac{c}{2 \cdot PRF}
$$

$$
d_{\max} = \frac{1540}{2 \times 8000} = \frac{1540}{16{,}000} = 0.09625 \, \text{m} \approx 9.6 \, \text{cm}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off Insight:** Notice the inverse relationship between maximum velocity and maximum depth in PW Doppler. To image deep vessels, you must use a low PRF, which lowers the Nyquist limit and causes aliasing. Modern systems use **high-PRF PW** or **CW Doppler** to bypass this constraint.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — CW vs PW Doppler

| Parameter | Continuous Wave (CW) | Pulsed Wave (PW) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Transducers | Two separate crystals | Single crystal |
| Emission | Continuous | Intermittent pulses |
| Range Resolution | **None** (range ambiguity) | **Yes** (sample volume gating) |
| Velocity Limit | **None** (no aliasing) | Limited by Nyquist ($v_{\max}$) |
| Aliasing | Cannot occur | Can occur above $v_{\max}$ |
| Sensitivity | Sensitive to all velocities | Sensitive within chosen gate |
| Typical Use | High-velocity jets (stenosis) | Targeted vessel sampling |

# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Doppler Ultrasound System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Oscillator f0] --> B[Transmitter Pulser]
    B --> C[Transducer Probe]
    C --> D[Moving RBCs in Tissue]
    D --> E[Echo Return f0 +/- df]
    E --> F[Receiver Amplifier]
    F --> G[Mixer Multiplier]
    A --> G
    G --> H[Doppler Shift Signal df]
    H --> I[Wall Filter High Pass]
    I --> J[Spectral Analyzer FFT]
    J --> K[Spectral Display]
    H --> L[Color Mapper Encoder]
    L --> M[Color Overlay on B-Mode Image]
    J --> N[Audio Speaker for Audible Shift]
```

**Block-Level Functional Walkthrough:**
- **Oscillator** generates the reference sinusoidal carrier at $f_0$.
- **Transmitter** amplifies and shapes pulses for the transducer.
- **Transducer** converts electrical energy to acoustic pressure and vice versa.
- **Mixer** multiplies the received echo with a reference signal to extract the difference frequency $\Delta f$ (the actual Doppler information).
- **Wall filter** removes low-frequency clutter from vessel wall motion.
- **FFT processor** decomposes the Doppler signal into its frequency components for spectral display.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Decision Tree for Selecting Doppler Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to Image Flow?] --> Q1{Need range resolution?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Velocity within Nyquist limit?}
    Q1 -- No --> CW[Use CW Doppler]
    Q2 -- Yes --> PW[Use PW Doppler]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Need any velocity info?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> HPRF[Use High PRF PW Doppler]
    Q3 -- No --> CW2[Use CW Doppler]
    Start --> Q4{Need 2D spatial flow map?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> CFI[Use Color Flow Imaging]
    Start --> Q5{Need angle-independent slow flow?}
    Q5 -- Yes --> PD[Use Power Doppler]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Phase Contrast MRI Velocity Encoding

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Spin Excitation RF Pulse] --> B[First Gradient Lobe +Gv]
    B --> C[Time Interval Delta T]
    C --> D[Second Gradient Lobe -Gv]
    D --> E[Signal Acquisition with Phase phi]
    E --> F[Phase Subtraction Map]
    F --> G[Velocity Map Voxel by Voxel]
    G --> H[Quantitative Flow Display]
```

**Explanation:** Bipolar gradients (+Gv, then −Gv) cause stationary spins to **dephase and rephase** with zero net phase. Moving spins accumulate a **net phase proportional to velocity**. Two acquisitions (with and without velocity encoding) are subtracted to isolate flow-induced phase shifts.

## 4.4 Comparison Schematic — Doppler vs Phase Contrast MRI

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UltrasoundModality
        U1[Doppler US]
        U2[Real-time, portable, cheap]
        U3[Operator dependent on angle theta]
        U4[No ionizing radiation]
    end
    subgraph MRIModality
        M1[Phase Contrast MRI]
        M2[Quantitative 3D velocity field]
        M3[No angle dependence]
        M4[Long scan time, expensive]
    end
    U1 --> U2
    U2 --> U3
    U3 --> U4
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4
```
