> - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shim System

# SHIM SYSTEM IN MEDICAL IMAGING (MRI)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Shim System (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> A **Shim System** in Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is a specialized hardware subsystem comprising passive ferromagnetic pieces (passive shims) and/or active current-carrying coils (shim coils) that generate corrective magnetic fields to compensate for spatial inhomogeneities in the main static magnetic field **B₀**, thereby producing a highly uniform magnetic field across the imaging volume (typically measured over a 40 cm Diameter Spherical Volume, DSV).

The uniformity of **B₀** is quantified in **parts per million (ppm)**, and modern MRI scanners demand a homogeneity of **< 1 ppm** over the imaging region of interest. Without an effective shim system, the Larmor frequency varies spatially, leading to severe image degradation.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy: Tuning a Grand Piano**
> Imagine you are a concert pianist. The main magnet in an MRI is like a giant grand piano that should ideally produce perfect, uniform sound across all 88 keys. However, due to manufacturing imperfections, some keys (regions of space) sound slightly off-pitch compared to others.
>
> The **Shim System** is the piano tuner's toolkit. The tuner (engineer) does NOT replace the piano (magnet) — instead, he listens carefully (field mapping) and makes tiny, precise adjustments using tuning forks and small metal pieces (shim coils and shims) to bring every key into perfect harmony.
>
> - **Passive shims** = small pieces of steel glued inside the piano frame (permanent, fixed correction).
> - **Active shim coils** = electronic tuning devices that dynamically adjust the tuning based on the patient and the region being scanned.

### Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Main Field Strength (B₀):** Typically **1.5 T** or **3.0 T** in clinical MRI; up to **7.0 T** in research systems.
- **Larmor Frequency:** $\gamma B_0 = 42.576 \text{ MHz/T}$, so a 1.5 T system operates at **63.87 MHz**.
- **Acceptable Homogeneity:** **< 1 ppm** over a 40 cm DSV (≈ 0.06 mT variation across the head).
- **Frequency Spread Tolerance:** ≤ **0.5 ppm** to avoid off-resonance artifacts in gradient-echo imaging.
- **Shim Coil Current Range:** Typically **±10 A** for low-order shims; **±1 A** for higher orders.
- **Gyromagnetic Ratio (γ) of ¹H:** $2.675 \times 10^8 \text{ rad·s}^{-1}\text{·T}^{-1}$ (**42.576 MHz/T**).

### Why is B₀ Inhomogeneity a Problem?

> [!NOTE]
> **Consequences of Poor Shimming**
> 1. **Geometric Distortion** — Especially severe in Echo Planar Imaging (EPI).
> 2. **Signal Loss** — Intravoxel dephasing reduces SNR.
> 3. **Fat Suppression Failure** — Frequency-selective fat saturation pulses miss the fat peak.
> 4. **Spectroscopy Errors** — MR Spectroscopy (MRS) peaks shift incorrectly.
> 5. **Susceptibility Artifact Amplification** — Especially at air-tissue interfaces (sinuses, lungs).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sources of B₀ Inhomogeneity

The static field in any real MRI magnet is never perfectly uniform. The major sources of inhomogeneity are:

1. **Manufacturing Imperfections** — Tolerances in superconducting wire winding.
2. **Environmental Influences** — Nearby ferromagnetic masses (building steel, elevators, cars).
3. **Patient-Induced Perturbations** — Different tissues have different magnetic susceptibilities ($\chi$):
   - Air: $\chi \approx 0$
   - Water/Tissue: $\chi \approx -9 \times 10^{-6}$ (SI)
   - Fat: $\chi \approx -7.5 \times 10^{-6}$
4. **Eddy Currents** — From gradient switching, which create transient fields.
5. **Magnet Drift** — Slow variation of B₀ over time (especially in permanent magnets).

### 2.2 Types of Shimming

#### A. Passive Shimming

- Uses small pieces of **ferromagnetic material** (typically steel sheets or disks) strategically placed in trays at the bore of the magnet.
- The shim pieces create **local field distortions** that *cancel* the existing inhomogeneities.
- **Advantages:** Inexpensive, no power consumption, stable over time.
- **Disadvantages:** Not adjustable, must be physically rearranged for each magnet installation.
- Performed **once during magnet installation** by trained shimming engineers.

#### B. Active Shimming

- Uses **electromagnetic coils** wound in specific geometries that, when energized with DC currents, produce well-defined spatial field profiles.
- Coils are typically constructed using **spherical harmonic basis functions**.
- **Advantages:** Adjustable per patient, can be dynamically updated.
- **Disadvantages:** Requires power supplies, can drift, generates heat.

### 2.3 Spherical Harmonic Shim Coil Design (Theory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The spatial variation of B₀ inside a magnet bore is best described using **spherical harmonic basis functions** (also called **solid harmonics** in Cartesian form). The z-component of the magnetic field is expanded as:
> $$B_z(x, y, z) = B_0 + \sum_{n=0}^{N} \sum_{m=0}^{n} \left[ A_{nm} \cdot f_{nm}(x, y, z) \right]$$
> where $A_{nm}$ are the shim amplitudes (coil currents multiplied by sensitivity constants) and $f_{nm}(x, y, z)$ are the spatial basis functions of order $n$ and degree $m$.

The first few orders in Cartesian form are:

| Order | Coefficient | Function $f_{nm}$ | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 0 | $A_{00}$ | $1$ (constant) | Overall B₀ field strength trim |
| 1 (axial) | $A_{10}$ | $z$ | Linear axial gradient (G_z) |
| 1 (transverse) | $A_{11}$ | $x$ | Linear X-gradient (G_x) |
| 1 (transverse) | $B_{11}$ | $y$ | Linear Y-gradient (G_y) |
| 2 (axial) | $A_{20}$ | $2z^2 - (x^2+y^2)$ | Quadratic axial (z²) |
| 2 (transverse) | $A_{21}$ | $xz$ | Cross-term (zx) |
| 2 (transverse) | $B_{21}$ | $yz$ | Cross-term (zy) |
| 2 (transverse) | $A_{22}$ | $x^2 - y^2$ | Quadratic X-Y term |
| 2 (transverse) | $B_{22}$ | $2xy$ | Quadratic XY term |

**Higher orders** ($n = 3, 4, 5, ...$) are added in high-field systems (3 T and above) to correct increasingly complex field patterns produced by the patient.

### 2.4 Step-by-Step Shimming Procedure (Active Shimming)

1. **Field Mapping:** Acquire two **gradient-echo images** at slightly different echo times (ΔTE). Compute the phase difference $\Delta\phi$ to obtain the local field map $B_z(x,y,z)$.
2. **Decomposition:** Express the measured field map as a weighted sum of spherical harmonic basis functions using **least-squares fitting**.
3. **Current Calculation:** Convert the fitted amplitudes $A_{nm}$ into required coil currents $I_{nm}$ using the coil sensitivity matrix $S_{nm}$:
   $$I_{nm} = \frac{A_{nm}}{S_{nm}}$$
4. **Application:** Energize the shim coils with the calculated currents.
5. **Verification:** Re-acquire the field map to confirm residual inhomogeneity is below the target (e.g., **< 0.5 ppm**).
6. **Iteration:** Repeat steps 2-5 if necessary (typically 2-3 iterations suffice).

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **high-yield** for KTU 2024 University Examinations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ | Larmor angular frequency | rad/s |
| 2 | $f_0 = \gamma B_0 / (2\pi)$ | Larmor frequency (42.576 MHz/T for ¹H) | Hz |
| 3 | $\Delta f = \gamma \Delta B_0$ | Frequency shift due to field inhomogeneity | Hz |
| 4 | $B_z = B_0 + \sum A_{nm} f_{nm}(x,y,z)$ | Field expansion in spherical harmonics | T |
| 5 | $\Delta \phi = \gamma \Delta B_z \cdot TE$ | Phase accrual in field mapping | rad |
| 6 | $\text{Homogeneity} = \frac{\Delta B_{0,\text{peak-to-peak}}}{B_0} \times 10^6$ | Homogeneity specification | ppm |
| 7 | $I_{nm} = A_{nm} / S_{nm}$ | Shim coil current calculation | A |
| 8 | $\chi_{\text{air}} \approx 0$; $\chi_{\text{tissue}} \approx -9 \times 10^{-6}$ | Magnetic susceptibility values | SI |
| 9 | $\delta B \approx \chi \cdot B_0$ | Susceptibility-induced field perturbation | T |
| 10 | $T_2^* \approx \frac{1}{\gamma \Delta B_0}$ | Effective transverse relaxation time | s |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Shim System is a **critical engineering subsystem** in every clinical MRI scanner. It enables:

- **High-resolution neuroimaging** (sub-millimeter voxels in fMRI).
- **Diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI)** without geometric distortion.
- **Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy (MRS)** for neurochemical analysis.
- **Functional MRI (fMRI)** for brain mapping.
- **Cardiac MRI** in regions of strong susceptibility gradients.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Spherical Harmonic Expansion of B₀ Field

**Starting Point — Maxwell's Equations (Magnetostatics):**

In a current-free region, the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ can be written as the gradient of a scalar potential $\Phi$:
$$\vec{B} = -\nabla \Phi$$

Since $\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$, the potential must satisfy **Laplace's equation**:
$$\nabla^2 \Phi = 0$$

**Solution Using Spherical Harmonics:**

The general solution to Laplace's equation in spherical coordinates $(r, \theta, \phi)$ is:
$$\Phi(r, \theta, \phi) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \sum_{m=-n}^{n} \left[ C_{nm} \, r^n \, Y_{nm}(\theta, \phi) \right]$$

where $Y_{nm}(\theta, \phi)$ are the spherical harmonic functions. The **z-component of B** is:
$$B_z = -\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial z}$$

**Conversion to Cartesian Form (Inside a Sphere of Radius R):**

For practical MRI use, the spherical harmonics are rewritten in Cartesian coordinates normalized to a reference radius $R$. The first three orders are:

**Order 0 (n = 0, m = 0):**
$$f_{00} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B_z^{(0)} = A_{00}$$

**Order 1 (n = 1):**
$$f_{10} = \frac{z}{R}, \quad f_{11} = \frac{x}{R}, \quad f_{1,-1} = \frac{y}{R}$$

Therefore:
$$B_z^{(1)} = A_{10} \left(\frac{z}{R}\right) + A_{11} \left(\frac{x}{R}\right) + A_{1,-1} \left(\frac{y}{R}\right)$$

**Order 2 (n = 2):**
$$f_{20} = \frac{2z^2 - (x^2 + y^2)}{2R^2}, \quad f_{21} = \frac{xz}{R^2}, \quad f_{22} = \frac{x^2 - y^2}{2R^2}, \quad f_{2,-1} = \frac{yz}{R^2}, \quad f_{2,-2} = \frac{xy}{R^2}$$

**Final Compact Expression:**
$$B_z(x, y, z) = B_0 + \sum_{n=0}^{N} \sum_{m=-n}^{n} A_{nm} \cdot f_{nm}(x, y, z)$$

This is the **fundamental equation** that defines the design of every active shim coil set in an MRI system.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Shim Current Calculation via Least-Squares

**Given:** A measured field map $B_{\text{meas}}(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ at $N_v$ voxel positions.

**Goal:** Find the shim amplitudes $A_{nm}$ that minimize the residual field error.

**Step 1 — Define the Design Matrix:**

Let $F$ be the matrix whose element $F_{ij} = f_{nm}(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ for the $i$-th voxel and the $j$-th shim term.

**Step 2 — Formulate the Least-Squares Problem:**

We wish to minimize:
$$J(\vec{A}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N_v} \left[ B_{\text{meas},i} - B_0 - \sum_{j} A_j F_{ij} \right]^2$$

**Step 3 — Set the Gradient to Zero:**

$$\frac{\partial J}{\partial A_k} = -2 \sum_{i} F_{ik} \left[ B_{\text{meas},i} - B_0 - \sum_{j} A_j F_{ij} \right] = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve the Normal Equation:**

This yields the **normal equation**:
$$F^T F \, \vec{A} = F^T \left( \vec{B}_{\text{meas}} - B_0 \vec{1} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Compute Shim Currents:**

Apply the **Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse** to solve for $\vec{A}$:
$$\vec{A} = \left( F^T F \right)^{-1} F^T \left( \vec{B}_{\text{meas}} - B_0 \vec{1} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Coil Currents:**

For each shim channel $j$, with coil sensitivity $S_j$ (in T/A):
$$I_j = \frac{A_j}{S_j}$$

This is the **complete engineering workflow** implemented in every clinical MRI scanner's shim software.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Example: 1.5 T Shim Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example (Frequently Tested in KTU Exams)**
>
> A 1.5 T MRI system has a measured field map showing a **linear gradient** of **0.5 mT/m** along the z-axis across a 40 cm DSV.
>
> **Step 1:** Convert the gradient to ppm:
> $$\Delta B_0 = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T/m} \times 0.4 \text{ m} = 2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ T}$$
> $$\text{Homogeneity} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{1.5} \times 10^6 \approx 133 \text{ ppm}$$
>
> **Step 2:** Compute the frequency spread:
> $$\Delta f = \gamma \Delta B_0 = (2.675 \times 10^8) \times (2 \times 10^{-4}) \approx 53.5 \text{ kHz}$$
>
> **Step 3:** Determine the required Z1 shim amplitude:
> $$A_{10} = \Delta B_0 / R = (2 \times 10^{-4}) / 0.2 = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T}$$
>
> **Step 4:** With coil sensitivity $S_{10} = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ T/A:
> $$I_{10} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-4}} = 10 \text{ A}$$
>
> **Result:** A 10 A current in the Z1 shim coil will cancel this inhomogeneity. The post-shim field will be uniform to within **< 1 ppm**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Shim Coil Nomenclature

| Coil ID | Function $f_{nm}$ | Clinical Use Case | Order |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Z0 | $1$ | B₀ trim, frequency adjustment | 0 |
| Z1 (Gz) | $z$ | Susceptibility along patient axis | 1 |
| X (Gx) | $x$ | Left-right field correction | 1 |
| Y (Gy) | $y$ | Anterior-posterior correction | 1 |
| Z2 | $2z^2 - (x^2+y^2)$ | Quadratic fall-off near coil ends | 2 |
| ZX | $xz$ | Cross-terms at off-center FOV | 2 |
| ZY | $yz$ | Cross-terms at off-center FOV | 2 |
| X2-Y2 | $x^2 - y^2$ | In-plane asymmetry | 2 |
| XY | $2xy$ | Diagonal asymmetry | 2 |
| Z3, Z4, Z5 | Higher powers of z | High-field (3 T, 7 T) brain shimming | 3-5 |
