Also, what gain (in dB) must the TGC amplifier apply to restore the original signal level? **Solution:** **Step 1 — Use the one-way attenuation formula:** $$\text{Attenuation}_{\text{one-way}} = \alpha \cdot f \cdot d$$ **Step 2 — Substitute:** $$\text{Attenuation}_{\text{one-way}} = 0.5 \times 5 \times 6 = 15 \, \text{dB}$$ **Step 3 — Double for round-trip echo travel:** $$\text{Attenuation}_{\text{round-trip}} = 2 \times 15 = 30 \, \text{dB}$$ **Step 4 — TGC must apply an equal and opposite gain:** $$G_{\text{TGC}} = +30 \, \text{dB}$$ **Answer:** A 30 dB round-trip loss occurs, and the TGC must apply a **+30 dB** amplification to bring the displayed echo amplitude back to its true value. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Echo Behavior at Common Tissue Interfaces | Interface (1 → 2) | $Z_1$ (Mrayl) | $Z_2$ (Mrayl) | $R_I$ | Clinical Echo Appearance | |---|---|---|---|---| | Soft tissue → Lung gas | 1.63 | 0.0004 | ≈ 1.00 (≈ 100%) | Very bright, total reflection — image ends at lung | | Soft tissue → Cortical bone | 1.63 | 7.80 | ≈ 0.41 (≈ 41%) | Very bright echo, posterior acoustic shadow | | Liver → Kidney | 1.66 | 1.62 | ≈ 0.00012 (≈ 0.01%) | Faint echo, beam penetrates well | | Blood → Vessel wall | 1.66 | 1.62 | ≈ 0.00012 | Faint, useful for vessel-wall imaging | | Fat → Liver | 1.38 | 1.66 | ≈ 0.0085 (≈ 0.85%) | Moderate echo, clearly visible organ boundary | | Water → Perspex (Plexiglas) | 1.50 | 3.20 | ≈ 0.13 (≈ 13%) | Strong echo (used in test phantoms) | > [!IMPORTANT] > The acoustic impedance of **air** is ≈ 400 rayl (a million times smaller than soft tissue). This is why **ultrasound gel** is mandatory between the transducer and the skin — to eliminate the air gap and let ultrasound enter the body at all. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Pulse-Echo System Block Architecture The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a clinical pulse-echo ultrasound scanner, showing the complete signal path from excitation to display. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Pulser / Transmitter
High voltage pulse generator] --> B[Piezoelectric Transducer
Converts voltage to acoustic pulse] B --> C[Soft Tissue Medium
c = 1540 m/s] C --> D{Tissue Interface
Change in acoustic impedance Z} D -->|Reflected echo| E[Same Transducer
Now in receive mode] D -->|Transmitted beam| F[Deeper Tissue Layers] E --> G[Receiver Preamplifier
Low noise amplification] G --> H[Time Gain Compensation TGC
Depth dependent gain] H --> I[Envelope Detector
Rectifier and low pass filter] I --> J[Analog to Digital Converter ADC] J --> K[Scan Converter Memory] K --> L[B Mode Display Monitor
Brightness vs depth] M[Master Clock and Timing Unit] --> A M --> E M --> K ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** A single clock coordinates three events simultaneously — the firing of the pulser, the gating of the receiver "listen" window, and the storage of each echo's brightness into the correct memory location. Without this synchronization, the image would be a smear of noise. --- ## 4.2 Echo Formation and Range Gating ```mermaid flowchart LR T0[Time t = 0
Transducer fires pulse] --> P1[Downward leg
t = d / c] P1 --> REF[Echo produced at interface
depth d] REF --> P2[Upward leg
t = d / c] P2 --> T1[Time t = 2d / c
Echo returns to transducer] T1 --> CALC[Scanner computes
d = c t / 2] CALC --> PIX[Pixel placed at depth d
Brightness proportional to echo amplitude] ``` --- ## 4.3 Time Gain Compensation Curve (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Shallow echoes
Receive time small
High amplitude already] --> B[TGC applies low gain
to early echoes] B --> C[Deep echoes
Receive time large
Attenuated heavily] C --> D[TGC applies high gain
to late echoes] D --> E[Final displayed image
Uniform brightness vs depth] ``` A typical TGC gain-versus-time curve is an **exponentially increasing function**, $G(t) = e^{\alpha f c t}$, precisely the inverse of the tissue attenuation law $I(d) = I_0 e^{-2 \alpha f d}$. --- ## 4.4 Specular vs Diffuse Echo Generation ```mermaid flowchart TD BEAM[Incident ultrasound beam] --> S1{Surface size relative to wavelength lambda} S1 -->|Surface size >> lambda| SPEC[Specular echo
Mirror like reflection
Strong, angle dependent] S1 -->|Surface size is comparable to lambda| DIFF[Diffuse echo
Scattered in all directions
Forms speckle texture] SPEC --> ORG[Originates from organ capsules
diaphragm, vessel walls] DIFF --> PAR[Originates from parenchyma
liver tissue, red blood cells] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to explain why liver parenchyma produces a uniform speckled echo pattern, the answer is *diffuse scattering from microscopic tissue inhomogeneities smaller than $\lambda$*. When asked why the diaphragm produces a sharp bright line, the answer is *specular reflection from a smooth, large interface*. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define an echo in the context of medical ultrasound imaging. State the standard value of the speed of sound in soft tissue used by all clinical scanners.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **echo** in medical ultrasound is the portion of an emitted acoustic pulse that is reflected back toward the transducer after striking a tissue interface characterized by a change in acoustic impedance. The scanner measures the round-trip time of each echo to determine the depth of the reflecting structure, and the echo amplitude is mapped to pixel brightness in B-mode imaging. The standard value of the speed of sound in soft tissue used universally by clinical ultrasound systems is: $$c = 1540 \, \text{m/s}$$ This value is used as a calibration constant to convert measured echo times into depth information. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Distinguish between specular and diffuse (scattered) echoes with one clinical example of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Specular Echo | Diffuse (Scattered) Echo | |---|---|---| | Origin | Smooth, large interface (size ≫ λ) | Small structures (size ≪ λ) | | Behavior | Mirror-like; obeys law of reflection | Radiates in all directions | | Dependence on angle | Strongly angle-dependent | Largely angle-independent | | Clinical example | Diaphragm, endocardial wall, organ capsule | Liver parenchymal texture, blood in Doppler | Specular echoes produce **sharp, bright lines**; diffuse echoes produce the **speckled texture** of soft-tissue parenchyma. --- ## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Regulation Note:** In the End Semester Examination (ESE), Module 2 carries 14 marks. Students are typically offered an internal choice between two full-module questions. Both options are provided below with complete sub-parts and model solutions. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] #### (a) Derive the pulse-echo range equation. An ultrasound echo returns to the transducer after 65 µs in soft tissue. Calculate the depth of the reflecting structure. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Derivation (4 marks):** *(See Section 3.1 above for the full derivation. Reproduce it here.)* Let the reflecting interface lie at depth $d$. The pulse travels downward a distance $d$ in time $t_{\text{down}} = d / c$, and the echo travels back upward the same distance in time $t_{\text{up}} = d / c$. The total round-trip time is: $$t_r = t_{\text{down}} + t_{\text{up}} = \frac{2d}{c}$$ Solving for $d$: $$\boxed{d = \frac{c \, t_r}{2}}$$ **Numerical Calculation (3 marks):** Given: $t_r = 65 \, \mu s = 65 \times 10^{-6} \, s$ and $c = 1540 \, \text{m/s}$. $$d = \frac{1540 \times 65 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \frac{0.10010}{2} = 0.05005 \, \text{m}$$ $$d = 5.005 \, \text{cm} \approx 5 \, \text{cm}$$ The interface is at a depth of **≈ 5.0 cm**. --- #### (b) Explain the principle of Time Gain Compensation (TGC) with the relevant mathematical expression. Why is TGC necessary in clinical ultrasound? (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Principle of TGC (4 marks):** As an ultrasound beam propagates through tissue, it loses intensity due to attenuation according to: $$I(d) = I_0 \, e^{-2 \alpha f d}$$ The factor of 2 in the exponent accounts for the **two-way** (round-trip) travel of the echo. A deep echo has therefore lost far more amplitude than a shallow one, even if both originate from interfaces of equal intrinsic reflectivity. If displayed uncompensated, the image would falsely appear very dark in the far field. To correct this, the scanner applies a **time-varying gain** to echoes as they are received. The gain is programmed as a function of receive-time $t = 2d / c$: $$G(t) = e^{+2 \alpha f \cdot (c t / 2)} = e^{\alpha f c t}$$ The total displayed signal amplitude is therefore: $$I_{\text{displayed}}(d) = I_0 \, e^{-2 \alpha f d} \cdot e^{+2 \alpha f d} = I_0$$ The displayed amplitude becomes **independent of depth** and reflects only the true reflectivity of the tissue. **Why TGC is necessary (3 marks):** 1. **Equalizes display brightness** across depth, enabling uniform interpretation. 2. **Compensates for frequency-dependent attenuation** — higher frequencies (used for shallow, high-resolution imaging) suffer greater loss, which TGC corrects. 3. **Without TGC**, deep structures (heart, deep abdomen) would be invisible despite having identical acoustic properties to their shallow counterparts. 4. **TGC is operator-adjustable** to allow fine-tuning of image appearance in challenging patients (e.g., heavy attenuation in obese patients). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2023] #### (a) Define acoustic impedance. Derive the intensity reflection coefficient for a wave striking a perpendicular interface. An ultrasound beam strikes perpendicular to the fat–liver interface. Given $Z_{\text{fat}} = 1.38 \times 10^6$ rayl and $Z_{\text{liver}} = 1.66 \times 10^6$ rayl, calculate the percentage of incident intensity that is reflected. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Definition (2 marks):** Acoustic impedance $Z$ is the product of the density $\rho$ of the medium and the speed of sound $c$ in that medium: $$Z = \rho c \quad \text{(units: rayl = kg·m}^{-2}\text{·s}^{-1}\text{)}$$ It represents the resistance of a medium to the passage of an acoustic wave. **Derivation of $R_I$ (3 marks):** For a plane wave incident perpendicularly on a boundary between two media of impedances $Z_1$ and $Z_2$, continuity of pressure and particle velocity at the interface gives: $$p_i + p_r = p_t \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{p_i - p_r}{Z_1} = \frac{p_t}{Z_2}$$ Solving these simultaneously, the pressure reflection coefficient is: $$R_p = \frac{p_r}{p_i} = \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1}$$ Since intensity is proportional to the square of the pressure amplitude, the intensity reflection coefficient is: $$\boxed{R_I = \left( \frac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2}$$ **Numerical Calculation (2 marks):** $$R_I = \left( \frac{1.66 \times 10^6 - 1.38 \times 10^6}{1.66 \times 10^6 + 1.38 \times 10^6} \right)^2 = \left( \frac{0.28}{3.04} \right)^2$$ $$R_I = (0.0921)^2 = 0.00848 \approx 0.85\%$$ **Answer:** About **0.85 %** of the incident intensity is reflected as an echo at the fat–liver boundary. --- #### (b) What is attenuation? A 5 MHz ultrasound beam travels through 8 cm of soft tissue with attenuation coefficient $\alpha = 0.5$ dB/cm/MHz. Calculate (i) the one-way attenuation in dB, (ii) the round-trip attenuation, and (iii) the gain that the TGC amplifier must apply. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Definition (1 mark):** Attenuation is the progressive loss of ultrasound intensity as the beam propagates through tissue, caused by **absorption** (conversion of acoustic energy to heat) and **scattering** (redirection of energy out of the main beam). **Attenuation law (2 marks):** $$\text{Attenuation (dB)} = \alpha \cdot f \cdot d$$ where $\alpha$ is in dB/cm/MHz, $f$ is in MHz, and $d$ is in cm. **(i) One-way attenuation (2 marks):** $$A_{\text{one-way}} = 0.5 \times 5 \times 8 = 20 \, \text{dB}$$ **(ii) Round-trip attenuation (1 mark):** $$A_{\text{round-trip}} = 2 \times 20 = 40 \, \text{dB}$$ **(iii) TGC gain required (1 mark):** $$G_{\text{TGC}} = +40 \, \text{dB}$$ The TGC must apply a +40 dB gain to late-arriving echoes from 8 cm depth to compensate fully for the round-trip loss. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost marks in echo-related questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the range equation** — writing $d = c t$ instead of $d = c t / 2$. The KTU board specifically tests whether students understand that the pulse travels *down and back*. This single error can cost 2–3 marks. > 2. **Using $c = 1500$ m/s or $c = 330$ m/s** — these are speeds in *water* and *air* respectively. The clinical value **1540 m/s** is mandatory. > 3. **Forgetting to convert µs to seconds** — plugging $t = 65$ directly into $d = (1540 \times 65) / 2$ gives nonsense. Always write units explicitly. > 4. **Confusing $R_I$ with $R_p$** — the intensity reflection coefficient is **squared**; the pressure reflection coefficient is not. Writing $R_p = (Z_2 - Z_1)/(Z_2 + Z_1)$ and *calling it* $R_I$ loses 1 mark. > 5. **Writing attenuation as $e^{-\alpha f d}$ (no factor of 2)** — round-trip echo attenuation requires the factor of 2. > 6. **Stating the speed of sound in soft tissue without a numerical value** — KTU marking schemes expect "1540 m/s" to appear in the answer. > 7. **Confusing specular with diffuse echoes** — these are tested almost every semester. Use the *size relative to wavelength* criterion. --- ## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Echo** = reflected acoustic wave from a tissue interface of differing impedance. - **Pulse-Echo principle** = a single transducer both transmits and receives. - **Universal calibration constant:** $c = 1540 \, \text{m/s}$ in soft tissue. - **Range equation (memorize):** $d = \dfrac{c t}{2}$ — the factor of 2 is non-negotiable. - **Acoustic impedance:** $Z = \rho c$ (units: rayl). - **Intensity reflection coefficient:** $R_I = \left( \dfrac{Z_2 - Z_1}{Z_2 + Z_1} \right)^2$ — intensity, not pressure, is squared. - **Average soft-tissue attenuation:** $\alpha \approx 0.5 \, \text{dB/cm/MHz}$. - **Attenuation law:** $I(d) = I_0 \, e^{-2 \alpha f d}$ (factor of 2 for round-trip). - **TGC gain:** $G(t) = e^{\alpha f c t}$ — exactly cancels the round-trip attenuation. - **Two echo types:** Specular (large, smooth, angle-dependent) and Diffuse (small, speckle, parenchymal). - **Air gap is fatal** — $R_I$ at tissue–air interface is ≈ 100%, which is why gel is mandatory. - **The same pulse-echo math** governs SONAR, RADAR, and industrial NDT — the wave speed $c$ is the only variable that changes.

topics.title: Signal Coding - Slice Encoding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Signal Coding - Slice Encoding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Slice Encoding (Slice Selection)** is the fundamental spatial encoding process in Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) that uses the simultaneous application of a linear magnetic field gradient (called the slice-select gradient, $G_{ss}$) along a chosen physical axis (typically the z-axis, $B_0$ direction) and a frequency-selective (bandwidth-limited) Radio-Frequency (RF) excitation pulse to selectively excite hydrogen protons ($^1H$ nuclei) residing exclusively within a thin, well-defined anatomical cross-sectional plane of the patient.

The selective excitation is governed directly by the **Larmor equation**, which states that the precessional frequency of a proton is strictly proportional to the local magnetic field strength it experiences:

$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_{eff}$$

where $\gamma$ is the **gyromagnetic ratio** (for hydrogen, $\gamma/2\pi \approx 42.58$ **MHz/T**), and $B_{eff}$ is the effective magnetic field at the proton's location.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Slice encoding is the FIRST of the three spatial encoding steps performed in any standard MRI pulse sequence. The other two are **Phase Encoding** and **Frequency (Readout) Encoding**. Together, they form the complete 3D spatial localization framework.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a long, dark corridor full of people, and you want to call only the people standing on **one specific tile** of the floor.

- You shine a **laser pointer (the RF pulse)** that is tuned to a very specific color (frequency).
- Simultaneously, you tilt the floor slightly so that each tile has a slightly different temperature (the **slice-select gradient** $G_{ss}$).
- Now, only the people standing on the tile whose temperature matches your laser's color will respond to your call.
- Everyone else stays quiet because the laser's color is wrong for their tile.

In MRI terms:
- The **temperature gradient** = the **slice-select magnetic gradient** ($G_{ss}$)
- The **laser color** = the **center frequency of the RF pulse** ($\omega_{RF}$)
- The **specific tile** = the **anatomical slice** being imaged
- The **width of the color band** = the **transmitter bandwidth (BW)** of the RF pulse, which defines the **slice thickness ($\Delta z$)**

> [!NOTE]
> The standard clinically used unit for MRI magnetic field strength is **Tesla (T)**, with common systems operating at **1.5 T** and **3.0 T**. The gyromagnetic ratio of hydrogen ($\gamma = 2.675 \times 10^8$ **rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹**) is a physical constant that NEVER changes.

### Why Slice Encoding is Indispensable

Without slice encoding, an MRI scanner would excite protons in the **entire volume** of the patient simultaneously, producing a single, useless signal that contains no spatial information. Slice encoding is the essential **first step** that converts a 3D imaging problem into a tractable **2D slice-by-slice acquisition**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physical Principle of Slice Selection — Step-by-Step

The slice selection process can be decomposed into the following sequential physics steps:

1. **Establish a static background field $B_0$** along the longitudinal (z) axis of the bore. All protons precess at a base Larmor frequency $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$.
2. **Superimpose a linear gradient $G_{ss}$** along the z-axis using dedicated gradient coils. The total magnetic field now varies linearly with position:
   $$B_z(z) = B_0 + G_{ss} \cdot z$$
3. **Apply a frequency-selective RF pulse** simultaneously with the gradient. The RF pulse contains a narrow band of frequencies centered at $\omega_{RF}$ with a total bandwidth $BW$.
4. **Resonance condition is satisfied only at the slice location**: Protons precess at $\omega(z) = \gamma(B_0 + G_{ss} z)$. Resonance (and therefore excitation) occurs ONLY where $\omega(z) = \omega_{RF}$.
5. **Solving for the slice center $z_0$**:
   $$z_0 = \frac{\omega_{RF}/\gamma - B_0}{G_{ss}}$$
6. **Determining the slice thickness $\Delta z$**: The slice extends over the region where the local Larmor frequency falls within the RF bandwidth. Therefore:
   $$\Delta z = \frac{BW}{\gamma \cdot G_{ss}}$$
7. **Turning off the gradient** after the RF pulse so that protons throughout the slice precess at the same frequency, ready for readout.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Insight:** To achieve a THINNER slice, you can either (a) increase $G_{ss}$ or (b) decrease $BW$. Decreasing $BW$ requires a longer RF pulse duration (since Time-Bandwidth product is constant), which reduces SNR. This is a fundamental engineering compromise in MRI pulse sequence design.

---

### 2.2 The Slice Profile and RF Pulse Shape

The ideal slice profile is a perfect rectangular function in space (uniform excitation inside the slice, zero outside). However, since the Fourier Transform relationship exists between the RF pulse shape (in time) and the slice profile (in frequency/space), a **sinc-shaped RF pulse** is used to approximate the ideal rectangular slice profile:

$$\text{sinc}(t) = \frac{\sin(\pi \cdot t / T_{p})}{\pi \cdot t / T_{p}}$$

- A **sinc pulse** in time → **rectangular slice profile** in space.
- A **Gaussian or Hamming-filtered sinc pulse** is often used in practice to reduce the long duration of the pure sinc pulse.

The **Time-Bandwidth Product (TBP)** of the RF pulse is a critical constant:

$$TBP = T_p \cdot BW$$

For a standard sinc pulse, $TBP \approx 4$ to $8$ (number of zero-crossings on each side).

---

### 2.3 Multi-Slice Imaging and Slice Gap

Clinical MRI typically acquires multiple parallel slices in an interleaved or sequential fashion:

- **Slice Gap**: A small physical spacing between adjacent slices (typically 10–20% of slice thickness) is used to prevent **cross-talk** caused by imperfect slice profiles.
- **Interleaved acquisition**: Even-numbered slices are acquired first, then odd-numbered slices, to minimize cross-excitation effects.
- **Total scan time** for multi-slice imaging:

$$T_{total} = N_{slices} \times N_{phase\_encodes} \times TR$$

where $TR$ is the repetition time.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Parameter | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Larmor Frequency (general) | $\omega_0 = \gamma B_{eff}$ | rad/s | Precession frequency of proton |
| 2 | Larmor Frequency (clinical) | $f_0 = (\gamma/2\pi) B_0$ | **Hz** or **MHz** | Frequency in MHz at 1 T = 42.58 MHz |
| 3 | Slice Position (offset) | $z_0 = (\omega_{RF}/\gamma - B_0)/G_{ss}$ | meters (m) | Center of selected slice |
| 4 | Slice Thickness | $\Delta z = BW / (\gamma \cdot G_{ss})$ | meters (m) | Width of excited region |
| 5 | Time-Bandwidth Product | $TBP = T_p \cdot BW$ | dimensionless | Pulse design invariant |
| 6 | Gradient Strength | $G_{ss} = BW / (\gamma \cdot \Delta z)$ | **T/m** or **mT/m** | Required gradient for desired thickness |
| 7 | RF Pulse Duration | $T_p = TBP / BW$ | seconds (s) | Length of selective RF pulse |
| 8 | Slice Gap | $Gap = f \cdot \Delta z$ | meters (m) | Spacing, $f \approx 0.1$–$0.2$ |
| 9 | Multi-slice Scan Time | $T_{total} = N_{slices} \cdot N_{PE} \cdot TR$ | seconds (s) | Total acquisition duration |
| 10 | Gyromagnetic Ratio ($^1H$) | $\gamma = 2.675 \times 10^8$ | rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹ | Fundamental constant for hydrogen |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Slice selection is the foundation of all clinical MRI protocols (T1-weighted, T2-weighted, FLAIR, DWI, etc.). Modern scanners can acquire **hundreds of slices** in a single 3D volume using additional **phase-encoding in the slice direction (3D encoding)**.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Significance

| Application Area | Role of Slice Encoding |
|---|---|
| **Diagnostic Radiology** | Defines the anatomical plane (axial, sagittal, coronal) for clinical interpretation. |
| **Functional MRI (fMRI)** | Provides slice-by-slice BOLD (Blood Oxygen Level Dependent) activation maps. |
| **Spectroscopic Imaging** | Localizes voxel positions for chemical shift analysis. |
| **Interventional MRI** | Real-time slice tracking for surgical guidance. |
| **Parallel Imaging (SENSE, GRAPPA)** | Uses multi-coil arrays to under-sample k-space within each slice. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Slice Position from the Larmor Equation

We begin with the fundamental Larmor relation and add the linear gradient term.

**Given:**
- Static field: $B_0$ (along z-axis)
- Slice-select gradient: $G_{ss}$ (along z-axis, units **T/m**)
- RF center frequency: $\omega_{RF}$ (units **rad/s**)

**Step 1: Total effective field at position z**

$$B_{eff}(z) = B_0 + G_{ss} \cdot z$$

**Step 2: Larmor frequency at position z**

$$\omega(z) = \gamma \cdot B_{eff}(z) = \gamma (B_0 + G_{ss} \cdot z)$$

**Step 3: Resonance condition** — excitation occurs where $\omega(z) = \omega_{RF}$:

$$\omega_{RF} = \gamma (B_0 + G_{ss} \cdot z_0)$$

**Step 4: Solve algebraically for $z_0$**:

$$\omega_{RF} = \gamma B_0 + \gamma G_{ss} z_0$$

$$\gamma G_{ss} z_0 = \omega_{RF} - \gamma B_0$$

$$z_0 = \frac{\omega_{RF} - \gamma B_0}{\gamma G_{ss}}$$

This can also be written in the equivalent, more intuitive form:

$$z_0 = \frac{\omega_{RF}/\gamma - B_0}{G_{ss}}$$

> **Interpretation:** The slice center $z_0$ shifts **linearly** with the RF center frequency. This is the basis of the scanner's "frequency offset" control, which the operator uses to move the slice up or down.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Slice Thickness from RF Bandwidth

**Given:**
- RF pulse bandwidth: $BW$ (Hz)
- Slice-select gradient: $G_{ss}$
- Gyromagnetic ratio: $\gamma$

**Step 1: The frequency range excited** extends from $\omega_{RF} - \pi \cdot BW$ to $\omega_{RF} + \pi \cdot BW$ (using the angular frequency convention; the factor of $2\pi$ depends on convention).

**Step 2: The total frequency width** is simply $BW$ (Hz) or $2\pi \cdot BW$ (rad/s).

**Step 3: Map the frequency range to spatial range.** Since $\omega(z) = \gamma B_0 + \gamma G_{ss} z$, the relationship between frequency and position is:

$$\frac{d\omega}{dz} = \gamma G_{ss}$$

**Step 4: Integrate** the gradient over the spatial extent of the slice:

$$\Delta \omega = \gamma G_{ss} \cdot \Delta z$$

**Step 5: Substitute $\Delta \omega = 2\pi \cdot BW$** (in Hz using standard convention):

$$2\pi \cdot BW = \gamma G_{ss} \cdot \Delta z$$

**Step 6: Final expression for slice thickness**:

$$\Delta z = \frac{2\pi \cdot BW}{\gamma G_{ss}}$$

Or, more commonly expressed in clinical literature using $\gamma$ in units of **Hz/T** (i.e., $\gamma/2\pi = 42.58$ MHz/T):

$$\Delta z = \frac{BW}{(\gamma/2\pi) \cdot G_{ss}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Required Gradient for a Given Slice Thickness

Rearranging the slice thickness equation:

$$G_{ss} = \frac{BW}{(\gamma/2\pi) \cdot \Delta z}$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style):**

> **Problem:** An MRI scanner operates at **1.5 T**. A clinician wants to image a slice of thickness **5 mm** using an RF pulse of bandwidth **2 kHz**. Calculate:
> (a) The required slice-select gradient $G_{ss}$.
> (b) The Larmor frequency at the slice center.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify constants**
- $\gamma/2\pi = 42.58$ MHz/T = $42.58 \times 10^6$ Hz/T
- $BW = 2$ kHz = $2000$ Hz
- $\Delta z = 5$ mm = $5 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $B_0 = 1.5$ T

**Step 2: Apply the gradient formula**

$$G_{ss} = \frac{2000 \text{ Hz}}{(42.58 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz/T}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m})}$$

$$G_{ss} = \frac{2000}{212900} \text{ T/m}$$

$$\boxed{G_{ss} \approx 9.39 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T/m} = 9.39 \text{ mT/m}}$$

**Step 3: Larmor frequency at the slice center**

$$f_0 = 42.58 \text{ MHz/T} \times 1.5 \text{ T} = \boxed{63.87 \text{ MHz}}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Time-Bandwidth Product and RF Pulse Duration

The Time-Bandwidth Product (TBP) is an **invariant** for a given RF pulse shape:

$$TBP = T_p \cdot BW = \text{constant (depends on pulse shape)}$$

**Derivation logic:**
For a sinc pulse with $N$ zero-crossings on each side of the central lobe, the bandwidth is inversely proportional to the time between zero-crossings. Therefore:

$$TBP = T_p \cdot BW = (2N + 1) \cdot (\text{unit factor})$$

For a typical Hamming-windowed sinc pulse, $TBP \approx 4$ to $8$.

**Example:** If $TBP = 6$ and $BW = 2$ kHz:

$$T_p = \frac{TBP}{BW} = \frac{6}{2000} = 3 \text{ ms}$$

This 3 ms pulse duration is clinically acceptable; longer pulses risk $T_2^*$ signal decay before readout.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Design Parameter | Effect of INCREASING $G_{ss}$ | Effect of INCREASING $BW$ | Effect of INCREASING $T_p$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Slice Thickness $\Delta z$ | **DECREASES** (thinner slice) | **INCREASES** (thicker slice) | No direct effect |
| Chemical Shift Artifact | **REDUCED** | **INCREASED** | No direct effect |
| RF Pulse Duration | No effect (set by $TBP/BW$) | **DECREASES** | **INCREASES** |
| SNR | Slightly reduces | Slightly increases | No direct effect |
| Scan Time | No effect | No effect | No effect |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Corner:** KTU problems often ask you to "design a slice-select protocol". Always specify ALL three parameters ($G_{ss}$, $BW$, $T_p$) and justify each using the constraints (slice thickness, TBP, chemical shift tolerance).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Slice Encoding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Main Magnet B0 - Static Field] --> C[Scanner Bore - Patient Region]
    B[Gradient Coil Subsystem] --> D[Gradient Amplifier]
    D --> E[Slice-Select Gradient Coil Gss - z-axis]
    E --> C
    F[RF Synthesizer] --> G[RF Pulse Shaper - Sinc Pulse Generator]
    G --> H[RF Power Amplifier]
    H --> I[RF Transmit Coil]
    I --> C
    C --> J[Excited Proton Slice - Delta z]
    J --> K[Phase Encoding Gradient Gpe - y-axis]
    K --> L[Frequency Encoding Gradient Gfe - x-axis]
    L --> M[RF Receive Coil]
    M --> N[Analog to Digital Converter]
    N --> O[k-space Sampler]
    O --> P[2D / 3D Image Reconstruction via FFT]
    P --> Q[Clinical MR Image]

    stepA[Operator Console] --> F
    stepA --> B
    stepA --> stepB[Sequence Timing Controller]
    stepB --> D
    stepB --> H
```

### 4.2 Pulse Sequence Timing Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant TR as Repetition Time TR
    participant RF as RF Pulse Line
    participant Gss as Slice-Select Gradient Gss
    participant Gpe as Phase-Encode Gradient Gpe
    participant Gfe as Frequency-Encode Gradient Gfe
    participant ADC as Analog-to-Digital Converter

    Note over TR: One TR cycle produces one line of k-space
    TR->>RF: Apply Selective Sinc RF Pulse (duration Tp)
    activate Gss
    TR->>Gss: Simultaneously Apply Gss along z
    deactivate Gss
    TR->>Gpe: Apply Phase-Encoding Blip along y
    TR->>Gfe: Apply Frequency-Encoding Gradient along x
    activate ADC
    TR->>ADC: Sample Echo Signal (Readout Window)
    deactivate ADC
    Note over TR: Repeat TR Nphase times with incremented Gpe
```

### 4.3 Slice Selection Geometry — Functional Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    direction LR
    A[Patient Volume - 3D Anatomy] --> B[Apply Linear Gradient Gss along z]
    B --> C[Position-Dependent Larmor Frequency Map omega z]
    C --> D[Apply Bandwidth-Limited RF Pulse at omegaRF]
    D --> E{Is omega z within RF bandwidth?}
    E -- YES --> F[Proton Excitation at Slice Center z0]
    E -- NO --> G[Proton Remains Unperturbed]
    F --> H[Result: Thin Excited Slice of thickness delta z]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Move to Phase Encoding Stage]
```

### 4.4 Slice Profile Formation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Desired Slice Thickness delta z] --> B[Choose RF Pulse Shape - Sinc]
    B --> C[Determine Time-Bandwidth Product TBP]
    C --> D[Choose Bandwidth BW]
    D --> E[Calculate RF Pulse Duration Tp equals TBP divided by BW]
    E --> F[Calculate Required Gradient Gss equals BW over gamma times delta z]
    F --> G[Configure Scanner Pulse Sequence]
    G --> H[Validate Slice Profile via Phantom Scan]
    H --> I{Profile Acceptable?}
    I -- YES --> J[Proceed to Clinical Imaging]
    I -- NO --> K[Iterate Parameters - Adjust BW or Gss]
    K --> A
```

### 4.5 Trade-off Triangle for Slice Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Slice Thickness delta z] --- B[RF Bandwidth BW]
    A --- C[Gradient Strength Gss]
    B --- D[RF Pulse Duration Tp]
    C --- E[Scanner Hardware Limit - max 40 to 80 mT per m]
    B --- F[Chemical Shift Artifact - higher BW reduces artifact]
    D --- G[T2 Signal Decay - long Tp reduces SNR]
    G --- H[Optimal Trade-off Region]
    F --> H
    E --> H
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define slice selection in MRI. State the Larmor equation and explain its role in the slice selection process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Slice selection in MRI is the process of selectively exciting hydrogen protons within a thin anatomical cross-section of the patient using a linear magnetic field gradient ($G_{ss}$) applied simultaneously with a bandwidth-limited radio-frequency (RF) excitation pulse.

The Larmor equation, $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$, gives the precessional frequency of protons in a magnetic field $B_0$. When a gradient $G_{ss}$ is applied along the z-axis, the local Larmor frequency becomes position-dependent: $\omega(z) = \gamma(B_0 + G_{ss} \cdot z)$. Only those protons whose Larmor frequency lies within the RF pulse bandwidth are excited, thereby localizing the excitation to a specific slice at position $z_0 = (\omega_{RF}/\gamma - B_0)/G_{ss}$. **(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 for Larmor equation + 1 for role in localization)**

---

#### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**With a neat diagram, explain the concept of slice thickness. How is it related to the RF bandwidth and slice-select gradient?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Slice thickness ($\Delta z$) is the physical width of the anatomical plane excited during slice selection. It is determined by the range of proton positions whose Larmor frequencies fall within the transmitter bandwidth ($BW$) of the RF pulse, in the presence of the slice-select gradient $G_{ss}$.

The mathematical relationship is:

$$\Delta z = \frac{BW}{\gamma \cdot G_{ss}}$$

A higher gradient or lower bandwidth produces a thinner slice. This is a key parameter set by the operator to balance spatial resolution (favoring thin slices) against Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR), which decreases with thinner slices. **(3 marks: 1 for concept + 1 for formula + 1 for trade-off explanation)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for slice thickness $\Delta z$ in MRI in terms of the RF pulse bandwidth $BW$, gyromagnetic ratio $\gamma$, and slice-select gradient $G_{ss}$. Discuss how the choice of RF pulse shape (e.g., sinc vs. rectangular) affects the resulting slice profile. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An MRI system operates at **3.0 T** with $\gamma/2\pi = 42.58$ **MHz/T**. The operator wishes to obtain a slice of thickness **3 mm** using a sinc RF pulse with a Time-Bandwidth Product of **6** and a transmitter bandwidth of **1.5 kHz**. Calculate:
   (i) The required slice-select gradient $G_{ss}$ in mT/m.
   (ii) The RF pulse duration $T_p$ in milliseconds.
   (iii) The Larmor frequency at the slice center. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of slice thickness (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** In the presence of a linear slice-select gradient $G_{ss}$ along the z-axis, the Larmor frequency at any position $z$ is:

$$\omega(z) = \gamma(B_0 + G_{ss} \cdot z)$$

**Step 2:** The rate of change of Larmor frequency with position is:

$$\frac{d\omega}{dz} = \gamma \cdot G_{ss}$$

**Step 3:** The RF excitation pulse contains a finite bandwidth $BW$ (in Hz). Protons within this frequency range are excited. The total frequency width in angular units is $2\pi \cdot BW$.

**Step 4:** Mapping the frequency range to spatial range:

$$\Delta \omega = 2\pi \cdot BW = \gamma \cdot G_{ss} \cdot \Delta z$$

**Step 5:** Solving for the slice thickness:

$$\boxed{\Delta z = \frac{2\pi \cdot BW}{\gamma \cdot G_{ss}} = \frac{BW}{(\gamma/2\pi) \cdot G_{ss}}}$$

**Step 6: Effect of RF pulse shape on slice profile:**
- A **rectangular (hard) RF pulse** in time has a **sinc-shaped frequency spectrum** → produces a slice profile with significant side-lobe excitation outside the intended slice, causing **cross-talk** between adjacent slices.
- A **sinc-shaped RF pulse** in time has (approximately) a **rectangular frequency spectrum** → produces a clean, well-defined slice with minimal side-lobe contamination.
- In practice, a **Hamming- or Hanning-windowed sinc pulse** is used to further suppress side-lobes at the cost of slightly broadening the transition region of the slice profile.

**(7 marks: 2 for derivation steps 1-3 + 1 for step 4 + 1 for final formula + 3 for pulse shape discussion)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical computation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $B_0 = 3.0$ T
- $\gamma/2\pi = 42.58$ MHz/T = $42.58 \times 10^6$ Hz/T
- $\Delta z = 3$ mm = $3 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- $BW = 1.5$ kHz = $1500$ Hz
- $TBP = 6$

**(i) Required slice-select gradient $G_{ss}$:**

$$G_{ss} = \frac{BW}{(\gamma/2\pi) \cdot \Delta z}$$

$$G_{ss} = \frac{1500}{(42.58 \times 10^6) \cdot (3 \times 10^{-3})}$$

$$G_{ss} = \frac{1500}{127740} \text{ T/m}$$

$$\boxed{G_{ss} \approx 1.174 \times 10^{-2} \text{ T/m} = 11.74 \text{ mT/m}}$$

**(3 marks)**

**(ii) RF pulse duration $T_p$:**

$$T_p = \frac{TBP}{BW} = \frac{6}{1500} = 4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{T_p = 4 \text{ ms}}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Larmor frequency at slice center:**

$$f_0 = (\gamma/2\pi) \cdot B_0 = 42.58 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz/T} \times 3.0 \text{ T}$$

$$\boxed{f_0 = 127.74 \text{ MHz}}$$

**(2 marks)**

---

#### **Question B [14 Marks – Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the MRI slice selection process. Explain the role of the slice-select gradient, the RF excitation pulse, and the concept of Larmor frequency in achieving spatial localization along the slice direction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A clinical MRI scanner is operated at **1.5 T**. The system uses a slice-select gradient of **10 mT/m** to excite a slice. The RF pulse used is a sinc pulse with bandwidth **2.5 kHz** and a Time-Bandwidth Product of **4**.
   (i) Calculate the resulting slice thickness in mm.
   (ii) If the radiologist wants to reduce the slice thickness to **2 mm** while keeping the bandwidth unchanged, what new gradient strength is required?
   (iii) State TWO practical consequences of using a much thinner slice in clinical imaging. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Slice selection process (7 marks):**

The MRI slice selection process involves the coordinated action of three subsystems:

1. **Main Magnet System:** Produces a uniform static field $B_0$ (e.g., 1.5 T) along the z-axis, causing all protons in the patient to precess at a base Larmor frequency $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$.

2. **Gradient Subsystem:** When slice selection is required, the **slice-select gradient coil** is energized, producing a linear magnetic field variation along the z-axis. The total field at any position $z$ is $B_z(z) = B_0 + G_{ss} \cdot z$. This makes the Larmor frequency position-dependent: $\omega(z) = \gamma(B_0 + G_{ss} \cdot z)$.

3. **RF Transmit System:** A frequency-selective **sinc RF pulse** of bandwidth $BW$ is transmitted simultaneously with the gradient. Only protons whose local Larmor frequency falls within this bandwidth — i.e., those located in the slice $z_0 \pm \Delta z/2$ — are excited.

The slice center is determined by the RF center frequency, and the slice thickness is determined by $BW/(\gamma \cdot G_{ss})$. The gradient is then switched off, and the excited protons precess freely, producing a signal that is subsequently phase- and frequency-encoded for full 3D localization.

**(7 marks: 2 for block diagram description + 2 for gradient role + 2 for RF role + 1 for Larmor localization)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical computation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $B_0 = 1.5$ T
- $G_{ss1} = 10$ mT/m = $10 \times 10^{-3}$ T/m
- $BW = 2.5$ kHz = $2500$ Hz
- $TBP = 4$
- $\gamma/2\pi = 42.58 \times 10^6$ Hz/T

**(i) Initial slice thickness:**

$$\Delta z_1 = \frac{BW}{(\gamma/2\pi) \cdot G_{ss1}}$$

$$\Delta z_1 = \frac{2500}{(42.58 \times 10^6) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-3})}$$

$$\Delta z_1 = \frac{2500}{425800} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta z_1 \approx 5.87 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 5.87 \text{ mm}}$$

**(3 marks)**

**(ii) New gradient for $\Delta z_2 = 2$ mm:**

$$G_{ss2} = \frac{BW}{(\gamma/2\pi) \cdot \Delta z_2}$$

$$G_{ss2} = \frac{2500}{(42.58 \times 10^6) \cdot (2 \times 10^{-3})} = \frac{2500}{85160}$$

$$\boxed{G_{ss2} \approx 29.36 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T/m} = 29.36 \text{ mT/m}}$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Two practical consequences of using a much thinner slice:**

1. **Reduced Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** Thinner slices contain fewer protons, producing a weaker MR signal relative to the constant thermal/electronic noise floor. This degrades image quality unless compensated by increasing the Number of Excitations (NEX), at the cost of longer scan time.

2. **Increased susceptibility to motion and partial-volume artifacts:** Thinner slices are more sensitive to patient motion (both voluntary and physiological, e.g., cardiac pulsation, respiration), and any anatomical structure that is not perfectly aligned with the slice plane may be missed or appear distorted. The number of slices required to cover the same anatomical volume also increases, lengthening total scan time.

**(2 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $2\pi$:** When using $\gamma$ in rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹ and $BW$ in Hz, ALWAYS convert to consistent units. KTU examiners specifically deduct 1 mark if the gyromagnetic ratio is used inconsistently.
> 2. **Confusing $TBP$ definition:** Time-Bandwidth Product is $T_p \times BW$ (where $BW$ is in Hz), NOT $T_p \times f_0$. Do not confuse with the Larmor frequency.
> 3. **Not stating units in the final answer:** Always report $G_{ss}$ in mT/m (clinical convention) and slice thickness in mm. A correct numerical value with WRONG units loses 0.5 mark.
> 4. **Missing the discussion of the sinc pulse in Part (a):** Marks are specifically allocated for explaining the Fourier relationship between pulse shape and slice profile. Do NOT skip this.
> 5. **Ignoring the trade-off triangle:** Any KTU answer about slice selection that does NOT mention the trade-off between slice thickness, SNR, and scan time is considered incomplete.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Slice encoding is the FIRST spatial encoding step** in any 2D MRI pulse sequence, preceding phase and frequency encoding.
- **Larmor equation:** $f_0 = (\gamma/2\pi) \cdot B_0$ — for hydrogen at 1.5 T, $f_0 = 63.87$ **MHz**; at 3.0 T, $f_0 = 127.74$ **MHz**.
- **Slice position formula:** $z_0 = (\omega_{RF}/\gamma - B_0)/G_{ss}$ — moving the RF center frequency moves the slice.
- **Slice thickness formula:** $\Delta z = BW / (\gamma \cdot G_{ss})$ — controlled by bandwidth and gradient.
- **Time-Bandwidth Product:** $TBP = T_p \cdot BW$ — a fixed invariant for a given pulse shape; dictates the minimum RF pulse duration.
- **Sinc pulse → rectangular slice profile** (Fourier duality).
- **Trade-off triangle to memorize:** Slice thickness ↔ SNR ↔ Scan time. A thinner slice = lower SNR, more slices needed = longer scan.
- **Slice gap (10–20%) is mandatory** to prevent cross-talk between adjacent slices in multi-slice imaging.
- **Chemical shift artifact** in slice selection direction is inversely proportional to $BW$ (in Hz); use higher $BW$ for fat-suppressed imaging.
- **Gyromagnetic ratio of hydrogen** $\gamma/2\pi = 42.58$ **MHz/T** is a **constant** you must commit to memory for KTU numericals.
- **Multi-slice scan time** = $N_{slices} \times N_{PE} \times TR$ — slice selection adds NO time penalty because slices are acquired within the same TR cycle (different RF frequencies).
- **Clinical planes:** Axial (transverse), Sagittal, Coronal — all defined by the orientation of $G_{ss}$.
- **3D volume imaging** uses additional phase encoding in the slice direction, eliminating the slice gap entirely.
- **Units consistency check:** Hz for $BW$, **mT/m** for $G_{ss}$, **mm** for $\Delta z$ — always convert SI base units before plugging in.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phase Encoding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Phase Encoding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Phase Encoding** is the second spatial encoding mechanism used in Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), applied along the *y-axis* (or any user-selected axis) of the imaging plane. It is performed by applying a brief, calibrated magnetic field gradient — known as the **Phase Encoding Gradient ($G_{pe}$)** — between the slice-selection (excitation) RF pulse and the signal readout (frequency encoding) gradient.

The purpose of this gradient is to introduce a **position-dependent phase shift** $\phi(y)$ to the transverse magnetization of spins located at different positions along the phase-encoding axis. Since this phase shift varies linearly with spatial position, the signal acquired during readout carries spatial information that can be decoded into an image by the **2D Inverse Fourier Transform**.

Mathematically, the phase accumulated by a spin at position $y$ is expressed as:

$$\phi(y) = \gamma \cdot G_{pe} \cdot y \cdot T_{pe}$$

where $\gamma$ is the **gyromagnetic ratio** (for hydrogen, $\gamma/2\pi \approx 42.58$ MHz/T), $G_{pe}$ is the amplitude of the phase encoding gradient, $y$ is the spatial coordinate, and $T_{pe}$ is the duration of the phase encoding gradient pulse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Phase encoding must be repeated **$N_{pe}$ times** (typically 128, 256, or 512) with incrementally varying gradient amplitudes to fill **$k$-space** completely. Each repetition corresponds to a different line in $k$-space and adds to the total scan time.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the finish line of a marathon, holding a stopwatch. Before the race starts, you blow a whistle — every runner begins moving. As they approach the finish line, you notice that runners who started farther away take a tiny bit longer to reach you, not because they are slower, but simply because they had a longer distance to cover. By measuring this tiny time delay (the "phase delay"), you can figure out where each runner started, even though they are all crossing the finish line at the same moment.

**The MRI analogy is exact:**
- The **whistle** = RF excitation pulse (puts all protons in the same excited state).
- The **finish line** = the moment the signal is read out.
- The **time delay** of each runner = the **phase shift** accumulated by the proton.
- The **stopwatch** = the MRI receiver (measures the phase via the Fourier Transform).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Unlike frequency encoding (where the gradient is *on* during readout, making spins precess at different frequencies *simultaneously*), phase encoding uses a gradient that is switched *off* before readout. The location information is "stored" in the phase, which is preserved in the transverse magnetization and decoded later by Fourier analysis.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (1.5T scanner) |
|---|---|---|
| Gyromagnetic ratio of ¹H | $\gamma$ | $2.675 \times 10^8$ rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹ |
| Phase encoding steps | $N_{pe}$ | 128, 256, 512 |
| Phase FOV | $FOV_{pe}$ | 20 – 40 cm |
| Phase gradient amplitude | $G_{pe}$ | 0 – ~20 mT/m |
| Phase gradient duration | $T_{pe}$ | 1 – 5 ms |
| Phase pixel size | $\Delta y$ | 0.5 – 1.0 mm |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Phase Encoding Pulse Sequence

Phase encoding is executed in **five structured steps** within a single TR (Repetition Time) cycle:

### Step 1 — Simultaneous Slice Excitation
An RF pulse is applied in the presence of the slice-select gradient $G_{ss}$. This tips the net magnetization $M_0$ into the transverse plane and defines a 2D slice of thickness $\Delta z$ within the patient.

### Step 2 — Phase Encoding Gradient Application
Immediately after excitation, the phase encoding gradient $G_{pe}$ is switched on for a short duration $T_{pe}$. Spins at different positions $y$ along the phase axis precess at slightly different Larmor frequencies during this interval:

$$\omega(y) = \gamma \cdot B_0 + \gamma \cdot G_{pe} \cdot y$$

The accumulated phase difference after the gradient is turned off is:

$$\Delta \phi(y) = \gamma \cdot G_{pe} \cdot y \cdot T_{pe}$$

### Step 3 — Gradient Switch-Off (Phase "Freezing")
When $G_{pe}$ is switched off, all spins revert to precessing at the same Larmor frequency. Crucially, the **phase offsets remain locked** in the transverse magnetization vector — this is the "memory" of where each spin was located.

### Step 4 — Frequency Encoding & Readout
The frequency encoding gradient $G_{ro}$ is turned on, and the receiver acquires the **Free Induction Decay (FID)** or echo signal. During this readout, spins are spatially encoded in the $x$-direction (frequency), and the previously stored phase encodes the $y$-direction.

### Step 5 — Repetition with Incremented Gradient
Steps 1–4 are repeated $N_{pe}$ times. In the $n$-th repetition, the phase encoding gradient amplitude is set to:

$$G_{pe}^{(n)} = n \cdot \Delta G_{pe}, \quad n = -N_{pe}/2, \dots, +N_{pe}/2 - 1$$

where $\Delta G_{pe}$ is the gradient step size. Each repetition fills **one line of $k$-space**.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why a gradient rather than a fixed phase shift?** A fixed phase would encode no spatial information. Only a *position-dependent* phase provides the spatial mapping needed for image reconstruction.
- **Why $N_{pe}$ repetitions?** The 2D Fourier Transform requires sampling of the object at $N_x \times N_y$ spatial frequencies. Each phase-encoding step samples a different row of $k$-space; one TR = one $k$-space line.
- **Why does scan time depend on $N_{pe}$?** Total scan time is $T_{scan} = TR \times N_{pe} \times N_{sa}$ (where $N_{sa}$ is the number of signal averages). Halving $N_{pe}$ halves the scan time — a key trade-off with image resolution.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU 2024 Formula Reference** — All quantities below are examinable.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi(y) = \gamma \, G_{pe} \, y \, T_{pe}$ | Phase accumulated by spin at position $y$ | radians |
| 2 | $T_{scan} = TR \times N_{pe} \times N_{sa}$ | Total scan time | seconds |
| 3 | $FOV_{pe} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\gamma \, \Delta G_{pe} \, T_{pe}}$ | Field of view in phase direction | meters |
| 4 | $\Delta y = \dfrac{FOV_{pe}}{N_{pe}}$ | Pixel size in phase direction | meters |
| 5 | $k_y^{(n)} = \dfrac{\gamma}{2\pi} \int_0^{T_{pe}} G_{pe}^{(n)} \, dt = \dfrac{\gamma \, G_{pe}^{(n)} \, T_{pe}}{2\pi}$ | $k$-space coordinate sampled at step $n$ | cycles/m |
| 6 | $k_{y,\max} = \dfrac{1}{2 \Delta y} = \dfrac{N_{pe}}{2 \, FOV_{pe}}$ | Maximum $k$-space extent | cycles/m |
| 7 | $\Delta k_y = \dfrac{1}{FOV_{pe}}$ | $k$-space step size | cycles/m |
| 8 | $G_{pe,\max} = \dfrac{2\pi \, k_{y,\max}}{\gamma \, T_{pe}} = \dfrac{\pi \, N_{pe}}{\gamma \, T_{pe} \, FOV_{pe}}$ | Maximum phase gradient amplitude | T/m |

**Note on notation:** In the table above, the symbol $\vert$ (when needed for absolute value) should be typeset as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Similarly, the FOV equations above use the **Nyquist sampling criterion** to prevent aliasing (phase wrap-around).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Phase encoding is the **rate-limiting bottleneck** of conventional MRI. Almost every modern acceleration technique attacks this step:

- **Echo Planar Imaging (EPI):** Acquires *all* $k$-space lines in a single shot using rapidly oscillating phase blips.
- **Parallel Imaging (SENSE, GRAPPA):** Undersamples $k$-space in the phase direction and reconstructs the missing lines using multiple receive coils.
- **Compressed Sensing:** Uses random undersampling of $k$-space in the phase direction and exploits image sparsity.

> [!NOTE]
> The phrase "phase encoding" in MRI must not be confused with **phase encoding in audio/signal processing** (which uses differential encoding of signal phases). In MRI, "phase" refers to the Larmor precession phase, and "encoding" refers to its deliberate spatial modulation by gradients.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Phase Accumulation Along the Phase Axis

We begin with the Larmor equation. The instantaneous precession frequency of a proton at position $y$ in a magnetic field $B_0 + G_{pe} \cdot y$ is:

$$\omega(y) = \gamma (B_0 + G_{pe} \cdot y)$$

**Step 1:** The phase accumulated over a time interval $dt$ is $d\phi = \omega(y) \, dt$. Integrating over the gradient duration $T_{pe}$:

$$\phi(y) = \int_0^{T_{pe}} \omega(y) \, dt = \int_0^{T_{pe}} \gamma (B_0 + G_{pe} \, y) \, dt$$

**Step 2:** Since $B_0$ and $y$ are constant during the short gradient pulse, the integral simplifies to:

$$\phi(y) = \gamma (B_0 + G_{pe} \, y) \, T_{pe}$$

**Step 3:** The term $\gamma B_0 T_{pe}$ is a **global phase offset** that is identical for all spins and is therefore discarded. The spatially informative part is:

$$\phi(y) = \gamma \, G_{pe} \, y \, T_{pe}$$

This is the fundamental encoding equation. **Q.E.D.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Field of View in the Phase Direction

When $G_{pe}$ is incremented in steps of $\Delta G_{pe}$, the phase at position $y$ during the $n$-th TR is:

$$\phi_n(y) = \gamma \, n \, \Delta G_{pe} \, y \, T_{pe}$$

**Step 1:** The phase-encoded signal from a continuous distribution of spins $\rho(y)$ is:

$$S_n = \int \rho(y) \, e^{i \gamma \, n \, \Delta G_{pe} \, y \, T_{pe}} \, dy$$

**Step 2:** Recognize this as a Fourier integral. Define the spatial frequency variable:

$$k_y^{(n)} = \frac{\gamma \, n \, \Delta G_{pe} \, T_{pe}}{2\pi} \quad \text{(units: cycles/m)}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute to obtain the standard Fourier form:

$$S_n = \int \rho(y) \, e^{i 2\pi k_y^{(n)} y} \, dy$$

This shows that each phase-encoding step samples $\rho(y)$ at a *different spatial frequency* $k_y^{(n)}$ — i.e., a different point in $k$-space.

**Step 4:** Apply the **Nyquist sampling criterion** in $k$-space. To avoid aliasing, consecutive $k$-space samples must be separated by no more than $\Delta k_y = 1/FOV_{pe}$:

$$\Delta k_y = \frac{\gamma \, \Delta G_{pe} \, T_{pe}}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{FOV_{pe}}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $FOV_{pe}$:

$$\boxed{FOV_{pe} = \frac{2\pi}{\gamma \, \Delta G_{pe} \, T_{pe}}}$$

The phase FOV must be **at least as large as the anatomy** in the phase direction. Smaller FOV → larger $\Delta k_y$ → larger $\Delta G_{pe}$ → less aliasing. **Q.E.D.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Total Scan Time Dependence on $N_{pe}$

Each TR interval yields **one** $k$-space line. Therefore, the number of phase-encoding steps required to fill $k$-space is exactly $N_{pe}$.

**Step 1:** The total scan duration for a single signal average ($N_{sa}=1$) is:

$$T_{scan} = TR \times N_{pe}$$

**Step 2:** Including $N_{sa}$ averages (for SNR improvement, which scales as $\sqrt{N_{sa}}$):

$$T_{scan} = TR \times N_{pe} \times N_{sa}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $N_{pe} = FOV_{pe} / \Delta y$ (from the formula sheet):

$$T_{scan} = TR \times N_{sa} \times \frac{FOV_{pe}}{\Delta y}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, **doubling the in-plane resolution** (halving $\Delta y$) **quadruples the scan time** if TR is held constant. This is why high-resolution MRI scans are long.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Error:** Confusing $N_{pe}$ (phase-encoding steps) with $N_{ro}$ (readout/frequency-encoding samples). $N_{ro}$ is acquired within a single TR, while $N_{pe}$ requires $N_{pe}$ separate TRs. This asymmetry is the fundamental reason phase encoding dominates scan time.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Parameter Changes

| If you change... | What happens to FOV_pe? | What happens to Δy? | What happens to T_scan? | What happens to SNR? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Increase $N_{pe}$ (more steps) | Unchanged | Decreases (finer) | **Increases linearly** | Unchanged (in phase direction) |
| Increase $FOV_{pe}$ | Increases | Increases | Unchanged (same $N_{pe}$) | Increases (more spins per voxel) |
| Halve $TR$ | Unchanged | Unchanged | **Halves** | Decreases by $\sqrt{2}$ |
| Increase $T_{pe}$ | Decreases (if other params fixed) | Decreases | Unchanged | Unchanged |
| Use parallel imaging (factor 2) | Unchanged | Unchanged | **Halves** | Decreases by $\sqrt{2}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Phase Encoding Pulse Sequence Timing

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant RF as RF Pulse
    participant Gss as Slice-Select Gradient
    participant Gpe as Phase Encoding Gradient
    participant Gro as Frequency Encoding Gradient
    participant ADC as Receiver (ADC)
    participant KS as k-Space

    Note over RF,Gss: Step 1: Excite slice
    RF->>RF: 90° pulse (sinc-shaped)
    Gss->>Gss: ON during RF pulse
    Gss->>Gss: Rephasing lobe

    Note over Gpe: Step 2: Phase encode
    Gpe->>Gpe: ON for T_pe (variable amplitude)
    Gpe->>Gpe: Amplitude = n × dG_pe
    Note right of Gpe: Each TR uses a different n

    Note over Gro,ADC: Step 3: Readout
    Gro->>Gro: Pre-phasing lobe
    Gro->>Gro: ON during ADC
    ADC->>KS: Fill one line of k-space
    Note right of ADC: Frequency encoding along x-axis

    Note over KS: Repeat TR × N_pe times
    KS->>KS: All N_pe k-space lines filled
    KS->>KS: 2D Inverse FT → Image
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Block-Level Architecture of Phase Encoding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MRI Console: User Selects FOV_pe and Resolution] --> B[Compute Required N_pe and dG_pe]
    B --> C[Set TR, T_pe, T_scan]
    C --> D[Initialize n = -N_pe/2]
    D --> E[Set G_pe amplitude to n × dG_pe]
    E --> F[Apply RF Slice-Selection Pulse]
    F --> G[Apply Phase Encoding Gradient G_pe for T_pe]
    G --> H[Spins Accumulate Position-Dependent Phase phi_y]
    H --> I[Apply Frequency Encoding Gradient G_ro]
    I --> J[Acquire Echo Signal via ADC]
    J --> K[Store as Line n of k-Space]
    K --> L{n = N_pe/2 - 1?}
    L -- No --> M[Increment n by 1]
    M --> E
    L -- Yes --> N[k-Space Fully Sampled]
    N --> O[Apply 2D Inverse Fourier Transform]
    O --> P[Reconstructed MR Image]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Phase Evolution Visual

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Spins_At_y1 [Spins at position y1]
        V1[Vector 1: Phase phi1]:::vec
    end
    subgraph Spins_At_y2 [Spins at position y2]
        V2[Vector 2: Phase phi2]:::vec
    end
    subgraph Spins_At_y3 [Spins at position y3]
        V3[Vector 3: Phase phi3]:::vec
    end
    PE[Phase Encoding Gradient G_pe] -->|different Larmor freq| V1
    PE -->|different Larmor freq| V2
    PE -->|different Larmor freq| V3
    V1 --> Net[Net Transverse Magnetization]
    V2 --> Net
    V3 --> Net
    Net -->|Fourier Decoding| Image[Pixel Values in Image]
    classDef vec fill:#e0f0ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** After the phase encoding gradient, the three vectors precess at different angular velocities, leaving them with distinct phases ($\phi_1 \neq \phi_2 \neq \phi_3$) when the gradient is switched off. The resulting vector sum has a *direction* and *magnitude* that encode the spatial distribution — this is precisely the information that the Inverse Fourier Transform decodes into pixel intensities.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**"Define phase encoding in MRI. Why is it necessary to repeat the phase encoding step multiple times within a single imaging sequence?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Phase encoding is a spatial encoding technique in MRI in which a magnetic field gradient $G_{pe}$ is applied briefly between RF excitation and signal readout. This gradient causes spins at different positions $y$ along the encoding axis to accumulate a position-dependent phase $\phi(y) = \gamma G_{pe} y T_{pe}$.

The phase encoding step must be repeated $N_{pe}$ times (typically 128, 256, or 512) because each repetition samples the object at a different spatial frequency $k_y^{(n)}$, thereby filling one distinct line of $k$-space. **(1 mark)** Only after all $N_{pe}$ lines are acquired can the full 2D Fourier Transform be performed to reconstruct the image. **(1 mark)** The number of phase encoding steps also directly determines the in-plane spatial resolution in the phase direction: $\Delta y = FOV_{pe}/N_{pe}$. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**"Distinguish between phase encoding and frequency encoding in MRI."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Phase Encoding | Frequency Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Axis of encoding | $y$-axis (second dimension) | $x$-axis (readout dimension) |
| Gradient state during readout | OFF | ON |
| Repetitions needed | $N_{pe}$ (multiple TRs) | 1 per TR (continuous during ADC) |
| Spatial information stored in | Phase of transverse magnetization | Instantaneous precession frequency |
| Dominant scan-time factor | Yes (each step = one TR) | No (acquired in single readout) |

**(1 mark per key distinguishing point; 3 distinct points = 3 marks.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper, 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the phase accumulated by a proton at position $y$ during a phase encoding gradient of amplitude $G_{pe}$ and duration $T_{pe}$. Explain why the global phase term can be discarded. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An MRI sequence uses a phase encoding gradient of maximum amplitude $G_{pe,\max} = 10$ mT/m, a gradient duration $T_{pe} = 2$ ms, a phase FOV of $25$ cm, and $N_{pe} = 256$ encoding steps. Calculate:
  (i) the gyromagnetic ratio of hydrogen used,  
  (ii) the maximum $k$-space extent $k_{y,\max}$,  
  (iii) the pixel size $\Delta y$,  
  (iv) the total scan time assuming $TR = 500$ ms and $N_{sa} = 1$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

The Larmor precession frequency of a proton at position $y$ under a total field $B_{total} = B_0 + G_{pe} y$ is:

$$\omega(y) = \gamma (B_0 + G_{pe} y)$$

The phase accumulated over the gradient duration $T_{pe}$ is obtained by integration: **(1 mark)**

$$\phi(y) = \int_0^{T_{pe}} \omega(y) \, dt = \gamma (B_0 + G_{pe} y) \, T_{pe}$$

Separating the terms: **(1 mark)**

$$\phi(y) = \underbrace{\gamma B_0 T_{pe}}_{\text{global phase}} + \underbrace{\gamma G_{pe} y T_{pe}}_{\text{position-dependent phase}}$$

The first term, $\gamma B_0 T_{pe}$, is independent of spatial position $y$ — it is the same for every spin in the imaging volume. **(1 mark)** Therefore, it contributes only a constant multiplicative factor $e^{i\gamma B_0 T_{pe}}$ to the signal, which is removed by the magnitude reconstruction and does not affect image intensity. **(1 mark)**

The spatially informative phase is therefore:

$$\boxed{\phi(y) = \gamma \, G_{pe} \, y \, T_{pe}} \quad \text{(2 marks for the boxed result and physical interpretation)}$$

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

Given data:
- $G_{pe,\max} = 10 \text{ mT/m} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ T/m} = 0.01$ T/m
- $T_{pe} = 2 \text{ ms} = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ s
- $FOV_{pe} = 25 \text{ cm} = 0.25$ m
- $N_{pe} = 256$
- $TR = 500 \text{ ms} = 0.5$ s
- $N_{sa} = 1$

**(i) Gyromagnetic ratio of hydrogen (1 mark):**

The standard value used in KTU problems is $\gamma = 2.675 \times 10^8$ rad·s⁻¹·T⁻¹, or equivalently $\gamma/2\pi = 42.58$ MHz/T.

**(ii) Maximum $k$-space extent (2 marks):**

$$k_{y,\max} = \frac{\gamma \, G_{pe,\max} \, T_{pe}}{2\pi} = \frac{(2.675 \times 10^8)(0.01)(2 \times 10^{-3})}{2\pi}$$

$$k_{y,\max} = \frac{5350}{6.2832} \approx 851.6 \text{ cycles/m}$$

Equivalently, $k_{y,\max} \approx 8.52$ cycles/cm.

**(iii) Pixel size in phase direction (2 marks):**

$$\Delta y = \frac{FOV_{pe}}{N_{pe}} = \frac{0.25 \text{ m}}{256} = 9.77 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \approx 0.977 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Total scan time (2 marks):**

$$T_{scan} = TR \times N_{pe} \times N_{sa} = 0.5 \text{ s} \times 256 \times 1 = 128 \text{ s} \approx 2 \text{ min } 8 \text{ s}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, 2024 Scheme, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of $k$-space and show how phase encoding fills the $k$-space matrix line by line. Describe the role of the Nyquist criterion in determining $FOV_{pe}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An MR system has $FOV_{pe} = 30$ cm and $N_{pe} = 128$. Calculate:
  (i) the $k$-space step size $\Delta k_y$,
  (ii) the maximum $k$-space extent $k_{y,\max}$,
  (iii) the pixel size $\Delta y$,
  (iv) the required phase encoding gradient step $\Delta G_{pe}$ if $T_{pe} = 3$ ms.
  How does the total scan time change if $N_{pe}$ is doubled? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) $k$-space and Nyquist criterion (7 marks):**

$k$-space is a 2D Fourier-domain representation of the MR image. Each point in $k$-space corresponds to a spatial frequency, and the full image is obtained by the 2D Inverse Fourier Transform of the complete $k$-space data. **(1 mark)**

Phase encoding fills $k$-space **one line at a time**:
- The frequency-encoding gradient $G_{ro}$ is on during readout, sweeping through the $k_x$ axis (one row of $k$-space is acquired).
- The phase encoding gradient $G_{pe}^{(n)}$ is incremented before each TR, shifting to a new $k_y$ value.
- After $N_{pe}$ repetitions, all rows of $k$-space are filled. **(2 marks)**

The **Nyquist sampling criterion** in the phase direction requires that:

$$\Delta k_y \leq \frac{1}{FOV_{pe}}$$

where $\Delta k_y = \gamma \Delta G_{pe} T_{pe} / (2\pi)$. **(1 mark)** If $\Delta k_y$ is too large (gradient step too coarse), spatially undersampling causes **phase wrap-around aliasing**: anatomy outside the FOV appears folded back into the image. **(1 mark)** To increase $FOV_{pe}$ without aliasing, one must *decrease* $\Delta G_{pe}$ (smaller phase gradient steps). **(1 mark)** The minimum sampling requirement is $\Delta k_y = 1/FOV_{pe}$ at the boundary of the object, ensuring that the image's phase extent is captured unambiguously. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks):**

Given: $FOV_{pe} = 0.30$ m, $N_{pe} = 128$, $T_{pe} = 3 \times 10^{-3}$ s.

**(i) $k$-space step size (1 mark):**

$$\Delta k_y = \frac{1}{FOV_{pe}} = \frac{1}{0.30} \approx 3.33 \text{ cycles/m}$$

**(ii) Maximum $k$-space extent (2 marks):**

$$k_{y,\max} = \frac{N_{pe}}{2 \, FOV_{pe}} = \frac{128}{2 \times 0.30} = \frac{128}{0.60} \approx 213.3 \text{ cycles/m}$$

**(iii) Pixel size (1 mark):**

$$\Delta y = \frac{FOV_{pe}}{N_{pe}} = \frac{0.30}{128} \approx 2.34 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 2.34 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Phase encoding gradient step (2 marks):**

From $\Delta k_y = \gamma \Delta G_{pe} T_{pe} / (2\pi)$:

$$\Delta G_{pe} = \frac{2\pi \, \Delta k_y}{\gamma \, T_{pe}} = \frac{2\pi \times 3.33}{(2.675 \times 10^8)(3 \times 10^{-3})}$$

$$\Delta G_{pe} = \frac{20.94}{802500} \approx 2.61 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T/m} \approx 26.1 \text{ μT/m}$$

**Effect of doubling $N_{pe}$ (1 mark):**

If $N_{pe}$ is doubled to 256, the total scan time $T_{scan} = TR \times N_{pe} \times N_{sa}$ also doubles. The pixel size $\Delta y$ is halved (higher resolution in the phase direction), and $k_{y,\max}$ doubles. To achieve the same $FOV_{pe}$, $\Delta k_y$ must halve, requiring $\Delta G_{pe}$ to halve as well.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Phase Encoding questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of $2\pi$** in $k_y = \gamma G_{pe} T_{pe} / (2\pi)$ — this is the single most common calculation error, costing 1–2 marks per question.
> 2. **Confusing FOV directions:** $FOV_{ro}$ is controlled by the *receiver bandwidth* and $G_{ro}$, while $FOV_{pe}$ is controlled by $\Delta G_{pe}$ and $T_{pe}$. Examiners frequently test this distinction.
> 3. **Ignoring units:** Mixing cm and m, or ms and s, in $FOV$ and $T_{pe}$ calculations. Always convert to SI units (meters, seconds) before substitution.
> 4. **Stating the relationship $T_{scan} = TR \times N_{pe}$** without explaining *why* — examiners expect the student to recognize that one TR fills one $k$-space line.
> 5. **Not distinguishing phase wrap (aliasing)** from frequency wrap. Phase wrap occurs in the *phase direction* when the object extends beyond $FOV_{pe}$; frequency wrap occurs in the *readout direction*.
> 6. **Omitting the $N_{sa}$ factor** in scan time formulas. Even if $N_{sa}=1$ is given, the general formula must include it for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Phase Encoding**

- ✅ Phase encoding uses a **brief gradient pulse** $G_{pe}$ between excitation and readout to introduce **position-dependent phase shifts**.
- ✅ The phase at position $y$ is $\phi(y) = \gamma G_{pe} y T_{pe}$.
- ✅ Each phase-encoding step samples **one row of $k$-space**; total $N_{pe}$ steps are required.
- ✅ **Scan time** is dominated by $T_{scan} = TR \times N_{pe} \times N_{sa}$ — phase encoding is the bottleneck.
- ✅ **Field of view** in phase direction: $FOV_{pe} = 2\pi / (\gamma \Delta G_{pe} T_{pe})$.
- ✅ **Pixel size** in phase direction: $\Delta y = FOV_{pe} / N_{pe}$.
- ✅ $k$-space coordinates: $k_y = \gamma G_{pe} T_{pe} / (2\pi)$, in **cycles per meter**.
- ✅ Maximum $k$-space extent: $k_{y,\max} = N_{pe} / (2 \, FOV_{pe}) = 1 / (2 \Delta y)$.
- ✅ **Nyquist criterion** $\Delta k_y \leq 1/FOV_{pe}$ prevents phase-wrap (aliasing) artifacts.
- ✅ Halving $\Delta y$ (doubling $N_{pe}$) **doubles scan time** but keeps $FOV_{pe}$ unchanged.
- ✅ Halving $FOV_{pe}$ requires **halving** $\Delta G_{pe}$ to maintain Nyquist sampling.
- ✅ Modern acceleration (EPI, SENSE, GRAPPA, compressed sensing) all attack the $N_{pe}$ bottleneck.
- ✅ **Key distinction:** Frequency encoding gradient is ON during readout; phase encoding gradient is OFF during readout.
- ✅ Trade-offs to remember: Resolution ↔ Scan Time ↔ SNR ↔ FOV.
- ✅ Examiners love: derivations of $\phi(y)$, $FOV_{pe}$, $T_{scan}$; numerical problems on $k_{y,\max}$ and $\Delta y$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency Encoding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Frequency Encoding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Frequency Encoding** is a spatial localization technique in Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) in which a linear magnetic field gradient — known as the **frequency-encoding gradient (or readout gradient)** — is switched ON *during* the signal acquisition window. Because the Larmor precession frequency of a spin is linearly proportional to the local magnetic field strength, spins at different spatial positions along the gradient axis precess at *different frequencies*. This deliberate frequency–position mapping allows the spatial information to be encoded directly into the **frequency spectrum of the received Free Induction Decay (FID) or echo signal**, which is then decoded by a one-dimensional Fourier Transform.

Mathematically, the position $x$ of a spin is encoded as a frequency offset $\Delta \omega$ from the central (isocenter) Larmor frequency $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$:

$$\Delta \omega(x) \;=\; \gamma \, G_x \, x$$

where $\gamma$ is the **gyromagnetic ratio** ($\gamma / 2\pi \approx 42.58$ MHz/T for hydrogen-1 protons) and $G_x = \partial B_z / \partial x$ is the linear gradient strength in Tesla per meter (T/m).

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Frequency encoding is one of the three pillars of MRI spatial localization, alongside **Slice Selection** and **Phase Encoding**. Together, these three gradients allow a 2D (or 3D) image to be reconstructed from raw k-space data.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Piano of Spins"

Imagine a long piano keyboard stretching across the patient. Each key on the keyboard corresponds to a small volume of tissue. Now imagine a magical mechanism that makes every key play a **slightly different musical note** depending on its position — keys on the left play low notes, keys on the right play high notes, in a perfectly linear fashion.

When you strike the keyboard (analogous to an RF excitation pulse), every key sounds simultaneously, producing a **chord of many frequencies**. By listening carefully to this chord and analyzing its frequency spectrum, you can immediately tell *which keys were struck and how hard* — even though you never looked at the keyboard. The piano's natural frequency–position relationship is doing the spatial encoding for you.

**Frequency encoding in MRI works on exactly this principle.** The readout gradient is the "tuner" that assigns each tissue location its own unique frequency, and the Fourier Transform of the received signal is the "listener" that decodes which frequencies — and therefore which positions — contributed to the signal.

## 1.3 Role in the MRI Pulse Sequence

In a standard 2D spin-echo or gradient-echo sequence, frequency encoding is performed **simultaneously with signal readout**:

| Step | Gradient Active | Purpose |
|:-----|:----------------|:--------|
| 1 | Slice-select $G_{ss}$ ON (with RF pulse) | Excites a thin slice |
| 2 | Phase-encode $G_{pe}$ ON (briefly) | Encodes Y-position into spin phase |
| 3 | **Frequency-encode $G_{ro}$ ON (during ADC)** | **Encodes X-position into signal frequency** |
| 4 | Signal digitized → Fourier Transformed | Yields one line of image data |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why "Readout" Gradient?** The frequency-encoding gradient is also called the **readout gradient** because it is active *while the data is being read out* by the analog-to-digital converter (ADC). This is in contrast to the phase-encoding gradient, which is applied *before* readout and is then switched off.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The following constants and typical operating ranges define the engineering envelope of frequency encoding:
> - **Gyromagnetic ratio of ¹H:** $\gamma/2\pi = 42.576$ MHz/T
> - **Typical readout gradient strength:** $G_x \approx 1$ to $25$ mT/m
> - **Typical readout bandwidth (full):** BW $\approx \pm 8$ to $\pm 100$ kHz (i.e. 16 to 200 kHz total)
> - **Typical readout duration ($T_{acq}$):** 1 to 16 ms
> - **Typical matrix size in frequency direction ($N_x$):** 128, 192, 256, 320, 384, 512, 768

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physics — Step by Step

### Step 1 — Uniform field case
In a perfectly uniform main field $B_0$, every proton precesses at the *same* Larmor frequency $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$. There is no way to distinguish where a particular signal originated; the FID contains no spatial information.

### Step 2 — Apply a linear gradient along x
Switch on a gradient coil pair (e.g. Maxwell pair geometry) such that the *z*-component of the field becomes position-dependent:

$$B_z(x) \;=\; B_0 \;+\; G_x \, x$$

The local Larmor frequency at position $x$ is now:

$$\omega(x) \;=\; \gamma B_z(x) \;=\; \gamma B_0 \;+\; \gamma G_x \, x \;=\; \omega_0 \;+\; \gamma G_x \, x$$

### Step 3 — Frequency is now a linear function of position
The frequency offset from isocenter is $\Delta \omega(x) = \gamma G_x \, x$. This is the heart of frequency encoding: **position $\leftrightarrow$ frequency is a one-to-one linear mapping**, controlled entirely by the gradient amplitude $G_x$.

### Step 4 — Sample the composite FID
The receiver coil collects signal from *all* positions in the excited slice simultaneously. The signal $S(t)$ is the integral (sum) of contributions from every position:

$$S(t) \;=\; \int \rho(x) \, e^{-i \omega(x) t} \, dx$$

Expanding the phase term:

$$S(t) \;=\; \int \rho(x) \, e^{-i(\omega_0 + \gamma G_x x) t} \, dx \;=\; e^{-i \omega_0 t} \int \rho(x) \, e^{-i \gamma G_x x \, t} \, dx$$

The constant-frequency term $e^{-i\omega_0 t}$ is removed by demodulation in the receiver. The remaining signal is a Fourier Transform of the spin density $\rho(x)$ evaluated at spatial frequency:

$$k_x(t) \;=\; \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} \int_0^t G_x(\tau) \, d\tau$$

For a *constant* readout gradient active throughout the acquisition window:

$$k_x(t) \;=\; \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} \, G_x \, t$$

### Step 5 — Fourier Transform recovers the image
A 1D Fourier Transform of $S(k_x)$ along the frequency-encoded direction directly yields the projection of $\rho$ along x. When combined with phase encoding (which fills the orthogonal k-space axis $k_y$), a 2D inverse Fourier Transform produces the full image.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> The following table contains the complete, exam-ready mathematical toolkit for frequency-encoding problems. All symbols use the **SI / KTU standard** definitions.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:--------------|
| 1 | $\omega(x) = \gamma B_0 + \gamma G_x x$ | Larmor frequency at position $x$ | rad/s |
| 2 | $\Delta f(x) = (\gamma/2\pi) \, G_x \, x$ | Frequency offset in Hz | Hz |
| 3 | $x = \Delta \omega / (\gamma G_x)$ | Position from frequency offset | m |
| 4 | $\mathrm{FOV}_x = \mathrm{BW}_{readout} / (\gamma G_x)$ | Field-of-view along readout | m |
| 5 | $k_x(t) = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} \int_0^t G_x(\tau) d\tau$ | k-space coordinate (general) | cycles/m |
| 6 | $k_x(t) = (\gamma/2\pi) \, G_x \, t$ | k-space coordinate (constant $G_x$) | cycles/m |
| 7 | $k_{x,\max} = 1 / (2 \Delta x)$ | Maximum k-space extent ↔ resolution | cycles/m |
| 8 | $\Delta x = \mathrm{FOV}_x / N_x$ | Pixel dimension in readout direction | m |
| 9 | $\mathrm{BW}_{pixel} = \mathrm{BW}_{readout} / N_x$ | Bandwidth per pixel | Hz |
| 10 | $\Delta t_{dwell} = 1 / \mathrm{BW}_{readout}$ | ADC dwell time | s |
| 11 | $T_{readout} = N_x \cdot \Delta t_{dwell}$ | Total readout duration | s |
| 12 | $G_x = \mathrm{BW}_{readout} / (\gamma \cdot \mathrm{FOV}_x)$ | Readout gradient amplitude (rearranged) | T/m |
| 13 | $\mathrm{SNR} \propto \sqrt{\mathrm{BW}_{pixel}}$ | SNR scales with square root of per-pixel BW | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Boundary / Validity Conditions:**
> - The gradient must be **linear** in space across the FOV; non-linearity produces geometric distortion.
> - Sampling must satisfy the **Nyquist criterion**: $\Delta k_x \le 1/\mathrm{FOV}_x$, equivalent to $\Delta t_{dwell} \le 1/\mathrm{BW}_{readout}$.
> - The receiver must pass the full range $\omega_0 \pm \pi \mathrm{BW}_{readout}$; otherwise high-frequency (edge-of-FOV) spins are filtered out (Gibbs / truncation artifact).
> - Chemical shift misregistration is **directly proportional to** readout bandwidth: a small $\mathrm{BW}_{readout}$ exaggerates fat–water shift.
