# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define k-space. Explain the significance of the center and the periphery of k-space in MRI imaging.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
**K-space** is a 2D matrix in the spatial frequency domain in which raw MRI data is stored before Fourier reconstruction into the diagnostic image. It is mathematically the 2D Fourier Transform of the transverse magnetization distribution m(x,y) of the imaged object.

- **Center of k-space (k = 0 region):** Contains **low spatial frequencies**, which encode the **overall image contrast, signal intensity, and gross shape** of the object. This is where most of the signal energy (SNR) is concentrated.
- **Periphery of k-space:** Contains **high spatial frequencies**, which encode the **fine anatomical detail and sharp edges** of the image. The maximum extent of k-space (k_max) determines the achievable spatial resolution (Δx = 1/(2k_max)).

This separation of contrast (center) and detail (periphery) is why fast MRI sequences can be designed to preferentially sample certain k-space regions.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Compare 2D and 3D MRI imaging techniques. List any two advantages of 3D over 2D imaging.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

| Aspect | 2D MRI | 3D MRI |
|---|---|---|
| Excited region | Single thin slice per TR | Entire slab / volume per TR |
| Phase-encoding directions | 1 (Y axis) | 2 (Y and Z partition) |
| Reconstruction | 2D FFT per slice | 3D FFT on full k-space |
| Slice thickness | Set by RF/gradient | Reconstructed from k_z data |

**Two advantages of 3D over 2D:**
1. **Higher SNR per partition:** SNR scales as √N_partition, since all spins in the excited slab contribute to every reconstructed partition.
2. **Thinner effective partitions:** Reconstructed slice thickness can be sub-millimeter without requiring an extremely selective RF pulse, enabling high through-plane resolution.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q: With a neat schematic, explain the MRI Fourier reconstruction process. Derive the relationship between k-space coordinates and applied magnetic field gradients. Discuss the role of FOV and spatial resolution in this process.**

#### Part (a): MRI Fourier Reconstruction Process (7 marks)

**Step 1 — K-space Definition:** The raw MRI signal S(k_x, k_y) is the 2D Fourier Transform of the transverse magnetization distribution m(x,y) of the imaged object:
$$S(k_x, k_y) = \iint m(x,y) \, e^{-i 2\pi (k_x x + k_y y)} \, dx \, dy$$

**Step 2 — K-space Traversal:** The k-space matrix is filled by applying:
- A **frequency-encoding gradient G_x** during signal readout, which sweeps the k-space trajectory along k_x.
- A **phase-encoding gradient G_y** (incremented per TR), which moves the trajectory to a new k_y line for each acquisition.

**Step 3 — Reconstruction:** A 2D Inverse Discrete Fourier Transform (implemented via FFT algorithm) is applied to the populated k-space matrix:
$$m(x,y) = \frac{1}{N_x N_y} \sum_{k_x} \sum_{k_y} S(k_x, k_y) \, e^{+i 2\pi (k_x x + k_y y)}$$

**Step 4 — Image Display:** The magnitude of the complex image is computed (magnitude reconstruction) and displayed.

#### Part (b): Derivation of k-Space Coordinates from Gradients (7 marks)

**Starting from the Larmor equation** for a spin at position (x,y) under gradients G_x(t) and G_y(t):
$$\omega(x,y,t) = \gamma \left[ x G_x(t) + y G_y(t) \right]$$

The accumulated phase over time t is:
$$\phi(x,y,t) = \int_0^t \gamma \left[ x G_x(\tau) + y G_y(\tau) \right] d\tau$$

Separating spatial and temporal variables:
$$\phi(x,y,t) = x \cdot \gamma \int_0^t G_x(\tau) d\tau + y \cdot \gamma \int_0^t G_y(\tau) d\tau$$

**Defining the k-space coordinates:**
$$k_x(t) = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} \int_0^t G_x(\tau) d\tau, \quad k_y(t) = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} \int_0^t G_y(\tau) d\tau$$

Substituting back:
$$\phi(x,y,t) = 2\pi (k_x \cdot x + k_y \cdot y)$$

The MRI signal becomes:
$$S(t) = \iint m(x,y) \, e^{-i 2\pi (k_x x + k_y y)} \, dx \, dy = \mathcal{F}\{m(x,y)\}$$

**This proves the MRI signal is the 2D Fourier Transform of the spin density, sampled at k-space coordinates (k_x, k_y) determined by the time-integrated gradient moments.**

#### Part (b) Continued: FOV and Spatial Resolution

**FOV:** FOV = 1/Δk. The k-space sampling interval Δk is set by the receiver bandwidth and the readout gradient. A small Δk (fine sampling) gives a large FOV; a large Δk gives a small FOV.

**Spatial Resolution:** Δx = FOV/N = 1/(2k_max). Higher resolution requires a larger k-space extent, which is achieved by either:
- Increasing the readout gradient strength (larger k_max at fixed BW)
- Increasing the matrix size N (more k-space samples)

**Trade-off:** Doubling resolution (halving Δx) requires doubling N, which doubles acquisition time and reduces SNR by a factor of √2.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q: Explain in detail the differences between 2D and 3D MRI acquisition. With a properly labeled block diagram, describe the k-space filling strategy for a 3D gradient echo sequence. A 3D acquisition uses FOV = 200 mm × 200 mm × 150 mm, matrix 256 × 256 × 64. Calculate: (i) in-plane resolution, (ii) k-space sampling intervals Δk_x, Δk_y, Δk_z, and (iii) the total k-space extent in each direction.**

#### Part (a): 2D vs 3D MRI Acquisition (7 marks)

**2D MRI Acquisition:**
- A thin slice is selectively excited using an RF pulse in the presence of G_ss.
- One phase-encoding gradient G_pe (along Y) is applied per TR.
- Frequency encoding G_ro (along X) during readout fills one k-space line per TR.
- Reconstruction: 2D FFT per slice; k-space is 2D (k_x, k_y).

**3D MRI Acquisition:**
- A slab-selective RF pulse excites the entire volume.
- TWO phase-encoding gradients are applied per TR: G_pe1 (Y) and G_pe2 (Z, partition encoding).
- Frequency encoding G_ro (X) during readout.
- Reconstruction: 3D FFT on the full 3D k-space volume (k_x, k_y, k_z).

**Block diagram of 3D k-space filling:**
```
Slab RF Excitation (Gss) → Gpe1 (Y) → Gpe2 (Z) → Gro (X) → ADC Readout
                                  ↓
                       (Repeat for all Npe1 × Npe2 combinations)
                                  ↓
                          3D k-space (kx, ky, kz) filled
                                  ↓
                          3D Inverse FFT → Volume m(x,y,z)
```

**Key advantages of 3D:** Higher SNR (√N_part gain), thinner reconstructed partitions, no inter-slice gap.

#### Part (b): Numerical Calculations (7 marks)

Given: FOV_x = 200 mm = 0.200 m, FOV_y = 200 mm = 0.200 m, FOV_z = 150 mm = 0.150 m, Matrix N_x = 256, N_y = 256, N_z = 64.

**(i) In-plane resolution:**
$$\Delta x = \frac{\text{FOV}_x}{N_x} = \frac{200 \text{ mm}}{256} = 0.781 \text{ mm}$$
$$\Delta y = \frac{\text{FOV}_y}{N_y} = \frac{200 \text{ mm}}{256} = 0.781 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) K-space sampling intervals:**
$$\Delta k_x = \frac{1}{\text{FOV}_x} = \frac{1}{0.200 \text{ m}} = 5.0 \text{ cycles/m}$$
$$\Delta k_y = \frac{1}{\text{FOV}_y} = \frac{1}{0.200 \text{ m}} = 5.0 \text{ cycles/m}$$
$$\Delta k_z = \frac{1}{\text{FOV}_z} = \frac{1}{0.150 \text{ m}} = 6.667 \text{ cycles/m}$$

**(iii) Total k-space extent:**
$$k_{\text{extent},x} = N_x \cdot \Delta k_x = 256 \cdot 5.0 = 1280 \text{ cycles/m}$$
$$k_{\text{extent},y} = N_y \cdot \Delta k_y = 256 \cdot 5.0 = 1280 \text{ cycles/m}$$
$$k_{\text{extent},z} = N_z \cdot \Delta k_z = 64 \cdot 6.667 = 426.7 \text{ cycles/m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** The k-space extent is *anisotropic* in 3D imaging — the through-plane extent (k_z) is smaller because N_z is smaller. This is why 3D MRI typically has *lower through-plane resolution* than in-plane resolution unless N_z is also large (which increases scan time as N_pe1 × N_pe2).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in KTU 2024 Exams on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing k_max and k_extent:** A frequent mistake is writing Δx = 1/k_max instead of the correct Δx = 1/(2·k_max). This single error can cost 2-3 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 1/(2π) in the k-space formula:** The correct k-space coordinate is k_x = (γ/2π)·∫G_x(τ)dτ. Writing k_x = γ·∫G_x(τ)dτ (omitting 2π) is incorrect.
>
> 3. **Confusing FOV and Resolution:** FOV is a *physical distance* (mm or cm); resolution is *per pixel* (mm/pixel). They are NOT the same quantity.
>
> 4. **Ignoring the Nyquist criterion:** When answering FOV-related questions, students often forget to mention the Nyquist sampling criterion (Δk ≤ 1/FOV) and its role in preventing aliasing.
>
> 5. **Mixing up phase and frequency encoding:** Phase encoding steps through k_y slowly (one line per TR); frequency encoding sweeps k_x rapidly (one full line per readout). Examiners will deduct marks for confusing these roles.
>
> 6. **Omitting the FFT in reconstruction:** Writing "k-space is the image" or "k-space is directly displayed" is a fundamental conceptual error costing 1-2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This the Night Before the Exam**

### 🎯 Core Concepts to Memorize
- ✅ K-space is the **spatial frequency domain** representation of MRI data; it is **NOT** the image.
- ✅ The MRI signal **is** the **2D Fourier Transform** of the spin density m(x,y).
- ✅ K-space coordinates: $k_x = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi}\int G_x d\tau$, $k_y = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi}\int G_y d\tau$.
- ✅ Reconstruction: Apply **2D IFFT** to k-space to get the image.

### 📐 Key Formulas (Must Know Cold)
- ✅ **FOV = 1/Δk** (reciprocal duality)
- ✅ **Δx = FOV / N** (pixel size)
- ✅ **Δx = 1/(2·k_max)** (resolution from k-space extent)
- ✅ **Δz = BW_ss / (γ·G_ss)** (slice thickness)
- ✅ **SNR_3D = SNR_2D · √N_partition** (3D SNR advantage)
- ✅ **T_acq = TR · N_pe · N_avg** (2D scan time)
- ✅ **T_acq = TR · N_pe1 · N_pe2 · N_avg** (3D scan time)
- ✅ **Nyquist: Δk ≤ 1/FOV** to avoid aliasing

### 🔄 2D vs 3D Imaging — Quick Comparison
- ✅ **2D:** Single slice per TR, one phase-encode direction, 2D FFT reconstruction.
- ✅ **3D:** Slab excitation, two phase-encode directions (Y and Z), 3D FFT, higher SNR.
- ✅ **3D trade-off:** Longer scan time, more data storage, computationally heavier reconstruction.

### 🎨 K-Space Anatomy
- ✅ **Center of k-space:** Low frequencies → **contrast, SNR, overall shape**.
- ✅ **Periphery of k-space:** High frequencies → **fine detail, edges, resolution**.
- ✅ **Total k-space extent:** Determines spatial resolution.
- ✅ **Sampling density (Δk):** Determines FOV (anti-aliasing limit).

### ⚖️ Trade-offs to Remember
- ✅ **Δx ↔ FOV:** Halving FOV (at fixed N) doubles in-plane resolution but risks aliasing.
- ✅ **Δx ↔ N:** Doubling N doubles resolution but doubles scan time and halves SNR by √2.
- ✅ **FOV ↔ Δk:** Halving FOV requires doubling Δk (coarser sampling), which only works if the object fits.
- ✅ **3D vs 2D:** 3D has √N_partition SNR gain but N_pe1·N_pe2 longer scan time.

### 🧮 Standard Constants and Units
- ✅ **γ (¹H) = 42.58 MHz/T** (gyromagnetic ratio of proton)
- ✅ **FOV units:** mm or cm
- ✅ **Spatial resolution units:** mm (typically 0.5–5 mm clinical range)
- ✅ **k-space units:** cycles/m (or m⁻¹)
- ✅ **Slice thickness:** mm

### 🩺 Clinical and Engineering Relevance
- ✅ Fourier reconstruction is the *universal* MRI reconstruction method (used in spin echo, gradient echo, EPI, spiral, radial).
- ✅ Choice between 2D and 3D depends on anatomy, required resolution, available scan time, and SNR budget.
- ✅ Modern clinical scanners (3T, 7T) and advanced sequences (fMRI, DTI, MRS) all rely on these fundamental k-space and Fourier principles.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acceleration Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Acceleration Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What Are Acceleration Methods in Medical Imaging?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Acceleration Methods* are a family of pulse sequence design strategies, data acquisition schemes, and post-processing reconstruction algorithms used in medical imaging — most prominently in **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** — to **reduce the total scan time** required to acquire a diagnostically adequate image, while ideally preserving spatial resolution, signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and contrast.

In the broader context of KTU Module 2 (which typically covers **Image Formation, Reconstruction, and Acquisition Strategies**), acceleration methods address the central engineering trade-off of medical imaging:

> **Acquisition Time ∝ (Phase-Encode Steps) × (Repetition Time TR)**

Reducing either factor (or both) yields faster scans. The three principal pillars discussed in the KTU syllabus are:

| Pillar | Strategy | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Parallel Imaging (PI)** | Spatial encoding via receiver coil arrays | Reduced SNR |
| **Partial Fourier (PF) Imaging** | Hermitian symmetry exploitation | Lower SNR, reconstruction artifacts |
| **Fast / Echo-Train Sequences** | Multi-echo per excitation (FSE/EPI) | T2* blurring, specific absorption rate (SAR) limits |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Many Lenses, One Photograph" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — Why do we need acceleration?**
> Imagine a **single photographer** trying to capture a panoramic landscape. He must rotate his head, take one strip at a time, and stitch them together. This is **slow** (the MRI analogy: one phase-encode line per TR). 

> Now imagine giving him **10 cameras** with slightly overlapping but distinct fields of view. By **combining** these 10 partial views intelligently, he can reconstruct the full panorama from a fraction of the strips. This is **parallel imaging** — the "cameras" are the **receiver coil array elements**.

> If he further realizes the left half of the panorama is roughly a **mirror image** of the right half (with small imperfections), he can photograph only **60%** of the scene and mathematically "fold" it to fill in the rest. This is **partial Fourier imaging**.

> Finally, instead of making one long exposure per strip, he uses a **machine gun** that fires many exposures from a single setup — **multi-echo / EPI sequences**.

This mental picture will serve as our anchor as we explore the rigorous mathematics below.

---

## 1.3 Why Acceleration Matters: The Clinical Imperative

- **Patient comfort** — shorter breath-holds reduce motion artifact.
- **Throughput** — hospitals can scan more patients per day.
- **Dynamic imaging** — real-time cardiac, perfusion, and functional studies become feasible.
- **Pediatric and uncooperative patient handling** — anesthesia time is reduced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — The SNR Penalty Law:**
> Every classical acceleration method obeys a near-universal rule — the SNR of the accelerated image is **lower** than the fully-sampled image by a geometry-dependent factor $g$ and the square-root of the acceleration factor $R$:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{accel}} = \frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{full}}}{g \cdot \sqrt{R}}
$$

> This is the *single most important equation* a KTU examiner will expect you to recall for any acceleration topic.

---

## 1.4 Scope of This Module Note

This note covers, in KTU-relevant depth:

1. **Parallel Imaging — SENSE (image-domain)** and **GRAPPA (k-space)** with full derivations.
2. **Partial Fourier / Half-Fourier** with homodyne and POCS reconstruction.
3. **Brief coverage** of EPI and FSE/TSE for completeness.
4. **Compressed Sensing** as a modern advanced technique.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Physics — Why Is MRI Slow?

In a standard **spin-warp (2D Fourier transform) MRI** sequence, one line of k-space is acquired per **TR** interval. The total scan time is therefore:

$$
T_{\text{scan}} = N_{\text{PE}} \times N_{\text{NEX}} \times \text{TR}
$$

where $N_{\text{PE}}$ = number of phase-encode steps, $N_{\text{NEX}}$ = number of excitations (averages), and **TR** = repetition time.

**Acceleration methods** directly attack one or more of these three multipliers. We now study each in detail.

---

## 2.2 Parallel Imaging (PI): The Core Concept

Parallel imaging exploits the fact that a **phased-array receiver coil** with $N_c$ elements provides $N_c$ **spatially distinct** measurements of the same anatomy. Each coil element has a known **spatial sensitivity profile** $C_\ell(x,y)$, which falls off away from that coil.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle (SENSE formulation by Pruessmann et al., 1999):**
> By *undersampling* k-space by a factor $R$, the reconstructed field-of-view (FOV) is reduced by $1/R$, causing **aliasing (wrap-around)**. The signal at each aliased pixel becomes a linear combination of $R$ true image pixels, weighted by the coil sensitivities. Solving this linear system **unfolds** the image.

### 2.2.1 SENSE — Image Domain Unaliasing

The signal $S_\ell$ measured by coil $\ell$ at an aliased pixel position $r$ is:

$$
S_\ell(r) = \sum_{j=1}^{R} C_\ell(r_j) \cdot \rho(r_j) + n_\ell
$$

In matrix form:

$$
\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{C} \cdot \boldsymbol{\rho} + \mathbf{n}
$$

- $\mathbf{S}$ is a vector of length $N_c$ (one complex value per coil at the aliased pixel).
- $\mathbf{C}$ is an $N_c \times R$ sensitivity matrix.
- $\boldsymbol{\rho}$ is the vector of $R$ true pixel values we wish to recover.
- $\mathbf{n}$ is noise.

**Reconstruction** uses the **Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse** with optional Tikhonov regularization:

$$
\hat{\boldsymbol{\rho}} = \left( \mathbf{C}^H \boldsymbol{\Psi}^{-1} \mathbf{C} \right)^{-1} \mathbf{C}^H \boldsymbol{\Psi}^{-1} \mathbf{S}
$$

where $\boldsymbol{\Psi}$ is the coil noise covariance matrix, and $H$ denotes the Hermitian (conjugate) transpose.

### 2.2.2 GRAPPA — k-Space Synthesis

The **GeneRalized Autocalibrating Partially Parallel Acquisition (GRAPPA)** method (Griswold et al., 2002) operates in **k-space**. Missing lines (e.g., the skipped phase-encode lines) are **synthesized** as a linear combination of acquired neighboring lines from *all* coils.

For an acceleration factor $R$ and an auto-calibration signal (ACS) region of size $N_{\text{ACS}}$ central lines:

$$
S_\ell(k_x, k_y + m \Delta k_y) = \sum_{j=1}^{N_c} \sum_{b=0}^{N_b-1} n_{j,b,m,\ell} \cdot S_j(k_x, k_y + b R \Delta k_y)
$$

where:
- $m = 1, 2, \ldots, R-1$ indexes the missing lines between two acquired lines.
- $N_b$ is the number of source blocks (typically 4–6).
- $n_{j,b,m,\ell}$ are the **GRAPPA weights** fitted from the ACS data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why GRAPPA is preferred in practice:**
> It does not require an *explicit* coil sensitivity map. The weights are learned from the fully-sampled central k-space region itself, making the method more **robust to B1 inhomogeneity** and motion than SENSE.

---

## 2.3 Partial Fourier Imaging (Half-Fourier)

MRI's k-space satisfies the **conjugate (Hermitian) symmetry** property for a *real-valued* object:

$$
S(-k_x, -k_y) = S^*(k_x, k_y)
$$

Therefore, **only half (plus a few extra lines)** of k-space needs to be acquired.

### 2.3.1 Reconstruction Strategies

| Method | Approach | Computational Cost | Robustness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Zero-Filling** | Fill missing region with zeros, then iFFT | Low | Poor — Gibbs artifact |
| **Conjugate Synthesis** | Directly copy conjugate of acquired half | Lowest | Acceptable for phase-insensitive contrasts |
| **Homodyne (Margosian)** | Low-pass filter the symmetric part to recover true phase | Moderate | Good |
| **POCS (Projection Onto Convex Sets)** | Iterative phase retrieval, enforcing data consistency + magnitude support | High | Best for low SNR data |

> [!NOTE]
> **Homodyne Reconstruction — Step Outline:**
> 1. Acquire slightly more than 50% of k-space (typically 60%, i.e., **PF factor 0.6**).
> 2. The acquired data is shifted so that the **k-space center is symmetric** around zero.
> 3. Apply a **low-resolution phase map** derived from the symmetric region.
> 4. Multiply the full k-space (with zeros in the missing half) by $e^{-i\phi(k)}$ to remove the phase.
> 5. Take the **real part** of the inverse Fourier transform.

---

## 2.4 Fast Imaging Sequences (Brief Coverage)

### 2.4.1 Echo Planar Imaging (EPI)

In **single-shot EPI**, the entire k-space is traversed in **one TR** by using a rapidly oscillating readout gradient. Speed-up factor can exceed $R = 64$ relative to conventional spin-warp.

> **Trade-offs:** Susceptibility artifact, T2* blurring, geometric distortion, and chemical shift artifact (fat-water shift in EPI).

### 2.4.2 Fast (Turbo) Spin Echo — FSE / TSE

Multiple spin echoes are generated within one TR using an **echo train** of $180°$ refocusing pulses. The **echo train length (ETL)** equals the acceleration factor $R$.

> **Trade-offs:** T2 decay during the train causes **blurring** along the phase-encode direction; SAR increases with ETL.

---

## 2.5 Compressed Sensing (CS) — A Modern Frontier

CS exploits the fact that medical images are **compressible** (sparse in some transform domain, e.g., wavelet). The reconstruction solves:

$$
\min_{\mathbf{x}} \Vert \mathbf{\Psi} \mathbf{x} \Vert_1 \quad \text{subject to} \quad \Vert \mathbf{F}_u \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y} \Vert_2 < \epsilon
$$

- $\mathbf{\Psi}$ = sparsifying transform (wavelet, total variation).
- $\mathbf{F}_u$ = undersampled Fourier operator.
- $\mathbf{y}$ = acquired k-space data.
- $\Vert \cdot \Vert_1$ = L1 norm (promotes sparsity).

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.A — Master Cheat Sheet (Commit to Memory)**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total scan time | $T_{\text{scan}} = N_{\text{PE}} \cdot N_{\text{NEX}} \cdot \text{TR}$ | seconds |
| 2 | Acceleration factor | $R = N_{\text{PE,full}} / N_{\text{PE,accel}}$ | dimensionless, $R \in \{2, 3, 4\}$ typical |
| 3 | PI SNR penalty | $\text{SNR}_{\text{acc}} = \text{SNR}_{\text{full}} / (g \sqrt{R})$ | $g$ = **geometry factor** ($g \ge 1$) |
| 4 | SENSE unaliasing | $\hat{\rho} = (C^H \Psi^{-1} C)^{-1} C^H \Psi^{-1} S$ | image-domain pseudoinverse |
| 5 | GRAPPA synthesis | $S_{\ell}^{\text{miss}} = \sum_j \sum_b n_{j,b,m,\ell} \cdot S_j^{\text{acq}}$ | k-space linear combination |
| 6 | Hermitian symmetry | $S(-k_x,-k_y) = S^*(k_x,k_y)$ | holds only for *real* object |
| 7 | EPI readout time | $T_{\text{readout}} = N_{\text{readout}} \cdot \text{dwell time}$ | typically 30–80 ms |
| 8 | FSE / TSE factor | $R = \text{ETL}$ (Echo Train Length) | subject to T2 decay |
| 9 | CS L1-minimization | $\min \Vert \Psi x \Vert_1$ s.t. $\Vert F_u x - y \Vert_2 \le \epsilon$ | nonlinear, iterative |
| 10 | Geometry factor | $g = \sqrt{[(C^H \Psi^{-1} C)^{-1}]_{jj} \cdot [C^H \Psi^{-1} C]_{jj}}$ | pixel-wise; measures noise amplification |

> **CRITICAL LaTeX TIP for KTU:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of the vertical bar `|` in tables. The pipe `|` *terminates* a markdown table column, breaking the rendering. The expression $\vert \mathbf{\Psi} \mathbf{x} \vert_1$ is *identical* to $\|\Psi x\|_1$ in LaTeX but table-safe.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Parallel Imaging SNR Penalty

> **Goal:** Show rigorously why $\text{SNR}_{\text{acc}} = \text{SNR}_{\text{full}} / (g\sqrt{R})$ for SENSE.

### Step 1 — Full-Scan (No Acceleration) SNR

For a fully-sampled acquisition with $N_{\text{PE,full}}$ phase encodes, the noise standard deviation in the reconstructed pixel $\rho_j$ of the **single-coil** sum-of-squares (SoS) image is:

$$
\sigma_{\text{full}} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\sqrt{N_{\text{PE,full}}}}
$$

where $\sigma_0$ is the per-sample noise standard deviation in raw k-space.

### Step 2 — Undersampled Acquisition

With acceleration $R$, only $N_{\text{PE,full}}/R$ lines are acquired. The per-pixel **raw noise** increases by $\sqrt{R}$:

$$
\sigma_{\text{raw,acc}} = \sigma_0 \cdot \sqrt{R / N_{\text{PE,full}}}
$$

### Step 3 — SENSE Reconstruction Noise Propagation

The SENSE reconstruction is a linear operator $\mathbf{A} = (C^H \Psi^{-1} C)^{-1} C^H \Psi^{-1}$. Applied to the noise vector $\mathbf{n} \sim \mathcal{CN}(0, \Psi)$, the reconstructed pixel $j$ has variance:

$$
\text{Var}(\hat{\rho}_j) = \sigma_0^2 \cdot R \cdot \left[(C^H \Psi^{-1} C)^{-1}\right]_{jj}
$$

### Step 4 — Define the Geometry Factor

The **diagonal element** of the inverse matrix is the per-pixel noise amplification:

$$
g_j = \sqrt{\left[(C^H \Psi^{-1} C)^{-1}\right]_{jj} \cdot \left[C^H \Psi^{-1} C\right]_{jj}}
$$

This $g_j$ is the **g-factor** at pixel $j$. For ideal, perfectly orthogonal coil sensitivities, $g = 1$.

### Step 5 — Form the Ratio

$$
\frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{full}}}{\text{SNR}_{\text{acc}}} = \frac{\sigma_0 / \sqrt{N_{\text{PE,full}}}}{\sigma_0 \cdot g \cdot \sqrt{R / N_{\text{PE,full}}}} = g \cdot \sqrt{R}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{SNR}_{\text{acc}} = \dfrac{\text{SNR}_{\text{full}}}{g \cdot \sqrt{R}}\;}
$$

> This derivation, in slightly compressed form, is a guaranteed KTU 14-marker or 7-marker sub-question.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem: Worked Example

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A 3 T MRI uses an 8-channel head coil. SENSE with $R=3$ is applied. Fully-sampled scan time was 4 min 12 s. The coil array's mean g-factor at the brain center is 1.1, while the peripheral g-factor reaches 1.8. The original full-scan SNR in white matter is 80.
>
> **Compute:** (a) Accelerated scan time, (b) Central SNR, (c) Peripheral SNR.

### Part (a) — Scan Time

$$
T_{\text{acc}} = \frac{T_{\text{full}}}{R} = \frac{252 \text{ s}}{3} = 84 \text{ s} = 1 \text{ min } 24 \text{ s}
$$

### Part (b) — Central SNR

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{central}} = \frac{80}{1.1 \cdot \sqrt{3}} = \frac{80}{1.905} \approx 41.99
$$

### Part (c) — Peripheral SNR

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{peripheral}} = \frac{80}{1.8 \cdot \sqrt{3}} = \frac{80}{3.118} \approx 25.66
$$

> **Insight:** A 3× speed-up costs ~50% SNR centrally but ~68% SNR peripherally — clinical PI protocols never exceed $R$ where peripheral g-factor explodes.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of GRAPPA Weights via Least-Squares Fitting

> **Goal:** Derive the GRAPPA weight set $\{n_{j,b,m,\ell}\}$ from ACS data.

### Step 1 — Organize Acquired and ACS Data

Let the ACS region contain $N_{\text{ACS}}$ fully-sampled lines, indexed by $k_y = -N_{\text{ACS}}/2, \ldots, +N_{\text{ACS}}/2$. For each target line offset $m$ (with $1 \le m \le R-1$), we form the column vector of acquired source samples and the column vector of ACS target samples.

### Step 2 — Form the Linear System

For target coil $\ell$ and target position $k_y + m\Delta k_y$:

$$
\mathbf{t} = \mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{n}
$$

- $\mathbf{t}$ is $N_{\text{ACS}} \times 1$ (target ACS values).
- $\mathbf{A}$ is $N_{\text{ACS}} \times (N_c \cdot N_b)$ (source ACS values).
- $\mathbf{n}$ is $(N_c \cdot N_b) \times 1$ (the GRAPPA weights to be solved).

### Step 3 — Solve in the Least-Squares Sense

$$
\hat{\mathbf{n}} = (\mathbf{A}^H \mathbf{A})^{-1} \mathbf{A}^H \mathbf{t}
$$

This is the standard **Moore–Penrose pseudoinverse** solution, identical in form to the SENSE formula but operating in k-space.

### Step 4 — Apply to Under-sampled Data

For each missing line in the imaging region, the synthesized k-space line is:

$$
\hat{S}_\ell(k_x, k_y + m\Delta k_y) = \sum_{j=1}^{N_c} \sum_{b=0}^{N_b - 1} \hat{n}_{j,b,m,\ell} \cdot S_j(k_x, k_y + bR\Delta k_y)
$$

### Step 5 — Final Image

The coil images are reconstructed by inverse Fourier transform, then combined via sum-of-squares (SoS):

$$
I(x,y) = \sqrt{\sum_{\ell=1}^{N_c} \vert I_\ell(x,y) \vert^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** In KTU answer scripts, mention that $\hat{\mathbf{n}}$ is a *complex-valued* weight vector (not magnitude-only) — this is a frequent student error.

---

## 3.4 Homodyne Reconstruction — Algebraic Walk-Through

The **acquired** partial k-space data is $S_{\text{acq}}(k_y)$ for $k_y \in [-k_{y,\max}, \, k_{y,\text{cut}}]$, where $k_{y,\text{cut}} > 0$ defines the PF fraction.

**Step 1 — Symmetrize:**

$$
S_{\text{sym}}(k_y) = \begin{cases} S_{\text{acq}}(k_y) & \text{if } k_y \le k_{y,\text{cut}} \\ S_{\text{acq}}^*(-k_y) & \text{if } k_y > k_{y,\text{cut}} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 2 — Low-Pass Phase Filter:** Let $H(k_y)$ be a smooth low-pass filter that equals 1 near the k-space center and 0 at the edge. The phase map is:

$$
\phi(\mathbf{r}) = \angle \left[ \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{ H(k_y) \cdot S_{\text{sym}}(k_y) \} \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Phase-Correct the Full Data:**

$$
S_{\text{PC}}(k_y) = S_{\text{acq}}(k_y) \cdot e^{-i\phi(k_y)}
$$

where $\phi(k_y) = \mathcal{F}\{\phi(\mathbf{r})\}$.

**Step 4 — Hermitian Symmetrize to Full k-Space:**

$$
S_{\text{full}}(k_y) = \begin{cases} S_{\text{PC}}(k_y) & k_y \le k_{y,\text{cut}} \\ S_{\text{PC}}^*(-k_y) & k_y > k_{y,\text{cut}} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 5 — Inverse Fourier Transform and Take Real Part:**

$$
I(x,y) = \text{Re}\left\{ \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{ S_{\text{full}}(k_y) \} \right\}
$$

> **Engineering significance:** The "real part" operation throws away any residual phase error, but also throws away half the noise — a key reason why Homodyne PF images have a *partial* SNR loss, not a full halving.

---

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix: SENSE vs. GRAPPA

| Property | SENSE | GRAPPA |
|---|---|---|
| Reconstruction domain | Image space | k-space |
| Coil sensitivity map | **Required (explicit)** | Not required (learned from ACS) |
| Sensitivity to motion | High (maps become invalid) | Low (weights re-fit from ACS) |
| Computational cost | Lower (single matrix inversion per aliased pixel set) | Higher (large least-squares fit) |
| Optimal use case | Static imaging, low field inhomogeneity | Dynamic imaging, cardiac, contrast-enhanced studies |
| Patent status | Royalty-bearing (Pruessmann) | Royalty-free (open, popular in research) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Acceleration Method Decision Tree

> The following Mermaid diagram (per V10 safeguard rules — all node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with `n`, labels contain no `**` or HTML) outlines the *engineer's decision flow* when selecting an acceleration method.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nStart([Clinical Imaging Task]) --> nQ1{Is motion a major concern?}
    nQ1 -- Yes --> nDyn[Use Dynamic / Single-Shot Method]
    nQ1 -- No --> nQ2{Is phase stability critical?}
    nQ2 -- Yes --> nPF[Choose Partial Fourier with Homodyne]
    nQ2 -- No --> nQ3{Are coil sensitivities well-characterized?}
    nQ3 -- Yes --> nSENSE[Apply SENSE in Image Domain]
    nQ3 -- No --> nQ4{Is high tolerance to B1 inhomogeneity needed?}
    nQ4 -- Yes --> nGRAPPA[Apply GRAPPA in k-Space]
    nQ4 -- No --> nCS[Consider Compressed Sensing]
    nDyn --> nEPI[Echo Planar Imaging]
    nDyn --> nSMS[Simultaneous Multi-Slice]
    nSENSE --> nOut1[Unaliasing with Pseudoinverse]
    nGRAPPA --> nOut2[GRAPPA Weight Fitting from ACS]
    nCS --> nOut3[Iterative L1 Reconstruction]
    nPF --> nOut4[Hermitian Symmetry + Phase Correction]
    nEPI --> nFinal([Reconstructed Image])
    nSMS --> nFinal
    nOut1 --> nFinal
    nOut2 --> nFinal
    nOut3 --> nFinal
    nOut4 --> nFinal
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: SENSE Reconstruction Pipeline

> This diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the image-domain SENSE algorithm.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nA[Raw k-Space from N_c Coils] --> nB[Parallel Acquisition R-fold Undersampled]
    nB --> nC[Inverse Fourier Transform per Coil]
    nC --> nD[Aliased Multi-Coil Images]
    nD --> nE[Coil Sensitivity Maps C_ell from Reference Scan]
    nE --> nF[Form Encoding Matrix C]
    nD --> nF
    nF --> nG[Apply SENSE Pseudoinverse A]
    nG --> nH[Unaliased Full-FOV Image]
    nH --> nI[Sum of Squares or Optimal Combination]
    nI --> nJ([Final Diagnostic Image])
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: GRAPPA Reconstruction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nK1[Undersampled k-Space Lines] --> nK2{Is Point in ACS Region?}
    nK2 -- Yes --> nK3[Use as Training Data for Weights]
    nK2 -- No --> nK4[Synthesize Missing Line via GRAPPA Weights]
    nK3 --> nK5[Form Linear System A x = t]
    nK5 --> nK6[Least-Squares Solve for Weights n]
    nK6 --> nK4
    nK4 --> nK7[Complete k-Space per Coil]
    nK7 --> nK8[Inverse Fourier Transform per Coil]
    nK8 --> nK9[Sum of Squares Coil Combination]
    nK9 --> nK10([Final Reconstructed Image])
```

---

## 4.4 k-Space Undersampling Pattern Schematic

> Since the Mermaid system cannot faithfully draw 2D k-space, the block diagram below represents the **logical acquisition mask** for a 2D-FT MRI sequence with $R=2$ parallel imaging.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nMaskStart([k-Space Acquisition Mask]) --> nM1[Phase Encode Line 1: ACQUIRED]
    nM1 --> nM2[Phase Encode Line 2: SKIPPED]
    nM2 --> nM3[Phase Encode Line 3: ACQUIRED]
    nM3 --> nM4[Phase Encode Line 4: SKIPPED]
    nM4 --> nM5[Continue Alternating Pattern]
    nM5 --> nM6[Center Region: ACS Fully Sampled]
    nM6 --> nM7([Mask Ratio Acquired to Total = 1/R])
```

> **Reading this for a KTU 7-marker answer:** A $R=2$ mask acquires 50% of the phase-encode lines; the central 8–24 lines are *fully sampled* to serve as the **Auto-Calibration Signal (ACS)** for GRAPPA weight training.

---

## 4.5 Cross-Sectional: Partial Fourier Acquisition Region

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nPF1[Full k_y Range: -k_max to +k_max] --> nPF2{Acquire Which Fraction?}
    nPF2 -- PF factor 1.0 --> nPF3[100 percent: Full k-Space]
    nPF2 -- PF factor 0.75 --> nPF4[75 percent Acquired]
    nPF2 -- PF factor 0.6 --> nPF5[60 percent Acquired Standard Half Fourier]
    nPF2 -- PF factor 0.5 --> nPF6[50 percent Minimum Theoretical Half]
    nPF4 --> nPF7[Hermitian Symmetry Reconstructs Remainder]
    nPF5 --> nPF7
    nPF6 --> nPF7
    nPF3 --> nPF8([No Acceleration Possible])
    nPF7 --> nPF9([Reconstructed Image with Phase Correction])
```
