### 4.3 Decision Tree — Radiographer's Parameter Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Required Image Density] --> Q1{Patient Size?}
    Q1 -->|Small / Pediatric| LOWKV[Reduce kV by 10 to 15 percent]
    Q1 -->|Average Adult| STD[Standard kV 70 to 90]
    Q1 -->|Large / Obese| HIGHKV[Increase kV by 10 to 15 percent]

    LOWKV --> CHECK1{Motion Risk?}
    STD --> CHECK1
    HIGHKV --> CHECK1

    CHECK1 -->|Yes| HIGHMA[Use high mA short time]
    CHECK1 -->|No| FLEX[Standard mA time combination]

    HIGHMA --> REC1[Compensate with adjusted mAs]
    FLEX --> REC1
    REC1[Apply 15 percent rule for fine tuning] --> FINAL[Final Exposure Parameters]

    style START fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17
    style FINAL fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define mA and kV in radiography. State which parameter controls the quantity and which controls the quality of the X-ray beam.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **mA (milliampere):** The tube current, measured in milliamperes, that determines the **number of electrons** flowing from cathode to anode per second. It governs the **quantity (intensity / number of photons)** of the X-ray beam. **(1.5 marks)**
- **kV (kilovoltage):** The peak potential difference, measured in kilovolts peak (kVp), applied across the X-ray tube. It determines the **kinetic energy of bombarding electrons** and hence the **quality (penetrating power / photon energy)** of the X-ray beam. **(1.5 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the 15% rule in radiography and write its clinical significance.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Statement:** Increasing the kV by **15%** (e.g., from 70 to 80.5 kVp) approximately **doubles** the X-ray beam intensity at the image receptor, equivalent to doubling the mAs. **(1.5 marks)**
- **Clinical Significance:**
  1. Allows **reduction of mAs** while maintaining image density → **lower patient dose** and **less tube heat**. **(0.75 mark)**
  2. Helps compensate for **increased patient thickness** without re-shooting. **(0.75 mark)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail how mA affects (i) image density, (ii) patient dose, (iii) image noise, and (iv) beam quality. Justify each effect with the underlying physical principle.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A radiographic exposure of 100 mAs at 70 kVp produces an optical density of 1.2 on a screen-film system. Find (i) the new mAs required at 80 kVp to obtain the same optical density, and (ii) the % reduction in patient dose achieved. Use the 15% rule.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

1. **(i) Image Density:** Image density (optical density on film) is **directly proportional to the number of photons reaching the receptor**, which is in turn **directly proportional to mAs**. Therefore, doubling mA doubles photon count and increases film density logarithmically as $OD = \gamma \log_{10}(mAs) + C$. Higher mA → darker image. **(2 marks)**

2. **(ii) Patient Dose:** Patient skin/entrance dose is directly proportional to the number of X-ray photons incident on the body, hence **directly proportional to mA** (and to exposure time). Therefore $D \propto mAs$. Higher mA → higher patient dose. **(1.5 marks)**

3. **(iii) Image Noise (Quantum Mottle):** Image noise in radiography is governed by **quantum statistics**. The relative noise scales as $1/\sqrt{N}$, where *N* is the number of photons forming the image. Higher mA → more photons → **less quantum mottle / noise**. **(1.5 marks)**

4. **(iv) Beam Quality:** Beam quality (defined by HVL or effective energy) is determined by kV, **not by mA**. mA only changes the *number* of photons, not their *energy*. Thus mA has **no effect on beam quality**. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- Initial exposure: $mAs_1 = 100$, $kV_1 = 70$ kVp, $OD_1 = 1.2$
- New kV: $kV_2 = 80$ kVp
- Use the 15% rule.

**Step 1 — Check 15% Rule Applicability:**

The change in kV: $\frac{80 - 70}{70} = \frac{10}{70} = 0.1429 \approx 14.3\%$

This is close to 15%, so we may apply the 15% rule.

**Step 2 — Apply the 15% Rule:**

A 15% (here ≈14.3%) increase in kV **doubles** the exposure at the receptor. To maintain the **same optical density**, we must **halve** the mAs:

$$mAs_2 = \frac{mAs_1}{2} = \frac{100}{2} = 50 \text{ mAs}$$

**Answer (i):** New mAs required = **50 mAs** **(3 marks)**

**Step 3 — Calculate % Reduction in Patient Dose:**

Since patient dose is proportional to mAs (kV change contribution is partially cancelled by reduced photoelectric absorption, but the dominant dose-reduction effect is from lower mAs):

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{mAs_1 - mAs_2}{mAs_1} \times 100 = \frac{100 - 50}{100} \times 100 = 50\%$$

**Answer (ii):** Patient dose reduced by **50%** **(4 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the effect of kV on (i) X-ray beam quality, (ii) image contrast, (iii) patient dose, and (iv) scatter radiation. Use appropriate formulas where necessary.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A chest X-ray is taken at 120 kVp and 5 mAs. To reduce patient dose, the radiographer increases kVp to 132 kVp. Using the 15% rule, calculate (i) the new mAs required to maintain the same receptor exposure, and (ii) the resulting % change in subject contrast if the contrast was originally 0.40 and decreases by 4% for every 1 kVp increase.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

1. **(i) Beam Quality:** Beam quality is a measure of the penetrating power of an X-ray beam, expressed as the Half-Value Layer (HVL) or effective energy. It is **directly controlled by kV**. Higher kV → higher photon energy → higher HVL. Quantitatively: $E_{max} = e \cdot kV_{p}$ and $\lambda_{min} = 1.24/kV_{p}$ (in nm). **(2 marks)**

2. **(ii) Image Contrast:** Subject contrast depends on the differential attenuation of tissues. At **low kV**, the photoelectric effect dominates, making differences between tissues (e.g., bone vs. soft tissue) more pronounced. At **high kV**, Compton scattering dominates and tissues attenuate more similarly → **reduced subject contrast**. So **contrast decreases as kV increases**. **(1.5 marks)**

3. **(iii) Patient Dose:** Although higher kV produces more photons per mAs, the dominant effect of increasing kV is that **fewer mAs are needed** to maintain the same receptor exposure (per 15% rule). This typically results in a **net reduction in patient dose**, because photoelectric absorption in the patient (which delivers dose) drops sharply with kV. **(1.5 marks)**

4. **(iv) Scatter Radiation:** Higher kV photons are more likely to undergo **Compton scattering** (which dominates above ~50 keV in soft tissue), producing a higher absolute number of scattered photons reaching the receptor. This degrades contrast but increases the total radiation field. **(2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:**
- $kV_1 = 120$ kVp, $mAs_1 = 5$ mAs
- $kV_2 = 132$ kVp
- Original contrast $C_1 = 0.40$
- Contrast decreases by 4% per 1 kVp increase

**Step 1 — Verify 15% Rule Condition:**

$$\frac{132 - 120}{120} = \frac{12}{120} = 0.10 = 10\%$$

The 15% rule is **not directly satisfied** (10% ≠ 15%). We must apply the general formula:

$$\frac{X_2}{X_1} = \left( \frac{kV_2}{kV_1} \right)^{5}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Intensity Ratio:**

$$\frac{X_2}{X_1} = \left( \frac{132}{120} \right)^{5} = (1.10)^{5} = 1.6105$$

The exposure at the receptor increases by a factor of 1.6105.

**Step 3 — Calculate New mAs for Same Exposure:**

To maintain the same exposure, the new mAs must be:

$$mAs_2 = \frac{mAs_1}{1.6105} = \frac{5}{1.6105} = 3.105 \text{ mAs}$$

**Answer (i):** New mAs ≈ **3.10 mAs** **(3 marks)**

**Step 4 — Calculate New Subject Contrast:**

kV increased by 12 kVp (from 120 to 132). Contrast drops by 4% per kVp:

$$C_2 = C_1 \times \left(1 - 0.04 \times 12\right) = 0.40 \times (1 - 0.48) = 0.40 \times 0.52 = 0.208$$

**Answer (ii):** New subject contrast = **0.208** (a 48% reduction). **(4 marks)**

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing quantity vs. quality:** Writing that mA controls energy or that kV controls photon count. Examiner deducts 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the 15% rule's non-linearity:** Applying it as a linear "15% kV = 15% intensity" relationship. The correct interpretation: 15% kV **doubles** intensity.
> 3. **Omitting the dose–contrast trade-off:** A full answer to kV effects must mention BOTH that dose decreases (good) AND contrast decreases (bad). Single-sided answers lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing mA and mAs in numerical problems:** Always use mAs in the proportionality equations; if mA is given separately, multiply by exposure time.
> 5. **Skipping units in formula sheets:** KTU examiners specifically award a half-mark for correct SI/radiological units.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **mA** = quantity factor → ↑ mA ⇒ ↑ photon count ⇒ ↑ density, ↑ dose, ↓ noise
- **kV** = quality factor → ↑ kV ⇒ ↑ photon energy ⇒ ↑ penetration, ↓ contrast, ↓ dose
- **mAs = mA × t** is the master quantity variable for receptor exposure
- **15% Rule:** ↑ kV by 15% ⇒ **doubles** receptor exposure ⇒ halve mAs to maintain density
- **Doubling mAs** increases film optical density by $0.301 \times \gamma$ (≈ 0.75 for $\gamma=2.5$)
- **kV controls** beam quality (HVL, $\lambda_{min}$, $E_{max}$)
- **mA does NOT** affect beam quality, contrast, or HVL
- **High kV** is preferred for thick patients and dose reduction (e.g., chest X-ray ~120 kVp)
- **Low kV** is preferred for high-contrast studies (e.g., mammography 22–35 kVp, bone detail)
- **Reciprocity law:** mA and time are interchangeable for a fixed mAs (but motion blur differs)
- **Trade-off master principle:** Every clinical exposure is a balance between *image quality* (density, contrast, noise) and *patient safety* (dose)
- **Key formulas to memorize:** $mAs = mA \cdot t$, $\lambda_{min} = 1.24/kV_p$, $(1+f)^5 = 2$ for 15% rule
- **Tube heat constraint:** High mA + long time + high kV = anode damage → always check tube rating charts

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Computed Tomography - Geometries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Principle of Computed Tomography - Geometries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computed Tomography (CT) Geometry** refers to the *spatial and kinematic arrangement* between the **X-ray source**, the **patient (object)**, and the **detector array** that defines how projection data is acquired from multiple angular views for digital cross-sectional image reconstruction.

In the context of KTU 2024 Module 3, the term "geometry" specifically addresses the **physical configuration** of the source-detector system (parallel-beam, fan-beam, cone-beam, and helical), the **data acquisition trajectories** (linear, rotational, and spiral), and the **mathematical coordinate framework** (rotating $x-y$ frame vs. fixed $X-Y$ frame) used to model the attenuation line integrals through the patient volume.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Slicing a Loaf of Bread"

Imagine you want to know exactly what is *inside* a sealed loaf of bread without opening the box. You cannot see through it, but you can:

1. **Shine light from one side** and measure what comes out the other side (an *attenuation profile*).
2. **Rotate the loaf slightly** and repeat.
3. **Do this 1,000 times around 360°**.

Each measurement tells you the *total "darkness"* (attenuation) along a thin line through the loaf. By combining thousands of such lines from all angles, a computer can mathematically **reconstruct a 2D cross-sectional slice** — just like CT does for the human body.

> [!NOTE]
> The *geometry* is the rule that decides **where** the light source, **where** the loaf sits, **where** the detector is, and **how** they all move together. Different geometries = different ways of collecting those "lines through the loaf."

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in CT Geometry

- **Linear Attenuation Coefficient (LAC):** $\mu$ — measured in **cm⁻¹** or more often reported in **Hounsfield Units (HU)**, where:
  $$\text{HU} = 1000 \cdot \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$
- **Air:** −1000 HU, **Water:** 0 HU, **Dense Bone:** +1000 HU (typical range ≈ **−1000 HU to +3000 HU**).
- **Standard Fan Angle:** 40°–55° (single-row detector CT).
- **Standard Patient Bore:** 70 cm diameter.
- **Tube Rotation Time:** down to **0.24 s** (modern 320-slice scanners).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Foundational CT Geometries

CT scanner design has historically been classified into **seven generations**, but at the KTU level we focus on the *three principal geometric configurations* from which all scanner designs are derived.

#### A) Parallel-Beam Geometry (1st / 2nd Generation)

- A **thin, collimated** fan of X-rays is used.
- Both the source and the detector array **translate linearly** across the patient, then **rotate by a small angle** and repeat.
- The X-ray beam is treated as a set of **parallel lines** through the object.
- Projection data is collected for each angle $\theta$ over a set of detector positions $t$.

#### B) Fan-Beam Geometry (3rd / 4th Generation)

- The source emits a **divergent fan-shaped** beam (typical fan angle 30°–55°).
- **3rd Generation:** Both source and a **curved detector array** rotate together around the patient.
- **4th Generation:** Only the source rotates inside a **stationary 360° ring of detectors**.
- Fan-beam data must be mathematically **re-binned into parallel projections** before reconstruction (or directly handled by fan-beam FBP).

#### C) Cone-Beam Geometry (Modern Multi-Slice CT)

- A **2D cone** of X-rays illuminates a **2D area detector** (e.g., 320-row flat panel).
- Multiple slices are acquired in a **single rotation**.
- Requires 3D reconstruction algorithms (Feldkamp–Davis–Kress or iterative methods).

### 2.2 Coordinate Systems in CT

Two reference frames are mandatory for KTU derivations:

- **Fixed laboratory frame $(X, Y)$** — attached to the patient/gantry.
- **Rotating source frame $(x, y)$** — attached to the source–detector assembly, rotated by angle $\theta$ around the isocenter.

The transformation between them is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= X\cos\theta + Y\sin\theta \\
y &= -X\sin\theta + Y\cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The isocenter is the *center of rotation* — the point around which the X-ray tube pivots and where the reconstructed image origin is placed.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates every equation a KTU student must memorize for Module 3 — geometries, projections, and reconstruction.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Beer–Lambert Law (single ray) | $I = I_0 e^{-\int \mu(x,y)\,ds}$ | $I, I_0$ detected/incident intensity | dimensionless |
| 2 | Total Attenuation (projection) | $p(\theta, t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mu(x,y)\,ds$ | Line integral of LAC along ray | dimensionless |
| 3 | Radon Transform | $R\{\mu\}(\theta, t) = p(\theta, t)$ | Projected profile at angle $\theta$ | — |
| 4 | HU Calibration | $\text{HU} = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{\mu - \mu_w}{\mu_w}$ | $\mu_w$ = LAC of water | HU |
| 5 | 2D Fourier Slice Theorem | $\mathcal{F}_1\{p(\theta, t)\} = M(\omega \cos\theta, \omega \sin\theta)$ | 1D-FFT of projection = radial slice of 2D object spectrum | — |
| 6 | Fan-to-Parallel Rebinning | $t = D \sin\gamma$ | $D$ = source-to-isocenter distance; $\gamma$ = fan angle | length |
| 7 | Filtered Back-Projection | $f(x,y) = \displaystyle\int_0^{\pi}\!\left[p(\theta, t) * h(t)\right] d\theta$ | $h(t)$ = ramp filter kernel | — |
| 8 | Ramp Filter Kernel | $h(t) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{4a^2}, & t=0 \\[2pt] 0, & t \text{ even multiple of } a \\[2pt] \dfrac{-1}{a^2 \pi^2 t^2}, & t \text{ odd} \end{cases}$ | $a$ = detector spacing | — |
| 9 | Pitch (Helical CT) | $\text{Pitch} = \dfrac{\text{Table Travel / Rotation}}{\text{Beam Collimation}}$ | — | dimensionless |
| 10 | Cone-Beam Half-Fan | $\sin\alpha_{\max} = \dfrac{R_{FOV}}{\text{SID}}$ | $R_{FOV}$ = reconstruction field-of-view; SID = source-to-image distance | radians |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of CT Geometries

- **3rd Gen Fan-Beam** → drives the *entire commercial CT industry* (GE, Siemens, Philips, Toshiba).
- **Cone-Beam CT (CBCT)** → used in **image-guided radiotherapy (IGRT)** and **dental/maxillofacial imaging** because it provides volumetric data in one rotation.
- **Helical/Spiral Geometry** → enables *continuous volumetric scanning* of the chest/abdomen in a single breath-hold.
- **Dual-Source CT (DSCT)** → two perpendicular tube-detector pairs enable *high-pitch* scanning at **pitch ≈ 3.4**, freezing cardiac motion.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The CT Projection (Radon Transform)

We start with the **Beer–Lambert Law** for a monochromatic X-ray traversing an infinitesimal slab $ds$ of material with linear attenuation coefficient $\mu$:

$$
\frac{dI}{ds} = -\mu(s)\, I(s)
$$

**Step 1 — Separate variables.**

$$
\frac{dI}{I} = -\mu(s)\, ds
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate along the ray path from source to detector** (path length $L$).

$$
\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -\int_{0}^{L} \mu(s)\, ds
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the left-hand integral** (logarithm result).

$$
\ln I - \ln I_0 = -\int_{0}^{L} \mu(s)\, ds
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange** to give the classical Beer–Lambert exponential form.

$$
I = I_0 \, e^{-\int_{0}^{L} \mu(s)\, ds}
$$

**Step 5 — Define the projection $p$** as the *log-attenuation* (the negative logarithm of the intensity ratio).

$$
p = \ln\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \int_{0}^{L} \mu(s)\, ds
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This single line integral $p$ is called a **ray-sum** or **projection**. CT geometry dictates *which* line integrals are sampled from the object.

**Step 6 — Express the projection in the rotating $(x, y)$ frame** (where the source-detector axis is $x$ and the detector axis is $t$ perpendicular to it):

$$
p(\theta, t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mu(x, y)\, dx
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the rotation** $x = X\cos\theta + Y\sin\theta$ and $y = -X\sin\theta + Y\cos\theta$. The result is the **Radon Transform** $R\{\mu\}(\theta,t)$:

$$
\boxed{\,R\{\mu\}(\theta, t) \;=\; \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mu\!\left(X\cos\theta + Y\sin\theta,\; -X\sin\theta + Y\cos\theta\right) dx\,}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Central Slice (Fourier Slice) Theorem

This theorem links **1D projections** to **2D object structure** — the heart of CT reconstruction.

**Step 1 — Take the 1D Fourier transform** of the projection $p(\theta, t)$ with respect to $t$:

$$
P(\theta, \omega) = \mathcal{F}_1\{p(\theta, t)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} p(\theta, t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the definition of $p(\theta, t)$** (the Radon transform of $\mu$):

$$
P(\theta, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mu(x, y)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dx\, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to the fixed $(X, Y)$ frame** using $t = -X\sin\theta + Y\cos\theta$ and the Jacobian of the rotation, which equals **1** (rotations are area-preserving). Hence $dx\, dt = dX\, dY$:

$$
P(\theta, \omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty}\!\!\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mu(X, Y)\, e^{-j\omega(-X\sin\theta + Y\cos\theta)}\, dX\, dY
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the exponent as** $-j(\omega \cos\theta) Y - j(\omega \sin\theta) X$, which matches the 2D Fourier transform kernel:

$$
\boxed{\,P(\theta, \omega) \;=\; M(\omega \cos\theta,\; \omega \sin\theta)\,}
$$

where $M(u, v) = \mathcal{F}_2\{\mu(X, Y)\}$ is the 2D Fourier transform of the object.

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric meaning:** Each 1D projection contributes *one radial line* (a "slice") through the 2D frequency plane of the object. As $\theta$ sweeps 0° → 180°, these radial slices fill the entire $(u, v)$ plane — which is why CT needs projections from a **full 180°** angular range (not 360°) for complete reconstruction.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Fan-to-Parallel Rebinning

A detector element in a fan-beam CT sees a ray at fan-angle $\gamma$. The equivalent parallel-beam detector coordinate $t$ on the central line is:

$$
t = D \sin\gamma
$$

**Step 1 — Define the source position** $S = (D\cos\phi, D\sin\phi)$ in the lab frame, where $\phi$ is the gantry angle.

**Step 2 — A ray from $S$** through a fan-angle $\gamma$ hits the detector at the position $t_{\text{fan}} = D\tan\gamma$ along the curved detector arc.

**Step 3 — Project this ray onto the equivalent parallel ray** that would pass through the isocenter at the same angle. By geometry of similar triangles:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{parallel}} &= D\sin\gamma \\
&= D\sin(\phi + \gamma) \cdot \cos\phi \;-\; D\cos(\phi + \gamma) \cdot \sin\phi \\
\text{(equivalent }\theta &= \phi + \gamma \text{ for that ray)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Rebinning** the data sorts all rays of equal $(\theta, t)$ into a single parallel projection $p(\theta, t)$. This allows the use of standard FBP algorithms on fan-beam scanners.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Helical CT Pitch Relation

Define:

- $T$ = table translation per gantry rotation (mm).
- $N \cdot W$ = total beam collimation width ($N$ = detector rows, $W$ = row width).
- Pitch $= p$ (dimensionless).

**Step 1 — Express coverage per rotation:**

$$
\text{Coverage} = T = p \cdot N \cdot W
$$

**Step 2 — For a 4-slice CT** with $N=4$, $W=1.25$ mm, and pitch $p=1.5$:

$$
T = 1.5 \times 4 \times 1.25 = 7.5 \text{ mm per rotation}
$$

**Step 3 — For a 64-slice scanner** ($N=64$, $W=0.625$ mm, $p=0.9$):

$$
T = 0.9 \times 64 \times 0.625 = 36 \text{ mm per rotation}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitch > 1** → gaps in $z$-coverage (faster, lower dose, possible artifacts).
> **Pitch < 1** → overlapping slices (higher dose, better longitudinal resolution).
> **Pitch = 1** → contiguous coverage.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of CT Generations

| Generation | Geometry | Source Motion | Detector Motion | Beam Shape | Scan Time (typical) |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1st | Parallel-beam | Translate + Rotate | Translate + Rotate | Pencil | 4–5 min / slice |
| 2nd | Narrow fan | Translate + Rotate | Translate + Rotate | Narrow fan (10°–20°) | 20–60 s / slice |
| 3rd | Wide fan-beam | Rotate only | Rotate (with source) | Wide fan (30°–55°) | 1–5 s / slice |
| 4th | Wide fan-beam | Rotate only | **Stationary ring** | Wide fan | 1–5 s / slice |
| 5th | Electron-beam (no mechanical motion) | Electron beam swept | Stationary arc | Fan | 50 ms / slice |
| 6th | Helical (slip-ring) | Continuous rotate + table translate | Continuous rotate | Fan | 0.3–1 s / volume |
| 7th | Cone-beam / Multi-slice | Continuous rotate | 2D area detector | Cone (axial) or helical | < 0.3 s / volume |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — CT Geometry Subsystems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[HV Generator 80-140 kVp] --> B[X-Ray Tube]
    B --> C[Collimator Assembly]
    C --> D[Patient Volume at Isocenter]
    D --> E[Detector Array Geometry]
    E --> F[Data Acquisition System DAS]
    F --> G[Pre-Processing: Log + Calibration]
    G --> H[Rebinning: Fan-to-Parallel]
    H --> I[Filtered Back Projection FBP]
    I --> J[Image Reconstruction f-of-x-y]
    J --> K[HU Calibration to Grayscale]
    K --> L[Display Workstation]

    subgraph Gantry
        B
        C
        D
        E
    end

    subgraph Reconstruction
        G
        H
        I
        J
    end
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Geometry Selection by Application

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Clinical Requirement] --> B{Anatomy Type}
    B -- Static Head/Bone --> C[Sequential Axial Fan-Beam]
    B -- Chest/Abdomen --> D[Helical Multi-Slice CT]
    B -- Cardiac --> E[Dual-Source High-Pitch]
    B -- Dental / IGRT --> F[Cone-Beam CT]
    B -- Pediatric / Low Dose --> G[Axial Step-and-Shoot]

    C --> H[Pitch = 0 or 1]
    D --> I[Pitch 0.9 to 1.5]
    E --> J[Pitch up to 3.4]
    F --> K[Single Rotation Volume]
    G --> L[Minimal Table Travel]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Comparison — 3rd vs 4th Generation Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Gen3 [3rd Generation - Rotating Source AND Detector]
        S3[X-Ray Source] -->|Fan Beam 40 deg| P3[Patient]
        P3 --> D3[Curved Detector Array]
        S3 -.rotate together.-> D3
    end

    subgraph Gen4 [4th Generation - Rotating Source, Stationary Ring]
        S4[X-Ray Source] -->|Fan Beam| P4[Patient]
        P4 --> D4[360 deg Stationary Detector Ring]
        S4 -.rotate alone.-> D4
    end
```

### 4.4 Mermaid — Helical Acquisition Trajectory

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start Position z=0] -->|table moves dZ per rotation| B[Rotation 1 at z=35 mm]
    B --> C[Rotation 2 at z=70 mm]
    C --> D[Rotation 3 at z=105 mm]
    D --> E[End Position]
    style A fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#9ff,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> In helical CT, the X-ray tube traces a **helix** (3D spiral) around the patient as the table moves continuously. This is mathematically equivalent to acquiring projections on a *tilted plane* — addressed by **z-interpolation** before standard 2D FBP.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**  
*Define the term "projection" in Computed Tomography. How is it mathematically represented?*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A *projection* in CT is the set of line integrals of the linear attenuation coefficient $\mu(x,y)$ along parallel rays through the patient at a given angle $\theta$. It is mathematically represented by the **Radon Transform**:
> $$p(\theta, t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mu(x,y)\, ds$$
> The detector array samples this projection at discrete values of $t$, producing a 1D *sinogram row* for that angle $\theta$. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**  
*State the Central Slice (Fourier Slice) Theorem and explain its significance in CT reconstruction.*

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Central Slice Theorem states that the **1D Fourier Transform of a projection** $p(\theta, t)$ acquired at angle $\theta$ equals a **radial line (slice) through the 2D Fourier Transform** of the object at the same angle:
> $$P(\theta, \omega) = M(\omega\cos\theta, \omega\sin\theta)$$
> **Significance:** It provides a direct mathematical bridge between measured projections and the 2D object structure, enabling the Filtered Back-Projection (FBP) algorithm and explaining why projections over a full 180° range are required. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Answer **any one full question** (a + b = 7 + 7 = 14 marks).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **different generations of CT scanners**, emphasizing the evolution of source-detector geometry. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Radon Transform** of a 2D object $\mu(x, y)$ and explain its role in CT image formation. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Seven Generations of CT** — 7 marks

- **1st Generation (Parallel Pencil Beam):** Single pencil beam, single detector; *translate* across patient, *rotate* by 1°, repeat ~180 times. Scan time ≈ 4–5 min/slice. *(1 mark)*
- **2nd Generation (Narrow Fan):** Fan of 5°–10°, 5–30 detectors. Translate + rotate, but fewer steps. Scan time ≈ 20–60 s/slice. *(1 mark)*
- **3rd Generation (Wide Fan, Rotating Detector):** Source and curved detector array *rotate together* around patient. Fan angle 30°–55°. No translation needed. Scan time 1–5 s/slice. *(1.5 marks)*
- **4th Generation (Stationary Detector Ring):** Only source rotates inside a *360° ring* of fixed detectors. Avoids cable-wrap but suffers from scatter. *(1.5 marks)*
- **5th Generation (Electron-Beam / Cine CT):** No mechanical motion — electron beam steered onto tungsten target ring around patient. 50 ms/slice → used for cardiac imaging. *(1 mark)*
- **6th Generation (Helical / Spiral):** Slip-ring technology allows *continuous rotation* while table translates → helical trajectory. *(0.5 mark)*
- **7th Generation (Multi-Slice Cone-Beam):** 2D detector arrays, 64–320 rows, cone-beam geometry. Volumetric acquisition in one rotation. *(0.5 mark)*

**(b) Derivation of the Radon Transform** — 7 marks

Start with Beer–Lambert (already derived in Section 3.1). The key points to write:

1. Beer–Lambert: $I = I_0 e^{-\int \mu\, ds}$ — *(1 mark)*
2. Log-transformation: $p = \ln(I_0/I) = \int \mu\, ds$ — *(1 mark)*
3. Express ray in rotated frame: $x = X\cos\theta + Y\sin\theta$, $t = -X\sin\theta + Y\cos\theta$ — *(1 mark)*
4. Write projection: $p(\theta, t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \mu(x,y)\, dx$ — *(1 mark)*
5. Substitute the rotation: $p(\theta,t) = \int \mu(X\cos\theta + Y\sin\theta, -X\sin\theta + Y\cos\theta) dx$ — *(1 mark)*
6. State the Radon transform: $R\{\mu\}(\theta,t) = p(\theta,t)$ — *(1 mark)*
7. Explain role: collection of all $(\theta, t)$ projections over 180° forms the *sinogram*; inversion of the Radon transform yields the 2D attenuation map $\mu(x,y)$ of the slice. — *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **parallel-beam and fan-beam geometries** in CT. Compare their projection data and reconstruction requirements. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **helical (spiral) CT**? Derive the **pitch formula** and explain the effect of different pitch values on image quality. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) Parallel-Beam vs Fan-Beam Geometry** — 7 marks

- **Parallel-Beam:** Beam consists of *mutually parallel rays*; source and detector both translate then rotate. *(1.5 marks)*
  - Data: 1D projection $p(\theta, t)$ sampled at uniform $t$.
  - Reconstruction: Direct FBP with ramp filter $h(t)$. *(1 mark)*
- **Fan-Beam:** Beam *diverges* from a point source through a fan angle $\gamma$. *(1.5 marks)*
  - Data: $p(\phi, \gamma)$ — function of gantry angle $\phi$ and fan angle $\gamma$.
  - Reconstruction: Either (i) **rebin** to parallel geometry using $t = D\sin\gamma$, then standard FBP; or (ii) direct **fan-beam FBP** with modified weighting. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Comparison table:** *(1.5 marks)*
  - Beam shape: parallel vs divergent
  - Acquisition: 1st/2nd gen vs 3rd/4th gen
  - Reconstruction: direct vs rebinned
  - Data redundancy: lower vs higher (oversampling at center)

**(b) Helical CT and Pitch** — 7 marks

- **Definition:** In helical CT, the gantry *rotates continuously* while the patient table *translates continuously* — the source describes a helix around the patient. Enabled by **slip-ring technology** (no cable wrap). *(2 marks)*
- **Pitch derivation:** *(3 marks)*
  $$\text{Pitch} = \frac{\text{Table Travel per Rotation}}{\text{Total Beam Collimation}} = \frac{T}{N \cdot W}$$
  - $T$ = table translation per 360° rotation
  - $N$ = number of detector rows
  - $W$ = width of each detector row
- **Effect of pitch:** *(2 marks)*
  - $p = 1$: contiguous, full coverage
  - $p < 1$: overlapping → higher dose, smoother 3D
  - $p > 1$: gaps → lower dose, possible step artifacts
  - Cardiac CT: high-pitch ($p \approx 3.4$) freezes motion

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in CT-Geometry questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to define symbols** — always write units for $D$, $t$, $\gamma$, $N$, $W$.
> 2. **Mixing up frames** — clearly distinguish the rotating $(x,y)$ frame from the fixed $(X,Y)$ frame.
> 3. **Skipping the Jacobian** — in re-derivation of the Fourier Slice Theorem, students often forget to show that $\,dx\,dt = dX\,dY$ (rotation Jacobian = 1). Examiners **do deduct 1 mark** for this.
> 4. **Pitch confusion** — students write $p = T/W$ instead of $p = T/(N \cdot W)$ for multi-slice CT. Always specify *per rotation* and include the row-count.
> 5. **Skipping the 180° vs 360° argument** — the Central Slice Theorem gives the geometric reason why **180°** of projections is sufficient. Mentioning this scores an *extra conceptual mark*.
> 6. **No diagram** — KTU examiners expect a block diagram or labeled geometry sketch in 7-mark sub-questions. *Plan for 2 minutes of drawing time.*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- **CT Geometry** = the spatial arrangement of source, patient, and detector.
- **Three primary geometries:** Parallel-beam, Fan-beam, Cone-beam.
- **Seven generations** trace the evolution of these geometries — know the source/detector motion of each.
- **Radon Transform** $R\{\mu\}(\theta, t) = \int \mu(x,y)\, ds$ defines the CT projection.
- **Central Slice Theorem** links 1D projection FFTs to radial slices of the 2D object spectrum.
- **Reconstruction** uses **Filtered Back-Projection (FBP)** with the **ramp filter** $h(t)$.
- **Fan-to-parallel rebinning** uses $t = D\sin\gamma$.
- **Helical CT pitch:** $p = T/(N \cdot W)$.
  - $p = 1$ → contiguous
  - $p < 1$ → overlapping (high dose)
  - $p > 1$ → gaps (low dose, possible artifacts)
- **HU scale:** $\text{HU} = 1000(\mu - \mu_w)/\mu_w$; air = −1000, water = 0, bone ≈ +1000.
- **Coordinate frames:** rotate $(x,y)$ from fixed $(X,Y)$ by gantry angle $\theta$.
- **3rd generation** is the *industry standard* for fan-beam CT.
- **4th generation** has a stationary 360° detector ring.
- **5th generation** uses electron-beam steering (no mechanical motion) — used in cardiac CT.
- **Cone-beam (7th gen)** requires 3D FDK reconstruction; used in IGRT and dental imaging.
- **Slip-ring technology** is the engineering enabler of continuous (helical) rotation.
- **Dual-source CT** uses two perpendicular tube-detector pairs at high pitch to freeze cardiac motion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** First

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 X-Rays: The Formal Engineering Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **X-rays are a form of electromagnetic radiation** with wavelengths in the range of **0.01 nm to 10 nm** (corresponding to photon energies from approximately **100 eV to 100 keV**), produced by the sudden deceleration of high-velocity electrons striking a metal target, or by inner-shell electron transitions in atoms.

For the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEBRT411 — Principles of Medical Imaging, Module 3), the foundational topic begins with the **production, properties, and spectrum of X-rays**, since this is the gateway to understanding **radiography, fluoroscopy, mammography, and Computed Tomography (CT)**.

| Property | Value / Range |
| :--- | :--- |
| Wavelength ($\lambda$) | **0.01 nm – 10 nm** |
| Frequency ($f$) | **$3 \times 10^{16}$ Hz – $3 \times 10^{19}$ Hz** |
| Photon Energy ($E$) | **100 eV – 100 keV** |
| Speed in vacuum ($c$) | **$3 \times 10^8$ m/s** |
| Nature | Ionizing electromagnetic radiation |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Braking Car" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine a high-speed car (electron) hurtling down a highway and suddenly slamming into a concrete wall (metal target). The car doesn't just stop — the kinetic energy it carried must go *somewhere*. Some of it becomes heat, some shakes the wall, and some is released as a brilliant flash of light. **X-rays are that "flash of light,"** but invisible, far more energetic, and produced at the moment the electron slams into the atoms of the target.

This analogy is critical for understanding the two distinct mechanisms of X-ray generation:
1. **Bremsstrahlung** — the "general crash" where any electron braking at any angle radiates a *continuous* spectrum.
2. **Characteristic radiation** — the "specific atomic response" where an inner-shell electron is knocked out, and an outer-shell electron drops in to fill the vacancy, emitting a *discrete* photon of fixed energy.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Medical Imaging

- **Radiographic imaging** of bones and dense tissues depends on differential X-ray absorption.
- **CT scanners** reconstruct cross-sections from thousands of X-ray projection angles.
- **Mammography** uses low-energy, soft X-rays to image soft breast tissue.
- **Fluoroscopy** delivers continuous low-dose X-rays for real-time surgical guidance.

Without a deep grasp of X-ray production physics, none of the above modalities can be designed, optimized, or interpreted correctly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The X-Ray Tube — Heart of Every Medical X-Ray System

The X-ray tube is a specialized **high-vacuum diode** that converts electrical energy into X-ray photons. The KTU board examiner expects the following subsystem-level understanding:

### (a) Cathode Assembly
- A **tungsten filament** heated to ~**2500 °C** emits electrons via **thermionic emission** (Edison/Richardson effect).
- A **focusing cup** directs the electron stream into a narrow beam aimed at a small focal spot on the target.

### (b) Anode Assembly
- A **tungsten target** (Z = 74, high melting point ~3422 °C) is embedded in a **copper anode** (high thermal conductivity) for heat dissipation.
- In diagnostic tubes, a **rotating anode** is used to spread the heat over a larger disc area, allowing higher tube currents.

### (c) High-Voltage Circuit
- A **step-up transformer** provides a peak accelerating voltage of typically **30 kVp – 150 kVp** between cathode and anode.
- A **full-wave rectifier** converts AC to pulsating DC.
- The exposure timer controls the **mAs** (milliampere-seconds), which determines the number of X-ray photons produced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The *tube voltage (kVp)* controls the **quality (penetrating power / energy)** of the X-ray beam, while the *tube current × time (mAs)* controls the **quantity (number of photons)**.

## 2.2 The Two Mechanisms of X-Ray Production

### Mechanism 1 — Bremsstrahlung ("Braking Radiation")
- A high-speed electron enters the Coulomb field of a target nucleus.
- The electron is **decelerated and deflected**, losing kinetic energy.
- The lost kinetic energy is emitted as an X-ray photon.
- Because the deceleration can range from zero to the full kinetic energy, the resulting **spectrum is continuous** from 0 up to a maximum photon energy.

### Mechanism 2 — Characteristic Radiation
- An incoming electron collides *directly* with an inner-shell (K-shell) electron of the target atom.
- The K-electron is ejected, leaving a vacancy.
- An electron from a higher shell (L, M, ...) drops down to fill the vacancy.
- The energy difference between the two shells is released as an X-ray photon.
- Since atomic energy levels are *discrete*, characteristic radiation produces **sharp peaks** at specific wavelengths that depend on the target material.

## 2.3 The X-Ray Emission Spectrum — Visualizing the Two Mechanisms

The X-ray spectrum is a plot of **intensity (I)** vs **wavelength ($\lambda$)**:

- A **continuous curve** (Bremsstrahlung) starting at $\lambda_{min}$ and extending to longer wavelengths.
- **Sharp peaks** (Characteristic lines) superimposed on the continuous curve at $\lambda_K$, $\lambda_L$, etc.

## 2.4 The Duane–Hunt Law — Setting the Lower Wavelength Limit

The minimum possible wavelength corresponds to a single photon carrying **all** the kinetic energy of an incident electron:

$$\lambda_{min} = \frac{h c}{e V}$$

where:
- $h$ = Planck's constant = **$6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s**
- $c$ = Speed of light = **$3 \times 10^8$ m/s**
- $e$ = Electron charge = **$1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C**
- $V$ = Accelerating voltage (in volts)

Numerically substituting the constants:

$$\lambda_{min} (\text{in nm}) = \frac{1.24}{V \text{ (in kV)}}$$

## 2.5 Moseley's Law — Predicting Characteristic Wavelengths

$$\nu = a^2 \cdot c \cdot (Z - b)^2$$

Or, equivalently, in terms of frequency:

$$\sqrt{\nu} = a \cdot (Z - b)$$

where:
- $\nu$ = Frequency of characteristic X-ray
- $Z$ = Atomic number of target
- $a, b$ = Constants that depend on the specific line series (K, L, M, ...)
- For K-series: $a \approx \frac{3}{4} c R$ and $b \approx 1$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Moseley's law was historically critical because it established that the periodic table is ordered by *atomic number*, not atomic weight. In medical imaging, it explains *why* tungsten (Z=74) produces higher-energy characteristic X-rays than molybdenum (Z=42) used in mammography.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E = h f = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | J (or eV) |
| 2 | $\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{h c}{e V} = \dfrac{1.24}{V(\text{kV})}$ | Duane–Hunt minimum wavelength | nm |
| 3 | $\sqrt{\nu} = a (Z - b)$ | Moseley's law (characteristic lines) | Hz$^{1/2}$ |
| 4 | $I \propto Z \cdot V^2$ | X-ray intensity (Bremsstrahlung output) | relative |
| 5 | $H \propto \dfrac{Z \cdot I}{V}$ | Heat at the target (design constraint) | W |
| 6 | $e V = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Accelerating electron's KE = photon energy at $\lambda_{min}$ | eV |
| 7 | $\lambda_{max} \approx 1.5 \lambda_{min}$ | Peak of continuous spectrum (empirical) | nm |
| 8 | $P_{brem} \propto \dfrac{Z \cdot E^3}{m^2}$ | Thin-target bremsstrahlung power | W |
| 9 | $I \propto I_{tube} \cdot Z \cdot V^2$ | Output intensity depends on tube current | — |
| 10 | $E_{photon} = V(\text{kV}) \times 1000$ eV (max) | Max photon energy = full KE of electron | eV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: In the table above, note the use of `\vert` style separators and `\dfrac` for fractions. Avoid using raw pipes `\vert` in `latex` inline that may be parsed as table column separators — this prevents markdown table breakage.**

## 2.7 Engineering Utility — Why These Equations Matter

- **Radiographer use:** The Duane–Hunt law lets the technologist instantly compute the lowest-wavelength photon emitted at, say, 120 kVp: $\lambda_{min} = 1.24/120 \approx 0.0103$ nm — in the diagnostic window.
- **Equipment design:** The $H \propto ZI/V$ heat formula dictates the maximum safe mA for a given kVp without melting the anode.
- **Clinical selection:** Moseley's law explains why a molybdenum target (Z=42) produces softer, lower-energy characteristic X-rays ideal for mammography (~17–20 keV), while tungsten (Z=74) is preferred for general radiography (higher-energy spectrum).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Duane–Hunt Law

**Starting premise:** An electron of charge $e$ is accelerated through a potential difference $V$. All its kinetic energy is converted to a single X-ray photon of maximum energy (the most energetic photon possible).

**Step 1 — Energy gained by the electron from the accelerating field:**

The work done by the electric field on the electron equals the product of charge and potential difference:

$$E_{electron} = eV$$

**Step 2 — This energy becomes a single X-ray photon at minimum wavelength:**

By the Einstein–Planck quantum relation, photon energy is $E = hf = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$. For minimum wavelength, the energy is *maximum*, so:

$$eV = \frac{hc}{\lambda_{min}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve explicitly for $\lambda_{min}$:**

Rearranging the equation by multiplying both sides by $\lambda_{min}$ and then dividing by $eV$:

$$\lambda_{min} = \frac{hc}{eV}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values of the constants:**

$$\lambda_{min} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, \text{J·s}) \times (3 \times 10^{8} \, \text{m/s})}{(1.602 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{C}) \times V}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the numerator and denominator constants:**

The numerator evaluates to:

$$h c = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8} = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25} \, \text{J·m}$$

**Step 6 — Combine and simplify:**

The expression $\dfrac{hc}{e}$ is a fundamental constant equal to approximately $1.24 \times 10^{-6}$ V·m. Therefore:

$$\lambda_{min} = \frac{1.24 \times 10^{-6}}{V} \text{ meters}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to nanometers for the KTU-friendly diagnostic range:**

Since $1 \text{ nm} = 10^{-9} \text{ m}$, we divide by $10^{-9}$:

$$\lambda_{min} \text{ (nm)} = \frac{1.24 \times 10^{-6}}{V} \times 10^{9} = \frac{1240}{V \text{ (in volts)}} \text{ nm}$$

Equivalently, if V is in kilovolts:

$$\boxed{\lambda_{min} \text{ (nm)} = \frac{1.24}{V \text{ (kV)}}}$$

**Step 8 — Numerical Example (typical KTU problem):**
For $V = 100$ kV:

$$\lambda_{min} = \frac{1.24}{100} = 0.0124 \text{ nm}$$

This lies in the diagnostic X-ray range, confirming that 100 kVp tubes emit photons useful for medical imaging.

## 3.2 Derivation of Moseley's Law (Conceptual / Bohr-Model Approach)

**Step 1 — Bohr model energy of an electron in the $n$-th shell:**

$$E_n = -\frac{Z^2 \cdot R_\infty \cdot h c}{n^2}$$

where $R_\infty \approx 1.097 \times 10^7 \, \text{m}^{-1}$ is the Rydberg constant, and $Z$ is the atomic number.

**Step 2 — Energy of the emitted characteristic photon (K-$\alpha$ line, transition from L-shell ($n=2$) to K-shell ($n=1$)):**

$$E_{K\alpha} = h\nu = E_2 - E_1 = R_\infty \cdot h c \cdot Z^2 \left( \frac{1}{1^2} - \frac{1}{2^2} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Expand the bracket:**

$$\frac{1}{1^2} - \frac{1}{2^2} = 1 - \frac{1}{4} = \frac{3}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and isolate the frequency:**

$$h\nu = \frac{3}{4} R_\infty \cdot h c \cdot Z^2$$

Dividing both sides by $h$:

$$\nu = \frac{3}{4} R_\infty \cdot c \cdot Z^2$$

**Step 5 — Refine using the screening constant $b$ (accounting for electron-electron shielding):**

In real atoms, the outer electron is partially shielded from the full nuclear charge. Empirically:

$$\boxed{\nu = a^2 \cdot c \cdot (Z - b)^2 \quad \text{where} \quad a = \frac{3}{4} R_\infty \text{ for K-series}, \, b \approx 1}$$

This is Moseley's law in its general form, showing the direct link between X-ray frequency and atomic number.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Bremsstrahlung Intensity Law

**Step 1 — Power radiated by a single accelerated electron (Larmor relation in non-relativistic form):**

$$P = \frac{e^2 a^2}{6 \pi \varepsilon_0 c^3}$$

where $a$ is the acceleration of the electron.

**Step 2 — Acceleration of an electron passing a nucleus at impact parameter $b$:**

By Coulomb's law, the force on the electron is $F = \dfrac{Ze^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2}$, and integrating over the trajectory yields a transverse acceleration that scales as $\dfrac{Ze^2}{m_e b^2}$.

**Step 3 — Total radiated power across all impact parameters:**

Integrating $P$ over the cross-sectional area presented by the target (number of nuclei encountered), one finds:

$$P_{total} \propto \frac{Z \cdot E_{electron}^2 \cdot I_{tube}}{m_e^2}$$

Combining the dependence on tube current $I$ (number of incident electrons per second) and target atomic number $Z$:

$$\boxed{I_{brem} \propto Z \cdot I_{tube} \cdot V^2}$$

This is the **intensity proportionality** memorized by every KTU board candidate.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Numerical Reference

| Tube Voltage $V$ (kVp) | $\lambda_{min}$ (nm) | Max Photon Energy (keV) | Clinical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 25 | 0.0496 | 25 | Mammography (soft tissue) |
| 50 | 0.0248 | 50 | Extremity imaging |
| 80 | 0.0155 | 80 | Chest radiography |
| 100 | 0.0124 | 100 | General adult radiography |
| 120 | 0.0103 | 120 | Abdomen, lumbar spine |
| 150 | 0.00827 | 150 | Thick body parts, CT |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
