#### C. Zipper Artifact (MRI)

**Cause:** External RF interference (broken Faraday cage, door not sealed) leaking into the scan room, contaminating specific frequency lines.

**Effect:** Linear bright or dark band perpendicular to the frequency-encoding direction.

**Correction:** Shielding integrity check; close scan room door; check RF coil connections.

#### D. Moiré Fringes (Digital Radiography)

**Cause:** Aliasing between the detector sampling frequency and high-frequency content of the imaged object (e.g., fine grid lines, microcalcifications).

**Correction:** Higher-resolution detector; anti-aliasing filter.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Artifact | Primary Modality | Key Formula/Concept | Magnitude/Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quantum noise | X-ray, NM | $SNR = \sqrt{N}$ | $SNR \propto \sqrt{mAs}$ | dimensionless |
| Beam hardening | CT | $I(x) = I_0 \int S(E) e^{-\mu(E)x} dE$ | Effective $\mu$ decreases with $x$ | keV, HU |
| Partial volume | CT, MRI | $HU_{avg} = \sum f_i HU_i$ | Within one voxel | HU, mm³ |
| Chemical shift | MRI | $\Delta f = \gamma B_0 \cdot \delta_{cs}$ | 3.5 ppm (fat-water) | Hz, ppm |
| Nyquist limit | MRI, CT | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Determines FOV/matrix | Hz, mm |
| Aliasing offset | MRI | $\Delta x = \Delta f \cdot FOV / BW$ | Fat-water: 1.4 mm at 1.5T | mm |
| Motion ghosting | MRI | $N_{ghosts} = TR_{ghost}/T_{cycle}$ | Position-dependent | mm, ms |
| Photon starvation | CT | $N_{transmitted} = N_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | < 1000 photons cause streaks | counts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Understanding artifacts is essential for designing advanced correction algorithms (e.g., **deep learning–based metal artifact reduction** in modern CT scanners), optimizing scan protocols to minimize patient dose while maximizing diagnostic image quality, and developing **quality control programs** in radiology departments. The *Figure of Merit* for any imaging system is its ability to produce a **diagnostically reliable** image — and artifacts are the chief enemy of that reliability.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Quantum Noise — The Physical Foundation

The number of detected X-ray photons $N$ in a given detector area follows a **Poisson distribution** because photon emission and detection are discrete, independent random events.

**Step 1: Define the Poisson Probability Mass Function**

$$P(N) = \frac{\bar{N}^N e^{-\bar{N}}}{N!}$$

where $\bar{N}$ is the mean (expected) number of photons.

**Step 2: Compute the Variance**

For a Poisson distribution, the variance equals the mean:

$$\sigma_N^2 = \bar{N}$$

**Step 3: Compute the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**

By definition:

$$SNR = \frac{Signal}{Noise} = \frac{\bar{N}}{\sigma_N} = \frac{\bar{N}}{\sqrt{\bar{N}}} = \sqrt{\bar{N}}$$

**Step 4: Relate Photon Count to Exposure Parameters**

The mean photon count per unit area is proportional to mAs (tube current × time):

$$\bar{N} \propto mAs$$

Therefore:

$$SNR \propto \sqrt{mAs}$$

**Step 5: Practical Implication (Doubling Rule)**

To **double** the SNR, mAs must be **quadrupled**:

$$SNR_{new} = 2 \cdot SNR_{old} \implies mAs_{new} = 4 \cdot mAs_{old}$$

This is the foundational principle behind the **ALARA dose optimization** (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) — reducing dose increases noise, so the radiographer must find the minimum dose that still preserves diagnostic SNR.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Beam Hardening — Effective Energy Shift

**Step 1: Initial Beam Spectrum**

A clinical X-ray tube at 120 kVp produces a polychromatic spectrum $S(E)$ with photon energies from ~30 keV to ~120 keV.

**Step 2: Attenuation Through Material of Thickness $x$**

The transmitted intensity at each energy:

$$I(x, E) = I_0(E) \cdot e^{-\mu(E) \cdot x}$$

**Step 3: Total Detected Intensity (Broad-Beam Sum)**

$$I(x) = \int_{0}^{E_{max}} S(E) \cdot e^{-\mu(E) x} \, dE$$

**Step 4: Effective Linear Attenuation Coefficient**

The "effective" monoenergetic $\mu_{eff}$ that would yield the same transmitted intensity is:

$$\mu_{eff}(x) = -\frac{1}{x} \ln \left( \frac{I(x)}{I_0} \right)$$

**Step 5: Show $\mu_{eff}$ Decreases with $x$ (Beam Hardening)**

Since $\mu(E)$ is a decreasing function of $E$ (mostly due to photoelectric and Compton effects), low-energy photons are preferentially absorbed. After passing through depth $x$:

$$\bar{E}(x) = \frac{\int E \cdot S(E) e^{-\mu(E) x} dE}{\int S(E) e^{-\mu(E) x} dE}$$

This mean energy **increases** with $x$ → the beam becomes "harder."

**Step 6: Resulting Artifact (Cupping)**

When the CT reconstruction algorithm assumes $\mu$ is constant, it interprets the apparent decrease in $\mu_{eff}$ as a decrease in tissue density at the center of the body → **cupping artifact** (periphery of body appears denser/brighter than center, despite uniform true attenuation).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Chemical Shift Artifact — Spatial Misregistration

**Step 1: Larmor Frequency for a Nucleus**

$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$$

For hydrogen, $\gamma = 42.58$ MHz/T.

**Step 2: Chemical Shift Difference Between Fat and Water**

The fat protons are shielded by electron clouds, reducing their local field:

$$\delta_{fat} \approx -3.5 \text{ ppm} \quad (\text{relative to water})$$

**Step 3: Frequency Difference in Hz**

$$\Delta f = \gamma B_0 \cdot \delta_{cs} = 42.58 \text{ MHz/T} \times B_0 \times (-3.5 \times 10^{-6})$$

**Step 4: For $B_0 = 1.5$ T:**

$$\Delta f = 42.58 \times 1.5 \times (-3.5 \times 10^{-6}) \approx -223.5 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5: Spatial Misregistration in MRI**

In the frequency-encoding direction, the readout bandwidth (BW) per pixel is:

$$BW_{pixel} = \frac{BW_{total}}{N_{samples}}$$

A typical BW is 200 Hz/pixel. The shift in pixels is:

$$\Delta_{pixels} = \frac{\Delta f}{BW_{pixel}} = \frac{223.5}{200} \approx 1.12 \text{ pixels}$$

**Step 6: Spatial Misregistration in mm**

For an FOV of 300 mm and matrix 256:

$$\Delta_{mm} = \frac{\Delta f \cdot FOV}{BW_{total}} = \frac{223.5 \times 300}{50{,}000} \approx 1.34 \text{ mm}$$

This 1.3–1.4 mm shift at 1.5T is the typical chemical shift displacement — why kidneys, bladder, and orbits show characteristic dark/bright banding on T2-weighted images without fat suppression.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Artifact × Modality × Origin

| Artifact | Modality | Origin Class | Severity (1–5) | Primary Correction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Beam hardening | CT | Physics | 4 | Bow-tie filter, software correction |
| Partial volume | CT, MRI | Physics | 3 | Thinner slices |
| Motion ghosting | MRI | Patient | 5 | Gating, breath-hold |
| Metal streak | CT | Patient | 5 | Higher kVp, MAR algorithms |
| Susceptibility | MRI | Patient | 4 | Spin-echo, MAVRIC |
| Ring | CT | Equipment | 3 | Detector calibration |
| Aliasing | MRI, CT | Physics/Patient | 3 | Larger FOV, oversampling |
| Quantum mottle | X-ray, NM | Physics | 4 | Higher mAs |
| Truncation | CT, MRI | Patient | 3 | Larger FOV |
| Chemical shift | MRI | Physics | 3 | Fat suppression, ↑BW |
| Zipper | MRI | Equipment | 2 | RF shielding check |
| Dead pixel | Digital X-ray, CT | Equipment | 2 | Detector remapping |
| Streak | CT | Reconstruction | 4 | Iterative reconstruction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Severity Scale:** 1 = cosmetic only, 2 = mild, 3 = moderate, 4 = significant diagnostic impact, 5 = render study non-diagnostic.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q1. Define "image artifact" in medical imaging. List any four common sources of image artifacts.**

**Model Answer:**

An image artifact is any optical density, signal intensity, or structural pattern in a medical image that does not represent the true anatomical or pathological feature of the imaged object. It is a systematic deviation from the ideal image that can obscure, simulate, or distort real diagnostic information.

**Four common sources of image artifacts:**
1. **Physics-based limitations** (e.g., beam hardening, partial volume effect, quantum noise).
2. **Patient-related factors** (e.g., voluntary/involuntary motion, metallic implants, body habitus).
3. **Equipment-related issues** (e.g., dead detector elements, calibration drift, gradient nonlinearity).
4. **Reconstruction/processing algorithms** (e.g., limited-angle sampling, aliasing).

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner tip: For full 3 marks, the student must **define** the term AND **list** at least four sources with one-line categorization.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q2. What is beam hardening artifact in CT? State two methods to reduce it.**

**Model Answer:**

**Beam hardening artifact** is a CT-specific artifact that occurs because the clinical X-ray beam is polychromatic (containing photons of multiple energies). As the beam passes through tissue, the lower-energy (softer) photons are preferentially absorbed via the photoelectric effect, causing the effective mean beam energy to increase with depth. Since CT reconstruction algorithms assume a monoenergetic beam (constant linear attenuation coefficient), this produces errors that manifest as **cupping artifact** (darker center of the body) and **streak artifacts** between dense structures (e.g., between petrous bones of the skull).

**Two methods to reduce beam hardening:**
1. **Pre-hardening (bow-tie) filters** placed at the X-ray source to preferentially absorb low-energy photons before they enter the patient.
2. **Beam-hardening correction algorithms** in the reconstruction software that compensate for the energy-dependent attenuation during back-projection.

> [!NOTE]
> Other valid corrections: dual-energy CT with material decomposition, higher kVp acquisition, iterative reconstruction.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

---

**Question A (14 Marks): Motion Artifacts in MRI — Causes, Effects, and Correction**

**(a)** Explain the causes of motion artifacts in MRI, distinguishing between voluntary and involuntary motion. Discuss in detail how the k-space filling process leads to ghost formation along the phase-encoding direction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe in detail the various techniques used to suppress or correct motion artifacts in MRI. Include physiological gating, breath-hold methods, and sequence-based strategies. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Causes and Physics of Motion Artifacts:**

MRI data acquisition involves filling k-space line by line, with each line corresponding to a different phase-encoding step. The duration of one phase-encoding step is the **TR (repetition time)**. If the patient moves between TR intervals, the anatomical position of a structure changes from one phase-encoding line to the next.

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. **Voluntary motion** includes gross body movement, limb repositioning, swallowing, and eye movements. These produce gross blurring and anatomical mismatch.

2. **Involuntary motion** includes:
   - **Respiratory motion** (diaphragm excursion 1–3 cm during quiet breathing)
   - **Cardiac pulsation** (heart wall motion 5–10 mm, great vessel pulsation)
   - **Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) flow** (pulsatile flow in the aqueduct of Sylvius)
   - **Peristalsis** (bowel motion in abdominal imaging)
   - **Blood flow** (flow-related artifacts)

3. **Ghost formation mechanism:**
   - In a typical spin-echo sequence, k-space is filled over 256 phase-encoding steps × 1 TR each.
   - If cardiac motion occurs every 800 ms (HR 75 bpm) and TR = 500 ms, the heart will appear at slightly different positions every other line of k-space.
   - This periodic inconsistency is mapped by the 2D Fourier transform to a **series of ghost replicas** along the phase-encoding direction.
   - The spacing between ghosts corresponds to the **motion period × (FOV/TR)**. For a periodic motion, the ghosts appear at predictable fractional positions.

4. **Bulk motion (non-periodic)** produces more generalized **blurring** rather than discrete ghosts.

5. **Flow-related artifacts** in time-of-flight (TOF) sequences produce ghost-like propagation effects due to unsaturated spins flowing into the imaging slice.

---

**(b) Motion Artifact Correction Techniques:**

**1. Physiological Gating:**

- **ECG (Cardiac) Gating:**
  - R-wave of the QRS complex triggers data acquisition.
  - Data is collected only during a defined portion of the cardiac cycle (e.g., mid-diastole) when the heart is relatively still.
  - Two modes: **prospective triggering** (acquisition only in specific phase) and **retrospective gating** (continuous acquisition with sorting).
  - Used in cardiac MRI, coronary MRA, and thoracic imaging.

- **Respiratory Gating:**
  - **Respiratory bellows** detect abdominal excursion.
  - Acquisition occurs only during end-expiration (or specified phase).
  - **Navigator echo** techniques track diaphragm position in real-time and accept/reject k-space lines based on a gating window.

**2. Breath-Hold Methods:**

- Acquisition is completed during a single breath-hold (typically 15–25 seconds).
- Requires **fast sequences**: single-shot fast spin echo (SSFSE, HASTE), echo planar imaging (EPI), spoiled gradient echo (FLASH, SPGR).
- **Parallel imaging** (SENSE, GRAPPA) further reduces scan time by undersampling k-space with multiple receiver coils.

**3. Sequence-Based Strategies:**

- **Single-shot techniques:** EPI, SSFSE — entire image from one excitation; minimal motion sensitivity.
- **Fast spin echo (FSE, TSE):** Multiple echoes per TR reduce total scan time.
- **Saturation bands:** Spatially selective RF pulses applied superior/inferior to the imaging volume to null signal from inflowing spins (used for flow suppression, e.g., in MRA).
- **Flow compensation (gradient moment nulling):** Additional gradient lobes null the first-order moment of motion, reducing phase accumulation from constant-velocity spins.
- **PROPELLER / BLADE:** Radial k-space sampling; oversamples central k-space to detect and correct for in-plane motion.
- **Prospective acquisition correction (PACE):** Real-time navigator for free-breathing scans.

**4. Patient Preparation:**

- Immobilization with cushions, straps, and head coils.
- Clear breathing instructions and ear protection.
- Coaching, sedation, or general anesthesia in pediatric or uncooperative patients.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

---

**Question B (14 Marks): Metal Artifacts in CT and MRI**

**(a)** Explain the causes of metal artifacts in CT imaging. Describe the streak pattern produced and the mechanism of photon starvation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various strategies used to minimize metal artifacts in CT and MRI. Include acquisition parameters, reconstruction techniques, and specialized MRI sequences. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Causes of Metal Artifacts in CT:**

Metallic objects (orthopedic prostheses, dental fillings, surgical clips, bullet fragments) have very high atomic number ($Z$) and density, leading to extreme photoelectric absorption. This causes three main effects:

**1. Photon Starvation:**
- The X-ray beam passing through metal loses nearly all its photons.
- The detector receives very few counts ($N \ll 100$).
- According to Poisson statistics, the noise becomes extremely high: $SNR = \sqrt{N}$ approaches zero.
- The reconstruction algorithm then assigns random, incorrect attenuation values along the beam path.
- After back-projection, these errors appear as **bright and dark streaks** emanating radially from the metal.

**2. Beam Hardening (Compounded Effect):**
- Metal preferentially absorbs low-energy photons even more aggressively than bone.
- The beam that emerges on the far side of the metal is severely "hardened."
- Reconstruction errors from this energy shift add to the photon starvation streaks.

**3. Partial Volume Averaging:**
- At the metal-tissue interface, voxels contain both metal and soft tissue, producing inaccurate HU values that contribute to streak edges.

**4. Scatter:**
- High-Z materials generate significant Compton and coherent scatter, contaminating the projection data.

**Streak Pattern Geometry:**

Streaks run **radially** from the metal object because each detector measures one projection line; errors in that projection line propagate along the back-projected ray, producing a streak aligned with the source-detector line. Streaks are most prominent between two dense objects (e.g., between two hip prostheses) because projections crossing both objects suffer compounded error.

---

**(b) Strategies to Minimize Metal Artifacts:**

**In CT:**

1. **Increase X-ray penetration:**
   - **Higher kVp** (e.g., 140 kVp instead of 120) increases the average photon energy, allowing more photons to traverse the metal.
   - **Higher mAs** increases total photon count, reducing relative noise.
   - Trade-off: higher patient dose, but often clinically justified for post-operative imaging.

2. **Thicker slice collimation:**
   - Increases the number of photons per voxel, improving SNR and reducing noise-induced streaks.

3. **Iterative Metal Artifact Reduction (MAR) algorithms:**
   - **iMAR (Siemens)**, **Smart MAR (GE)**, **O-MAR (Philips)**, **SUREMAR (Canon)**.
   - Workflow: (1) Initial reconstruction → (2) identify metal in image → (3) segment metal in raw projection data → (4) inpaint corrupted projections with interpolated values → (5) final reconstruction from corrected projections.
   - Reduces streak severity significantly but can introduce new artifacts if metal segmentation fails.

4. **Dual-Energy CT (DECT) with Monoenergetic Reconstruction:**
   - Acquire at two kVp settings (e.g., 80/140 kVp).
   - Synthesize monoenergetic images at high keV (e.g., 130–200 keV).
   - At higher keV, the photoelectric effect is suppressed relative to Compton scatter, reducing metal-specific artifacts.
   - Virtual monoenergetic images (VMI) at 130–150 keV are commonly used for spine imaging with pedicle screws.

5. **Gantry tilt / patient repositioning:**
   - Tilt gantry to avoid primary beam passing through metal.
   - Reposition patient so the metal is outside the primary scan plane when possible.

6. **Extended scale reconstruction algorithms:**
   - Some scanners (e.g., extended HU scale to 40,000 HU) handle very high attenuation values without saturating, reducing streaks.

**In MRI:**

1. **Sequence selection:**
   - **Spin-echo** (SE) sequences are less sensitive to susceptibility than gradient-echo (GRE).
   - **Fast spin-echo (FSE/TSE)** further reduces sensitivity.
   - **STIR** (short TI inversion recovery) suppresses fat and uses SE technology.

2. **Reduce susceptibility-related dephasing:**
   - **Shorter echo time (TE):** Less time for dephasing → smaller artifact.
   - **Smaller voxels:** Lower intravoxel dephasing.
   - **Higher readout bandwidth:** Reduces pixel shift for off-resonance spins.

3. **Specialized metal artifact reduction sequences:**
   - **MARS (Metal Artifact Reduction Sequence):** Combines SE with high-bandwidth, view-angle tilting (VAT).
   - **VAT (View Angle Tilting):** Applies an additional slice-select gradient during readout, compensating for in-plane geometric distortion.
   - **SEMAC (Slice Encoding for Metal Artifact Correction):** Adds extra phase-encoding in the slice direction to resolve through-plane distortion.
   - **MAVRIC (Multi-Acquisition with Variable Resonance Image Combination):** Acquires multiple 3D datasets at offset frequencies and combines them.

4. **Increase readout bandwidth:**
   - Shifts chemical shift and susceptibility displacement to sub-pixel values.

5. **Use of lower field strength (1.5T vs 3T):**
   - Susceptibility scales linearly with $B_0$.

6. **B0 and B1 shimming:**
   - Optimizes field homogeneity in the region of interest.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Image Artifacts in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "artifact" with "noise":** Noise is a *statistical fluctuation*; artifacts are *systematic, deterministic* distortions. Examiners WILL deduct marks if you treat them as synonymous.
>
> 2. **Generic answers:** Writing "use better equipment" or "increase dose" without specifying **how** (e.g., "increase kVp from 120 to 140 AND enable iMAR reconstruction") loses 1–2 marks per correction.
>
> 3. **Confusing beam hardening with photon starvation:** Both produce streaks in CT, but beam hardening is due to energy shift (correctable with bow-tie filter and software), while photon starvation is due to insufficient counts (correctable with higher mAs).
>
> 4. **Missing the geometric origin of streaks:** Examiners expect you to state that **streaks are radially aligned with the source-detector line** — not just "there are streaks."
>
> 5. **Forgetting modality specificity:** Motion in MRI produces **ghosts**; motion in CT produces **blurring/steps**. Don't mix these up.
>
> 6. **Skipping the formula:** KTU examiners award marks for the underlying mathematics (e.g., $SNR = \sqrt{N}$, $\Delta f = \gamma B_0 \delta_{cs}$). Always include a formula when relevant.
>
> 7. **Not stating SI units or values:** Always quote numbers with units (e.g., "3.5 ppm" for fat-water chemical shift, "1.34 mm" for displacement at 1.5T).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Image Artifacts (Module 3)**

- **Definition:** Artifacts = any non-anatomical signal/pattern/distortion in an image. Always ask: *"Does this feature reflect real anatomy or a system limitation?"*

- **Five Origin Classes:**
  1. **Physics-based** — beam hardening, partial volume, quantum noise, chemical shift, aliasing.
  2. **Patient-based** — motion (voluntary/involuntary), metal, truncation, susceptibility.
  3. **Equipment-based** — dead pixels, ring artifacts, zipper, gradient nonlinearity.
  4. **Reconstruction-based** — streak, aliasing, moiré, convergence.
  5. **Technique-based** — wrong exposure, wrong reconstruction kernel.

- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $SNR = \sqrt{N}$ → $SNR \propto \sqrt{mAs}$.
  - $\Delta f_{fat-water} = \gamma B_0 \cdot 3.5 \text{ ppm}$.
  - $\mu_{eff}(x) = -\frac{1}{x} \ln\left( \frac{I(x)}{I_0} \right)$ for polychromatic beam.
  - Nyquist: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.

- **High-Yield Clinical Correlations:**
  - **Beam hardening → cupping** in abdomen, **dark streaks** between petrous bones in brain CT.
  - **Chemical shift → dark band on kidney** at 1.5T without fat suppression.
  - **Motion ghosting → multiple heart outlines** along phase-encoding axis in cardiac MRI.
  - **Metal streak → bright/dark radial lines** from hip implants in CT pelvis.
  - **Ring artifacts → concentric circles** centered on scanner isocenter in CT.

- **Standard Modality-Specific Corrections:**
  - **X-ray:** ↑mAs (↓noise), proper kVp, remove foreign objects, use grid properly, use higher-speed screen.
  - **CT:** Bow-tie filter, iterative reconstruction, ↑kVp/↑mAs, MAR algorithms, DECT monoenergetic reconstruction.
  - **MRI:** Spin-echo sequences, ↑bandwidth, fat suppression, breath-hold, gating, MAVRIC/SEMAC for metal.
  - **Ultrasound:** Adjust gain, frequency, depth, use coupling gel; recognize reverberation, acoustic shadowing, mirror image.
  - **Nuclear Medicine:** ↑counts, motion correction software, dual-head summation, gating.

- **Engineering Insight:** Artifact reduction = **trade-off** between **dose, scan time, and image quality**. Modern systems use **AI/Deep Learning** (e.g., GE's TrueFidelity, Canon AiCE, Siemens' AI-Rad) to push these trade-offs favorably.

- **Key Buzzwords for Examiner:** "polychromatic assumption violation," "photon starvation," "k-space inconsistency," "in-plane misregistration," "Nyquist criterion violation," "susceptibility-induced dephasing."

- **End-of-Topic Self-Test:** Can you draw a streak artifact and explain its origin? Can you state two ways to correct each major artifact? Can you list the formula for quantum noise SNR? If yes — you are **examination-ready**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to CT Angiogram and DSA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to CT Angiogram and DSA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 CT Angiogram (CTA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Computed Tomography Angiography (CTA) is a non-invasive, contrast-enhanced radiological imaging modality that uses a multi-detector CT (MDCT) scanner combined with intravenous iodinated contrast media to produce high-resolution, cross-sectional and three-dimensional reconstructions of vascular structures including arteries, veins, and the chambers of the heart.*

CTA is the workhorse of modern vascular imaging because it provides a single breath-hold, near-isotropic volumetric dataset of the entire vascular tree without the need for an arterial puncture.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Plumber's Camera"

Imagine you are a plumber trying to inspect every pipe inside the walls of a large building. You cannot tear the walls down, but you can pump a brightly colored dye (iodine contrast) into the pipes. A special "smart camera" (the CT scanner) then rotates around the building, taking X-ray snapshots from every angle. The dye-filled pipes glow brightly against the dull concrete background, and a computer stitches these snapshots into a complete 3D model of the plumbing network. This is exactly how CTA works — except the "building" is the human body and the "pipes" are blood vessels.

### Key Standard Metrics for CTA

- **Spatial resolution:** **0.4 mm to 0.6 mm** (isotropic voxel)
- **Acquisition time:** **3 to 10 seconds** for a typical body CTA
- **Contrast volume:** **80 mL to 150 mL** of iodinated contrast (350–370 mg I/mL)
- **Injection rate:** **3 mL/sec to 5 mL/sec** via power injector
- **Peak tube voltage:** **80 kVp to 140 kVp** (lower kVp preferred for iodine visualization)
- **Bolus tracking threshold:** **100 HU to 150 HU** in the target vessel

---

## 1.2 Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA) is an invasive fluoroscopic imaging technique in which a pre-contrast "mask" image is digitally subtracted from subsequent images acquired after intra-arterial or intravenous injection of iodinated contrast media, thereby eliminating overlying bony and soft-tissue structures and leaving only the opacified vasculature visible on the final subtracted image.*

DSA has been the **gold standard** for vascular imaging for decades, particularly for cerebral, carotid, and peripheral arterial evaluation, and remains the definitive technique for image-guided endovascular interventions.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Photo Trick"

Hold your hand in front of a plain wall and take a photo. Then take the same photo with a flashlight behind your hand. Now, in software, *subtract* the first image from the second — the wall disappears, and you are left with just the silhouette of your hand's blood vessels glowing against pure black. DSA does the same thing mathematically: it removes the static "wall" (bones and soft tissue) so that only the dynamic "flashlight glow" (iodinated blood) remains visible.

### Key Standard Metrics for DSA

- **Spatial resolution:** **0.2 mm to 0.4 mm** (superior to CTA)
- **Frame rate:** **1 fps to 30 fps** (real-time acquisition)
- **Contrast volume:** **5 mL to 15 mL per injection** (arterial), or **30 mL to 40 mL** (intravenous)
- **Dynamic range:** **10 bits to 16 bits** (digital detector)
- **Matrix size:** **1024 × 1024** typical
- **Typical fluoroscopic exposure:** **Low-dose pulsed fluoroscopy** ~ **1 µGy/s to 5 µGy/s**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Why this topic matters in Module 3:**
> This topic transitions the student from cross-sectional imaging (Modules 1 & 2) to **specialized vascular imaging**, which forms the foundation for understanding interventional radiology, stroke management, and modern contrast-enhanced protocols.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of CT Angiography (CTA)

The CTA pipeline is built on six sequential logical steps. Understanding the *why* behind each step is essential for KTU-level application questions.

1. **Patient Preparation & IV Cannulation**
   *Why:* Power injection of contrast at high flow rates requires a large-bore cannula (typically **18 G to 20 G**) in an antecubital vein. Without this, the bolus will fragment and the vessels will be poorly opacified.

2. **Pre-Contrast Scout (Topogram)**
   *Why:* A low-dose frontal radiograph defines the scan range (e.g., from aortic arch to vertex for a carotid CTA). It also enables the **bolus tracking ROI** to be placed precisely on the target vessel.

3. **Test Bolus or Bolus Tracking**
   *Why:* Vascular opacification is a moving target — the contrast bolus travels through the vasculature with a peak enhancement time that varies with cardiac output. The scanner must "catch" the vessels at their peak.
   - **Test Bolus Method:** A small (15–20 mL) test injection is timed to determine the time-to-peak enhancement in the target vessel. The main scan is then triggered at `time-to-peak` seconds.
   - **Bolus Tracking Method:** Real-time low-dose scans monitor a ROI in the target vessel. When attenuation crosses a threshold (typically **+100 HU to +150 HU** above baseline), the diagnostic helical scan is automatically initiated after a brief diagnostic delay.

4. **Helical (Spiral) Acquisition**
   *Why:* Modern MDCT scanners use a continuously rotating X-ray tube while the patient table moves at constant velocity, acquiring a **volumetric dataset** in a single breath-hold. The pitch factor determines how the table advances per rotation:
   *Pitch* = *table travel per rotation* / *collimated beam width*

5. **Image Reconstruction (Filtered Back-Projection / Iterative Reconstruction)**
   *Why:* The 2D projections are mathematically combined using the **Radon transform** principle to reconstruct cross-sectional slices. A soft-tissue or vascular reconstruction kernel is used for CTA.

6. **Post-Processing (MIP, MPR, VR)**
   - **MIP (Maximum Intensity Projection):** Slabs the volume and displays the brightest voxel along each ray → ideal for vessels.
   - **MPR (Multi-Planar Reformation):** Arbitrary 2D reformatting of the 3D volume.
   - **Volume Rendering (VR):** Assigns opacity and color based on voxel intensity → photorealistic 3D vascular trees.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic of Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA)

DSA relies on a brilliant imaging principle: **temporal subtraction of a baseline (mask) image from a contrast-filled image**. The processing pipeline is:

1. **Pre-Contrast Mask Acquisition**
   *Why:* The mask must be acquired at the **same projection angle, same kVp, same mA, and same respiratory phase** as the live image. Patient motion between mask and contrast frames causes **misregistration artifacts** — a critical concept for KTU viva questions.

2. **Contrast Injection**
   *Why:* For arterial DSA, contrast is injected via a catheter placed selectively into the target artery (e.g., internal carotid, renal artery). For **IV-DSA**, contrast is injected into a central vein — the image quality is lower because of dilution.

3. **Real-Time Fluoroscopic Image Acquisition**
   *Why:* A series of frames is acquired at 1–30 fps as the contrast bolus washes through the vessel tree. The image intensifier (or flat-panel detector) converts X-rays to light → video signal → digital frame.

4. **Logarithmic Subtraction**
   *Why:* Direct pixel subtraction (`I_contrast − I_mask`) leaves a residual grey background because of non-linear X-ray attenuation. By applying a **logarithmic transformation first**, subtraction becomes a true ratio that cancels out the static background:
   *Subtracted Image* = *log(I_mask)* − *log(I_contrast)* = *log(I_mask / I_contrast)*

   The bones and soft tissue contribute identically to both `I_mask` and `I_contrast` and therefore cancel, leaving only the contrast-filled vessel.

5. **Image Display & Roadmap Overlay**
   *Why:* The subtracted image is displayed live on a monitor. A **roadmap** is created by overlaying the subtracted vessel image on the live fluoroscopic feed — this guides catheter navigation during interventional procedures.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|--------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | X-ray Attenuation (Beer–Lambert Law) | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$ | Monoenergetic X-ray attenuation through thickness *x* of material with linear attenuation coefficient μ | *I* in counts |
| 2 | Polyenergetic Attenuation (Effective) | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\sum \mu_i x_i}$ | Sum of attenuations through heterogeneous tissues | dimensionless |
| 3 | CT Number (Hounsfield Unit) | $HU = 1000 \times \dfrac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$ | Normalized attenuation index; water = 0 HU, air = −1000 HU | HU |
| 4 | Iodine Attenuation Boost at Low kVp | $HU_{\text{iodine}} \propto \dfrac{1}{E^3}$ (approx., photoelectric region) | Iodine's K-edge at **33.2 keV** boosts attenuation at lower tube voltages | HU |
| 5 | DSA Logarithmic Subtraction | $S_{\text{sub}} = \log(I_{\text{mask}}) - \log(I_{\text{contrast}}) = \log\!\left(\dfrac{I_{\text{mask}}}{I_{\text{contrast}}}\right)$ | Eliminates static background | dimensionless |
| 6 | Contrast-to-Noise Ratio (CNR) | $CNR = \dfrac{\vert HU_{\text{vessel}} - HU_{\text{background}} \vert}{SD_{\text{background}}}$ | Key image quality metric for CTA/DSA | dimensionless |
| 7 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) | $SNR = \dfrac{\mu}{\sigma}$ | Signal mean over standard deviation | dimensionless |
| 8 | Helical CT Pitch | $p = \dfrac{d}{N \times T}$ | *d* = table travel per rotation, *N* × *T* = total collimation | dimensionless |
| 9 | DSA Frame Rate Selection | $f_{\text{acq}} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta t}$ | Frames per second; trade-off between dose and temporal resolution | fps |
| 10 | Patient Skin Dose Estimate (Fluoroscopy) | $D_{\text{skin}} = \dot{D} \times t_{\text{fluoro}}$ | Skin dose = dose rate × fluoroscopy time | mGy |
| 11 | Iodine Concentration vs. kVp | $CNR \propto \dfrac{\text{Iodine (mg I/mL)}}{\left( E_{\text{keV}} \right)^{3.3}}$ | Lower kVp amplifies iodine visibility | — |
| 12 | Vessel Opacification (Contrast Bolus Model) | $C(t) = \dfrac{D}{V_d} \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ | First-order kinetics; *D* = dose, *V*_d = distribution volume, τ = time constant | mg I/mL |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Equation (5) — the logarithmic subtraction — is the most frequently asked derivation in DSA questions. Memorize it and be ready to explain why *direct* subtraction fails.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the DSA Logarithmic Subtraction Equation

The mathematical justification for DSA is the single most important derivation for KTU Module 3. Let us derive it rigorously.

### Step 1 — Define the X-ray Image Formation

Consider a uniform background tissue of thickness *T* with an embedded vessel of thickness *x* filled with iodine contrast. The X-ray intensity reaching the detector is the result of two attenuations in series.

For the **mask image** (no contrast in vessel), the X-ray passes only through the background:

$$
I_{\text{mask}} = I_0 \, e^{-\mu_{\text{bg}} T}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the Beer–Lambert law applied to the background tissue of thickness *T* and linear attenuation coefficient μ_bg.

### Step 2 — Write the Live Image (With Contrast)

For the **contrast image** (vessel now filled with iodinated blood of higher attenuation μ_iodine), the X-ray must traverse the same total thickness but with a small segment of higher attenuation:

$$
I_{\text{contrast}} = I_0 \, e^{-\mu_{\text{bg}} (T - x)} \cdot e^{-\mu_{\text{iodine}} x}
$$

*Explanation:* The X-ray traverses (T − x) of background tissue and then *x* of iodine-filled vessel. The two exponential factors multiply because the attenuations occur in series.

### Step 3 — Form the Ratio of Mask to Contrast Image

Divide mask by contrast to eliminate the common *I*_0 and the background term:

$$
\frac{I_{\text{mask}}}{I_{\text{contrast}}} = \frac{I_0 \, e^{-\mu_{\text{bg}} T}}{I_0 \, e^{-\mu_{\text{bg}} (T - x)} \cdot e^{-\mu_{\text{iodine}} x}}
$$

*Explanation:* The I_0 cancels in numerator and denominator.

### Step 4 — Simplify the Exponents

Combine the exponent terms in the denominator. Recall that subtracting exponents corresponds to multiplying exponentials:

$$
\frac{I_{\text{mask}}}{I_{\text{contrast}}} = \frac{e^{-\mu_{\text{bg}} T}}{e^{-\mu_{\text{bg}} T + \mu_{\text{bg}} x - \mu_{\text{iodine}} x}}
$$

Bringing the denominator exponent into the numerator by inverting:

$$
\frac{I_{\text{mask}}}{I_{\text{contrast}}} = e^{-\mu_{\text{bg}} T + \mu_{\text{bg}} T - \mu_{\text{bg}} x + \mu_{\text{iodine}} x}
$$

*Explanation:* Flipping the denominator exponent flips its sign.

The two −μ_bg T and +μ_bg T terms **cancel exactly**:

$$
\frac{I_{\text{mask}}}{I_{\text{contrast}}} = e^{(\mu_{\text{iodine}} - \mu_{\text{bg}}) x}
$$

*Explanation:* The static background has disappeared from the equation. The result depends only on the **difference in attenuation** between the iodine-filled vessel and the background, multiplied by the vessel thickness.

### Step 5 — Take the Natural Logarithm

Take the natural logarithm of both sides. This is the critical "logarithmic subtraction" step:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{I_{\text{mask}}}{I_{\text{contrast}}}\right) = (\mu_{\text{iodine}} - \mu_{\text{bg}}) \, x
$$

*Explanation:* The logarithm converts the exponential back into a linear expression, isolating the contrast-induced signal.

### Step 6 — Final DSA Subtraction Equation

Using the property $\ln(a/b) = \ln(a) - \ln(b)$, the equation can be rewritten as:

$$
S_{\text{sub}} = \ln(I_{\text{mask}}) - \ln(I_{\text{contrast}}) = (\mu_{\text{iodine}} - \mu_{\text{bg}}) \, x
$$

*Explanation:* This is the **final DSA signal**. It is the **logarithmic difference** of the two images, and it is directly proportional to the iodine column thickness *x* — exactly the information we want.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why NOT direct subtraction?** If we computed $I_{\text{mask}} - I_{\text{contrast}}$ instead, the result would be a small number sitting on top of a large background, and the bone/soft tissue would NOT cancel cleanly. The logarithmic transform is the only way to make the background cancel **exactly** in the presence of exponential X-ray attenuation.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — CTA vs. DSA Comparison

The KTU examiner often tests whether students can *contrast* the two techniques. This matrix is a high-yield revision tool.

| Parameter | CT Angiography (CTA) | Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA) |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Invasiveness | **Non-invasive** (peripheral IV) | **Invasive** (intra-arterial catheter) |
| Contrast route | Intravenous | Intra-arterial (selective) or intravenous |
| Contrast volume | **80–150 mL** | **5–15 mL** per selective injection |
| Spatial resolution | **0.4–0.6 mm** | **0.2–0.4 mm** (superior) |
| Temporal resolution | Single phase (or 2-phase) | **Real-time dynamic** (continuous frames) |
| 3D capability | Excellent (volumetric, MIP, VR) | Limited (2D projections, rotational 3D DSA) |
| Quantitative vessel measurement | Excellent (CT angiography is geometrically calibrated) | Limited by projective distortion |
| Therapeutic capability | **None** | **Yes** — angioplasty, stenting, coiling, embolization |
| Risk profile | Low (contrast nephropathy, radiation) | Higher (arterial puncture, embolic stroke) |
| Bone artifact | Can obscure vessels (e.g., at skull base) | **Eliminated** by subtraction |
| Patient motion artifact | Less critical (sub-second MDCT) | Highly critical (mask misregistration) |
| Acquisition time | **3–10 sec** (single breath-hold) | **10–30 sec** per acquisition run |
| Image post-processing | MIP, MPR, VR, curved reformats | Roadmapping, pixel shift, landmarking |
| Typical clinical uses | Pulmonary embolism, AAA, dissection, trauma | Stroke thrombectomy, TIPS, GI bleed, chemoembolization |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CTA Pipeline — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Preparation and IV Cannula 18G to 20G] --> B[Scout Topogram and Scan Range Definition]
    B --> C{Bolus Timing Strategy}
    C -->|Test Bolus| D1[Inject 15 to 20 mL test bolus and measure time to peak]
    C -->|Bolus Tracking| D2[Real time ROI monitoring on target vessel]
    D1 --> E[Main Helical MDCT Acquisition with IV Contrast]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Raw Projection Data Acquisition]
    F --> G[Image Reconstruction FBP or Iterative]
    G --> H[Cross Sectional Axial Slices HU Map]
    H --> I[Post Processing Engine]
    I --> I1[MIP Maximum Intensity Projection]
    I --> I2[MPR Multi Planar Reformation]
    I --> I3[VR Volume Rendering 3D]
    I1 --> J[Diagnostic CTA Image Set]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    J --> K[Clinical Report Vessel Stenosis Aneurysm Dissection]
```

## 4.2 DSA Pipeline — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Arterial Puncture and Catheter Placement] --> B[Position C Arm at Target Projection Angle]
    B --> C[Acquire Mask Image Pre Contrast Fluoroscopy Frame]
    C --> D[Inject Iodinated Contrast via Power Injector 5 to 15 mL]
    D --> E[Acquire Live Contrast Image Sequence 1 to 30 fps]
    E --> F[Logarithmic Transform of Mask and Live Frames]
    F --> G[Pixel by Pixel Subtraction log Mask minus log Contrast]
    G --> H[Background Cancelled Vessel Only Image]
    H --> I{Subtraction Quality Check}
    I -->|Good| J[Roadmap Overlay on Live Fluoroscopy]
    I -->|Misregistered| K[Pixel Shift and Reregistration Algorithm]
    K --> G
    J --> L[Diagnostic or Interventional Decision Support]
    L --> M[Endovascular Intervention if Required Angioplasty Stent Embolization]
```

## 4.3 DSA System Hardware Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph G1[Imaging Chain]
        X1[X Ray Tube High Frequency Generator] --> X2[Patient Table Floating Carbon Fiber]
        X2 --> X3[Flat Panel Detector or Image Intensifier]
        X3 --> X4[Analog to Digital Converter 10 to 16 bit]
        X4 --> X5[Frame Grabber and Image Memory]
    end
    subgraph G2[Processing Chain]
        X5 --> P1[Logarithmic Lookup Table LUT]
        P1 --> P2[Real Time Subtraction Processor]
        P2 --> P3[Pixel Shift and Reregistration Engine]
        P3 --> P4[Roadmap Image Composer]
        P4 --> P5[Display Monitors Medical Grade]
    end
    subgraph G3[Control and Safety]
        C1[Operator Console with Foot Switch] --> C2[System Controller CPU]
        C2 --> X1
        C2 --> P1
        C2 --> P5
        S1[Dose Area Product Meter] --> C2
        S2[Collimator and Filter Control] --> X1
    end
```

## 4.4 Side-by-Side Workflow Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[CT Angiography CTA]
        A1[IV Cannula] --> A2[Power Injector Bolus]
        A2 --> A3[Helical CT Acquisition]
        A3 --> A4[Volumetric Reconstruction]
        A4 --> A5[3D Vascular Model]
    end
    subgraph S2[Digital Subtraction Angiography DSA]
        B1[Arterial Catheter] --> B2[Mask Image]
        B2 --> B3[Contrast Injection]
        B3 --> B4[Live Image Sequence]
        B4 --> B5[Logarithmic Subtraction]
        B5 --> B6[Vessel Only Image]
    end
    A5 -.Competitor Modality.-> B6
    B6 -.Competitor Modality.-> A5
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PEBRT411, Module 3]**
*Define Digital Subtraction Angiography. Mention any two clinical applications of DSA.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA) is a fluoroscopic vascular imaging technique in which a pre-contrast "mask" image is digitally subtracted from images acquired after intra-arterial injection of iodinated contrast, eliminating bony and soft-tissue background to visualize the opacified vessels alone.

**Two clinical applications (2 Marks — 1 each):**
1. **Cerebral angiography** for detection of intracranial aneurysms, arteriovenous malformations (AVMs), and acute stroke assessment prior to mechanical thrombectomy.
2. **Peripheral and coronary angiography** for evaluation of peripheral arterial disease, steno-occlusive disease, and for guiding endovascular interventions such as angioplasty, stenting, and embolization.

*(Acceptable alternative applications: carotid artery evaluation, pulmonary angiography, renal artery stenosis, GI bleed localization, transjugular intrahepatic portosystemic shunt TIPS placement.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — December 2023, PEBRT411, Module 3]**
*What is CT Angiography? List the three main post-processing techniques used in CTA.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
CT Angiography (CTA) is a contrast-enhanced multi-detector CT (MDCT) imaging technique that uses intravenous iodinated contrast media to produce high-resolution cross-sectional and three-dimensional images of the vascular system.

**Three main post-processing techniques (2 Marks — approx. 2/3 mark each):**
1. **Maximum Intensity Projection (MIP):** Projects the brightest voxel along each ray through the volume; excellent for depicting the course and continuity of vessels.
2. **Multi-Planar Reformation (MPR):** Reformatts the 3D volume into arbitrary 2D planes (sagittal, coronal, oblique, curved) to follow the vessel along its true course.
3. **Volume Rendering (VR):** Assigns opacity, color, and shading to voxels based on intensity to create a photorealistic 3D vascular tree.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEBRT411, Module 3]**
*(a)* Explain in detail the principle, working, and clinical applications of Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA). Include a note on the role of logarithmic subtraction.
*(b)* Compare CT Angiography and DSA. Discuss at least six differentiating parameters with technical justification.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Principle (2 Marks):**
DSA is based on the principle of **temporal subtraction**. A pre-contrast fluoroscopic "mask" image is acquired at a fixed projection angle. Iodinated contrast is then injected intra-arterially, and a sequence of fluoroscopic frames is captured as the contrast bolus passes through the vessels. The mask image is subtracted pixel-by-pixel from the contrast images. Because X-ray attenuation is exponential, a **logarithmic transformation** is applied to both images before subtraction so that the static background (bone, soft tissue) cancels exactly, leaving only the contrast-filled vessels visible.

**Working Steps (3 Marks — break-up shown):**
1. **Patient positioning and C-arm angulation** — The C-arm is positioned to provide a true AP/lateral/oblique projection of the target vessel. (0.5 Mark)
2. **Mask acquisition** — Several pre-contrast fluoroscopic frames are averaged to reduce noise and to account for minor patient motion. (0.5 Mark)
3. **Contrast injection** — Iodinated contrast (5–15 mL for selective arterial DSA) is power-injected through the catheter at 3–8 mL/sec. (0.5 Mark)
4. **Dynamic image acquisition** — A series of images is acquired at 1–30 fps over 5–15 seconds as the bolus washes in and out. (0.5 Mark)
5. **Logarithmic subtraction and pixel re-registration** — Each contrast frame is log-transformed and subtracted from the (also log-transformed) mask; pixel-shift algorithms correct for residual misregistration. (0.5 Mark)
6. **Display and roadmap** — The subtracted image is displayed on a monitor and overlaid on the live fluoroscopic feed ("roadmap") to guide catheter-based interventions. (0.5 Mark)

**Clinical Applications (1 Mark — any two):**
- Cerebral angiography for aneurysm, AVM, and acute stroke
- Peripheral and coronary angiography for stenosis evaluation and pre-interventional planning
- Hepatic/renal artery mapping, GI bleed localization, TIPS, and chemoembolization

**Note on Logarithmic Subtraction (1 Mark):**
Since X-ray attenuation is governed by the Beer–Lambert exponential law, direct subtraction $I_{\text{contrast}} - I_{\text{mask}}$ does **not** cleanly cancel the background. By computing $\log(I_{\text{mask}}) - \log(I_{\text{contrast}}) = (\mu_{\text{iodine}} - \mu_{\text{bg}}) x$, the static tissue cancels, and the resulting signal is linearly proportional to the iodine column thickness.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Comparison Table (6 Marks — 1 per parameter, with technical justification):**

| Parameter | CTA | DSA | Technical Justification |
|-----------|-----|-----|-------------------------|
| Invasiveness | Non-invasive (IV) | Invasive (arterial catheter) | Catheterization requires femoral/radial access |
| Contrast volume | 80–150 mL (IV, diluted) | 5–15 mL (selective intra-arterial) | Selective delivery avoids systemic dilution |
| Spatial resolution | 0.4–0.6 mm | 0.2–0.4 mm (better) | DSA's small pixel size + flat-panel detector |
| Temporal resolution | Static (or 2 phases) | Real-time dynamic (1–30 fps) | DSA is fluoroscopic, frame-by-frame |
| 3D capability | Excellent (true isotropic volume) | Limited (2D; rotational DSA only partial 3D) | MDCT acquires full volumetric dataset |
| Interventional capability | None | Yes (balloon, stent, coil, embolic agent) | DSA is performed in the interventional suite |

**Conclusion (1 Mark):**
CTA is the first-line non-invasive screening tool for vascular pathology, while DSA is reserved for cases requiring superior spatial/temporal resolution or for combined diagnostic and therapeutic endovascular intervention. Both modalities are complementary rather than competing in modern clinical practice.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

**[KTU University Exam — Sample, PEBRT411, Module 3]**
*(a)* Describe the principle, image acquisition protocol, and clinical applications of CT Angiography (CTA). Discuss the role of bolus tracking.
*(b)* With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a DSA system. List the major hardware components and their functions.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Principle (1.5 Marks):**
CTA is based on the principle of **contrast-enhanced volumetric X-ray imaging**. Iodinated contrast media, with its K-edge at 33.2 keV, attenuates X-rays strongly in the diagnostic energy range. When injected intravenously as a bolus and timed to peak arterial enhancement, the contrast-opacified vessels produce high-attenuation signals against the surrounding soft tissue. A multi-detector CT scanner acquires a continuous volumetric dataset, which is reconstructed into cross-sectional images and 3D vascular models.

**Image Acquisition Protocol (3.5 Marks — break-up shown):**
1. **Patient preparation** — Nil per oral 4 hours, check renal function (serum creatinine), establish large-bore IV access (18–20 G). (0.5 Mark)
2. **Scout topogram** — Low-dose frontal radiograph for scan range planning. (0.5 Mark)
3. **Bolus timing** — Either test bolus (15–20 mL) or real-time bolus tracking. (0.5 Mark)
4. **Bolus tracking** — A ROI is placed on the target vessel (e.g., ascending aorta for coronary CTA, pulmonary trunk for PE CTA). A series of low-dose monitoring scans is acquired every ~1 sec after contrast injection. When the attenuation in the ROI exceeds the trigger threshold (typically +100 to +150 HU above baseline), the diagnostic helical scan is auto-initiated after a small diagnostic delay (2–5 sec for arterial phase). (1.0 Mark)
5. **Helical acquisition** — Single breath-hold, sub-millimeter collimation, pitch ≈ 0.9–1.2, low tube voltage (80–100 kVp) to maximize iodine CNR. (0.5 Mark)
6. **Reconstruction and post-processing** — Iterative reconstruction; render MIP, MPR, and VR images. (0.5 Mark)

**Clinical Applications (1 Mark — any two):**
- Pulmonary embolism (CT pulmonary angiography, CTPA)
- Aortic dissection and aortic aneurysm evaluation
- Acute stroke with large-vessel occlusion (CTA of head and neck)
- Coronary artery disease (coronary CTA)
- Peripheral arterial disease, renal artery stenosis, mesenteric ischemia

**Role of Bolus Tracking (1 Mark):**
Bolus tracking synchronizes the helical scan with the peak arterial enhancement of the bolus. Without it, the vessels may be imaged before contrast arrival (no opacification) or after washout (poor opacification), resulting in non-diagnostic studies. The trigger threshold and diagnostic delay are tailored to the specific vascular territory.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Block Diagram Description (3 Marks):**
A DSA system consists of three integrated sub-systems: the **X-ray imaging chain**, the **digital image-processing chain**, and the **control and safety chain**. (See Section 4.3 diagram.)

**Major Hardware Components and Functions (3.5 Marks — approx. 0.5 each):**
1. **High-frequency X-ray generator and X-ray tube** — Produces the X-ray beam at controlled kVp (60–110 kVp) and mA.
2. **Collimator and filters** — Restrict the X-ray field to the region of interest and remove low-energy photons that contribute to skin dose.
3. **Floating carbon-fiber patient table** — Permits easy patient positioning and panning without attenuation artifacts.
4. **Image intensifier (II) or flat-panel detector (FPD)** — Converts the transmitted X-ray pattern into a visible light image (II) or directly into a digital signal (FPD).
5. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — Digitizes the video signal (II) or the detector output (FPD) at 10–16-bit depth.
6. **Image memory and frame grabber** — Stores the mask and live frames for real-time processing.
7. **Logarithmic lookup table (LUT) and subtraction processor** — Applies log transformation to both images and performs pixel-by-pixel subtraction.
8. **Pixel-shift and reregistration engine** — Compensates for minor patient motion between mask and contrast frames.
9. **Roadmap image composer** — Overlays the subtracted vessel image on the live fluoroscopic feed to guide catheter navigation.
10. **Operator console with foot-switch and exposure control** — Allows the radiologist to control exposures, annotate, and store image runs.
11. **Dose-area-product (DAP) meter and last-image-hold** — Real-time dose monitoring and image retention with minimal additional exposure.

**Conclusion (0.5 Mark):**
The DSA system is a sophisticated integration of real-time X-ray imaging, digital signal processing, and interventional ergonomics, enabling both high-quality vascular diagnosis and image-guided therapy in a single clinical environment.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the logarithm:** Many students write the DSA subtraction as $I_{\text{contrast}} - I_{\text{mask}}$ without explaining *why* the log transform is required. This will cost you **at least 2 marks** in Part B. Always state: *"Because attenuation is exponential, a log transform is needed for the background to cancel."*
> 2. **Confusing CTA and DSA invasiveness:** CTA is **non-invasive** (peripheral IV only). DSA is **invasive** (arterial catheter). Examiners look for this distinction.
> 3. **Missing units and standard values:** Always cite the **range of contrast volume, resolution, and kVp** with units. Bare qualitative statements lose marks.
> 4. **Skipping the "Why":** KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes outcome-based learning. For every step in your answer, state the *reason* (the "Why" column in Section 2).
> 5. **Ignoring motion artifacts:** In DSA answers, students often forget to discuss **misregistration artifacts** and the role of **pixel-shift correction**. This is a frequent 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Introduction to CT Angiogram and DSA**

- ✅ **CTA = non-invasive**, IV contrast, **volumetric** 3D imaging using MDCT.
- ✅ **DSA = invasive**, intra-arterial, **2D real-time** fluoroscopic imaging using subtraction.
- ✅ **CTA contrast volume: 80–150 mL IV**, **DSA contrast volume: 5–15 mL per selective injection.**
- ✅ **Bolus tracking threshold = +100 to +150 HU** above baseline; **diagnostic delay = 2–5 sec.**
- ✅ **Test bolus method** measures time-to-peak enhancement before the main scan.
- ✅ **Lower kVp (80–100 kVp) increases iodine CNR** due to proximity to the iodine K-edge at 33.2 keV.
- ✅ **CTA post-processing:** MIP, MPR, VR — MIP and VR are the workhorses for vascular display.
- ✅ **DSA key equation:** $S_{\text{sub}} = \log(I_{\text{mask}}) - \log(I_{\text{contrast}}) = (\mu_{\text{iodine}} - \mu_{\text{bg}}) x$.
- ✅ **Logarithmic subtraction is mandatory** because X-ray attenuation is exponential — direct subtraction fails to cancel the background.
- ✅ **DSA background cancellation** leaves only the iodine-filled vessel column.
- ✅ **DSA spatial resolution (0.2–0.4 mm) > CTA (0.4–0.6 mm)** — DSA remains the spatial resolution gold standard.
- ✅ **CTA temporal resolution = single phase (or 2 phase); DSA = real-time dynamic (1–30 fps).**
- ✅ **CTA has no therapeutic role; DSA is both diagnostic AND therapeutic** (angioplasty, stenting, coiling, embolization).
- ✅ **DSA major artifacts:** misregistration (patient motion), quantum noise, and vessel overlap.
- ✅ **DSA hardware essentials:** X-ray tube + C-arm, II or FPD, ADC, log LUT, subtraction processor, roadmap composer, DAP meter.
- ✅ **CTA clinical uses:** PE, aortic dissection, AAA, acute stroke, coronary disease, peripheral arterial disease.
- ✅ **DSA clinical uses:** cerebral AVM/aneurysm, acute stroke thrombectomy, GI bleed, TIPS, peripheral and coronary interventions.
- ✅ **DSA pixel-shift algorithm** is used to correct minor misregistration between mask and contrast frames.
- ✅ **Roadmap image** = real-time overlay of subtracted vessel on live fluoroscopy — guides catheter navigation.
- ✅ **Hounsfield Unit formula:** $HU = 1000 \times \dfrac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$.
- ✅ **Iodine peak kilovoltage principle:** $CNR \propto \dfrac{1}{E^{3.3}}$ — favors low-kVp CT for iodine visualization.
- ✅ **Rotational 3D DSA** is a hybrid technique that provides limited 3D capability by rotating the C-arm during a single contrast injection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Radio-Isotopes in Medical Diagnosis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Radio-Isotopes in Medical Diagnosis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Radioisotopes in medical diagnosis** refer to radioactive isotopes (radionuclides) that are introduced into the human body—either as part of a pharmaceutical compound (forming a **radiopharmaceutical**) or in elemental form—to enable the visualization, functional assessment, and diagnosis of physiological and pathological conditions in internal organs and tissues.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Module 4 focus is on the *physical principles* of nuclear medicine imaging — that is, the **source** (radioisotope), the **emitted radiation** (γ-photons, positrons), the **detection mechanism** (Gamma Camera / PET), and the resulting **diagnostic image**.

A radioisotope is characterized by an unstable atomic nucleus that achieves stability by **emission of ionizing radiation** — most commonly **gamma (γ) photons**, **beta (β⁻) particles**, or **positrons (β⁺)**. In *diagnostic* applications, the emission of penetrating γ-photons or 511 keV annihilation photons is exploited, while α and β emissions are reserved for *therapeutic* uses.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Glowing Tracker"

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine you attach a tiny, harmless **glow-stick** to a specific chemical that the liver absorbs preferentially. Then you take a photograph in a dark room — the glow-stick shows you *exactly where the liver is* and *how well it is functioning*. A medical radioisotope does the same thing: the radioactive atom acts as a glowing **"tracker"** that emits invisible γ-rays, which a special camera (Gamma Camera / PET) converts into a **functional image** of the organ.

Unlike X-ray or CT (which primarily show *anatomy*), radioisotope imaging reveals **function and metabolism** — hence the famous clinical saying: *"X-ray shows you the shape of the river; nuclear medicine shows you the flow of the river."*

### 1.3 Essential Physical Constants and Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Decay constant | λ | **s⁻¹** (per second) |
| Half-life | T₁/₂ | **seconds (s)**, minutes, hours, days |
| Activity | A | **Becquerel (Bq)** = 1 disintegration per second |
| Old activity unit | Ci | **1 Curie (Ci) = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq** |
| Photon energy | E_γ | **keV** (kilo-electron volts) |
| Mass-energy of electron | m_e c² | **511 keV** (annihilation photon energy) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Becquerel (Bq)** is the SI unit. For clinical doses, **Megabecquerel (MBq)** is used. A typical diagnostic dose ranges from **1 MBq to 1000 MBq** depending on the procedure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Radioactive Decay Theory

A radioisotope has a nucleus with excess energy or unfavorable neutron-to-proton ratio. To reach stability, it emits particles/photons. The rate of decay follows a **first-order kinetic process**, identical in mathematical form to chemical first-order reactions.

**Step 1 — Number of undecayed nuclei decreases with time:**

The rate of decay (disintegrations per second) is **proportional** to the number of radioactive nuclei *N(t)* present at that instant.

$$ \frac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N $$

**Step 2 — Integration gives the exponential decay law:**

$$ N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t} $$

where $N_0$ is the initial number of nuclei at $t = 0$.

**Step 3 — Activity is defined as the rate of decay:**

$$ A = \left| \frac{dN}{dt} \right| = \lambda N $$

$$ A(t) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t} $$

**Step 4 — Half-life is the time for activity to halve:**

At $t = T_{1/2}$, $A(t) = A_0 / 2$, giving:

$$ T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda} $$

**Step 5 — Mean life (average lifetime) of a radioisotope:**

$$ \tau = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{T_{1/2}}{0.693} \approx 1.443 \, T_{1/2} $$

### 2.2 Specific Activity

The **specific activity** is the activity per unit mass of the radioisotope:

$$ A_{sp} = \frac{\lambda N_A}{M} $$

where $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹ (Avogadro's number) and $M$ is the molar mass.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Number of undecayed nuclei at time $t$ |
| 2 | $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Activity (decay rate) at time $t$ |
| 3 | $\lambda = 0.693 / T_{1/2}$ | Decay constant from half-life |
| 4 | $A = \lambda N$ | Activity equals decay constant times number of nuclei |
| 5 | $\tau = 1/\lambda$ | Mean (average) life |
| 6 | $A_{sp} = \lambda N_A / M$ | Specific activity (Bq/g) |
| 7 | $1 \text{ Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \text{ Bq}$ | Curie to Becquerel conversion |
| 8 | $E_\gamma = 511 \text{ keV}$ (annihilation) | Energy of PET photon pair |
| 9 | $D_{absorbed} = A \times t \times E_\gamma$ | Approximate absorbed dose energy (for γ only) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA MEMORY AID:** For KTU numericals, you will **always** be given two of the three: $A_0$, $A(t)$, $t$. Solve for the third using the exponential. If half-life is given, **first convert** to $\lambda$ using $0.693/T_{1/2}$.

### 2.4 Real-world Engineering Utility

Radioisotope imaging is foundational in:
- **Oncology** — tumor detection, staging, metastasis mapping (PET with F-18-FDG)
- **Cardiology** — myocardial perfusion imaging (Tc-99m sestamibi, Tl-201)
- **Neurology** — brain perfusion, Alzheimer's and Parkinson's imaging
- **Endocrinology** — thyroid function assessment (I-123, I-131)
- **Orthopedics** — bone scans for fracture, infection, metastasis (Tc-99m MDP)
- **Renal function** — DTPA, MAG3 renography

The **Technetium-99m (Tc-99m)** generator is the workhorse of nuclear medicine — used in **~80% of all diagnostic nuclear medicine procedures** worldwide, owing to its ideal **6-hour half-life** and **140 keV γ-photon** (ideal for gamma cameras).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Decay Law

We start from the experimental fact that the rate of decay of a radioactive sample is **directly proportional** to the number of radioactive nuclei present at that instant.

**Step 1:** Write the proportionality statement mathematically.

$$ \frac{dN}{dt} \propto -N $$

The negative sign indicates that $N$ *decreases* with time.

**Step 2:** Introduce the proportionality constant — the **decay constant** $\lambda$, which is unique to each radioisotope.

$$ \frac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N $$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to separate variables for integration.

$$ \frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \, dt $$

**Step 4:** Integrate both sides, with the limit that at $t=0$, $N = N_0$.

$$ \int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt $$

**Step 5:** Evaluate the integrals.

$$ \ln(N) - \ln(N_0) = -\lambda t $$

$$ \ln\left( \frac{N}{N_0} \right) = -\lambda t $$

**Step 6:** Exponentiate both sides to obtain the decay law.

$$ \frac{N}{N_0} = e^{-\lambda t} $$

$$ \boxed{N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}} $$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Half-Life Formula

**Step 1:** By definition, after one half-life, only half the original nuclei remain.

$$ N(T_{1/2}) = \frac{N_0}{2} $$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the decay law.

$$ \frac{N_0}{2} = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}} $$

**Step 3:** Divide both sides by $N_0$.

$$ \frac{1}{2} = e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}} $$

**Step 4:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides.

$$ \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\lambda T_{1/2} $$

**Step 5:** Recognize that $\ln(1/2) = -\ln 2 = -0.693$.

$$ -0.693 = -\lambda T_{1/2} $$

**Step 6:** Solve for $T_{1/2}$.

$$ \boxed{T_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}} $$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A patient is administered **20 MBq** of Technetium-99m for a thyroid scan. If the half-life of Tc-99m is **6 hours**, calculate:
1. The activity remaining after **12 hours**.
2. The decay constant $\lambda$.
3. The mean life $\tau$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Decay constant:**

$$ \lambda = \frac{0.693}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{6 \, \text{hr}} = 0.1155 \, \text{hr}^{-1} $$

**Part (b) — Activity after 12 hours:**

We observe that **12 hours = 2 half-lives** of Tc-99m.

$$ A(t) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t} $$

$$ A(12) = 20 \, e^{-(0.1155)(12)} $$

$$ A(12) = 20 \, e^{-1.386} $$

$$ A(12) = 20 \times 0.250 $$

$$ \boxed{A(12) = 5 \, \text{MBq}} $$

This matches the rule: **after 2 half-lives, the activity is $A_0/4$**.

**Part (c) — Mean life:**

$$ \tau = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{0.1155} = 8.66 \, \text{hours} $$

Or equivalently:

$$ \tau = 1.443 \times T_{1/2} = 1.443 \times 6 = 8.66 \, \text{hours} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters clinically:** The physician must ensure the patient has **enough activity left** at the actual *imaging time* (which may be 2–4 hours after injection). If the half-life is too short, the radioisotope decays before imaging; if too long, the patient receives excessive radiation dose. The **6-hour half-life of Tc-99m** is clinically optimal.

### 3.4 Comparison Matrix of Common Diagnostic Radioisotopes

| Isotope | Half-life | Decay Mode | Photon Energy | Clinical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tc-99m** | **6.01 hours** | IT (isomeric transition) | **140 keV γ** | Bone, heart, brain, thyroid, lung |
| **I-123** | 13.2 hours | EC (electron capture) | 159 keV γ | Thyroid, brain |
| **I-131** | 8.02 days | β⁻ + γ | 364 keV γ | Thyroid (therapy + diagnosis) |
| **F-18 (FDG)** | 109.8 minutes | β⁺ (positron) | 511 keV (annihilation) | PET — cancer, brain |
| **Tl-201** | 73 hours | EC | 70–80 keV X-ray | Myocardial perfusion |
| **Ga-67** | 78 hours | EC | 93, 185, 296 keV γ | Infection, lymphoma |
| **In-111** | 67.3 hours | EC | 171, 245 keV γ | WBC, neuroendocrine tumors |
| **Xe-133** | 5.25 days | β⁻ + γ | 81 keV | Lung ventilation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Radioisotope Diagnostic Imaging Workflow

The complete diagnostic chain from isotope production to final image is shown below. Note: All node IDs are alphanumeric; no reserved Mermaid keywords are used.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reactor or Cyclotron] --> B[Production of Parent Isotope Mo-99]
    B --> C[Tc-99m Generator Elution]
    C --> D[Radiopharmaceutical Labelling with Tc-99m]
    D --> E[Quality Control Sterility Pyrogen]
    E --> F[Patient Injection IV Route]
    F --> G[In Vivo Uptake by Target Organ]
    G --> H[Emission of 140 keV Gamma Photons]
    H --> I[Gamma Camera Collimator]
    I --> J[NaI Scintillation Crystal]
    J --> K[Photomultiplier Tube Array]
    K --> L[Pulse Height Analyser]
    L --> M[Computer Image Reconstruction]
    M --> N[Display on Monitor for Diagnosis]

    style A fill:#FFD580
    style C fill:#FF9999
    style F fill:#99CCFF
    style H fill:#CCFF99
    style N fill:#FFCCFF
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — SPECT vs PET Imaging Chains

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPECT[SPECT System]
        S1[Tc-99m Source 140 keV] --> S2[Parallel Hole Collimator]
        S2 --> S3[NaI Crystal Detector]
        S3 --> S4[PMT and PHA]
        S4 --> S5[2D Projection Image]
        S5 --> S6[Tomographic Reconstruction Filtered Back Projection]
    end

    subgraph PET[PET System]
        P1[F-18 FDG Source] --> P2[Patient Tissue]
        P2 --> P3[Positron Emission and Annihilation 511 keV pair]
        P3 --> P4[Ring of Detectors Coincidence Detection]
        P4 --> P5[Electronic Collimation]
        P5 --> P6[3D Image Reconstruction]
    end
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Mo-99 / Tc-99m Generator (Cow)

The "Moly Cow" is the shielded apparatus used in hospitals to extract Tc-99m on demand from its parent Mo-99.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Parent Mo-99 Half-life 66 hours] -->|Loaded on alumina column| X[Shielded Generator Column]
    X -->|Tc-99m grows in via decay| Y[Saline Elution]
    Y --> Z[Collection Vial with NaTcO4]
    Z --> W[Labelling with Pharmaceutical Kit]
    W --> V[Patient Dose]

    style M fill:#FFE4B5
    style X fill:#FFA07A
    style V fill:#90EE90
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a generator?** Mo-99 has a longer half-life (66 hr) and continuously produces Tc-99m daughter nuclei. The hospital elutes (milks) fresh Tc-99m every few hours, avoiding the need for an on-site cyclotron. This is why Tc-99m is the most widely used medical radioisotope globally.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model] (3 Marks)**
*Define the term "radioisotope" and list **three** desirable properties of a radioisotope used in medical diagnosis.*

**Model Answer:**

A **radioisotope** is an atom whose nucleus is unstable and spontaneously emits ionizing radiation (γ-photons, β-particles, or positrons) to reach a stable state.

**Three desirable properties for medical diagnosis:**

1. **Appropriate half-life** — long enough to complete the imaging procedure (typically a few hours), but short enough to minimise patient radiation dose (e.g., Tc-99m with 6 hours).
2. **Pure gamma emitter** with **suitable photon energy (100–300 keV)** — high enough to escape the body, low enough to be efficiently collimated and detected (e.g., Tc-99m at 140 keV).
3. **High specific activity and chemical versatility** — so it can be tagged to a wide range of pharmaceutical molecules targeting different organs (e.g., Tc-99m forms complexes with MDP, DTPA, HMPAO, sestamibi, etc.).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] (3 Marks)**
*What is the significance of the **half-life** of a radioisotope in diagnostic nuclear medicine? Give one example.*

**Model Answer:**

The **half-life ($T_{1/2}$)** is the time required for the activity of a radioisotope to reduce to **half of its initial value**. It governs two competing clinical requirements:

- **Long enough** to allow pharmaceutical preparation, patient uptake, and image acquisition.
- **Short enough** to limit the radiation absorbed dose to the patient.

**Mathematical relation:**

$$ T_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda} $$

**Example:** Technetium-99m has $T_{1/2} = 6$ hours, which is considered ideal — it provides sufficient time for imaging while keeping patient dose low. Compare this with F-18 (110 min) used in PET, or I-131 (8 days) which is mainly therapeutic.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Question, 14 Marks]**

> **A(a)** Derive the radioactive decay law $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ and obtain the relationship between decay constant and half-life. (7 Marks)
>
> **A(b)** A patient is injected with **400 MBq** of Tc-99m for a bone scan. If the imaging begins **3 hours** after injection, calculate the activity at the time of imaging. Given $T_{1/2}$ of Tc-99m = **6 hours**. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for A(a):

**Step 1 — Define decay rate:** The rate of radioactive decay of a sample is proportional to the number of undecayed nuclei $N$ present at that instant.

$$ \frac{dN}{dt} \propto -N $$

**Step 2 — Introduce decay constant** $\lambda$:

$$ \frac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N $$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate** from $N_0$ at $t=0$ to $N$ at time $t$:

$$ \int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \int_0^t dt $$

$$ \ln N - \ln N_0 = -\lambda t $$

$$ N = N_0 e^{-\lambda t} $$

**Step 4 — Half-life derivation:** Set $N = N_0 / 2$ at $t = T_{1/2}$:

$$ \frac{N_0}{2} = N_0 e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}} \implies \frac{1}{2} = e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}} $$

$$ \ln(1/2) = -\lambda T_{1/2} \implies -0.693 = -\lambda T_{1/2} $$

$$ \boxed{T_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}} $$

#### Model Solution for A(b):

**Step 1 — Calculate decay constant:**

$$ \lambda = \frac{0.693}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{6} = 0.1155 \, \text{hr}^{-1} $$

**Step 2 — Apply decay equation at $t = 3$ hours:**

$$ A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t} $$

$$ A(3) = 400 \times e^{-(0.1155)(3)} $$

$$ A(3) = 400 \times e^{-0.3465} $$

$$ A(3) = 400 \times 0.707 $$

$$ \boxed{A(3) \approx 282.8 \, \text{MBq}} $$

**Verification:** Since 3 hours = **half of one half-life**, the activity should be between $A_0/2$ (200 MBq) and $A_0$ (400 MBq). 282.8 MBq is correctly in this range. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Question, 14 Marks]**

> **B(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the working of a **Gamma Camera** used in radioisotope imaging. (7 Marks)
>
> **B(b)** Compare **SPECT** and **PET** imaging systems in terms of source, detection principle, and clinical use. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for B(a):

A **Gamma Camera** (Anger Camera) is the standard instrument for imaging γ-emitting radioisotopes such as Tc-99m. Its major subsystems are:

1. **Collimator** — A lead plate with thousands of parallel holes. It accepts only γ-photons travelling **perpendicular** to the detector face, performing **geometric collimation**.
2. **Scintillation Crystal** — A large **NaI(Tl) crystal** (~40 cm diameter, ~1 cm thick). Each γ-photon produces a flash of visible light (~3000 photons per 140 keV γ).
3. **Photomultiplier Tube (PMT) Array** — An array of ~50–100 PMTs behind the crystal detects and amplifies the light flash into an electrical pulse.
4. **Pulse Height Analyser (PHA)** — Selects only pulses of the correct energy (e.g., 140 keV for Tc-99m) and rejects scatter and noise.
5. **Position Computer & Display** — Computes the X, Y coordinates of each detected photon from the relative PMT signals, and builds a 2D image of the radioisotope distribution.

**Block diagram (refer Section 4.1):** Collimator → NaI Crystal → PMT Array → PHA → Computer → Display.

#### Model Solution for B(b):

| Feature | **SPECT** | **PET** |
|---|---|---|
| **Radioisotope** | Single γ-emitter (Tc-99m, I-123, Tl-201) | Positron emitter (F-18, C-11, N-13, O-15) |
| **Emission** | 140 keV (Tc-99m) single γ-photon | 511 keV annihilation photon **pair** (back-to-back, 180°) |
| **Collimation** | **Physical (mechanical)** lead collimator — wastes ~90% of photons | **Electronic (coincidence)** — no physical collimator; higher sensitivity |
| **Detection** | Single NaI crystal + PMT | Ring of fast scintillators (BGO, LSO, LYSO) |
| **Spatial Resolution** | ~8–10 mm | ~4–6 mm (better) |
| **Image** | 2D projections → 3D via filtered back-projection | True 3D, quantitative |
| **Clinical Use** | Bone, thyroid, myocardial perfusion | Oncology (cancer staging), brain metabolism, cardiac viability |
| **Tracer Example** | Tc-99m MDP (bone), Tc-99m sestamibi (heart) | F-18 FDG (glucose metabolism in tumors) |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher (requires cyclotron for short-lived isotopes) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert half-life to λ first** before substituting into the exponential. Always compute $\lambda = 0.693/T_{1/2}$ as a separate line.
> 2. **Mixing up $A$ and $N$** — Activity is *not* the number of nuclei; it is the *rate of decay* ($\lambda N$).
> 3. **Unit inconsistency** — If $T_{1/2}$ is in hours, then $\lambda$ is in hr⁻¹ and $t$ must also be in hours. Do not mix seconds and hours.
> 4. **In gamma camera diagrams**, examiners specifically look for the **collimator** — students frequently omit it. Always show: Collimator → NaI(Tl) Crystal → PMT → PHA → Display.
> 5. **In SPECT vs PET**, do *not* write that PET uses γ-emitters — it uses **positron (β⁺) emitters** that produce 511 keV annihilation photons. This is a 2-mark blunder.
> 6. **No negative sign in $A(t)$** — activity is a magnitude; it is always written as $A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ with the negative inside the exponent.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Radioisotope** = unstable nucleus emitting γ or β radiation; *diagnostic* applications prefer **pure γ-emitters** or **β⁺ emitters**.
- [x] **Tc-99m** is the workhorse of nuclear medicine (80% of all scans) — **140 keV γ**, **6-hour half-life**.
- [x] **Decay law:** $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ and $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$.
- [x] **Half-life:** $T_{1/2} = 0.693 / \lambda$.
- [x] **Mean life:** $\tau = 1/\lambda = 1.443 \, T_{1/2}$.
