- [x] **Activity unit (SI):** Becquerel (Bq) = 1 disintegration/sec. **1 Ci = 3.7 × 10¹⁰ Bq**.
- [x] **PET uses positron emitters (F-18, C-11, N-13, O-15)**, produces **511 keV annihilation photon pairs** detected in **coincidence** — no physical collimator.
- [x] **SPECT uses γ-emitters (Tc-99m, I-123, Tl-201)** with a **physical lead collimator** and a gamma camera.
- [x] **Mo-99/Tc-99m generator** (the "Moly Cow") provides Tc-99m on demand by saline elution.
- [x] **Gamma Camera** components: Collimator → NaI(Tl) crystal → PMT array → PHA → Position computer → Display.
- [x] **Common clinical uses:** Bone scan (Tc-99m MDP), Thyroid (I-123/I-131), Myocardial perfusion (Tl-201/Tc-99m sestamibi), Tumor imaging (F-18 FDG-PET).
- [x] **Always check units** — match $T_{1/2}$, $\lambda$, and $t$ in the same time unit (s, min, hr, or day).
- [x] **Pure β-emitters (no γ)** are **not** suitable for imaging — they are used in **therapy** (e.g., I-131 β⁻ for hyperthyroidism).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interaction of Nuclear Particles with Matter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interaction of Nuclear Particles with Matter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **interaction of nuclear particles with matter** is the fundamental physical process by which subatomic and atomic particles (such as alpha particles, beta particles, gamma photons, neutrons, protons, and positrons) transfer their kinetic energy to the atoms and molecules of an absorbing medium. This energy transfer occurs through discrete, quantized collision mechanisms governed by the **electromagnetic force**, the **weak nuclear force**, and the **strong nuclear force**, depending on the nature of the incident particle.

In medical imaging, this discipline is critical because every diagnostic modality — from planar X-ray radiography and Computed Tomography (CT) to Single Photon Emission Computed Tomography (SPECT) and Positron Emission Tomography (PET) — relies on precisely understanding how the imaging photons or emitted particles deposit energy in, or escape from, biological tissue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The interaction mechanisms form the **physical foundation** of radiation dosimetry, image contrast formation, and detector design. Every imaging equation (Hounsfield Units, attenuation coefficients, scatter correction) ultimately traces back to these interaction cross-sections.

The four principal classes of interactions considered in this module are:

1. **Interaction of Heavy Charged Particles** (alpha particles, protons, heavy ions) — dominated by **Coulomb scattering** with orbital electrons and nuclei.
2. **Interaction of Light Charged Particles** (beta particles, that is, electrons and positrons) — featuring **ionization, radiative (bremsstrahlung) losses, and annihilation**.
3. **Interaction of Uncharged Photons** (X-rays and gamma rays) — governed by the **photoelectric effect, Compton scattering, and pair/triplet production**.
4. **Interaction of Uncharged Heavy Particles** (neutrons) — involving **elastic scattering, inelastic scattering, and neutron capture**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bowling Ball and the Pin Field:**

Imagine a bowling ball (the nuclear particle) rolling through a forest of pins (atoms of the absorbing medium).

- A **heavy bowling ball** (alpha particle) is massive and slow. Each pin it strikes is knocked over completely, but the ball barely deflects. It ploughs in a **straight line** and stops abruptly after a fixed number of collisions. This is why alpha particles have a **short, well-defined range**.
- A **tennis ball** (beta particle / electron) is light and fast. It ricochets off pins in **erratic zig-zag paths** and can also push them aside. It travels much further than an alpha particle but with a **diffuse, scattered trajectory**.
- A **beam of light** (X-ray / gamma photon) does not collide with pins in the same way; it is either **absorbed entirely** (pin removed, photoelectric effect), **deflected** (pin knocked sideways, Compton scattering), or **transformed into matter** (pair production near a heavy nucleus).
- A **ghost** (neutron) is electrically neutral and ignores the pin forest entirely, passing straight through unless it happens to bump into a heavy iron pin (hydrogenous material), where it bounces off elastically.

This intuitive picture captures the essence of **range**, **stopping power**, **scattering**, and **energy deposition** — the four pillars of nuclear particle interaction.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

The following values are used throughout this module and are explicitly **highlighted for KTU numerical problems**:

- **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \ \text{mol}^{-1}$
- **Electron Rest Mass Energy:** $m_e c^2 = 0.511 \ \text{MeV}$
- **Proton Rest Mass Energy:** $m_p c^2 = 938.27 \ \text{MeV}$
- **Classical Electron Radius:** $r_e = 2.818 \times 10^{-15} \ \text{m}$
- **Bohr Radius:** $a_0 = 5.29 \times 10^{-11} \ \text{m}$
- **Speed of Light in Vacuum:** $c = 2.998 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$
- **Planck's Constant (reduced):** $\hbar = 1.055 \times 10^{-34} \ \text{J \cdot s}$
- **Fine Structure Constant:** $\alpha_{fs} \approx 1/137$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** These constants underpin every radiation detection calculation in nuclear medicine, including **gamma camera efficiency**, **PET coincidence timing resolution**, and **CT number (HU) calibration**.

### 1.4 The Three Master Variables of Particle Interaction

Every interaction in this module is described by three fundamental quantities that the student must internalize:

- **Stopping Power ($S$):** The rate of energy loss per unit path length, $S = -dE/dx$, measured in **MeV/cm** or **keV/$\mu$m**.
- **Range ($R$):** The total path length a particle travels before coming to rest, $R = \int_0^{E_0} (-dE/dx)^{-1} \, dE$.
- **Linear Energy Transfer (LET):** The energy deposited locally per unit path length, $L_\Delta = (dE/dx)_\Delta$, crucial for radiobiological effectiveness.

These three quantities are the **linga franca** of medical radiation physics and will reappear in every subsequent module on detectors, dosimetry, and image reconstruction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Nuclear Particles by Interaction Mechanism

Nuclear particles are categorized into **four physical families**, each exhibiting a distinct interaction signature when traversing matter:

#### 2.1.1 Heavy Charged Particles (Alpha Particles, Protons, Heavy Ions)

- **Charge State:** $Z e \geq +1e$ (typically $+2e$ for alphas).
- **Dominant Interaction:** **Inelastic Coulomb scattering** with atomic electrons (causing excitation and ionization).
- **Secondary Interaction:** Elastic Coulomb scattering with the atomic nucleus (Rutherford scattering, but most energetic encounters are screened).
- **Mass Dominance:** Particle mass $M \gg m_e$ (electron mass), so the particle is not significantly deflected in single collisions.
- **Trajectory:** Essentially **straight line** with a well-defined, finite range.
- **Energy Loss Mode:** Almost exclusively via **collision losses (ionization/excitation)**. Bremsstrahlung is negligible because it scales as $Z^2/M^2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for Imaging:** Although alpha particles are not used in diagnostic imaging (they are stopped by a sheet of paper), the principles governing their stopping power (Bethe-Bloch formula) are used directly to model **proton therapy beams** and to understand **cosmic-ray shielding** in aircraft electronics and spacecraft.

#### 2.1.2 Light Charged Particles (Beta Particles: Electrons and Positrons)

- **Charge State:** $\pm e$.
- **Dominant Interactions:**
  - **Collisional losses** (ionizing atomic electrons).
  - **Radiative losses (bremsstrahlung)** in the Coulomb field of nuclei.
  - **Annihilation** (positrons only): $e^+ + e^- \rightarrow 2\gamma$ (each $\gamma$ of **0.511 MeV**).
- **Trajectory:** **Highly tortuous**, with large-angle deflections per collision because the particle mass equals the target electron mass.
- **Range:** **Straggled and diffused** — described by a continuous-slowing-down-approximation (CSDA) range with significant statistical spread.

> [!NOTE]
> **Imaging Relevance:** Beta particles are the therapeutic or diagnostic emission from radioisotopes such as **$^{32}\text{P}$**, **$^{90}\text{Y}$** (used in radioembolization), and the positrons emitted by **$^{18}\text{F}$** (FDG-PET).

#### 2.1.3 Uncharged Electromagnetic Photons (X-rays and Gamma Rays)

Photons have **zero rest mass** and **zero charge**, and therefore do not lose energy continuously like charged particles. Instead, they interact in **discrete, probabilistic events**:

- **Photoelectric Effect:** Photon absorbed, electron ejected, characteristic X-ray or Auger electron may follow.
- **Compton Scattering (Incoherent Scattering):** Photon scatters off a quasi-free outer-shell electron.
- **Pair Production:** Photon converts into an electron-positron pair in the Coulomb field of a nucleus (threshold $E_\gamma \geq 1.022 \ \text{MeV} = 2 m_e c^2$).
- **Triplet Production:** Same as pair production but in the field of an orbital electron (threshold $E_\gamma \geq 2.044 \ \text{MeV}$).
- **Rayleigh (Coherent) Scattering:** Photon scatters off bound atomic electrons without energy loss (important at low energies < 30 keV).
- **Photodisintegration:** Photon triggers a nuclear reaction (only at very high energies, $> 10 \ \text{MeV}$, rarely relevant in medical imaging).

The total photon interaction probability is governed by the **linear attenuation coefficient** $\mu$ (in cm$^{-1}$), the cornerstone of CT and X-ray imaging.

#### 2.1.4 Uncharged Heavy Particles (Neutrons)

- **Charge State:** Zero — neutrons are invisible to the electromagnetic force.
- **Dominant Interactions:**
  - **Elastic Scattering (n, n):** Neutron bounces off a nucleus, transferring kinetic energy. Dominant in **hydrogen-rich materials** (water, tissue, paraffin).
  - **Inelastic Scattering (n, n'):** Neutron excites the target nucleus, which de-excites by emitting a gamma photon.
  - **Neutron Capture (n, $\gamma$):** Neutron is absorbed; compound nucleus emits gamma(s) (e.g., $^1\text{H}(n,\gamma)^2\text{H}$ in boron-neutron capture therapy).
  - **Charged Particle Emission (n, p), (n, $\alpha$):** e.g., $^{10}\text{B}(n,\alpha)^7\text{Li}$ in BNCT.
  - **Fission (n, f):** Only in heavy nuclei like $^{235}\text{U}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Medical Relevance:** Neutron interactions form the basis of **Boron Neutron Capture Therapy (BNCT)**, **neutron radiography** (industrial non-destructive testing of turbine blades and reactor fuel), and the **design of radiation shielding** around medical linear accelerators.

### 2.2 Mathematical Framework: Cross-Sections

The **interaction cross-section** $\sigma$ is the effective "target area" presented by an atom (or nucleus, or electron) to an incident particle. It has units of **m²** (or conveniently **barn**, where $1 \ \text{barn} = 10^{-28} \ \text{m}^2$).

The **macroscopic** counterpart is the **linear attenuation coefficient**:

$$\mu = n \cdot \sigma$$

where $n$ is the number density of target atoms (atoms per m³). Equivalently:

$$\mu = \rho \cdot \frac{N_A}{A} \cdot \sigma$$

where $\rho$ is the mass density (kg/m³) and $A$ is the atomic mass number (kg/mol).

The probability that a photon survives a distance $x$ without interaction is:

$$I(x) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$$

This is the **Beer-Lambert Law**, the single most important equation in transmission radiography and CT.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the comprehensive KTU 2024 high-yield formula sheet for this topic. Memorize every row.**

| **Phenomenon** | **Formula** | **Variable Definitions** | **Units / Notes** |
|----------------|-------------|------------------------|-------------------|
| Bethe-Bloch Stopping Power (heavy charged particles) | $-\frac{dE}{dx} = \frac{4 \pi z^2 e^4}{m_e v^2} \cdot n \cdot \left[ \ln \frac{2 m_e v^2}{I} - \ln(1 - \beta^2) - \beta^2 \right]$ | $z$ = charge of incident particle, $v$ = velocity, $n$ = electron density, $I$ = mean excitation energy ($\approx 10$ eV in water, $\approx 70$ eV in lead) | MeV/cm; valid for $0.05 \leq \beta \leq 0.5$ |
| Mass Stopping Power | $-\frac{1}{\rho} \frac{dE}{dx}$ | $\rho$ = material density | MeV·cm²/g |
| CSDA Range | $R = \int_0^{E_0} \left(-\frac{dE}{dx}\right)^{-1} dE$ | $E_0$ = initial kinetic energy | g/cm² or cm |
| Empirical Range of Beta Particles in g/cm² | $R_\beta \approx 0.412 \cdot E_\beta^{1.265 - 0.0954 \ln E_\beta}$ | $E_\beta$ in MeV, $0.01 \leq E_\beta \leq 2.5$ MeV | g/cm² (Feather's formula variant) |
| Bremsstrahlung Yield | $Y \approx \frac{6 \times 10^{-4} \cdot Z \cdot E}{m_e c^2}$ | $Z$ = atomic number of target, $E$ = electron kinetic energy | dimensionless fraction; for $E$ in MeV |
| Mass Attenuation Coefficient (photons) | $\frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{\mu_{photo}}{\rho} + \frac{\mu_{Compton}}{\rho} + \frac{\mu_{pair}}{\rho}$ | sum over all interaction channels | cm²/g |
| Linear Attenuation Coefficient (Beer-Lambert) | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | $I_0$ = incident intensity, $x$ = path length | dimensionless ratio |
| Half-Value Layer (HVL) | $x_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}$ | direct consequence of Beer-Lambert | cm |
| Mean Free Path | $\lambda = \frac{1}{\mu}$ | average distance between interactions | cm |
| Photoelectric Cross-Section (K-shell) | $_\text{photo}\sigma_K \propto \frac{Z^{4.5}}{E_\gamma^3}$ | strongly $Z$-dependent | dominant at low $E$, high $Z$ |
| Compton Cross-Section (Klein-Nishina) | $\frac{d\sigma}{d\Omega} = \frac{r_e^2}{2} \left( \frac{E'}{E} \right)^2 \left( \frac{E'}{E} + \frac{E}{E'} - \sin^2 \theta \right)$ | $E'$ = scattered photon energy, $\theta$ = scatter angle | m²/sr |
| Compton Energy Shift | $E' = \frac{E}{1 + \frac{E}{m_e c^2}(1 - \cos \theta)}$ | $E$ = incident photon energy | MeV |
| Pair Production Threshold | $E_{th} = 2 m_e c^2 = 1.022$ MeV | kinematic threshold | MeV |
| Pair Production Cross-Section | $_\text{pair}\sigma \propto Z^2 \cdot \ln(2E)$ | increases with $E$ and $Z$ | dominant at high $E$, high $Z$ |
| Total Mass Attenuation (mixtures) | $\frac{\mu}{\rho} = \sum_i w_i \left(\frac{\mu}{\rho}\right)_i$ | $w_i$ = weight fraction of element $i$ | cm²/g |
| Electron Density (rule of thumb) | $n_e = \rho \cdot N_A \cdot \frac{Z}{A} \approx \rho \cdot N_A \cdot 0.5$ | for low-$Z$ biological tissue $Z/A \approx 0.5$ | electrons/m³ |
| Specific Ionization | $S_i = \frac{1}{W} \cdot \frac{dE}{dx}$ | $W$ = average energy per ion pair ($\approx 33.97$ eV in air) | ion pairs per cm |
| LET (Linear Energy Transfer) | $L_\Delta = \left( \frac{dE}{dx} \right)_\Delta$ | energy deposited locally with energy transfers $\leq \Delta$ | keV/$\mu$m |
| Stopping Number ($B$) | $B = \ln \frac{2 m_e v^2}{I}$ | appears in Bethe-Bloch | dimensionless |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Diagnostic Radiology (X-ray, CT):** The **photoelectric effect** provides the primary image contrast because $\sigma_{photo} \propto Z^{4.5}$. This is why **iodine ($Z = 53$)** and **barium ($Z = 56$)** are used as contrast agents, and why bone ($Z_{eff} \approx 13.8$) appears bright versus soft tissue ($Z_{eff} \approx 7.4$).
- **Nuclear Medicine (SPECT):** **Gamma photons** (typically 70–365 keV) interact via **photoelectric and Compton** channels. The Compton continuum is a noise source that must be subtracted using energy-window scatter correction.
- **PET Imaging:** The 511 keV annihilation photons undergo primarily **Compton scattering** in tissue. The probability of a photoelectric interaction at this energy is low, which is why PET requires high-density scintillators (BGO, LSO, LYSO) with $Z_{eff} > 60$ for efficient detection.
- **Radiation Therapy:** Stopping power governs **treatment planning** for proton and carbon-ion therapy. The **Bragg peak** — the sharp maximum of energy deposition at the end of the particle range — is exploited to concentrate dose in tumors while sparing surrounding tissue.
- **Radiation Shielding Design:** For a therapy linac producing 6–25 MV X-rays, the dominant interaction shifts from Compton (at diagnostic energies) to **pair production** at high energies, requiring **high-$Z$ lead or tungsten** shielding and dedicated **maze** designs for neutron containment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Convention:** All multi-line mathematical developments are wrapped inside `\begin{aligned}...\end{aligned}` environments, with one explanatory text row following every algebraic transition.

### 3.1 Derivation: Stopping Power of Heavy Charged Particles (Bethe-Bloch Formula)

We derive the most fundamental equation in charged-particle dosimetry: the **Bethe-Bloch formula** for the rate of energy loss of a heavy charged particle in an absorber.

#### Step 1: Setup the Geometry of a Single Collision

Consider a heavy particle of mass $M$, charge $z e$, and velocity $v$ passing at impact parameter $b$ past a stationary atomic electron of mass $m_e$ at rest. The transverse Coulomb force during the brief interaction is:

$$F_\perp(t) = \frac{z e^2}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 (b^2 + v^2 t^2)} \cdot \frac{b}{\sqrt{b^2 + v^2 t^2}}$$

The momentum transferred to the electron perpendicular to the particle's direction is the time integral of the transverse force:

$$p_\perp = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} F_\perp(t) \, dt$$

#### Step 2: Perform the Time Integration

Substituting the force expression:

$$p_\perp = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{z e^2 \, b}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 (b^2 + v^2 t^2)^{3/2}} \, dt$$

Let $u = v t / b$, so $du = v \, dt / b$, hence $dt = b \, du / v$. The integral becomes:

$$p_\perp = \frac{z e^2}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{1}{b v} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{du}{(1 + u^2)^{3/2}}$$

The integral evaluates to the well-known value $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} (1+u^2)^{-3/2} du = 2$. Therefore:

$$p_\perp = \frac{z e^2}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2}{b v} = \frac{z e^2}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 b v}$$

> **Logic Note:** The momentum transfer is inversely proportional to the impact parameter — a close encounter transfers more momentum than a distant one.

#### Step 3: Relate Momentum Transfer to Energy Transfer

For an electron initially at rest, the kinetic energy acquired in a single collision is, in the non-relativistic limit:

$$\Delta E(b) = \frac{p_\perp^2}{2 m_e} = \frac{1}{2 m_e} \left( \frac{z e^2}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 b v} \right)^2 = \frac{z^2 e^4}{8 \pi^2 \epsilon_0^2 m_e b^2 v^2}$$

> **Logic Note:** This is the energy lost by the heavy particle, which equals the energy gained by the electron (energy conservation in a single binary collision).

#### Step 4: Sum Over All Electrons in a Cylindrical Shell

The particle traverses a thin slab of thickness $dx$. The number of electrons in a cylindrical shell of radius $b$, thickness $db$, and length $dx$ is:

$$dN = n_e \cdot 2 \pi b \, db \, dx$$

where $n_e$ is the number density of electrons. The energy lost in this shell is:

$$d^2 E = \Delta E(b) \cdot dN = \frac{z^2 e^4}{8 \pi^2 \epsilon_0^2 m_e b^2 v^2} \cdot n_e \cdot 2 \pi b \, db \, dx$$

Simplifying:

$$d^2 E = \frac{z^2 e^4 n_e}{4 \pi \epsilon_0^2 m_e v^2} \cdot \frac{db}{b} \cdot dx$$

#### Step 5: Integrate Over All Allowed Impact Parameters

The total energy loss per unit path length is obtained by integrating $b$ from a minimum $b_{min}$ to a maximum $b_{max}$:

$$-\frac{dE}{dx} = \frac{z^2 e^4 n_e}{4 \pi \epsilon_0^2 m_e v^2} \int_{b_{min}}^{b_{max}} \frac{db}{b} = \frac{z^2 e^4 n_e}{4 \pi \epsilon_0^2 m_e v^2} \ln \frac{b_{max}}{b_{min}}$$

> **Logic Note:** The logarithmic dependence on the impact parameter bounds is the hallmark of the Coulomb interaction.

#### Step 6: Establish the Cutoffs

- **$b_{min}$:** Set by the maximum kinematically allowed energy transfer, $T_{max}$:

$$T_{max} = \frac{2 m_e c^2 \beta^2 \gamma^2}{1 + 2 \gamma m_e / M + (m_e / M)^2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad b_{min} = \frac{z e^2}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 v \sqrt{2 m_e T_{max}}}$$

- **$b_{max}$:** Set by the **adiabatic cutoff** — the maximum impact parameter for which the interaction is fast enough to excite the atom:

$$b_{max} = \frac{v}{\nu_{atom}} = \frac{\hbar v}{I}$$

where $I$ is the mean excitation energy of the atom (Bethe's $I$-value).

#### Step 7: Assemble the Final Non-Relativistic Bethe Formula

Substituting the cutoffs and collecting terms (in SI units, $4 \pi \epsilon_0$ factors handled by $e^2/(4 \pi \epsilon_0) = k_e e^2$):

$$-\frac{dE}{dx} = \frac{4 \pi z^2 e^4}{m_e v^2} \cdot n_e \cdot \ln \frac{2 m_e v^2}{I}$$

The **relativistic Bethe-Bloch** form, including the density-effect correction $\delta$ and shell correction $C$:

$$
-\frac{dE}{dx} = \frac{4 \pi z^2 e^4}{m_e v^2} \cdot n_e \cdot \left[ \ln \frac{2 m_e c^2 \beta^2 \gamma^2 \cdot T_{max}}{I^2} - 2 \beta^2 - \delta - 2 \frac{C}{Z} \right]
$$

> **Logic Note:** The $\beta^2 \gamma^2$ term in the logarithm causes the stopping power to reach a **minimum** at $\beta \gamma \approx 3$ (the "minimum ionizing particle" regime), then rises slowly as $\ln(\beta^2 \gamma^2)$. The density correction $\delta$ suppresses this rise in dense media.

### 3.2 Derivation: Compton Scattered Photon Energy

We now derive the energy of a photon after Compton scattering, the cornerstone of nuclear-medicine scatter correction.

#### Step 1: Write the Energy-Momentum Conservation

Let a photon of energy $E$ and momentum $E/c$ strike a stationary electron of rest mass $m_e$. After scattering, the photon has energy $E'$ and momentum $E'/c$ at angle $\theta$ to the original direction, while the recoil electron has energy $T_e = E - E' + m_e c^2$.

Conservation of energy:

$$E + m_e c^2 = E' + T_e + m_e c^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_e = E - E'$$

Conservation of momentum (components parallel and perpendicular to the original photon direction):

$$\frac{E}{c} = \frac{E'}{c} \cos \theta + p_e \cos \phi$$

$$\frac{E'}{c} \sin \theta = p_e \sin \phi$$

#### Step 2: Use the Relativistic Energy-Momentum Relation for the Electron

$$(T_e + m_e c^2)^2 = (p_e c)^2 + (m_e c^2)^2$$

#### Step 3: Eliminate $p_e$ and Solve for $E'$

Squaring and adding the momentum equations:

$$p_e^2 c^2 = E^2 + E'^2 - 2 E E' \cos \theta$$

Substituting into the relativistic relation:

$$(E - E' + m_e c^2)^2 = E^2 + E'^2 - 2 E E' \cos \theta + m_e^2 c^4$$

Expanding the left side:

$$E^2 + E'^2 + m_e^2 c^4 - 2 E E' + 2 E m_e c^2 - 2 E' m_e c^2 = E^2 + E'^2 - 2 E E' \cos \theta + m_e^2 c^4$$

#### Step 4: Cancel and Rearrange

Cancelling $E^2$, $E'^2$, and $m_e^2 c^4$ from both sides:

$$- 2 E E' + 2 E m_e c^2 - 2 E' m_e c^2 = - 2 E E' \cos \theta$$

Divide by $2 E E'$:

$$-1 + \frac{m_e c^2}{E'} - \frac{m_e c^2}{E} = -\cos \theta$$

Rearrange to isolate $E'$:

$$\frac{m_e c^2}{E'} = 1 + \frac{m_e c^2}{E} - \cos \theta = 1 + \frac{m_e c^2}{E}(1 - \cos \theta)$$

#### Step 5: Final Compton Scattering Formula

$$\boxed{\; E' = \frac{E}{1 + \frac{E}{m_e c^2}(1 - \cos \theta)} \;}$$

> **Logic Note:** The maximum energy loss occurs at $\theta = \pi$ (backscatter), where the scattered photon energy is $E'_{min} = E / (1 + 2E / m_e c^2)$. At $E = 511$ keV, this gives $E'_{min} = 170.3$ keV, which is the basis for PET scatter-window correction.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

> **Problem:** A 5 MeV alpha particle traverses water ($\rho = 1 \ \text{g/cm}^3$). Estimate the **CSDA range** assuming a constant mass stopping power of $2 \ \text{MeV \cdot cm^2 / g}$.

**Solution:**

The CSDA range in g/cm² is:

$$R_{CSDA} = \int_0^{E_0} \left(-\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{dE}{dx}\right)^{-1} dE$$

Since the mass stopping power is given as constant at $S_{mass} = 2 \ \text{MeV \cdot cm^2/g}$:

$$R_{CSDA} = \int_0^{5 \ \text{MeV}} \frac{dE}{2 \ \text{MeV \cdot cm^2/g}} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5 \ \text{mg/cm}^2$$

Convert to linear range in water:

$$R = \frac{R_{CSDA}}{\rho} = \frac{2.5 \ \text{mg/cm}^2}{1 \ \text{g/cm}^3} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{g/cm}^2}{1 \ \text{g/cm}^3} = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{cm} = 25 \ \ \mu\text{m}$$

> **Logic Note:** This illustrates why alpha particles cannot penetrate the stratum corneum of the skin — a critical safety fact for nuclear medicine departments handling alpha-emitting radioisotopes.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Photon Interaction Regime Map

The dominant photon interaction channel depends on photon energy and absorber atomic number $Z$. The following **decision matrix** is invaluable for KTU problems:

| **Photon Energy Range** | **Low Z (water, tissue, $Z \leq 8$)** | **Medium Z (bone, contrast media, $20 \leq Z \leq 50$)** | **High Z (lead, tungsten, $Z \geq 70$)** |
|------------------------|--------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|
| < 30 keV | Photoelectric (rising) | **Photoelectric dominant** | **Photoelectric overwhelming** |
| 30 – 100 keV | **Compton dominant** | Photoelectric + Compton comparable | Photoelectric dominant |
| 100 keV – 1 MeV (typical SPECT) | **Compton dominant** | Compton dominant | Photoelectric + pair production beginning |
| 1 – 5 MeV (linac range) | Compton + pair onset | Compton + pair production | **Pair production** + Compton |
| > 5 MeV | Pair production | **Pair production dominant** | Pair production dominant |

> **Logic Note:** A diagnostic chest X-ray (60–120 kVp, $E_\gamma \sim 30$–$80$ keV) operates in a regime where **Compton scattering** dominates in soft tissue but the **photoelectric effect** in bone (higher $Z_{eff}$) provides the contrast — the foundational reason bones appear white on radiographs.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting the $\ln 2$ in HVL:** Many students write $x_{1/2} = 1/\mu$ instead of $x_{1/2} = 0.693/\mu$. This is a guaranteed 0.5–1 mark deduction.
> - **Mixing up mass vs linear coefficients:** In CT, the Hounsfield Unit formula uses the **linear** coefficient $\mu$ relative to water, not $\mu/\rho$.
> - **Skipping units in numerical answers:** KTU examiners specifically deduct marks if stopping power is given without **MeV/cm** or **keV/$\mu$m**.
> - **Compton angle dependence:** Forgetting that the **maximum energy transfer** to the electron corresponds to **backscatter ($\theta = \pi$)** is a common conceptual error.

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Distinguish clearly between **specific ionization** and **linear energy transfer (LET)**. Why are both quantities essential in characterizing the radiobiological effectiveness of a radiation beam?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Specific Ionization** is defined as the number of ion pairs produced per unit path length of the charged particle in the medium, typically expressed in **ion pairs per cm** (in air, approximately $S_i = (1/W)(-dE/dx)$ with $W = 33.97$ eV).

**Linear Energy Transfer (LET)** is the energy deposited locally per unit path length, restricted to energy transfers less than a cutoff $\Delta$, expressed in **keV/$\mu$m** (or MeV/cm).

Both are essential because:

- **Specific ionization** quantifies the *number* of ionization events, governing the **charge collected** in gas-filled detectors (ion chambers, GM counters).
- **LET** quantifies the *spatial density* of energy deposition, governing the **biological damage** per unit track length. A high-LET radiation (alpha, neutron) deposits energy in a small volume, producing clustered DNA damage that is harder for cells to repair, yielding a higher **Relative Biological Effectiveness (RBE)** than low-LET radiation (beta, X-ray).

> **Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for definition of each term + 1 mark for the radiobiological distinction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Explain why the **photoelectric effect** dominates image contrast in diagnostic X-ray imaging and why **iodine ($Z = 53$)** is chosen as a contrast agent.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The probability of a photoelectric interaction in the K-shell is given by $_\text{photo}\sigma_K \propto Z^{4.5} / E_\gamma^3$. The strong **$Z^{4.5}$ dependence** means that even small differences in effective atomic number between adjacent tissues (e.g., $Z_{eff} = 7.4$ for soft tissue versus $Z_{eff} = 13.8$ for bone) produce a significant difference in attenuation, creating image contrast.

**Iodine** ($Z = 53$) is used as a contrast agent because:

1. Its K-edge lies at **33.2 keV**, which falls within the diagnostic X-ray energy range, providing an abrupt increase in photoelectric absorption just above this energy.
2. The high $Z$ produces a **large photoelectric cross-section**, dramatically increasing attenuation in blood vessels and iodine-avid tissues.
3. Iodine compounds are **biologically tolerable** and can be injected intravenously.

> **Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for $Z^{4.5}$ dependence + 1 mark for K-edge explanation + 1 mark for biocompatibility.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 Scheme, each Part B question offers an internal choice. Below, Question A and Question B are two independent alternatives.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Heavy Charged Particle Interactions [KTU University Exam – 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the **Bethe-Bloch stopping power formula** for a heavy charged particle, clearly stating all assumptions. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 4 MeV alpha particle enters air at NTP. Calculate (i) the velocity of the alpha particle, (ii) the classical stopping power assuming $I = 85$ eV, and (iii) the approximate range using the constant-stopping-power approximation with $S = 1.8 \ \text{MeV \cdot cm^2/g}$. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

The full derivation follows the seven steps in Section 3.1 of these notes. The key assumptions are:

1. The particle is heavy ($M \gg m_e$), so its trajectory is not significantly deflected in single collisions.
2. The interaction is non-relativistic (classical limit).
3. The electron is initially at rest and free (Born approximation).
4. Energy transfer in a single collision is small (perturbative).
5. The absorber is statistically isotropic.

The result is:

$$-\frac{dE}{dx} = \frac{4 \pi z^2 e^4}{m_e v^2} \cdot n_e \cdot \ln \frac{2 m_e v^2}{I}$$

> **Mark Allocation:** 1 mark for assumptions + 4 marks for derivation + 2 marks for assembling the final formula and discussing its $1/v^2$ dependence (the famous "rise" of stopping power as the particle slows down).

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Velocity of the 4 MeV alpha particle:**

The non-relativistic kinetic energy is $E = \tfrac{1}{2} M v^2$, where $M_\alpha \approx 4 \ \text{amu} = 4 \times 1.66 \times 10^{-27}$ kg:

$$M_\alpha = 6.64 \times 10^{-27} \ \text{kg}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2E}{M_\alpha}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-13} \ \text{J}}{6.64 \times 10^{-27} \ \text{kg}}}$$

Calculate the numerator: $2 \times 4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-13} = 1.28 \times 10^{-12}$ J.

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{1.28 \times 10^{-12}}{6.64 \times 10^{-27}}} = \sqrt{1.928 \times 10^{14}} = 1.388 \times 10^7 \ \text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 1.39 \times 10^7 \ \text{m/s} \approx 0.046 \, c}$$

**(ii) Classical Stopping Power:**

Electron density in air at NTP: $n_e = \rho N_A Z / A$. For air, $\rho = 1.293$ kg/m³, $Z/A \approx 0.499$:

$$n_e = 1.293 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 0.499 = 3.88 \times 10^{23} \ \text{electrons/m}^3$$

Substitute into Bethe's formula (using $e^2 / (4 \pi \epsilon_0) = 1.44$ eV·nm):

$$\frac{4 \pi (z e)^2 e^2}{m_e v^2} = \frac{4 \pi \times (2e)^2 \times 1.44 \ \text{eV·nm}}{m_e c^2} \cdot \frac{c^2}{v^2} \cdot \text{(unit conversion)}$$

Compute the stopping number:

$$\frac{2 m_e v^2}{I} = \frac{2 \times 0.511 \ \text{MeV} \times (0.046)^2}{85 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{MeV}} = \frac{0.0463 \times 10^{-1}}{85 \times 10^{-6}}$$

Let me recompute carefully: $0.0463 \times 0.1 = 4.63 \times 10^{-3}$ ... actually $(0.046)^2 = 2.116 \times 10^{-3}$, so $2 \times 0.511 \times 2.116 \times 10^{-3} = 2.16 \times 10^{-3}$ MeV, and $I = 8.5 \times 10^{-5}$ MeV. The ratio is $2.16 \times 10^{-3} / 8.5 \times 10^{-5} = 25.4$, so $\ln(25.4) = 3.235$.

In SI, $-dE/dx \approx 4 \pi \times 4 \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19})^2 \times 1.44 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times n_e / (m_e v^2)$ ... using the simplified form:

$$-\frac{dE}{dx} \approx 0.3071 \ \frac{\text{MeV}}{\text{g/cm}^2} \cdot \frac{z^2}{\beta^2} \cdot \frac{Z}{A} \cdot \ln \frac{2 m_e c^2 \beta^2}{I}$$

With $z = 2$, $\beta = 0.046$, $Z/A = 0.499$:

$$-\frac{dE}{dx} = 0.3071 \times \frac{4}{(0.046)^2} \times 0.499 \times 3.235$$

$(0.046)^2 = 2.116 \times 10^{-3}$, so $4 / 2.116 \times 10^{-3} = 1890$:

$$-\frac{dE}{dx} = 0.3071 \times 1890 \times 0.499 \times 3.235 \approx 0.3071 \times 1890 \times 1.614$$

$$-\frac{dE}{dx} \approx 0.3071 \times 3050.5 \approx 936 \ \text{keV·cm}^2/\text{g}$$

Convert to MeV/cm²/g: $\approx 0.94 \ \text{MeV \cdot cm^2/g}$ (close to the constant-stopping-power assumption).

**(iii) Approximate Range:**

$$R \approx \frac{E_0}{S_{mass}} = \frac{4 \ \text{MeV}}{1.8 \ \text{MeV \cdot cm}^2/\text{g}} = 2.22 \ \text{mg/cm}^2$$

In air at NTP, $R \approx 2.22 \ \text{mg/cm}^2 / (1.293 \ \text{mg/cm}^3) = 1.72 \ \text{cm}$.

> **Mark Allocation:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 3 marks, (iii) 2 marks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Photon Interactions [KTU University Exam – 2024 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of the **Klein-Nishina formula**, discuss the energy dependence of the Compton scattering cross-section in the low-energy (Thomson) and high-energy limits. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A 200 keV photon undergoes a Compton scatter at $\theta = 90°$ in soft tissue. Calculate (i) the scattered photon energy, (ii) the recoil electron energy, and (iii) the scattered photon angle if the recoil electron is observed at $30°$ from the original photon direction. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:**

The Klein-Nishina differential cross-section for Compton scattering is:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{d\Omega} = \frac{r_e^2}{2} \left( \frac{E'}{E} \right)^2 \left( \frac{E'}{E} + \frac{E}{E'} - \sin^2 \theta \right)$$

where $E' = E / [1 + (E/m_e c^2)(1 - \cos \theta)]$.

- **Low-energy limit ($E \ll m_e c^2$):** The argument of the logarithm is small, $E' \approx E$, and the cross-section reduces to the **Thomson formula**:

$$\frac{d\sigma}{d\Omega} \Big|_{Thomson} = \frac{r_e^2}{2} (1 + \cos^2 \theta)$$

The total Thomson cross-section is $\sigma_T = (8\pi/3) r_e^2 = 0.665$ barn, **independent of photon energy**.

- **High-energy limit ($E \gg m_e c^2$):** The scattered photon is strongly forward-peaked. The total cross-section falls as $\sigma_{KN} \propto \ln(2E) / E$, decreasing rapidly with energy. The forward-to-backward ratio of scattered photons increases dramatically.

> **Mark Allocation:** 2 marks for stating the formula + 2 marks for Thomson limit + 2 marks for high-energy limit + 1 mark for engineering relevance (e.g., Compton scatter dominates in CT at ~70 keV where Thomson limit is approximately valid).

**Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Scattered photon energy:**

$$E' = \frac{E}{1 + \frac{E}{m_e c^2}(1 - \cos \theta)} = \frac{200 \ \text{keV}}{1 + \frac{200}{511}(1 - 0)} = \frac{200}{1 + 0.3914}$$

$$E' = \frac{200}{1.3914} = 143.7 \ \text{keV}$$

**(ii) Recoil electron energy:**

$$T_e = E - E' = 200 - 143.7 = 56.3 \ \text{keV}$$

**(iii) Scattered photon angle given electron recoil at $\phi = 30°$:**

From momentum conservation, the photon and electron scatter in a plane. Apply energy-momentum conservation:

$$p_\gamma = E/c, \quad p_{\gamma'} = E'/c, \quad p_e c = \sqrt{T_e^2 + 2 T_e m_e c^2}$$

Momentum components:

$$E = E' \cos \theta + p_e c \cos \phi$$
$$E' \sin \theta = p_e c \sin \phi$$

Solve for $\theta$ using the second equation (the electron is at $30°$, so the photon is at $180° - 30° = 150°$ from the original direction, but we must check via energy conservation).

Compute $p_e c$:

$$p_e c = \sqrt{(56.3)^2 + 2 \times 56.3 \times 511} = \sqrt{3169 + 57539} = \sqrt{60708} = 246.4 \ \text{keV}$$

From $\sin \theta = (p_e c / E') \sin \phi$:

First, we need $E'$. Use energy conservation: $E = E' + T_e \Rightarrow E' = 200 - 56.3 = 143.7$ keV. Now:

$$\sin \theta = \frac{246.4}{143.7} \sin 30° = 1.715 \times 0.5 = 0.857$$

$$\theta = \arcsin(0.857) = 59.1°$$

$$\boxed{\theta \approx 59.1°}$$

> **Mark Allocation:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 2 marks, (iii) 3 marks.

---

### **TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER**

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4**

- **The Beer-Lambert Law** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ is the foundation of all transmission imaging. The **HVL** is $x_{1/2} = \ln 2 / \mu$.
- **Photoelectric effect** scales as $Z^{4.5} / E^3$ — it is the basis of **bone vs soft tissue contrast** in X-ray and of **iodine/barium contrast agents**.
- **Compton scattering** is dominant in soft tissue at diagnostic energies. The **Klein-Nishina** formula governs the energy-angle relation $E' = E / [1 + (E/m_e c^2)(1 - \cos \theta)]$.
- **Pair production** requires $E_\gamma \geq 1.022$ MeV. The resulting 511 keV annihilation photons are the imaging signal in **PET**.
- **Bethe-Bloch stopping power** for heavy charged particles has a $1/v^2$ dependence, producing the famous **Bragg peak** at the end of range.
- **Alpha particles**: short range (~tens of $\mu$m in tissue), high LET, straight-line trajectory, $R \propto E^{3/2}$ approximately.
- **Beta particles**: tortuous trajectory, CSDA range from Feather's formula, bremsstrahlung yield $Y \propto Z E / m_e c^2$.
- **Neutrons**: electrically neutral, interact via nuclear forces, require hydrogenous shielding (water, paraffin, polyethylene), produce recoil protons in tissue.
- **Mass attenuation coefficients** are additive: $(\mu/\rho)_{mix} = \sum_i w_i (\mu/\rho)_i$ — useful for compound materials and contrast agents.
- **Engineering rule of thumb:** In water/tissue, $Z/A \approx 0.5$, so $n_e \approx 0.5 \rho N_A$ — used in nearly every stopping-power and CT calibration problem.
- **Cross-section terminology:** Linear ($\mu$, cm⁻¹) vs mass ($\mu/\rho$, cm²/g) — be precise with the units in numerical problems to avoid KTU mark loss.
- **PET-specific:** The 511 keV annihilation photons have a low photoelectric probability in tissue (Compton-dominant), so high-$Z$ detectors (BGO, LSO) are required.
- **Stopping power units:** SI: J/m; conventional: MeV/cm or keV/$\mu$m; always quote the unit explicitly.
- **Let history guide you:** Chadwick discovered the neutron (1932) by analyzing recoil protons from $^{210}$Po-Be neutron source — direct application of elastic scattering kinematics.
- **Clinical safety:** Alpha emitters (e.g., $^{223}$Ra, $^{225}$Ac) deliver high-LET dose over a few cell diameters — useful for **targeted alpha therapy** of micrometastases.

# MODULE 4 — RADIONUCLIDE GENERATORS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Radionuclide Generator (RT DEFINITION — KTU Board Expected)**
> A *radionuclide generator* is a self-contained system that houses a long-lived **parent radionuclide** (typically immobilized on a chromatographic column) whose radioactive decay continuously produces a **short-lived daughter radionuclide**. The daughter is periodically and selectively separated from the parent by a process called **elution**, providing a fresh, ready-to-use supply of the daughter radionuclide for medical imaging — even at sites far from a radiochemical cyclotron or reactor.

In nuclear medicine, the most clinically significant generator is the **Molybdenum-99 / Technetium-99m (⁹⁹Mo / ⁹⁹ᵐTc) generator**, often called a **"Moly cow"** or **"Tc-99m cow"**, because ⁹⁹ᵐTc accounts for nearly **80 %** of all diagnostic nuclear medicine procedures worldwide.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Milking Cow"

Imagine a **dairy cow** (the parent ⁹⁹Mo) that continuously produces **milk** (the daughter ⁹⁹ᵐTc) inside her body. You cannot carry the cow around the city, but you *can* go to the cowshed every morning, "milk" her, and take only the fresh milk — leaving the cow undisturbed to produce more milk for tomorrow. The radionuclide generator works on the exact same principle:

- The **cow** = parent radionuclide (⁹⁹Mo, $T_{1/2}$ = 65.94 h).
- The **milk** = daughter radionuclide (⁹⁹ᵐTc, $T_{1/2}$ = 6.01 h).
- **Milking** = elution (chemical separation).
- The **cowshed** = shielded chromatographic column.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The clinical power of a generator lies in the fact that the **half-life of the daughter is much shorter** than that of the parent, but the daughter activity is **replenished** after each elution, allowing the same generator to be used for **1–2 weeks**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Parent Nuclide ($P$)** | Long-lived radionuclide adsorbed on the column. |
| **Daughter Nuclide ($D$)** | Short-lived radionuclide produced by decay of the parent. |
| **Elution (Milking)** | The chemical separation of the daughter from the parent. |
| **Eluate** | The solution obtained after elution (contains the daughter). |
| **Eluent** | The solution passed through the column (e.g., sterile saline). |
| **Yield** | Fraction of the maximum possible daughter activity actually obtained. |
| **Breakthrough** | Unwanted leakage of the parent isotope into the eluate. |
| **Parent–Daughter Equilibrium** | The transient or secular steady-state between $P$ and $D$. |

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Nuclear Medicine Generators

- **Generator half-life** is effectively the **parent half-life** (since $T_{1/2,P} \gg T_{1/2,D}$).
- **Elution efficiency** of a modern ⁹⁹Mo/⁹⁹ᵐTc generator is typically **> 95 %**.
- **Mo-99 breakthrough** specification: **< 0.1 µCi ⁹⁹Mo per 5 mCi ⁹⁹ᵐTc** at elution time.
- The ⁹⁹ᵐTc radioisotope emits a **monoenergetic 140 keV gamma photon** — ideal for gamma cameras.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step by Step

A radionuclide generator is built on the following operational logic:

1. **Loading the Column:** The parent radionuclide (e.g., ⁹⁹Mo as ammonium molybdate, $\text{NH}_4{}^{99}\text{MoO}_4$) is adsorbed onto a small **alumina ($\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$) chromatographic column**.
2. **Decay & Growth:** The parent decays by **beta emission** to the metastable daughter ⁹⁹ᵐTc (pertechnetate, $\text{TcO}_4^{-}$).
3. **Selective Chemistry:** The daughter, being in a different chemical form, has a much lower affinity for the alumina and can be selectively washed off.
4. **Elution:** A sterile **0.9 % NaCl solution** is passed through the column under vacuum. The $\text{TcO}_4^{-}$ ions dissolve into the saline and are collected in an evacuated vial.
5. **Re-growth:** Within the column, the parent ⁹⁹Mo continues to decay, producing fresh ⁹⁹ᵐTc that grows back to a maximum within ~ 23 hours (≈ 4 daughter half-lives).
6. **Repeat:** The cycle can be repeated for the useful life of the generator (~ 1–2 weeks).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because the parent is not chemically consumed, the **column acts as a "factory"** that can be milked repeatedly. This is the fundamental engineering advantage of a generator over directly purchasing a short-lived isotope.

## 2.2 Types of Parent–Daughter Equilibrium

The mathematical behavior of the daughter activity depends entirely on the relationship between the parent and daughter decay constants. There are **three** possible regimes:

### A) Secular Equilibrium
**Condition:** $\lambda_P \ll \lambda_D$ (i.e., $T_{1/2,P} \gg T_{1/2,D}$).

- After a transient buildup, the daughter activity **equals** the parent activity and decays with the parent's half-life.
- Example: **⁹⁰Sr / ⁹⁰Y generator** ($T_{1/2}$: 28.8 y / 64.0 h).

### B) Transient Equilibrium
**Condition:** $\lambda_P < \lambda_D$ but of the **same order of magnitude** (i.e., $T_{1/2,P} \sim$ few times $T_{1/2,D}$).

- The daughter activity grows to a **maximum greater than the parent activity** and then decays with the parent's half-life.
- Example: **⁹⁹Mo / ⁹⁹ᵐTc generator** ($T_{1/2}$: 65.94 h / 6.01 h).

### C) No Equilibrium
**Condition:** $\lambda_P > \lambda_D$ ($T_{1/2,P} < T_{1/2,D}$).

- Daughter activity reaches a maximum and then decays with its own half-life; parent vanishes quickly. **Not useful as a generator.**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Variable Key |
| :--: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Parent Activity (decay) | $A_P(t) = A_P(0) \cdot e^{-\lambda_P t}$ | $A_P(0)$: initial parent activity |
| 2 | Daughter Activity (general) | $A_D(t) = \dfrac{\lambda_D}{\lambda_D - \lambda_P} A_P(0) \left( e^{-\lambda_P t} - e^{-\lambda_D t} \right)$ | Holds for *transient* case |
| 3 | Maximum Daughter Activity (transient) | $A_D^{max} = A_P(0) \left( \dfrac{\lambda_D}{\lambda_D - \lambda_P} \right)$ | Reached at $t_{max}$ |
| 4 | Time of Maximum (transient) | $t_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_D - \lambda_P} \ln \left( \dfrac{\lambda_D}{\lambda_P} \right)$ | First maximum after elution |
| 5 | Secular Limit ($t \to \infty$) | $A_D \to A_P(0) \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_D}{\lambda_P}$ | Daughter $\gg$ Parent activity |
| 6 | Re-growth after elution (transient) | $A_D(t) = A_D^{max} \left( 1 - e^{-(\lambda_D - \lambda_P) t} \right)$ | $A_D^{max}$ is the asymptotic max |
| 7 | Decay Constant Relationship | $\lambda = \dfrac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} = \dfrac{0.693}{T_{1/2}}$ | Universal |
| 8 | Generator Useful Life | $\approx (3$ to $5) \times T_{1/2,P}$ | Engineering rule of thumb |
| 9 | Elution Yield | $\eta = \dfrac{A_D^{\text{eluted}}}{A_D^{\text{present on column}}} \times 100\,\%$ | Typical $> 95\,\%$ |
| 10 | Breakthrough (parent leakage) | $B = \dfrac{A_{P}^{\text{eluted}}}{A_{D}^{\text{eluted}}} \times 100\,\%$ | Must be $< 0.01\,\%$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hospitals without cyclotrons** can still obtain short-lived isotopes on demand from a generator.
- **Rural and mobile imaging units** (e.g., cardiac ambulances) rely entirely on ⁹⁹ᵐTc generators.
- **Radiopharmaceuticals:** Eluted Na⁹⁹ᵐTcO₄ is the starting material for kits such as HMPAO (brain), MAA (lung), MDP (bone), DTPA (kidney), and sestamibi (myocardium).
- **Theranostics:** Generators like **⁶⁸Ge / ⁶⁸Ga** (PET) and **¹⁸⁸W / ¹⁸⁸Re** (therapy) underpin modern theranostic practice.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — General Daughter Activity Equation

Let $N_P(t)$ and $N_D(t)$ be the number of atoms of the parent and daughter at time $t$.

The parent decays exponentially, giving:

$$\dfrac{dN_P}{dt} = -\lambda_P N_P \quad \Longrightarrow \quad N_P(t) = N_P(0)\, e^{-\lambda_P t}$$

The daughter is simultaneously **produced** (by parent decay at rate $\lambda_P N_P$) and **destroyed** (by its own decay at rate $\lambda_D N_D$):

$$\dfrac{dN_D}{dt} = \lambda_P N_P(t) - \lambda_D N_D(t)$$

Substituting $N_P(t)$:

$$\dfrac{dN_D}{dt} + \lambda_D N_D = \lambda_P N_P(0)\, e^{-\lambda_P t}$$

This is a **first-order linear ODE**. The standard integrating-factor method gives:

$$N_D(t)\,e^{\lambda_D t} = \lambda_P N_P(0) \int_0^{t} e^{(\lambda_D - \lambda_P) \tau}\, d\tau$$

Evaluating the integral and solving for $N_D(t)$ (with $N_D(0) = 0$ — a freshly eluted column):

$$N_D(t) = \dfrac{\lambda_P}{\lambda_D - \lambda_P}\, N_P(0) \left( e^{-\lambda_P t} - e^{-\lambda_D t} \right)$$

Since activity $A = \lambda N$, multiply by $\lambda_D$ to obtain the **Bateman Equation (2-isotope case)**:

$$\boxed{\,A_D(t) = \dfrac{\lambda_D}{\lambda_D - \lambda_P}\, A_P(0) \left( e^{-\lambda_P t} - e^{-\lambda_D t} \right)\,}$$

## 3.2 Derivation — Condition for Maximum Daughter Activity

The daughter activity reaches a maximum when $\dfrac{dA_D}{dt} = 0$.

Differentiating the Bateman equation:

$$\dfrac{dA_D}{dt} = \dfrac{\lambda_D}{\lambda_D - \lambda_P}\, A_P(0) \left( -\lambda_P e^{-\lambda_P t} + \lambda_D e^{-\lambda_D t} \right) = 0$$

This requires the bracketed term to vanish:

$$-\lambda_P e^{-\lambda_P t} + \lambda_D e^{-\lambda_D t} = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \lambda_D e^{-\lambda_D t} = \lambda_P e^{-\lambda_P t}$$

Rearranging:

$$e^{(\lambda_D - \lambda_P) t} = \dfrac{\lambda_D}{\lambda_P}$$

Taking the natural logarithm gives the **time of maximum daughter activity**:

$$\boxed{\,t_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_D - \lambda_P} \ln \!\left( \dfrac{\lambda_D}{\lambda_P} \right)\,}$$

## 3.3 Derivation — Maximum Daughter Activity Value

At $t = t_{max}$, the bracketed difference in the Bateman equation is:

$$e^{-\lambda_P t_{max}} - e^{-\lambda_D t_{max}} = e^{-\lambda_P t_{max}} \left( 1 - e^{-(\lambda_D - \lambda_P) t_{max}} \right)$$

But from the condition derived above, $e^{-(\lambda_D - \lambda_P) t_{max}} = \dfrac{\lambda_P}{\lambda_D}$. Substituting:

$$e^{-\lambda_P t_{max}} - e^{-\lambda_D t_{max}} = e^{-\lambda_P t_{max}} \left( 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_P}{\lambda_D} \right) = e^{-\lambda_P t_{max}} \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_D - \lambda_P}{\lambda_D}$$

Plugging back into the Bateman equation:

$$A_D^{max} = \dfrac{\lambda_D}{\lambda_D - \lambda_P}\, A_P(0) \cdot e^{-\lambda_P t_{max}} \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_D - \lambda_P}{\lambda_D}$$

The $\lambda_D / (\lambda_D - \lambda_P)$ terms cancel cleanly:

$$\boxed{\,A_D^{max} = A_P(0)\, e^{-\lambda_P t_{max}} = A_P(0) \left( \dfrac{\lambda_D}{\lambda_P} \right)^{\!\!-\lambda_P/(\lambda_D - \lambda_P)}\,}$$

## 3.4 Application — Numerical Example for ⁹⁹Mo / ⁹⁹ᵐTc

**Given data (KTU 2024 standard values):**

$$T_{1/2}(^{99}\text{Mo}) = 65.94 \text{ h}, \quad T_{1/2}(^{99m}\text{Tc}) = 6.01 \text{ h}$$

Compute the decay constants:

$$\lambda_P = \dfrac{0.693}{65.94 \text{ h}} = 0.01051 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

$$\lambda_D = \dfrac{0.693}{6.01 \text{ h}} = 0.11531 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

Time to maximum daughter activity:

$$t_{max} = \dfrac{1}{0.11531 - 0.01051} \ln \!\left( \dfrac{0.11531}{0.01051} \right) = \dfrac{1}{0.10480} \ln (10.972) = \dfrac{2.395}{0.10480} \approx 22.85 \text{ h}$$

So **~ 23 h** after an elution, the ⁹⁹ᵐTc activity in the column reaches its **maximum**.

Maximum daughter activity in terms of the original parent activity:

$$A_D^{max} = A_P(0) \cdot \dfrac{\lambda_D}{\lambda_D - \lambda_P} \cdot (\text{very small correction}) \approx 1.10 \, A_P(0)$$

This means the maximum ⁹⁹ᵐTc activity is **~ 10 % greater than** the original ⁹⁹Mo activity at the time of elution.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Problem-Solving Heuristic:** For a ⁹⁹Mo / ⁹⁹ᵐTc generator, the most commonly asked value is **$t_{max} \approx 23$ h**. Memorize this — it appears in almost every university exam.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Regime | Condition on $\lambda$ | $A_D$ Behavior at Long Time | Clinical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Secular** | $\lambda_P \ll \lambda_D$ | $A_D \to A_P(0)\dfrac{\lambda_D}{\lambda_P}$ | ⁹⁰Sr/⁹⁰Y, ⁶⁸Ge/⁶⁸Ga |
| **Transient** | $\lambda_P < \lambda_D$ (same order) | $A_D$ decays with $\lambda_P$ | ⁹⁹Mo/⁹⁹ᵐTc, ¹⁸⁸W/¹⁸⁸Re |
| **No Equilibrium** | $\lambda_P > \lambda_D$ | Daughter decays with $\lambda_D$ | Not a generator |


> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation:**
> 1. **Mo-99 (column-bound)** decays by $\beta^{-}$ emission (87.5 %) to the metastable **Tc-99m**, which then decays by isomeric transition (IT) emitting the diagnostically perfect **140 keV gamma photon** before becoming stable **Tc-99**.
> 2. The **shielding** (5–10 cm of lead) protects the operator from the **high-energy betas** and any bremsstrahlung produced.
> 3. **Quality control** at the eluate stage is mandatory: testing for **Mo-99 breakthrough** (parent leakage) and **Aluminum ion breakthrough** (column bleeding).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2023]`
**Define a radionuclide generator. Why is ⁹⁹Mo / ⁹⁹ᵐTc the most widely used generator in nuclear medicine?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A radionuclide generator is a sealed system containing a long-lived parent radionuclide immobilized on a chromatographic column, from which a short-lived daughter radionuclide can be repeatedly separated by elution. (1 mark)
- It provides an **on-site, on-demand supply** of short-lived isotopes at hospitals without a cyclotron. (1 mark)
- ⁹⁹Mo/⁹⁹ᵐTc is preferred because ⁹⁹ᵐTc has an ideal **140 keV gamma photon** for gamma cameras, a short **6.01 h half-life** (low patient dose), and the parent ⁹⁹Mo has a conveniently long **65.94 h half-life** enabling use of the generator for 1–2 weeks. (1 mark)

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**Differentiate between transient and secular equilibrium in a radionuclide generator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Secular equilibrium** occurs when the parent half-life is **much greater** than the daughter half-life ($\lambda_P \ll \lambda_D$); daughter activity eventually equals parent activity, decaying with the parent half-life. Example: ⁹⁰Sr / ⁹⁰Y. (1.5 marks)
- **Transient equilibrium** occurs when the parent half-life is only a **few times** the daughter half-life ($\lambda_P < \lambda_D$, same order); daughter activity exceeds parent activity and decays with the parent half-life. Example: ⁹⁹Mo / ⁹⁹ᵐTc. (1.5 marks)

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of the Bateman equations, derive the expression for the activity of the daughter nuclide as a function of time in a radionuclide generator. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** For a ⁹⁹Mo / ⁹⁹ᵐTc generator, given $T_{1/2}(^{99}\text{Mo}) = 66$ h and $T_{1/2}(^{99m}\text{Tc}) = 6$ h, calculate (i) the time required to reach maximum ⁹⁹ᵐTc activity after elution, and (ii) the maximum ⁹⁹ᵐTc activity as a fraction of the parent activity. **\[7 marks\]**

---

**Solution to Question A:**

### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Parent decay (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{dN_P}{dt} = -\lambda_P N_P \quad \Longrightarrow \quad N_P(t) = N_P(0)\, e^{-\lambda_P t}$$

**Step 2 — Daughter rate equation (1 mark):** Daughter is produced by parent decay and destroyed by its own decay:

$$\dfrac{dN_D}{dt} = \lambda_P N_P(t) - \lambda_D N_D(t)$$

**Step 3 — Solve using integrating factor (2 marks):** Multiply both sides by $e^{\lambda_D t}$:

$$\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left( N_D e^{\lambda_D t} \right) = \lambda_P N_P(0) e^{(\lambda_D - \lambda_P) t}$$

Integrate from $0$ to $t$ (using $N_D(0) = 0$ — fresh elution):

$$N_D e^{\lambda_D t} = \dfrac{\lambda_P N_P(0)}{\lambda_D - \lambda_P} \left( e^{(\lambda_D - \lambda_P) t} - 1 \right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $N_D(t)$ (1 mark):**

$$N_D(t) = \dfrac{\lambda_P}{\lambda_D - \lambda_P} N_P(0) \left( e^{-\lambda_P t} - e^{-\lambda_D t} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Convert to activity (1 mark):** Multiplying by $\lambda_D$:

$$A_D(t) = \dfrac{\lambda_D}{\lambda_D - \lambda_P}\, A_P(0) \left( e^{-\lambda_P t} - e^{-\lambda_D t} \right)$$

**Step 6 — Comment (1 mark):** This is the **Bateman equation**. For $\lambda_D > \lambda_P$ (transient case), $A_D(t)$ reaches a maximum at:

$$t_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_D - \lambda_P} \ln \!\left( \dfrac{\lambda_D}{\lambda_P} \right)$$

### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute decay constants (1.5 marks):**

$$\lambda_P = \dfrac{0.693}{66} = 0.0105 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

$$\lambda_D = \dfrac{0.693}{6} = 0.1155 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $t_{max}$ (2 marks):**

$$t_{max} = \dfrac{1}{0.1155 - 0.0105} \ln \!\left( \dfrac{0.1155}{0.0105} \right)$$

$$t_{max} = \dfrac{1}{0.1050} \ln (11.0) = \dfrac{2.398}{0.1050} \approx 22.84 \text{ h}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum activity expression (1.5 marks):**

$$A_D^{max} = A_P(0) \left( \dfrac{\lambda_D}{\lambda_D - \lambda_P} \right) e^{-\lambda_P t_{max}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation (1.5 marks):**

$$A_D^{max} = A_P(0) \cdot \left( \dfrac{0.1155}{0.1050} \right) e^{-0.0105 \times 22.84}$$

$$A_D^{max} = A_P(0) \cdot 1.10 \cdot e^{-0.240} = A_P(0) \cdot 1.10 \cdot 0.787$$

$$A_D^{max} \approx 0.866 \, A_P(0)$$

**Step 5 — Final answer (0.5 marks):** $t_{max} \approx 22.8$ h; $A_D^{max} \approx 0.87 \, A_P(0)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on sign:** Some textbooks quote the **transient equilibrium plateau value** (the activity approached at very long times after the peak) as $A_D \approx (\lambda_D / \lambda_P) A_P(0)$ when the parent decays much more slowly — this is the secular limit. KTU answers should state which interpretation is being used.

---

### 🔹 Question B `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a ⁹⁹Mo / ⁹⁹ᵐTc generator with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of alumina column and lead shielding. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** Define the terms: (i) elution yield, (ii) Mo-99 breakthrough, (iii) parent–daughter equilibrium. A ⁹⁹Mo/⁹⁹ᵐTc generator is eluted at 8:00 AM. Calculate the ⁹⁹ᵐTc activity available at 2:00 PM the same day if the eluted activity at 8:00 AM was 500 mCi. ($T_{1/2}$ of ⁹⁹ᵐTc = 6 h) **\[7 marks\]**

---

**Solution to Question B:**

### Part (a) — Construction & Working (7 marks)

**1. Construction (3.5 marks):**
- **Glass/plastic column** (2–5 cm long) packed with **acid-washed alumina ($\text{Al}_2\text{O}_3$)** (1 mark).
- The column is loaded with **⁹⁹Mo as ammonium molybdate ($\text{NH}_4{}^{99}\text{MoO}_4$)**, which strongly adsorbs onto the alumina. (1 mark)
- The column is enclosed inside a **lead pot (5–10 cm Pb)** for radiation protection against the 740 keV–1 MeV betas and bremsstrahlung from ⁹⁹Mo decay. (0.5 mark)
- Two **sterile needles/ports** connect the column to evacuated vials and a saline reservoir. (0.5 mark)
- All components are sealed inside a **sterile, disposable plastic container** for GMP compliance. (0.5 mark)

**2. Working (3.5 marks):**
- ⁹⁹Mo decays by $\beta^{-}$ emission to **⁹⁹ᵐTc** which, being a different chemical species, does **not** stick to the alumina. (1 mark)
- The ⁹⁹ᵐTc accumulates on the column as **pertechnetate ($\text{TcO}_4^{-}$)**, weakly adsorbed. (0.5 mark)
- A measured volume of **sterile 0.9 % NaCl** is drawn through the column under vacuum into an evacuated vial. (1 mark)
- The resulting **eluate** contains **Na⁹⁹ᵐTcO₄** at high specific activity, ready for radiopharmaceutical kit preparation. (0.5 mark)
- The parent ⁹⁹Mo remains on the column, continuing to produce more ⁹⁹ᵐTc, allowing **repeat elutions every 6–24 h** for 1–2 weeks. (0.5 mark)

### Part (b) — Definitions & Decay Calculation (7 marks)

**1. Definitions (3 marks — 1 each):**
- **Elution Yield:** The percentage of the theoretically available daughter activity that is actually recovered in the eluate. Formula: $\eta = (A_D^{\text{eluted}} / A_D^{\text{present}}) \times 100\,\%$.
- **Mo-99 Breakthrough:** The unwanted presence of parent ⁹⁹Mo in the ⁹⁹ᵐTc eluate, expressed as a percentage of daughter activity. Must be **< 0.01 %** for clinical use.
- **Parent–Daughter Equilibrium:** A steady-state condition in which the daughter activity decays at the same rate as the parent activity (transient or secular equilibrium).

**2. Decay calculation (4 marks):**
**Given:** $A_0 = 500$ mCi at 8:00 AM, $T_{1/2} = 6$ h, $t = 6$ h (8 AM → 2 PM).

Using the radioactive decay law:

$$A(t) = A_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t} = A_0 \cdot \left( \tfrac{1}{2} \right)^{t/T_{1/2}}$$

$$A(6 \text{ h}) = 500 \cdot \left( \tfrac{1}{2} \right)^{6/6} = 500 \cdot \tfrac{1}{2} = 250 \text{ mCi}$$

**Final Answer:** The ⁹⁹ᵐTc activity available at 2:00 PM is **250 mCi**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing parent and daughter decay rates** — students often wrongly assume ⁹⁹ᵐTc reaches max in 1 half-life. The correct $t_{max}$ depends on *both* $\lambda_P$ and $\lambda_D$.
> 2. **Using the wrong formula** — the "re-growth" formula $A_D(t) = A_D^{max}(1 - e^{-(\lambda_D - \lambda_P)t})$ applies **only after a fresh elution**, not for the very first growth from a new generator.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert units** — always convert $T_{1/2}$ to seconds or hours consistently; mixing units is the #1 source of numerical errors in KTU papers.
> 4. **Skipping the integration step in the derivation** — full marks (7/7) require showing the **integrating-factor method** explicitly, not just writing the final Bateman equation.
> 5. **Missing the "Why 140 keV is special"** — KTU often awards 1 bonus mark for explaining that 140 keV lies in the **optimum detection window** for NaI(Tl) gamma cameras and gives low patient dose.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📌 **Definition:** A radionuclide generator is a sealed column containing a long-lived parent that produces a short-lived daughter for periodic elution.
- 📌 **The "Moly Cow":** ⁹⁹Mo (65.94 h) → ⁹⁹ᵐTc (6.01 h, 140 keV gamma) is the gold standard generator.
- 📌 **Bateman Equation:** $A_D(t) = \dfrac{\lambda_D}{\lambda_D - \lambda_P}\, A_P(0) \left( e^{-\lambda_P t} - e^{-\lambda_D t} \right)$.
- 📌 **Time of Maximum Daughter:** $t_{max} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_D - \lambda_P} \ln \!\left( \dfrac{\lambda_D}{\lambda_P} \right)$.
- 📌 **For ⁹⁹Mo/⁹⁹ᵐTc:** $t_{max} \approx 23$ h; $A_D^{max} \approx 1.10 \, A_P(0)$.
- 📌 **Secular equilibrium:** $A_D \to A_P(0) \cdot (\lambda_D / \lambda_P)$ when $\lambda_P \ll \lambda_D$.
- 📌 **Transient equilibrium:** $A_D$ decays with $\lambda_P$ after the peak (parent half-life dominates at long times).
- 📌 **Column packing:** Acid-washed **alumina** adsorbs the parent; daughter is washed off with **sterile saline**.
- 📌 **Quality control parameters:** Elution yield > 95 %, ⁹⁹Mo breakthrough < 0.1 µCi/5 mCi, Al³⁺ < 10 µg/mL.
- 📌 **Elution schedule:** Every **6 h** for routine clinical use, **24 h** for overnight pre-elution.
- 📌 **Lead shielding:** 5–10 cm Pb protects operator from betas and bremsstrahlung of ⁹⁹Mo.
- 📌 **Clinical dependence:** ⁹⁹ᵐTc is used in **~ 80 %** of all diagnostic nuclear medicine scans worldwide (bone, heart, brain, lung, kidney, thyroid).
- 📌 **Other generators to know:** ⁶⁸Ge/⁶⁸Ga (PET), ⁹⁰Sr/⁹⁰Y (therapy), ¹⁸⁸W/¹⁸⁸Re (therapy), ⁸²Sr/⁸²Rb (cardiac PET).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nuclear Radiation Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Nuclear Radiation Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Nuclear Radiation Detectors** are devices that interact with ionizing radiation (α-particles, β-particles, γ-rays, X-rays, and neutrons) and convert the energy deposited by these radiations into a measurable electrical signal. In the context of medical imaging, they form the foundational sensing elements of nuclear medicine modalities such as **Gamma Cameras (SPECT)**, **Positron Emission Tomography (PET)**, and **Scintigraphy**.

The detection mechanism exploits the **ionizing nature of nuclear radiation** — its ability to create electron-ion pairs, excite atoms, or generate electron-hole pairs in a sensitive medium. The detector medium may be a **gas** (in ionization chambers, proportional counters, Geiger-Müller tubes), a **scintillator crystal** (NaI(Tl), BGO, LSO), or a **semiconductor** (Si, Ge, CdZnTe, CZT).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must be able to explain the construction, working principle, characteristic features (pulse output, gas multiplication, dead time, efficiency), and medical application suitability of the major detector classes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a dark, empty swimming pool holding a bucket. Invisible bullets (γ-photons) are being fired randomly over the pool. Each bullet, when it hits the water, creates a splash. The size of the splash depends on the bullet's energy. You (the detector) must:
1. **Catch the splash** (interaction of radiation with the detector medium),
2. **Convert it into a visible signal** (light pulse, electrical pulse),
3. **Measure how big the splash was** (pulse height → energy of photon),
4. **Count how many splashes occur per second** (count rate).

Different detectors are like different "bucket designs":
- **Ionization chamber** → like a still pond; you only measure the small ripple (no amplification).
- **Proportional counter** → like a pond with a small amplifier; ripples are multiplied into waves.
- **Geiger-Müller tube** → like a pool connected to loudspeakers; every drop triggers a tsunami (complete discharge, but loses energy information).
- **Scintillation detector** → like a fluorescent pool; each splash becomes a flash of light you can photograph.
- **Semiconductor detector** → like a solid block of jelly; you directly measure the electric current produced inside.

## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are mandatory for KTU 2024 numerical problems:

| Symbol / Term | Value / Standard |
|---|---|
| $\mathbf{W}$ (average energy to create an ion pair in air) | **33.97 eV** ≈ **34 eV** |
| $\mathbf{W}$ (in Argon gas) | **26.4 eV** |
| $\mathbf{W}$ (in typical scintillator like NaI(Tl)) | **~100 eV** for an electron-hole/photoelectron pair equivalent |
| $\mathbf{e}$ (electronic charge) | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** |
| $\mathbf{c}$ (speed of light) | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** |
| Energy of 1 eV | **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J** |
| Mass-energy of electron | **511 keV** |
| Decay constant → Activity relation | $A = \lambda N$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $W$ matters:** To convert the absorbed radiation energy $E$ (in eV) into a measurable number of ion pairs, we use the fundamental relation $\mathbf{N_{ion} = E / W}$. This is the first step in **every gas-filled detector calculation**.

## 1.4 Classification of Detectors (KTU-style)

```
Nuclear Radiation Detectors
├── Based on Medium of Detection
│   ├── GAS-FILLED DETECTORS
│   │   ├── Ionization Chamber (low E-field → no multiplication)
│   │   ├── Proportional Counter (intermediate E-field → gas multiplication ~10³–10⁴)
│   │   └── Geiger-Müller (GM) Counter (high E-field → Townsend avalanche, complete discharge)
│   ├── SCINTILLATION DETECTORS
│   │   ├── Inorganic (NaI(Tl), CsI(Tl), BGO, LSO, LYSO)
│   │   └── Organic (Anthracene, Plastic scintillators)
│   └── SEMICONDUCTOR DETECTORS
│       ├── Si(Li), HPGe, CdTe, CdZnTe (CZT)
│       └── Used when high energy resolution is required
└── Based on Output
    ├── Pulse-type (counting individual events)
    └── Current-type (measuring average rate, e.g., dosimetry)
```

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** The **energy resolution** of detectors follows the order — Semiconductor > Scintillator > Proportional > Ionization ≈ GM (worst).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 General Working Principle of a Gas-Filled Detector

A gas-filled detector is essentially a **cylindrical or parallel-plate capacitor** filled with a suitable gas (often Argon with a small percentage of a quenching agent like CH₄, CO₂, or Br₂) under pressure. A high voltage $V$ is applied between a central anode wire (small radius $a$) and the outer cathode (radius $b$).

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

1. **Radiation enters** the gas volume through a thin window (e.g., mica, beryllium).
2. **Primary ionization** occurs: ionizing radiation (charged particles or secondary electrons from γ-photons) creates **electron-ion pairs** along its track.
3. In the absence of an electric field, electrons and ions simply recombine. With a field, they separate and drift toward the electrodes.
4. The **electric field $E(r)$ at radius $r$** in a cylindrical geometry is given by:

$$E(r) = \frac{V}{r \ln(b/a)}$$

5. As the applied voltage $V$ increases (with a fixed radioactive source), the output current/pulse height passes through **six distinct regions** (see Mermaid diagram in Section 4).

## 2.2 The Six Regions of Operation

| Region | Voltage Range (typical) | Multiplication | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I. Recombination** | Very low V | None — all ions recombine | No useful signal |
| **II. Ionization (Saturation)** | Low V (50–300 V) | **M = 1** (no gas multiplication) | **Current ∝ Energy deposited** (used in **ionization chambers**) |
| **III. Proportional** | Medium V (300–800 V) | **M = 10² to 10⁴** | **Pulse height ∝ Energy** (used in **proportional counters**) |
| **IV. Limited Proportional (Geiger)** | High V (800–1000 V) | M non-constant | Non-linear region (avoided) |
| **V. Geiger-Müller** | Higher V (~1000 V) | **M = 10⁸ to 10¹⁰** (complete discharge along entire anode) | **Same pulse height for all events** (energy info lost) |
| **VI. Continuous Discharge** | Excessive V | Self-sustaining arc | **Damages the tube** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Key Point:** The "knee" between regions II and III marks the onset of gas multiplication. The "Geiger plateau" (region V) is where the pulse height is independent of the original ionization — this is both the *advantage* (large signals, simple electronics) and the *disadvantage* (no energy discrimination).

## 2.3 The Gas Multiplication Factor ($M$)

When the electric field is strong enough, the primary electrons gain enough kinetic energy between collisions to **ionize additional gas molecules** — a process called **Townsend Avalanche**. The gas multiplication factor $M$ is given by:

$$M = \exp\left[\int_{a}^{r_c} (\alpha - \eta) \, dr\right]$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = **Townsend's first ionization coefficient** (ionizations per unit length per electron)
- $\eta$ = **Attachment coefficient** (electrons captured by electronegative gas molecules)
- $r_c$ = radius at which avalanche begins

An equivalent simplified form often used in KTU problems:

$$M = \exp\left[\alpha \cdot (r_c - a)\right] \quad \text{when } \eta \ll \alpha$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of ion pairs created | $N_{ion} = E_{dep} / W$ | $E_{dep}$ in eV, $W \approx 34$ eV (air) |
| 2 | Charge collected per event | $Q = N_{ion} \cdot e \cdot M$ | Coulombs |
| 3 | Charge from a 1 MeV event in air (no M) | $Q = (10^6 / 34) \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ | ≈ **4.7 × 10⁻¹⁵ C** |
| 4 | Electric field in cylindrical chamber | $E(r) = V / [r \ln(b/a)]$ | V/m |
| 5 | Saturation current (Region II) | $I_{sat} = n \cdot e \cdot (d/t)$ | where $n$ = pairs/s, $d$ = drift distance |
| 6 | Pulse height in proportional region | $V_p = (N_{ion} \cdot e \cdot M) / C$ | $C$ = anode capacitance |
| 7 | Resolving time / Dead time | $T = T_d + T_r$ (paralyzable) or $T = T_d$ (non-paralyzable) | seconds |
| 8 | Counting loss (non-paralyzable) | $n = n_0 / (1 + n_0 T_d)$ | $n_0$ = true rate, $n$ = observed |
| 9 | Counting loss (paralyzable) | $n = n_0 \cdot e^{-n_0 T_d}$ | iterative solution |
| 10 | Statistical (Poisson) standard deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{N}$ | $N$ = total counts |
| 11 | Activity | $A = \lambda N$ | Becquerels (Bq) |
| 12 | Photon energy from scintillation | $E_{photon} = h\nu = hc/\lambda$ | J or eV |
| 13 | Photocathode quantum efficiency | $QE = N_{photoelectrons} / N_{incident\,photons}$ | fraction (0.2–0.3 typical) |
| 14 | Dynode multiplication (PMT) | $G = \delta^n$ | $\delta$ = secondary emission ratio (~4–10), $n$ = number of dynodes |
| 15 | Energy resolution | $R = (\Delta E / E) \times 100\%$ | % FWHM |
| 16 | Intrinsic photopeak efficiency | $\epsilon_i = (\text{counts in photopeak}) / (\text{photons hitting detector})$ | fraction |
| 17 | Solid angle of detection | $\Omega = A / (4\pi d^2)$ | steradians, for point source at distance $d$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Important — Units for the Q formula:** $E_{dep}$ must be in **eV** (not J) when using $W = 34$ eV. The result $N_{ion}$ is dimensionless (number of pairs). Then multiplying by $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C gives the charge in Coulombs.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in Medical Fields

- **Ionization chambers** are used in **radiation therapy dosimetry** (absolute dose measurement, calibrated against National Standards) because of their flat energy response and accurate current measurement.
- **GM counters** are used as **personal radiation monitors** and **contamination monitors** in nuclear medicine departments.
- **Proportional counters** find niche use in **neutron detection** (using ³He or BF₃ gas) and **X-ray spectroscopy**.
- **Scintillation detectors** (NaI(Tl) coupled to PMT) are the **workhorse of SPECT gamma cameras**.
- **Semiconductor detectors** (CZT) are used in **dedicated cardiac SPECT cameras** and in **handheld intraoperative probes** because of their compact size and excellent energy resolution.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Ion Pairs from a 1 MeV β-particle in an Ionization Chamber

**Problem Setup:** A 1 MeV β-particle is completely stopped in the gas of an ionization chamber. Given $W = 33.97$ eV, calculate:
(a) The number of ion pairs formed
(b) The total charge collected
(c) The minimum charge that must be measurable (assuming electronic noise floor of 10⁻¹⁴ C)

---

**Step 1 — Identify the deposited energy.**

$$E_{dep} = 1 \text{ MeV} = 1 \times 10^6 \text{ eV}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ion pair formula.**

We use the relationship: **one ion pair is created for every $W$ eV of energy deposited**.

$$N_{ion} = \frac{E_{dep}}{W}$$

$$N_{ion} = \frac{1 \times 10^6 \text{ eV}}{33.97 \text{ eV/ion pair}}$$

$$\boxed{N_{ion} \approx 29,438 \text{ ion pairs} \approx 2.94 \times 10^4 \text{ pairs}}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the total positive (or negative) charge.**

The charge is the number of ion pairs times the electronic charge $e$:

$$Q = N_{ion} \cdot e$$

$$Q = 2.94 \times 10^4 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 4.71 \times 10^{-15} \text{ C}}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with noise floor.**

$$Q_{noise} = 10^{-14} \text{ C} = 10 \times 10^{-15} \text{ C}$$

The collected charge is **about 2.1 times smaller** than the noise floor. Therefore, the **ionization chamber cannot directly detect single 1 MeV events** without external amplification. This is the engineering reason for moving to **proportional** or **GM** regions of operation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Counting Loss Correction (Non-Paralyzable Dead Time Model)

**Problem Setup:** A radiation source is measured to give an observed count rate of $n = 8000$ counts/s. The detector has a measured dead time of $T_d = 100 \,\mu s = 10^{-4}$ s. Calculate:
(a) The **true count rate** $n_0$
(b) The **percentage counting loss**

---

**Step 1 — Write the non-paralyzable dead-time equation.**

In a non-paralyzable system, every event that occurs during the dead time is simply lost, and the detector does **not** extend its dead time.

$$n = \frac{n_0}{1 + n_0 T_d}$$

where:
- $n$ = observed count rate (counts/s)
- $n_0$ = true count rate (counts/s)
- $T_d$ = dead time per event (s)

**Step 2 — Solve algebraically for $n_0$.**

Rearrange the formula to isolate $n_0$:

$$n (1 + n_0 T_d) = n_0$$

$$n + n \cdot n_0 T_d = n_0$$

$$n = n_0 - n \cdot n_0 T_d$$

$$n = n_0 (1 - n T_d)$$

$$n_0 = \frac{n}{1 - n T_d}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$n_0 = \frac{8000}{1 - (8000)(10^{-4})}$$

$$n_0 = \frac{8000}{1 - 0.8}$$

$$n_0 = \frac{8000}{0.2}$$

$$\boxed{n_0 = 40{,}000 \text{ counts/s}}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the percentage counting loss.**

$$\% \text{ Loss} = \frac{n_0 - n}{n_0} \times 100\%$$

$$\% \text{ Loss} = \frac{40000 - 8000}{40000} \times 100\%$$

$$\% \text{ Loss} = \frac{32000}{40000} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{ Loss} = 80\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** When $n T_d$ approaches 1, the denominator $\to 0$ and the formula diverges — this is the **saturation limit** of the detector. For $n_0 = 40{,}000$ c/s, the detector is missing 4 out of every 5 events! This is why GM counters are unsuitable for high-activity nuclear medicine work; **proportional or scintillation counters** are preferred.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pulse Height Output of a Proportional Counter

**Problem Setup:** A proportional counter has anode capacitance $C = 10$ pF. A 100 keV photon is fully absorbed, producing primary ion pairs. If the gas multiplication factor is $M = 1000$, calculate the output pulse height voltage $V_p$.

---

**Step 1 — Calculate primary ion pairs.**

$$N_{ion} = \frac{E_{dep}}{W} = \frac{100 \times 10^3 \text{ eV}}{33.97 \text{ eV}}$$

$$N_{ion} \approx 2944 \text{ pairs}$$

**Step 2 — Apply gas multiplication to get total electrons collected.**

$$N_{total} = N_{ion} \cdot M = 2944 \times 1000 = 2.944 \times 10^6 \text{ electrons}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate total collected charge.**

$$Q = N_{total} \cdot e = 2.944 \times 10^6 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$$

$$Q \approx 4.72 \times 10^{-13} \text{ C}$$

**Step 4 — Compute pulse height using $Q = C V_p$.**

$$V_p = \frac{Q}{C} = \frac{4.72 \times 10^{-13} \text{ C}}{10 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F}}$$

$$\boxed{V_p \approx 47.2 \text{ mV}}$$

This pulse is large enough to be easily handled by standard electronics — illustrating why **proportional counters can resolve individual photon energies** even at low primary ionizations.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Scintillation Detector: Photon Statistics and Energy Resolution

**Problem Setup:** A NaI(Tl) crystal detects 140 keV γ-rays (⁹⁹ᵐTc photopeak). Given:
- Light yield ≈ 38 photons/keV → ~5300 photons per event
- Photocathode quantum efficiency $QE = 0.25$
- PMT with 10 dynodes, $\delta = 4$ (secondary emission ratio)

Calculate:
(a) Number of photoelectrons at photocathode
(b) Total electrons at anode (PMT gain)
(c) Theoretical energy resolution (Poisson limit)

---

**Step 1 — Photoelectrons at photocathode.**

$$N_{pe} = N_{photons} \times QE = 5300 \times 0.25$$

$$\boxed{N_{pe} = 1325 \text{ photoelectrons}}$$

**Step 2 — PMT total gain.**

$$G = \delta^n = 4^{10}$$

$$G = (4^5)^2 = 1024^2 = 1{,}048{,}576 \approx 1.05 \times 10^6$$

**Step 3 — Total electrons at anode.**

$$N_{anode} = N_{pe} \times G = 1325 \times 1.05 \times 10^6$$

$$\boxed{N_{anode} \approx 1.39 \times 10^9 \text{ electrons}}$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical fractional energy resolution (Poisson limit).**

For Poisson statistics, $\sigma = \sqrt{N_{pe}}$, so:

$$R = \frac{2.35 \cdot \sigma}{N_{pe}} = \frac{2.35}{\sqrt{N_{pe}}}$$

$$R = \frac{2.35}{\sqrt{1325}} = \frac{2.35}{36.4}$$

$$\boxed{R_{theoretical} \approx 6.46\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, NaI(Tl) PMT systems give about 8–10% resolution at 140 keV because of additional noise sources (crystal non-uniformity, photocathode variance, electronic noise).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Detector Comparison

| Property | Ionization Chamber | Proportional Counter | GM Counter | NaI(Tl) Scintillator | CZT Semiconductor |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Operating region | II | III | V | — | — |
| Gas multiplication $M$ | 1 | 10²–10⁴ | 10⁸–10¹⁰ | N/A | N/A |
