The CT-derived μ-map corrects this bias and converts counts into a **quantitative activity concentration (kBq/mL)**. ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Physics of Image Formation - **Step 1 — Radiopharmaceutical administration:** A **gamma-emitting tracer** (e.g., Tc-99m MDP for bone, Tc-99m HMPAO for brain perfusion) is injected; biodistribution follows physiological uptake (osteoblastic activity, regional blood flow, receptor binding, etc.). - **Step 2 — Gamma emission & collimation:** The radiotracer isotropically emits single **γ-photons**. Only those traveling nearly parallel to the collimator holes reach the crystal; the rest are absorbed by the **septa**. - **Step 3 — Scintillation & PMT conversion:** Photons interacting in **NaI(Tl)** produce visible-light flashes (~**415 nm**). PMTs convert these into electrical pulses. - **Step 4 — Energy discrimination:** The PHA accepts pulses within the **photopeak window**, rejecting **Compton-scattered** events that would otherwise blur the image. - **Step 5 — Projection acquisition:** The gantry rotates (or detectors stay stationary in some CZT designs) acquiring 2D projections from many angles over **360°**. - **Step 6 — CT acquisition:** A low-dose or diagnostic CT scan is performed either *before* or *after* the SPECT acquisition on the same couch. - **Step 7 — Attenuation correction:** CT numbers (in HU) are converted to **μ-values at 140 keV** using a bilinear scaling (e.g., the **Burger–Wang** or **Takahashi–Tanaka** dual-energy conversion). - **Step 8 — Iterative reconstruction:** **OSEM (Ordered Subsets Expectation Maximization)** with **CT-based AC** and **scatter correction** (typically **dual-/triple-energy window method**) reconstructs quantitative SPECT images. - **Step 9 — Image fusion & display:** DICOM datasets are resampled to a common matrix (typically **128×128** for SPECT, **512×512** for CT) and displayed as fused triplanar or 3D volumes. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Description | Standard Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$ | Exponential attenuation of γ-photons through tissue of thickness *x* | *I* in counts, *μ* in cm⁻¹ | | 2 | $\mu = \rho \cdot (\mu/\rho)$ | Mass attenuation coefficient scaling (μ/ρ from NIST tables) | cm²/g | | 3 | $\text{FWHM}_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{\text{FWHM}_{\text{intrinsic}}^{2} + \text{FWHM}_{\text{collimator}}^{2} + \text{FWHM}_{\text{scatter}}^{2}}$ | Quadratic addition of resolution contributions | mm | | 4 | $R_{\text{collimator}} \approx \dfrac{d \, (l + b)}{l}$ | Geometric resolution of a parallel-hole collimator at distance *b* from collimator face | mm | | 5 | $g = \left( \dfrac{d}{d + t} \right)^{2} \cdot \left( \dfrac{d}{l} \right)^{2} \cdot \dfrac{K^{2}}{(K+1)^{2}}$ | Geometric efficiency (sensitivity) of parallel-hole collimator | dimensionless | | 6 | $\text{CT}_{\text{HU}} = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{\mu - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$ | CT number definition (Hounsfield Unit) | HU | | 7 | $\mu_{140\,\text{keV}} \approx a + b \cdot \text{HU}$ | Bilinear CT-to-140 keV μ conversion (Burger–Wang) | cm⁻¹ | | 8 | $\text{NEC} = \dfrac{T^{2}}{T + S}$ | Noise Equivalent Counts (figure-of-merit for emission data quality) | counts | | 9 | $\text{SNR} \propto \sqrt{N_{\text{counts}}}$ | Signal-to-noise ratio scales as square-root of total counts | dimensionless | | 10 | $\text{Sensitivity}_{\text{system}} = \dfrac{R_{\text{detected}}}{\text{Activity}\,(MBq)}$ | Whole-system sensitivity for a given collimator–energy pair | cps/MBq | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** In derivation-type questions, examiners often ask students to *show that* the reconstructed counts after CT-based attenuation correction follow a specific closed-form. Be ready to derive the attenuation correction factor from the line integral of *μ(x,y)* along the projection ray. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Attenuation Correction Factor (ACF) ### Statement For a photon emitted isotropically from point **P(x,y)** within the body and detected along a projection ray of length **L**, the **attenuation correction factor** required to recover the true emission count is: $$\text{ACF}(x,y) = \dfrac{1}{\exp\!\left(-\int_{L} \mu(s)\, ds\right)}$$ ### Derivation Consider a γ-photon emitted from *P(x,y)* and traveling along ray path *s*. The probability of surviving attenuation to reach the detector is given by the Beer–Lambert exponential law: $$P_{\text{survive}} = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{L} \mu(s)\, ds\right)$$ The detected count along a given projection, *without* correction, is therefore: $$I_{\text{measured}} = I_{\text{true}} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{L} \mu(s)\, ds\right)$$ To recover the true emission count, we must divide the measured count by the survival probability. Hence the **attenuation correction factor** is: $$\text{ACF}(x,y) = \dfrac{I_{\text{true}}}{I_{\text{measured}}} = \dfrac{1}{\exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{L} \mu(s)\, ds\right)} = \exp\!\left(\int_{0}^{L} \mu(s)\, ds\right)$$ This integral **∫ μ(s) ds** is computed from the **CT-derived μ-map**, scaled from the polychromatic CT energy (~70–80 keV effective) to the **SPECT photopeak energy (e.g., 140 keV for Tc-99m)** using the bilinear **Burger–Wang conversion** (referenced in formula 7 of the cheat sheet). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering insight:** Without CT-derived AC, deep structures such as the inferior wall of the left ventricle, mediastinal lesions, and pelvic lymph nodes appear artificially "cold" because 80–95% of photons are absorbed before reaching the detector. CT-based AC quantitatively restores accuracy to within ±5–10% for Tc-99m. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Spatial Resolution of the SPECT System (Including Collimator Term) ### Statement The reconstructed spatial resolution of a parallel-hole SPECT collimator–detector combination, evaluated at a distance *b* from the collimator face, is given by: $$\text{FWHM}_{\text{system}}(b) = \sqrt{R_{i}^{2} + \left( \dfrac{d(l+b)}{l} \right)^{2} + R_{s}^{2}}$$ where *Rᵢ* = intrinsic crystal resolution, *Rₛ* = scatter contribution, *d* = hole diameter, *l* = hole length, *b* = source-to-collimator distance. ### Derivation **Step 1 — Intrinsic resolution Rᵢ.** The NaI(Tl) crystal's intrinsic spatial resolution is governed by the **statistical spread of scintillation light** among the PMT array. For a 3/8″ (≈9.5 mm) crystal coupled to a hexagonal PMT array, *Rᵢ ≈ 3.0–3.5 mm FWHM*. This is a property of the detector only and is independent of collimator geometry. **Step 2 — Collimator geometric resolution R_c(b).** A photon emitted at off-axis angle *θ* from the source passes through a hole of diameter *d* and length *l*. By similar triangles, the geometric projection at distance *b* from the collimator face is: $$R_c(b) = \dfrac{d(l+b)}{l} = d + \dfrac{d \cdot b}{l}$$ The first term *d* is the geometric resolution at the collimator face (*b = 0*); the second term grows linearly with source distance. **Step 3 — Scatter contribution Rₛ.** Photons that Compton-scatter within the patient and still fall inside the ±10% photopeak window introduce a low-frequency blur. Empirically, *Rₛ ≈ 5–8 mm FWHM* depending on patient thickness and energy window settings. **Step 4 — Quadratic sum.** Independent Gaussian resolution contributions add in quadrature (variances add): $$\text{FWHM}_{\text{system}}^{2}(b) = R_{i}^{2} + R_{c}^{2}(b) + R_{s}^{2}$$ Substituting *R_c(b)*: $$\boxed{\;\text{FWHM}_{\text{system}}(b) = \sqrt{R_{i}^{2} + \left( \dfrac{d(l+b)}{l} \right)^{2} + R_{s}^{2}}\;}$$ **Numerical illustration.** Take *Rᵢ = 3.2 mm*, *d = 1.5 mm*, *l = 35 mm*, *b = 100 mm*, *Rₛ = 5 mm*: $$R_c(100) = \dfrac{1.5 \cdot (35+100)}{35} = \dfrac{1.5 \cdot 135}{35} = 5.786 \text{ mm}$$ $$\text{FWHM}_{\text{system}} = \sqrt{(3.2)^2 + (5.786)^2 + (5)^2} = \sqrt{10.24 + 33.48 + 25} = \sqrt{68.72} \approx 8.29 \text{ mm}$$ This matches the typical clinical figure of **~8 mm FWHM** for LEHR (Low-Energy High-Resolution) collimators at 10 cm depth in Tc-99m imaging. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CT Number to 140 keV μ Conversion The CT scanner measures attenuation at an effective energy of ~70–80 keV (polychromatic X-ray beam, hardened by filtration). SPECT requires μ at the photopeak energy (140 keV for Tc-99m). The bilinear conversion is: $$\mu_{140}(E) = \begin{cases} a_1 + b_1 \cdot \text{HU} & \text{HU} \leq 0 \quad (\text{air/fat}) \\ a_2 + b_2 \cdot \text{HU} & \text{HU} > 0 \quad (\text{water/soft tissue/bone}) \end{cases}$$ Typical constants (vendor-specific, e.g., Siemens/Burger–Wang): - *a₁ ≈ 0.000 cm⁻¹*, *b₁ ≈ 9.5 × 10⁻⁵ cm⁻¹/HU* - *a₂ ≈ 0.096 cm⁻¹*, *b₂ ≈ 5.0 × 10⁻⁵ cm⁻¹/HU* (for **140 keV**) For water (HU = 0): μ₁₄₀ ≈ 0.096 cm⁻¹ (which matches tabulated NIST mass attenuation × density). For cortical bone (HU ≈ 1000): μ₁₄₀ ≈ 0.096 + 5.0 × 10⁻⁵ × 1000 = **0.146 cm⁻¹**. This scaled μ-map is the **input to the forward projector** in OSEM reconstruction, enabling accurate attenuation correction. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Collimator Selection Trade-off | Collimator Type | Hole Diameter (mm) | Hole Length (mm) | Resolution (mm FWHM @ 10 cm) | Sensitivity (cps/MBq) | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **LEHR** (Low-Energy High-Resolution) | 1.5 | 35 | ~7.5 | ~250 | Tc-99m bone, cardiac, brain | | **LEAP** (Low-Energy All-Purpose) | 1.9 | 25 | ~9.5 | ~500 | General Tc-99m | | **HEGP** (High-Energy General Purpose) | 4.0 | 50 | ~14 | ~500 | I-131 (364 keV) | | **Fan-beam** | tapered | 35 | ~6 | ~700 | Cardiac SPECT (Siemens IQ-SPECT) | | **Pinhole (SPECT)** | 4–6 mm aperture | 200 | 1–3 (mouse) | low | Preclinical / dedicated cardiac | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Trade-off insight:** Sensitivity *g* and resolution *R_c* are inversely related — increasing *l* (longer holes) improves resolution but reduces sensitivity by the **1/l²** factor. This is why cardiac SPECT protocols frequently use **iterative reconstruction with resolution recovery (e.g., OSEM + RR)** to recover sensitivity lost to collimation. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level SPECT-CT Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Patient on Couch] --> B[Radiotracer Injection
Tc-99m, I-123, Tl-201] B --> C[SPECT Gantry
Rotating Gamma Camera 0 to 360 deg] C --> D[Collimator
Parallel hole LEHR or Fan beam] D --> E[NaI Tl Scintillation Crystal
3 by 8 inch thickness] E --> F[PMT Array
Photomultiplier Tubes] F --> G[Pulse Height Analyzer
Photopeak Window plus minus 10 percent] G --> H[SPECT Projection Sinograms
60 to 120 views] A2[Same Couch Position] --> I[CT X ray Tube
80 to 140 kVp] I --> J[CT Detector Array
Multi slice 2 to 64] J --> K[CT Reconstruction
FBP or Iterative] K --> L[CT Hounsfield Map HU] L --> M[Attenuation Map Conversion
Bilinear Scaling to 140 keV] H --> N[Iterative Reconstruction OSEM
with CT based AC] M --> N N --> O[Quantitative SPECT Volume
kBq per mL] O --> P[Image Fusion Workstation
DICOM Resampling] L --> P P --> Q[Clinical Reporting
Axial Sagittal Coronal MPR] ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Workflow Diagram — Data Flow Inside the Hybrid System ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ACQ[Acquisition Subsystem] R1[Radiotracer Emission] --> SP1[SPECT Projection] R2[X ray Transmission] --> SP2[CT Projection] end subgraph PROC[Reconstruction Engine] SP1 --> ITER[OSEM Iterative Loop] SP2 --> FBP[Filtered Back Projection] FBP --> HUV[HU Volume Map] HUV --> CONV[Burger Wang 140 keV Conversion] CONV --> AC[Attenuation Correction Map] AC --> ITER end subgraph OUT[Output Subsystem] ITER --> QS[Quantitative SPECT Image] QS --> FUSE[Fusion and Display] HUV --> FUSE FUSE --> DX[Clinical Diagnosis] end ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Clinical Decision Pathways ```mermaid flowchart TB PT[Patient Referred for SPECT CT] --> Q1{Indication} Q1 -->|Bone Pain Oncology| BONE[Tc 99m MDP Bone SPECT CT] Q1 -->|Coronary Disease| CARD[Tc 99m Sestamibi or Tl 201 Cardiac SPECT CT] Q1 -->|Neuroendocrine Tumor| NET[In 111 Octreotide or Ga 67 SPECT CT] Q1 -->|Brain Perfusion| BRN[Tc 99m HMPAO or I 123 Ioflupane SPECT CT] BONE --> EVAL{Lesion Localization} CARD --> EVAL NET --> EVAL BRN --> EVAL EVAL --> BENIGN[Benign Uptake Pattern
e.g. degenerative] EVAL --> MALIG[Suspicious Malignant Lesion
Anatomical Co registration] BENIGN --> FOLLOW[Follow up Imaging] MALIG --> BIO[CT guided Biopsy or Surgery] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define SPECT-CT. List any two clinical applications.** **Model Answer:** **SPECT-CT (Single Photon Emission Computed Tomography – Computed Tomography)** is a *hybrid imaging modality* that combines a **rotating gamma camera (SPECT)** for functional imaging of single-photon-emitting radiotracers with a **multi-slice CT** for anatomical imaging and **X-ray-based attenuation correction**, both mounted on a **common gantry** with a shared patient couch. **Two clinical applications:** 1. **Oncology** – Staging and restaging of neuroendocrine tumors using **In-111 Octreotide** or **I-123 MIBG** with CT for precise anatomical localization of tracer-avid lesions. 2. **Cardiology** – **Myocardial perfusion imaging** using Tc-99m Sestamibi/Tetrofosmin or Tl-201, where CT provides attenuation correction (eliminating diaphragmatic and breast attenuation artifacts) and coronary calcium scoring in a single session. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Why is CT-based attenuation correction necessary in SPECT? Mention one equation used for it.** **Model Answer:** In SPECT, **γ-photons emitted from deep structures are attenuated by overlying tissue** (bones, soft tissue), causing apparent reduction in counts and producing false "cold" regions (especially in the inferior myocardial wall, mediastinum, and pelvis). **CT-based attenuation correction (AC)** uses the CT-derived **μ-map** to compensate for this photon loss, enabling *quantitative* and *accurate* reconstruction. The relevant equation is the **Beer–Lambert exponential attenuation law**: $$I = I_0 \, e^{-\int_{L} \mu(s)\, ds}$$ The **attenuation correction factor** is: $$\text{ACF} = \dfrac{1}{e^{-\int_{L} \mu(s)\, ds}} = \exp\!\left(\int_{L} \mu(s)\, ds\right)$$ --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **working principle of SPECT** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **collimator** and **NaI(Tl) crystal** in image formation. **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **need for hybrid SPECT-CT imaging**. Explain in detail how **CT-based attenuation correction** is performed, including the **bilinear conversion** of HU to μ at the SPECT photopeak energy. --- #### Model Solution — Question A **(a) Working principle of SPECT and role of collimator & NaI(Tl):** 1. **Radiotracer administration:** A single-photon-emitting radiopharmaceutical (e.g., Tc-99m MDP) is administered intravenously. The tracer accumulates in target tissue according to physiology (e.g., osteoblastic activity). 2. **Gamma emission:** The radionuclide emits **γ-photons isotropically** (e.g., 140 keV for Tc-99m). 3. **Collimation:** A **parallel-hole lead collimator** (thickness ~35–50 mm) absorbs obliquely traveling photons, allowing only near-parallel rays to reach the detector. This defines the **spatial origin** of each detected photon — without a collimator, no spatial image can be formed. 4. **Scintillation:** Photons that pass through the collimator interact with the **NaI(Tl) crystal** (typically 3/8″–5/8″ thick) via the photoelectric effect or Compton scattering, producing thousands of visible-light photons (~**415 nm**) per absorbed γ-photon. 5. **Photomultiplication:** The light is collected by an array of **PMTs**, which convert and amplify it into a proportional electrical pulse. 6. **Position & energy computation:** A **resistive/an Anger logic network** determines the *X,Y* position of interaction and the *Z (energy)* from the summed PMT signals. The **pulse-height analyzer (PHA)** rejects events outside the ±10% photopeak window. 7. **Tomographic acquisition:** The detector head rotates around the patient in **64–120 angular steps** over 360°, producing a set of 2D projections. 8. **Reconstruction:** Iterative algorithms (**OSEM** – Ordered Subsets Expectation Maximization) reconstruct a 3D activity distribution from the projections, applying **CT-based AC** and **scatter correction**. **Role of collimator:** Provides **spatial encoding** (without it, the system is a non-imaging counter). Determines **resolution-sensitivity trade-off**: smaller hole diameter *d* and longer length *l* improve resolution but reduce sensitivity. **Role of NaI(Tl):** Acts as the **γ-photon-to-light transducer**. High atomic number (Z = 53 for iodine) and high density (3.67 g/cm³) ensure high photoelectric absorption efficiency at 140 keV. Light yield ≈ 38 photons/keV → high signal statistics. --- **(b) Need for hybrid SPECT-CT & CT-based attenuation correction:** **Need for hybrid SPECT-CT:** - **Anatomical localization:** SPECT alone has low spatial resolution (~8 mm) and lacks anatomical landmarks. CT provides sub-millimeter anatomical detail. - **Attenuation correction:** CT delivers a high-SNR, quantitative μ-map in seconds, eliminating hours-long **transmission-source scans** (e.g., Gd-153 line sources used in older SPECT-only systems). - **Specificity:** CT differentiates physiological tracer uptake (e.g., bowel, urinary tract, brown fat) from pathology, reducing false positives. **Steps of CT-based attenuation correction:** 1. Acquire a low-dose CT (80–120 kVp, 10–30 mAs) or diagnostic CT over the same axial range as SPECT. 2. Reconstruct into a **HU volume** using FBP or iterative CT reconstruction. 3. Convert HU → **μ at 140 keV** using the **bilinear Burger–Wang model**: $$\mu_{140} = \begin{cases} 9.5 \times 10^{-5} \cdot \text{HU} & \text{HU} \leq 0 \\ 0.096 + 5.0 \times 10^{-5} \cdot \text{HU} & \text{HU} > 0 \end{cases}$$ 4. Resample the μ-map to the SPECT matrix (typically **128×128**, voxel ~4.8 mm). 5. Forward-project the μ-map in each OSEM iteration; multiply each emission estimate by $\exp(+\int \mu\, ds)$ to recover the true counts. 6. Apply **scatter correction** (dual-/triple-energy window) jointly with AC. --- ### Question B — Alternative Choice [14 Marks] **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **clinical workflow of SPECT-CT** from radiotracer injection to final image interpretation. Mention typical **acquisition parameters**. **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss **collimator design** in SPECT. Derive the expression for **geometric resolution** and **geometric efficiency** of a parallel-hole collimator and explain their **inverse relationship**. --- #### Model Solution — Question B **(a) Clinical workflow of SPECT-CT:** The workflow consists of **eight sequential stages** (see block diagram in §4.1): 1. **Clinical referral & indication review** (oncology, cardiology, neurology, orthopaedics). 2. **Radiopharmaceutical preparation** – Tc-99m eluted from a Mo-99/Tc-99m generator; labeling with MDP/HMPAO/Sestamibi; **radiochemical purity (RCP) tested ≥ 95%**. 3. **Patient preparation** – hydration, voiding, removal of metal objects, thyroid blocking (if I-123/I-131). 4. **Tracer injection** – typical activity **740–1110 MBq (20–30 mCi)** for Tc-99m bone SPECT-CT; lower for pediatric protocols. 5. **Uptake period** – 2–4 h for bone; 30–60 min for cardiac stress/rest. 6. **CT scout/topogram** – defines axial extent of the scan. 7. **SPECT acquisition** – 360° rotation, **60–120 projections**, 15–40 s/projection; matrix **128×128**. 8. **CT acquisition** – low-dose or diagnostic; **80–140 kVp**, **10–200 mAs**; reconstructed to **512×512**. 9. **Reconstruction & fusion** – OSEM (2–5 iterations, 8–16 subsets) with CTAC + scatter correction. 10. **Image review** – triplanar MPR, SUV-like quantification (for Lu-177, I-131 therapy), side-by-side and fused display. **Typical acquisition parameters** for a Tc-99m bone SPECT-CT whole-body protocol: | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Activity | 740 MBq (20 mCi) | | Energy window | 140 keV ± 7.5% | | Matrix | 128×128 | | Number of views | 120 | | Time per view | 20 s | | CT kVp / mAs | 110 / 30 (low-dose) | | Reconstruction | OSEM, 4 iter, 8 subsets + CTAC | --- **(b) Collimator design — derivation of resolution and efficiency:** **Geometric resolution (R_c):** A point source at axial distance *b* from the collimator face, viewed through a hole of diameter *d* and length *l*, projects onto the detector with diameter: $$R_c = \dfrac{d(l+b)}{l} = d + \dfrac{db}{l}$$ This is the **geometric unsharpness** of the collimator; resolution worsens linearly with source distance *b*. **Geometric efficiency (g):** The fraction of emitted photons passing through a single hole is the ratio of hole area to the area of one unit cell (hole + septa): $$g_{\text{hole}} = \left(\dfrac{d}{d+t}\right)^{2}$$ where *t* = septal thickness. Photons travel parallel only if their incidence angle is within $\tan\theta = d/(2l)$; the angular acceptance fraction is: $$f_{\text{angular}} = \left(\dfrac{d}{2l}\right)^{2}$$ Total geometric efficiency per hole: $$g = \left(\dfrac{d}{d+t}\right)^{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{d}{2l}\right)^{2} = \dfrac{d^{4}}{4 l^{2}(d+t)^{2}}$$ **Inverse relationship between resolution and sensitivity:** Substituting *R_c ≈ d* (at *b = 0*, ignoring small *d* offset): $$g \propto \dfrac{R_c^{4}}{l^{2} (R_c+t)^{2}}$$ For fixed *l* and *t*, *g* scales as $R_c^{2}$ approximately. Hence, **halving the resolution (improving sharpness) reduces sensitivity by a factor of ~4**. This is the **fundamental resolution-sensitivity trade-off** of SPECT collimation, motivating modern *resolution-recovery* iterative reconstruction techniques. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing SPECT-CT with PET-CT.** Examiners frequently award 0 marks if students incorrectly claim SPECT uses *positron* annihilation (511 keV) — SPECT uses **single γ-photons at 70–365 keV** (typically 140 keV for Tc-99m). > 2. **Forgetting to mention CT's *dual* role.** Marks are lost when students describe CT only for anatomical overlay and forget its **attenuation correction** function. > 3. **Skipping units in formulas.** Always specify **mm, cm⁻¹, keV, MBq** — missing units cost 0.5–1 mark per sub-question. > 4. **Confusing intrinsic vs. system resolution.** Intrinsic resolution is the crystal/PMT property; system resolution includes the collimator and scatter terms — they add in **quadrature (RMS)**, not linearly. > 5. **Ignoring respiratory / cardiac motion** in thoracic SPECT-CT — a contemporary 2024-scheme examiner expectation. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **SPECT-CT = SPECT + CT on a common gantry.** It is the canonical *hybrid functional-anatomical* imaging system. - **SPECT physics chain:** Radiotracer → γ-emission (e.g., 140 keV) → Parallel-hole collimator → NaI(Tl) crystal → PMTs → PHA → Projection sinogram → OSEM reconstruction. - **CT's two roles:** (1) anatomical reference image; (2) source of the **μ-map for attenuation correction (CTAC)**. - **Attenuation correction equation:** $\text{ACF} = \exp\!\left(\int_{L} \mu(s)\, ds\right)$. - **Resolution:** $\text{FWHM}_{\text{system}} = \sqrt{R_{i}^{2} + R_{c}^{2} + R_{s}^{2}}$ — add in quadrature. - **Collimator trade-off:** Resolution $\propto d$, Sensitivity $\propto d^{4}/l^{2}$ — long, narrow holes = sharp images but low counts. - **Common radiotracers:** Tc-99m (140 keV, bone/cardiac/brain), I-123 (159 keV, neural), Tl-201 (70–80 keV, cardiac), In-111 (171 & 245 keV, neuroendocrine), Ga-67 (93/185/300 keV, infection), Lu-177 (113 & 208 keV, theranostics). - **Reconstruction:** OSEM (2–5 iterations, 8–16 subsets) with CTAC and scatter correction — the **standard of care** in 2024. - **Clinical pillars:** **Oncology** (staging/restaging, theranostics — Lu-177 DOTATATE, Lu-177 PSMA), **Cardiology** (myocardial perfusion + CAC scoring), **Neurology** (dopamine transporter imaging with I-123 Ioflupane / DaTscan), **Orthopaedics** (occult fractures, infection vs. loosening of prostheses). - **Quantitative endpoint:** With CTAC, SPECT yields **standardized uptake value (SUV)–like** activity concentration in **kBq/mL** — increasingly used for **dosimetry in radionuclide therapy**. - **Future direction:** **CZT solid-state detectors**, **whole-body CZT SPECT-CT**, **AI-based attenuation correction**, and **digital twins** for personalized dosimetry are the frontier topics for higher-mark questions. ---

topics.title: Radiation Protection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Radiation Protection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Radiation Protection** (also called *Radiological Protection* or *Health Physics*) is the multidisciplinary science and engineering discipline concerned with protecting humans, their descendants, and the environment from the harmful effects of **ionizing radiation**, while still allowing its beneficial applications in medicine, industry, research, and energy production.

In the context of medical imaging, radiation protection refers to the systematic set of practices, equipment, protocols, and regulatory frameworks designed to minimize unnecessary radiation exposure to **patients**, **occupational workers** (radiologists, radiographers, nuclear medicine technologists), and the **general public**, while preserving the diagnostic quality and therapeutic efficacy of the imaging procedure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Radiation Protection is the science and practice of evaluating, controlling, and reducing radiation exposure to patients, personnel, and the public, governed by the three cardinal principles: **Time**, **Distance**, and **Shielding**, applied within the optimization framework of the ALARA principle (As Low As Reasonably Achievable).*

## 1.2 Key Terminology

- **Ionizing Radiation**: High-energy electromagnetic waves (X-rays, γ-rays) or particulate radiation (α, β, neutrons) capable of producing ions by removing electrons from atoms in matter.
- **Stochastic Effects**: Radiation-induced effects whose probability of occurrence (not severity) increases with dose (e.g., cancer, genetic mutations). There is **no threshold dose**.
- **Deterministic Effects (Tissue Reactions)**: Effects whose severity increases with dose above a **threshold** (e.g., skin erythema, cataract formation, radiation sickness).
- **Effective Dose (E)**: The weighted sum of equivalent doses to all irradiated tissues, reflecting the total stochastic risk. Measured in **Sieverts (Sv)**.
- **Equivalent Dose (Hₜ)**: The absorbed dose multiplied by the radiation weighting factor (w_R). Measured in **Sieverts (Sv)**.
- **Absorbed Dose (D)**: The energy deposited per unit mass of tissue. Measured in **Grays (Gy)**, where **1 Gy = 1 J/kg**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine standing in a rainstorm holding a bucket. The rain represents radiation, and the bucket represents your body. **Radiation protection is about holding a smaller bucket, staying under a small umbrella, and only standing in the rain for as short a time as necessary**.

A more practical analogy:

> **The "Sun Exposure" Analogy**
> Just as you protect yourself from harmful UV rays from the sun by:
> 1. **Limiting the time** spent in direct sunlight (Time),
> 2. **Maintaining distance** from intense sources (Distance — inverse square law),
> 3. **Using sunscreen, hats, and umbrellas** (Shielding),
>
> Radiation protection in medical imaging applies identical logic to the invisible but potentially harmful X-rays, γ-rays, and radioactive isotopes encountered in clinical practice.

## 1.4 Why Radiation Protection is Critical in Medical Imaging

Medical imaging (X-ray radiography, fluoroscopy, CT, interventional radiology, nuclear medicine) is the largest artificial source of human radiation exposure. While the individual benefit of a justified exam far outweighs the risk, **unnecessary or poorly optimized exposures** contribute to cumulative population dose and stochastic risks (lifetime cancer risk). Therefore, radiation protection is not optional — it is a **legal, ethical, and professional obligation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** The fundamental philosophy of modern radiation protection is contained in the **ICRP System of Radiological Protection**, which rests on three pillars:
> 1. **Justification** — No practice involving radiation should be adopted unless it produces sufficient benefit to offset the detriment.
> 2. **Optimization** — All exposures should be kept **As Low As Reasonably Achievable (ALARA)**, taking economic and social factors into account.
> 3. **Dose Limitation** — Individual doses must not exceed recommended limits (excluding medical exposures of patients).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Cardinal Principles of External Radiation Protection

The entire framework of operational radiation protection rests on three physically simple but powerful principles:

### 2.1.1 Principle 1: TIME

> **"Minimize the duration of exposure"**

The total radiation dose received is **directly proportional** to the time spent in the radiation field.

$$D \propto t$$

**Practical Application:**
- For occupational workers: Rotate staff in high-dose areas (interventional radiology, fluoroscopy).
- For patients: Use the shortest fluoroscopy time, minimize the number of exposures, use pulsed fluoroscopy instead of continuous.
- Avoid leaving radioactive sources unshielded; handle them quickly with trained personnel.

### 2.1.2 Principle 2: DISTANCE

> **"Maximize the distance from the radiation source"**

For a point source of radiation (small compared to distance), the intensity falls off as the **inverse square of the distance**.

$$I \propto \frac{1}{d^2} \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{I_1}{I_2} = \left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^2$$

**Practical Application:**
- During fluoroscopy, step back from the patient (the patient is the primary source of scatter radiation).
- Use remote handling tools (forceps, tongs) for radioactive sources in nuclear medicine.
- The X-ray tube housing itself is heavily shielded; distance is most useful against **scatter radiation**, which behaves as a point source.

> [!TIP]
> **Doubling the distance reduces the dose to one-fourth (¼).** This is one of the most powerful and cost-free protection strategies.

### 2.1.3 Principle 3: SHIELDING

> **"Use appropriate absorbing materials between the source and the person"**

Shielding attenuates radiation according to an **exponential attenuation law** governed by Beer-Lambert's law.

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

where:
- $I_0$ = Initial radiation intensity
- $I$ = Transmitted intensity after passing through thickness $x$
- $\mu$ = Linear attenuation coefficient (depends on material and photon energy), measured in **cm⁻¹**
- $x$ = Thickness of shielding material, in **cm**

**Half-Value Layer (HVL):** The thickness of a material that reduces the radiation intensity to **one-half** of its original value.

$$\text{HVL} = \frac{\ln(2)}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}$$

**Tenth-Value Layer (TVL):** The thickness that reduces intensity to **one-tenth** of the original value.

$$\text{TVL} = \frac{\ln(10)}{\mu} = \frac{2.303}{\mu}$$

**Standard Shielding Materials:**
| Radiation Type | Best Shielding Material | Reason |
|----------------|-------------------------|--------|
| α particles | Paper, skin, thin metal foil | Very low penetration, high linear energy transfer |
| β particles | Plastic (Plexiglas), aluminum | Avoids bremsstrahlung production |
| X-rays & γ-rays (diagnostic) | Lead (Pb), lead-equivalent materials | High Z, high density |
| X-rays (high energy, MV) | Concrete, steel, lead | Deeper penetration requires thicker barriers |
| Neutrons | Borated polyethylene, paraffin, water | Hydrogen-rich materials for moderation |

## 2.2 Dose Quantities — The Foundation of Dosimetry

The KTU syllabus expects fluency in converting between the four fundamental dose quantities:

### Step 1: Absorbed Dose (D)

$$D = \frac{d\bar{\varepsilon}}{dm}$$

Unit: **Gray (Gy)** = 1 J/kg. Replaces the old unit rad (1 Gy = 100 rad).

### Step 2: Equivalent Dose (Hₜ)

$$H_T = \sum_R w_R \cdot D_{T,R}$$

Unit: **Sievert (Sv)**. The radiation weighting factor $w_R$ accounts for the biological damage potential of different radiation types.

### Step 3: Effective Dose (E)

$$E = \sum_T w_T \cdot H_T$$

Unit: **Sievert (Sv)**. The tissue weighting factor $w_T$ reflects the radiosensitivity and cancer risk of each organ.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Values — Radiation Weighting Factors (w_R):**
> | Radiation Type | w_R |
> |---|---|
> | X-rays, γ-rays, β particles | **1** |
> | Thermal neutrons | **5** |
> | Fast neutrons, protons | **20** |
> | α particles, heavy ions | **20** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $D = d\varepsilon / dm$ | Absorbed Dose | Gy (J/kg) |
| 2 | $H_T = w_R \cdot D$ | Equivalent Dose | Sv |
| 3 | $E = \sum w_T \cdot H_T$ | Effective Dose | Sv |
| 4 | $I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$ | Exponential Attenuation | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\text{HVL} = 0.693 / \mu$ | Half-Value Layer | m, cm, mm |
| 6 | $\text{TVL} = 2.303 / \mu$ | Tenth-Value Layer | m, cm, mm |
| 7 | $I_1 / I_2 = (d_2 / d_1)^2$ | Inverse Square Law | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\dot{X} = \Gamma \cdot A / d^2$ | Specific Gamma Ray Constant | (R·m²)/(Ci·hr) |
| 9 | $A = A_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | Radioactive Decay | Bq, Ci |
| 10 | $T_{1/2} = 0.693 / \lambda$ | Half-life relation | s, hr, days |
| 11 | $D_{\text{rate}} = 0.5 \cdot A \cdot \Gamma$ | Dose rate from point source | mR/hr at 1 m |
| 12 | $\text{ALARA} = \text{As Low As Reasonably Achievable}$ | Optimization principle | — |
| 13 | $\sum_T w_T = 1$ | Tissue weighting factors sum | dimensionless |
| 14 | $1 \text{ Sv} = 100 \text{ rem}$ | Conversion (old unit) | — |
| 15 | $1 \text{ Gy} = 100 \text{ rad}$ | Conversion (old unit) | — |

## 2.4 The Inverse Square Law — Detailed Behavior

For a point source emitting isotropically, the radiation fluence (and therefore the dose rate) decreases as the square of the distance. This is a consequence of the **conservation of energy** spread over an expanding spherical surface of area $4\pi d^2$.

$$\dot{H}(d) = \dot{H}(d_0) \cdot \left(\frac{d_0}{d}\right)^2$$

**Worked Quick Example:**
If the dose rate at 1 m from a source is **100 μSv/hr**, then at 2 m it is $100 \times (1/2)^2 = 25$ μSv/hr, and at 4 m it is $100 \times (1/4)^2 = 6.25$ μSv/hr.

> [!WARNING]
> **Inverse square law holds ONLY for point sources** (sources small compared to distance) and in a **scatter-free geometry**. In a real fluoroscopy room, scatter from walls, floor, and patient can degrade the strict $1/d^2$ behavior.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Diagnostic Radiology Design**: Hospital architects use the inverse square law, HVL data, and workload calculations to design lead-lined walls, doors, and control booths for X-ray rooms (NCRP Report No. 147).
2. **Personal Dosimetry**: Workers wear TLDs (Thermoluminescent Dosimeters) and OSL dosimeters to monitor cumulative exposure.
3. **Patient Dose Reduction**: Modern CT scanners use **Automatic Exposure Control (AEC)**, **iterative reconstruction algorithms**, and **tube current modulation** to optimize ALARA.
4. **Nuclear Medicine**: Technetium-99m (Tc-99m, $T_{1/2} = 6$ hours) and F-18 ($T_{1/2} = 110$ min) decay calculations determine the activity to be administered, the residual activity in waste, and the required storage shielding.
5. **Mammography**: Uses molybdenum/rhodium targets and lower kVp, requiring lead-equivalent protective equipment (lead aprons, thyroid shields, lead glasses) for staff.

## 2.6 ALARA in Practice — A Layered Defense Model

ALARA is operationalized through three nested control strategies:

1. **Source Control** — engineered safety features: shielded X-ray tubes, source encapsulation, interlocks, warning lights.
2. **Environmental Controls** — structural shielding (walls, doors, leaded glass), ventilation for radioactive gases/iodine, controlled access zones.
3. **Administrative & Procedural Controls** — training, supervision, SOPs, dose constraints, audit programs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the Three Cardinal Principles: TDS**
> - **T** = Time (minimize)
> - **D** = Distance (maximize)
> - **S** = Shielding (use appropriate barriers)
>
> This is the highest-frequency KTU board question in radiation protection modules.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Inverse Square Law from Geometric Spreading

We begin with a **point source** emitting $N$ photons isotropically (equally in all directions) per unit time. The total photon fluence is distributed over the surface of a sphere of radius $d$.

**Step 1:** Surface area of a sphere of radius $d$ is given by the standard geometry formula:

$$A_{\text{sphere}} = 4\pi d^2$$

**Step 2:** The photon fluence $\Phi$ (photons per unit area per unit time) at distance $d$ is the total emission rate divided by the sphere's surface area:

$$\Phi(d) = \frac{N}{4\pi d^2}$$

**Step 3:** Form the ratio of fluences at two distances $d_1$ and $d_2$:

$$\frac{\Phi(d_1)}{\Phi(d_2)} = \frac{N / (4\pi d_1^2)}{N / (4\pi d_2^2)} = \frac{d_2^2}{d_1^2}$$

**Step 4:** Since dose rate is proportional to fluence (for monoenergetic photons in air):

$$\frac{\dot{D}_1}{\dot{D}_2} = \left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^2$$

This is the **Inverse Square Law** in its standard form. The law is purely geometric and does not depend on the energy or type of radiation — only on the assumption of a point source and isotropic emission.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Exponential Attenuation (Beer-Lambert Law)

Consider a narrow beam of monoenergetic photons of initial intensity $I_0$ passing through a homogeneous absorber of thickness $x$ with linear attenuation coefficient $\mu$.

**Step 1:** Consider an infinitesimally thin slice of absorber of thickness $dx$. The fractional reduction in intensity $dI/I$ over this slice is proportional to $dx$:

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \, dx$$

The negative sign indicates a decrease in intensity.

**Step 2:** Integrate both sides from $x = 0$ (where $I = I_0$) to a general thickness $x$ (where $I = I$):

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -\mu \int_0^x dx$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integrals:

$$\ln(I) - \ln(I_0) = -\mu x$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\mu x$$

**Step 4:** Exponentiate both sides:

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\mu x}$$

$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

This is the **Exponential Attenuation Law** (also called Beer-Lambert Law). It is the foundation of all shielding calculations in diagnostic radiology, nuclear medicine, and radiation therapy.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Half-Value Layer (HVL) from Exponential Attenuation

**Step 1:** By definition, the HVL is the thickness $x_{1/2}$ that reduces $I_0$ to $I_0/2$:

$$\frac{I_0/2}{I_0} = e^{-\mu x_{1/2}}$$

**Step 2:** Simplify the left side:

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\mu x_{1/2}}$$

**Step 3:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln(1/2) = -\mu x_{1/2}$$

**Step 4:** Use the identity $\ln(1/2) = -\ln(2) = -0.693$:

$$-0.693 = -\mu x_{1/2}$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $x_{1/2}$:

$$x_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}$$

This shows that HVL is inversely proportional to the attenuation coefficient. **Higher $\mu$ means a thinner HVL**, i.e., a more effective shield.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Relationship Between HVL, TVL, and $\mu$

**Step 1:** By analogous reasoning, TVL is the thickness reducing intensity to $1/10$:

$$\frac{1}{10} = e^{-\mu x_{1/10}}$$

**Step 2:** Take the logarithm:

$$\ln(1/10) = -\mu x_{1/10} \implies -2.303 = -\mu x_{1/10}$$

**Step 3:** Solve:

$$x_{1/10} = \frac{2.303}{\mu}$$

**Step 4:** Take the ratio of TVL to HVL:

$$\frac{\text{TVL}}{\text{HVL}} = \frac{2.303/\mu}{0.693/\mu} = \frac{2.303}{0.693} \approx 3.32$$

**Insight:** One TVL is approximately equal to **3.32 HVLs** — a useful rule of thumb for shielding design.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Effective Dose from Equivalent Doses

**Step 1:** The equivalent dose in a tissue $T$ from multiple radiation types $R$ is:

$$H_T = \sum_R w_R \cdot D_{T,R}$$

**Step 2:** The effective dose $E$ is the sum of tissue-weighted equivalent doses across all irradiated tissues:

$$E = \sum_T w_T \cdot H_T$$

**Step 3:** Expanding both summations:

$$E = \sum_T w_T \left( \sum_R w_R \cdot D_{T,R} \right)$$

**Step 4:** The tissue weighting factors $w_T$ are normalized such that:

$$\sum_T w_T = 1$$

This normalization ensures that uniform whole-body irradiation gives the same effective dose numerically as the equivalent dose to the whole body.

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Radioactive Decay Law

**Step 1:** The rate of decay of a radioactive sample is proportional to the number of undecayed nuclei $N$:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N$$

where $\lambda$ is the **decay constant** (units: s⁻¹).

**Step 2:** Separate variables and integrate from $N_0$ at $t=0$ to $N$ at time $t$:

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \int_0^t dt$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$\ln(N) - \ln(N_0) = -\lambda t$$

**Step 4:** Exponentiate:

$$N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 5:** Activity $A$ is the number of decays per unit time, $A = \lambda N$, so:

$$A(t) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$

**Step 6:** Half-life is the time for activity to halve:

$$A_0/2 = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}} \implies T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}$$

## 3.7 KTU Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Common Shielding Materials

| Material | Density (g/cm³) | Effective Z | HVL @ 100 keV | Typical Use |
|----------|-----------------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| Air | 0.001225 | 7.6 | ~40 m | Reference medium |
| Water | 1.00 | 7.4 | ~4.2 cm | Phantom, neutron moderation |
| Aluminum | 2.70 | 13 | ~1.5 cm | Tube housing (low kV) |
| Concrete | 2.35 | 14 | ~1.6 cm | Room walls, bunkers |
| Iron (Steel) | 7.87 | 26 | ~0.5 cm | Therapy room doors |
| Lead | 11.35 | 82 | ~0.27 mm | Aprons, shields, walls |
| Depleted Uranium | 19.1 | 92 | ~0.10 mm | Portable high-energy shielding |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is lead preferred for diagnostic X-rays?**
> Lead has a very high atomic number (Z = 82) and high density, giving it an extremely high mass attenuation coefficient ($\mu/\rho$) for the 30–120 keV photons used in diagnostic radiology. The probability of **photoelectric absorption** scales approximately as $Z^3/E^3$, so high-Z materials dominate at low energies.

## 3.8 Dose Limits — ICRP Recommended Annual Limits (Occupational & Public)

| Category | Effective Dose Limit | Equivalent Dose Limit (Lens) | Equivalent Dose Limit (Skin, Hands, Feet) |
|----------|----------------------|------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Occupational Worker** | **20 mSv/yr** (averaged over 5 yrs) | **20 mSv/yr** | **500 mSv/yr** |
| **Trainee (<18 yrs)** | **6 mSv/yr** | **20 mSv/yr** | **150 mSv/yr** |
| **General Public** | **1 mSv/yr** | **15 mSv/yr** | **50 mSv/yr** |
| **Embryo/Fetus (occupational)** | **1 mSv** (over pregnancy) | — | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **The lens of the eye limit was revised downward from 150 mSv/yr to 20 mSv/yr (ICRP 2011 Statement on Tissue Reactions)** because of observed cataract formation at much lower doses than previously believed. This is a frequent KTU short-answer question.

## 3.9 Typical Effective Doses from Common Medical Imaging Procedures

| Procedure | Typical Effective Dose (mSv) | Equivalent to: |
|-----------|------------------------------|----------------|
| Chest X-ray (PA) | **0.02** | 2–3 days of natural background |
| Dental X-ray | 0.005–0.01 | 1 day of natural background |
| Mammography (2 views) | 0.4 | 7 weeks of natural background |
| Abdomen X-ray | 0.7 | 3 months of natural background |
| CT Head | **2** | 8 months of natural background |
| CT Chest | 7 | 2 years of natural background |
| CT Abdomen/Pelvis | **10–20** | 3–7 years of natural background |
| Coronary Angiography | 5–15 | 2–5 years of natural background |
| Nuclear Bone Scan (Tc-99m) | 4–5 | 1.5–2 years of natural background |
| PET-CT (F-18 FDG) | **15–25** | 5–8 years of natural background |
| Natural Background (annual) | **2–3** | — |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "ALARA". Explain its significance in diagnostic radiology practice.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

ALARA stands for **"As Low As Reasonably Achievable"** and is the central optimization principle of the ICRP System of Radiological Protection. It requires that all radiation exposures — to patients, staff, and the public — be kept as low as is reasonably achievable, taking into account economic and social factors.

In diagnostic radiology, ALARA is implemented by:
- Using the **lowest diagnostic kVp and mAs** that produces an image of acceptable quality.
- Applying **beam collimation** to limit the irradiated volume.
- Using **pulsed fluoroscopy** instead of continuous fluoroscopy.
- Employing **Automatic Exposure Control (AEC)** and **iterative reconstruction** in CT.
- Using **protective shielding** (lead aprons, thyroid shields, gonadal shields).
- Maintaining **distance** during exposures (e.g., stepping back during fluoroscopy).

ALARA is significant because it ensures the **diagnostic benefit of imaging is delivered with minimum stochastic and deterministic risk**, supporting the ethical principle of *primum non nocere* (first, do no harm) in medical practice.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between stochastic and deterministic effects of radiation. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Stochastic Effects | Deterministic Effects (Tissue Reactions) |
|---------|-------------------|------------------------------------------|
| Dose-response | **No threshold**; probability rises with dose | **Threshold dose** below which effect is not seen |
| Severity | Independent of dose | **Severity increases with dose** |
| Onset | Latent (years to decades) | Early (days to weeks) |
| Mechanism | DNA damage → cancer / genetic mutation | Cell killing → tissue / organ dysfunction |
| Example | Radiation-induced leukemia, breast cancer | Skin erythema, cataract, radiation sickness, epilation |

**One example each:** Stochastic — *radiation-induced thyroid cancer*; Deterministic — *skin erythema at 2 Gy*.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions — Module Internal Choice (Choose ANY ONE)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Module 4 Internal Choice]
**(a)** Define **absorbed dose, equivalent dose, and effective dose**. Derive the relationship between them with proper SI units. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The dose rate at 1 meter from a radioactive point source is **150 μSv/hr**. Calculate the dose rate at (i) 2 m, (ii) 4 m, and (iii) 0.5 m. State the law used. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION

### Part (a) — Definition and Relationship

**Absorbed Dose (D):** The energy deposited by ionizing radiation per unit mass of the absorbing medium.

$$D = \frac{d\bar{\varepsilon}}{dm}$$

**Unit:** Gray (Gy), where **1 Gy = 1 J/kg**.

**Equivalent Dose (Hₜ):** The absorbed dose multiplied by the radiation weighting factor ($w_R$), which accounts for the differing biological damage caused by different radiation types for the same absorbed dose.

$$H_T = \sum_R w_R \cdot D_{T,R}$$

**Unit:** Sievert (Sv). (1 Sv = 1 J/kg biologically weighted)

**Effective Dose (E):** The sum of the equivalent doses to all irradiated tissues, each multiplied by the corresponding tissue weighting factor ($w_T$), which represents the radiation sensitivity and cancer risk of that organ.

$$E = \sum_T w_T \cdot H_T = \sum_T w_T \left( \sum_R w_R \cdot D_{T,R} \right)$$

**Unit:** Sievert (Sv).

**Key relationships:**
- $w_R = 1$ for X-rays, γ-rays, and β particles; $w_R = 20$ for α particles and neutrons (high-LET radiation).
- $\sum_T w_T = 1$ (tissue weighting factors are normalized).
- Conversion: **1 Sv = 100 rem**; **1 Gy = 100 rad** (legacy units).

These three quantities form a **chain**: absorbed dose captures *energy deposition*, equivalent dose captures *biological damage per unit energy*, and effective dose captures *whole-body stochastic risk*.

---

### Part (b) — Inverse Square Law Calculation

**Given:**
- Dose rate at $d_1 = 1$ m is $\dot{H}_1 = 150$ μSv/hr.
- Law to use: **Inverse Square Law**:

$$\frac{\dot{H}_1}{\dot{H}_2} = \left(\frac{d_2}{d_1}\right)^2 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \dot{H}(d) = \dot{H}_0 \cdot \left(\frac{d_0}{d}\right)^2$$

**Case (i):** $d_2 = 2$ m

$$\dot{H}_2 = 150 \times \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2 = 150 \times \frac{1}{4} = 37.5 \, \mu\text{Sv/hr}$$

**Case (ii):** $d_2 = 4$ m

$$\dot{H}_3 = 150 \times \left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^2 = 150 \times \frac{1}{16} = 9.375 \, \mu\text{Sv/hr}$$

**Case (iii):** $d_2 = 0.5$ m

$$\dot{H}_4 = 150 \times \left(\frac{1}{0.5}\right)^2 = 150 \times 4 = 600 \, \mu\text{Sv/hr}$$

**Results Summary:**
| Distance (m) | Dose Rate (μSv/hr) |
|--------------|---------------------|
| 0.5 | 600 |
| 1.0 | 150 (reference) |
| 2.0 | 37.5 |
| 4.0 | 9.375 |

**Conclusion:** Doubling the distance reduces the dose to one-fourth; halving the distance quadruples the dose — confirming the inverse square law.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Module 4 Internal Choice (Alternative)]
**(a)** State and explain the **three cardinal principles of external radiation protection**. Why is lead the preferred shielding material for diagnostic X-rays? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3 cm thickness of a shielding material reduces the radiation intensity from 1000 to 125 units. Calculate: (i) the linear attenuation coefficient (μ), (ii) the **Half-Value Layer (HVL)**, and (iii) the **Tenth-Value Layer (TVL)**. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION

### Part (a) — The Three Cardinal Principles of External Radiation Protection

The three cardinal principles (often remembered by the mnemonic **TDS — Time, Distance, Shielding**) form the operational core of external radiation protection.

**1. Time:**
The total dose received is directly proportional to the duration of exposure:
$$D \propto t$$
Therefore, **minimizing the time** in a radiation field proportionally reduces the dose. Practical examples include using the shortest fluoroscopy time, performing rapid radiography, and rotating staff in high-dose areas.

**2. Distance:**
For a point source, the radiation intensity follows the **inverse square law**:
$$I \propto \frac{1}{d^2}$$
**Maximizing the distance** between the source and the person dramatically reduces exposure. Doubling the distance reduces the dose to one-quarter. In fluoroscopy, the operator steps back from the patient (the main source of scatter) — ideally behind a shielded control booth.

**3. Shielding:**
Inserting an absorbing material between the source and the person attenuates the radiation exponentially:
$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$
The effectiveness of shielding depends on the material's **atomic number (Z)**, **density (ρ)**, and **thickness (x)**, and on the **photon energy**.

**Why lead is preferred for diagnostic X-rays:**

Lead is the shielding material of choice for diagnostic X-rays (30–120 keV) because of:
- **High atomic number (Z = 82)**, leading to a very high probability of **photoelectric absorption**, which scales approximately as $Z^3 / E^3$. At diagnostic energies, photoelectric absorption dominates, making lead extraordinarily efficient.
- **High density (ρ = 11.35 g/cm³)**, providing many atoms per unit volume.
- **Low cost, high malleability**, and easy fabrication into aprons, sheets, gloves, and leaded glass.
- For example, the HVL of lead for a 100 keV photon is about 0.27 mm, meaning a thin lead sheet is sufficient to halve the beam.

---

### Part (b) — Shielding Calculation

**Given:**
- Initial intensity $I_0 = 1000$ (arbitrary units)
- Transmitted intensity $I = 125$
- Shield thickness $x = 3$ cm

**Step 1 — Calculate the linear attenuation coefficient (μ):**

Using the exponential attenuation law:
$$I = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

Substitute values:
$$125 = 1000 \cdot e^{-\mu \times 3}$$

Divide both sides by 1000:
$$0.125 = e^{-3\mu}$$

Take the natural logarithm:
$$\ln(0.125) = -3\mu$$

We know $\ln(0.125) = \ln(1/8) = -3 \ln 2 = -3 \times 0.693 = -2.079$:

$$-2.079 = -3\mu$$

$$\mu = \frac{2.079}{3} = 0.693 \, \text{cm}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Half-Value Layer (HVL):**

Using the derived formula:
$$\text{HVL} = \frac{0.693}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{0.693} = 1.0 \, \text{cm}$$

**Verification:** One HVL of 1 cm reduces 1000 to 500; two HVLs reduce 500 to 250; three HVLs (3 cm) reduce 250 to 125 ✓

**Step 3 — Calculate the Tenth-Value Layer (TVL):**

Using the derived formula:
$$\text{TVL} = \frac{2.303}{\mu} = \frac{2.303}{0.693} \approx 3.32 \, \text{cm}$$

**Verification:** One TVL of 3.32 cm reduces 1000 to 100; our 3 cm shield reduces 1000 to 125, very close to one TVL ✓

**Summary of Results:**
| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Linear attenuation coefficient μ | **0.693 cm⁻¹** |
| Half-Value Layer (HVL) | **1.0 cm** |
| Tenth-Value Layer (TVL) | **3.32 cm** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes:** Forgetting to express μ in cm⁻¹ or m⁻¹ leads to dimensional errors. Always carry units through.
> 2. **Misapplying inverse square law:** The inverse square law applies to **point sources in free space**, not to wide beams with scatter.
> 3. **Conflating HVL and TVL:** HVL = 0.693/μ; TVL = 2.303/μ. TVL ≈ 3.32 × HVL. A common board error is swapping the constants.
> 4. **Dose quantity confusion:** Absorbed dose is in **Gy**, equivalent and effective dose are in **Sv**. Mixing units (e.g., writing equivalent dose in Gy) loses a mark.
> 5. **Forgetting the tissue/radiation weighting factors:** When asked for *equivalent* dose, multiply absorbed dose by $w_R$. When asked for *effective* dose, also multiply by $w_T$. Many students miss the second weighting.
> 6. **ALARA interpretation:** Do not describe ALARA merely as "use less radiation" — mention the role of **economic and social factors** and **justification** explicitly to score full marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme Examination**

- **Three Cardinal Principles:** Time (minimize), Distance (maximize, inverse square law), Shielding (attenuate via exponential law). Mnemonic: **TDS**.
- **ALARA:** As Low As Reasonably Achievable — the optimization pillar of ICRP.
- **Three Pillars of ICRP:** **Justification**, **Optimization (ALARA)**, **Dose Limitation**.
- **Dose Quantities Chain:** Absorbed Dose (Gy) → multiply by **w_R** → Equivalent Dose (Sv) → multiply by **w_T** → Effective Dose (Sv).
- **Conversions:** 1 Gy = 100 rad; 1 Sv = 100 rem.
- **w_R values:** X-rays, γ, β = 1; α, neutrons = 20.
- **Attenuation Law:** $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ — memorize and be able to derive.
- **HVL = 0.693 / μ**; **TVL = 2.303 / μ**; **TVL ≈ 3.32 × HVL**.
- **Inverse Square Law:** $I_1/I_2 = (d_2/d_1)^2$ — point sources only.
- **Dose Limits (annual):** Occupational = 20 mSv; Public = 1 mSv; Lens of eye = 20 mSv; Fetus = 1 mSv per pregnancy.
- **Lead (Z = 82, ρ = 11.35 g/cm³)** is preferred for diagnostic X-ray shielding due to high photoelectric absorption (scales as $Z^3/E^3$).
- **Stochastic Effects:** No threshold; probability increases with dose (e.g., cancer). **Deterministic Effects:** Threshold-dependent; severity increases with dose (e.g., skin erythema, cataract).
- **Personal Dosimeters:** TLD, OSL, Film Badge — used for monitoring occupational exposure.
- **ALARA Operationalization:** Source control → Environmental controls → Administrative/Procedural controls.
- **Radioactive Decay:** $A = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$; $T_{1/2} = 0.693 / \lambda$.
- **Typical Effective Doses (memorize order of magnitude):** Chest X-ray ≈ 0.02 mSv; CT Head ≈ 2 mSv; CT Abdomen ≈ 10 mSv; PET-CT ≈ 20 mSv.
- **Key Terminology:** HVL, TVL, μ, w_R, w_T, ALARA, ICRP, NCRP, BARC, AERB (regulatory bodies), stochastic, deterministic, somatic, genetic.
- **Photoelectric Effect** dominates at low energies and high Z — the basis of lead shielding and image contrast in diagnostic X-ray imaging.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Infrared Imaging and New Imaging Types: Infrared Imaging - Physics of Thermography - IR Detectors - Photon and Thermal Detectors - Thermal Uncooled IR Detectors: Resistive Microbolometers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Infrared Imaging (IR Imaging) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Infrared (IR) Imaging* is a non-invasive, non-ionizing, contactless diagnostic and analytical imaging modality that captures, quantifies, and visualizes the **spontaneous or induced thermal emission** of a subject in the **electromagnetic spectrum wavelength range of 0.7 μm to 1000 μm** by converting incident infrared photons (or absorbed thermal energy) into a corresponding electrical signal, which is then processed into a *thermogram* (a 2-D pseudo-color map of surface temperature distribution).

Infrared radiation occupies the EM spectrum region between the visible band (≈ 0.4–0.7 μm) and microwaves (≥ 1 mm). Because human skin is semi-opaque to visible light but radiates strongly in the **8–14 μm** *long-wave infrared (LWIR)* window (where the atmosphere is also transparent), the human body acts almost as a perfect *blackbody emitter* of IR energy.

### The IR Spectrum Sub-Bands (Engineering Reference)

| Sub-Band | Wavelength (μm) | Typical Source / Application |
|---|---|---|
| Near-Infrared (NIR) | 0.75 – 1.4 | Optical fiber communication, NIR spectroscopy |
| Short-Wave IR (SWIR) | 1.4 – 3 | Night vision, food inspection |
| Mid-Wave IR (MWIR) | 3 – 8 | Military targeting, gas sensing |
| Long-Wave IR (LWIR) | 8 – 15 | **Medical thermography, fever screening** |
| Far-Infrared (FIR) | 15 – 1000 | Spectroscopy, astronomical observation |

## 1.2 Physics of Thermography — The Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermography** is the *science and art of mapping temperature* by detecting emitted infrared energy. All objects with a temperature above **absolute zero (0 K or −273.15 °C)** continuously emit IR radiation per Planck's law.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Human as a Light Bulb"
Imagine a person standing in a completely dark room. You cannot see them because they emit **no visible light**. However, your palm held near their face would feel warmth — that warmth is *infrared radiation*. A thermal camera is essentially a *device that "sees" this invisible warmth* and paints a color picture where hotter regions glow brighter (or in false-color palettes like *ironbow, rainbow, grayscale*).

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Fundamental Laws Govern Thermography:**
> 1. **Planck's Radiation Law** — Describes the spectral exitance (emitted power per unit area per unit wavelength) of a blackbody.
> 2. **Stefan–Boltzmann Law** — States the *total* radiated power scales with the **fourth power of absolute temperature**: $M = \sigma T^{4}$.

This is why even a 1 °C change in skin temperature produces a measurable change in emitted power:
$$\frac{\Delta M}{M} \approx 4 \frac{\Delta T}{T}$$
At T = 305 K (skin), a ΔT of just 1 K changes *M* by **~1.3%** — enough to be detected by modern microbolometers.

### 1.3 Active vs. Passive Thermography (KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight)

| Type | Energy Source | Medical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Passive Thermography** | Natural body heat (endogenous) | Tumor detection, vascular disorders, fever screening |
| **Active Thermography** | External stimulus (laser, halogen flash, ultrasound) | Burn-depth assessment, dental caries, breast cancer contrast-enhanced imaging |

### 1.4 Blackbody Radiation — The Foundation

A **blackbody** is an idealized perfect absorber and emitter of radiation. The human body, with emissivity **ε ≈ 0.98** in the LWIR band, behaves nearly as one. The spectral radiant exitance is given by:

$$M_{\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{h c}{\lambda k_{B} T}\right) - 1 \right]^{-1} \quad \text{[W · m}^{-2}\text{ · m}^{-1}\text{]}$$

**Constants Used Throughout This Module:**
- Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ **J·s**
- Speed of light: $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s**
- Boltzmann constant: $k_{B} = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**
- Stefan–Boltzmann constant: $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ **W · m⁻² · K⁻⁴**
- Wien's displacement constant: $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ **m·K**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wien's Displacement Law** tells us where a blackbody of temperature T emits *most strongly*:
> $$\lambda_{\text{max}} = \frac{b}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{T} \quad \text{[m]}$$
> For human skin (T ≈ 305 K): **λ_max ≈ 9.5 μm** — squarely in the LWIR atmospheric window (8–14 μm), explaining why medical thermography uses this band.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IR Detector Classification — The Master Taxonomy

> [!NOTE]
> **All IR detectors fall into one of two major families**, distinguished by the physical mechanism by which the absorbed IR energy is converted into an electrical signal.

```
IR DETECTORS
├── 1. PHOTON DETECTORS  (Quantum Detectors)
│   ├── Photoconductive (PC)
│   ├── Photovoltaic (PV)
│   └── Photoemissive (PE)
│
└── 2. THERMAL DETECTORS
    ├── Bolometer (resistive microbolometer)
    ├── Thermopile / Thermocouple
    ├── Pyroelectric
    └── Golay Cell
```

## 2.2 Photon Detectors (Quantum Detectors) — Deep Analysis

### Operating Principle (Why and How)
1. **Why:** Incoming IR photons with energy $E_{ph} = h\nu = hc/\lambda$ *greater than* the semiconductor bandgap $E_{g}$ are absorbed.
2. **How:** The absorbed photon promotes an electron from the valence band to the conduction band, generating free charge carriers that alter the electrical conductivity (PC), generate a voltage at a p-n junction (PV), or eject an electron from a photocathode (PE).

### Photoconductive (PC) Detectors
- Photon absorption → change in electrical conductivity.
- **Material examples:** Lead sulfide (PbS) for SWIR, Mercury Cadmium Telluride (HgCdTe or MCT) for MWIR/LWIR, Indium Antimonide (InSb) for MWIR.
- Must be **cryogenically cooled** (e.g., 77 K liquid nitrogen for MCT) to suppress thermally generated carriers (dark current).
- **Advantage:** Very fast response (ns to μs), high detectivity.

### Photovoltaic (PV) Detectors
- Operate as reverse-biased photodiodes. The absorbed photon creates an electron–hole pair which is separated by the junction's built-in field, producing a photocurrent.
- Operate in **photovoltaic mode** (zero bias) for low noise or **photoconductive mode** (reverse bias) for high speed.
- **Material examples:** InGaAs (NIR/SWIR), MCT (MWIR/LWIR), Quantum Well Infrared Photodetectors (QWIPs), Type-II Superlattices (T2SL).

### Photoemissive (PE) Detectors
- Photon strikes a photocathode; if $h\nu \geq \phi$ (work function), an electron is ejected into vacuum and accelerated to an anode.
- Example: Photomultiplier tubes (PMTs) for UV/visible; image intensifiers.

### 2.3 Thermal Detectors — Deep Analysis

### Operating Principle (Why and How)
1. **Why:** Incoming IR energy is *absorbed and converted to heat*, raising the detector's temperature.
2. **How:** The temperature rise $\Delta T$ is measured by a temperature-dependent property:
   - Resistance change → **Bolometer**
   - Seebeck voltage → **Thermopile**
   - Spontaneous polarization change → **Pyroelectric**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Difference vs. Photon Detectors:** Thermal detectors respond to *total absorbed power* (broadband), are **wavelength-independent**, and **do not require cryogenic cooling** — making them cheaper, smaller, and ideal for uncooled portable medical cameras.

### 2.4 Sub-Types of Thermal Detectors

| Detector Type | Physical Mechanism | Typical Material | Medical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermopile** | Seebeck effect (junction EMF) | Bi₂Te₃/Sb₂Te₃, Chromel–Alumel | Ear thermometers, contact sensors |
| **Pyroelectric** | Spontaneous polarization changes with T | LiTaO₃, PZT ceramics | Motion sensors, MWIR cameras |
| **Golay Cell** | Gas expansion deflects a membrane | Xe / N₂ gas | Far-IR spectroscopy |
| **Bolometer (Micro)** | Resistance changes with T | VOx, a-Si, SiGe, YBaCuO | **Uncooled LWIR imaging** |

## 2.5 Thermal Uncooled IR Detectors — Resistive Microbolometers (Module 4 Core)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Module 4 Exact):**
> A *resistive microbolometer* is a *MEMS-fabricated, suspended, thermally-isolated pixel* whose **electrical resistance changes with temperature** when absorbing incident infrared radiation. The change in resistance is read out as a voltage signal by an underlying CMOS *Read-Out Integrated Circuit (ROIC)*.

### Pixel Architecture
A single microbolometer pixel is a **two-level MEMS bridge**:
- **Top level:** A thin-film IR *absorber* (often VOx, a-Si, or a tuned metamaterial) supported on a *thermistor* (the temperature-sensitive resistor).
- **Bottom level:** A CMOS ROIC (Read-Out Integrated Circuit) on the silicon substrate.
- **Suspension:** Two thin *support legs* that anchor the pixel to the substrate. These legs are designed to have **low thermal conductance ℾ** (for high sensitivity) and **moderate electrical conductivity** (for readout).
- **Optical cavity:** A quarter-wavelength vacuum gap separates the pixel from the substrate to form a *Fabry–Pérot resonant absorber*, maximizing absorption in the 8–14 μm band.

### Key Performance Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature Coefficient of Resistance (TCR) — α** is the *figure of merit* of the bolometer material.
> $$\alpha = \frac{1}{R} \frac{dR}{dT} \quad \text{[K}^{-1}\text{ or \%/K]}$$
> Typical values: **VOx: α ≈ −2 to −3 %/K**; **a-Si: α ≈ −2.5 to −5 %/K**; **SiGe: α ≈ −2 to −3 %/K**.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spectral exitance (Planck) | $M_{\lambda} = \frac{2\pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}}\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{hc}{\lambda k_{B} T}\right)-1\right]^{-1}$ | W·m⁻²·m⁻¹ | Power emitted per unit area, per unit wavelength |
| 2 | Stefan–Boltzmann | $M = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ | W·m⁻² | Total emitted power per unit area |
| 3 | Wien's Displacement | $\lambda_{\max} = b/T$ | m | Peak emission wavelength |
| 4 | Bolometer Resistance vs. T | $R(T) = R_{0}\,[1 + \alpha \Delta T]$ | Ω | Linearized resistance change |
| 5 | TCR | $\alpha = \frac{1}{R}\frac{dR}{dT}$ | K⁻¹ | Material sensitivity |
| 6 | Responsivity | $\mathcal{R} = \frac{V_{s}}{P_{\text{in}}} = \frac{\alpha \eta V_{b} I_{b} R_{0}\varepsilon_{th}}{\sqrt{(1+\omega^{2}\tau^{2})(G_{th}^{2}+\omega^{2}C_{th}^{2})}}$ | V/W | Output voltage per incident watt |
| 7 | Thermal Time Constant | $\tau_{th} = C_{th}/G_{th}$ | s | Speed of response |
| 8 | Noise-Equivalent Power | $\text{NEP} = V_{n}/\mathcal{R}$ | W | Minimum detectable power |
| 9 | Specific Detectivity | $D^{\ast} = \frac{\sqrt{A \cdot \Delta f}}{\text{NEP}}$ | cm·Hz⁰·⁵·W⁻¹ or Jones | Normalized SNR figure of merit |
| 10 | NETD (Noise-Equivalent ΔT) | $\text{NETD} = \frac{4 F^{2} \sqrt{k_{B} T^{2} C_{th}}}{\alpha \eta \tau_{opt} A_{d} \varepsilon \sigma T^{3}}$ | K | Smallest detectable temperature difference |
| 11 | Resistance change | $\Delta R = R_{0}\,\alpha\,\Delta T$ | Ω | Direct output driver |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formulas above, $\varepsilon_{th} = \varepsilon_{r}\varepsilon_{a}$ is the *thermal efficiency* (radiative × absorptive), $V_{b}$ is the bias voltage, $I_{b}$ the bias current, $G_{th}$ the thermal conductance, $C_{th}$ the thermal capacitance, $\eta$ the absorptance, $F$ the lens f-number, $\tau_{opt}$ the optical transmission, and $A_{d}$ the pixel area.

### 2.6 Why Microbolometers Are the *Workhorse* of Modern Medical Thermography

1. **Uncooled operation** — eliminates bulky, expensive cryocoolers; enables pocket-sized cameras.
2. **LWIR sensitivity** (8–14 μm) — matches the human body emission peak.
3. **NETD < 50 mK** — sufficient to detect 0.1 °C skin changes (cancer screening, vascular disease).
4. **CMOS-compatible MEMS fabrication** — wafer-scale, low-cost mass production.
5. **Real-time video (30–60 Hz)** — dynamic thermal studies (breath, perfusion).
6. **Solid-state robustness** — no moving parts, low maintenance.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Emitted Power from Planck's Law (Stefan–Boltzmann)

The total power emitted per unit area is the integral of the spectral exitance over all wavelengths:

$$M(T) = \int_{0}^{\infty} M_{\lambda}(\lambda, T)\, d\lambda = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2\pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{hc}{\lambda k_{B} T}\right)-1\right]^{-1} d\lambda$$

**Step 1 — Substitute variable.** Let $x = \frac{hc}{\lambda k_{B} T}$, so that $\lambda = \frac{hc}{x k_{B} T}$ and $d\lambda = -\frac{hc}{x^{2} k_{B} T}\, dx$.

**Step 2 — Transform limits.** When $\lambda \to 0$, $x \to \infty$; when $\lambda \to \infty$, $x \to 0$. The negative sign flips integration limits.

$$M(T) = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2\pi h c^{2}}{(hc/xk_{B}T)^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{x}-1} \cdot \frac{hc}{x^{2} k_{B} T}\, dx$$

**Step 3 — Simplify powers of $\lambda$.** $(hc/xk_{B}T)^{5} = h^{5}c^{5}\,x^{-5}\,k_{B}^{-5}\,T^{-5}$.

$$M(T) = \frac{2\pi h c^{2}\,x^{5} k_{B}^{5} T^{5}}{h^{5}c^{5}} \cdot \frac{hc}{k_{B} T} \cdot \frac{1}{x^{2}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{2}}{e^{x}-1}\, dx$$

**Step 4 — Collect constants.** The leading factor becomes $\frac{2\pi k_{B}^{4} T^{4}}{h^{3} c^{2}}$ (the $x$ powers cancel: $x^{5}/x^{2} = x^{3}$; careful re-simplification yields $x^{3}$, which is absorbed into the standard integral $I_{3}=\pi^{4}/15$).

$$M(T) = \frac{2\pi k_{B}^{4} T^{4}}{h^{3} c^{2}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x}-1}\, dx$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the standard Bose–Einstein integral.**
$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x}-1}\, dx = \Gamma(4)\,\zeta(4) = 6 \cdot \frac{\pi^{4}}{90} = \frac{\pi^{4}}{15}$$

**Step 6 — Combine.**
$$M(T) = \frac{2\pi^{5} k_{B}^{4}}{15\, h^{3} c^{2}}\, T^{4} = \sigma T^{4}$$

where $\sigma = \frac{2\pi^{5} k_{B}^{4}}{15\, h^{3} c^{2}} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-4}$ (proves Stefan–Boltzmann from Planck).

## 3.2 Derivation: Bolometer Response and Thermal Model

A microbolometer pixel is an *electrical–thermal* system. Define:
- $G_{th}$ = thermal conductance to substrate (W/K)
- $C_{th}$ = thermal capacitance (J/K)
- $P_{in}$ = absorbed incident IR power (W)
- $\Delta T$ = pixel temperature rise (K)

**Step 1 — Energy balance (thermal ODE).** Absorbed power heats the pixel; heat flows to the substrate.
$$C_{th}\,\frac{d(\Delta T)}{dt} + G_{th}\,\Delta T = \eta\, P_{in}(t)$$

**Step 2 — Solve for sinusoidal steady-state.** Let $P_{in}(t) = P_{0}\, e^{j\omega t}$.
$$\Delta T(\omega) = \frac{\eta P_{0}}{G_{th} + j\omega C_{th}} = \frac{\eta P_{0}}{G_{th}\sqrt{1+\omega^{2}\tau_{th}^{2}}}$$

where the **thermal time constant** is $\tau_{th} = C_{th}/G_{th}$.

**Step 3 — Resistance change.** With $R(T) = R_{0}\,(1+\alpha \Delta T)$:
$$\Delta R = R_{0}\,\alpha\,\Delta T = \frac{R_{0}\,\alpha\,\eta\, P_{0}}{G_{th}\sqrt{1+\omega^{2}\tau_{th}^{2}}}$$

**Step 4 — Output voltage (with bias $V_{b}$ or current $I_{b}$).** Using a Wheatstone bridge / differential readout, the output signal is:
$$V_{s} = I_{b}\,\Delta R = \frac{I_{b}\,R_{0}\,\alpha\,\eta\, P_{0}}{G_{th}\sqrt{1+\omega^{2}\tau_{th}^{2}}}$$

**Step 5 — Responsivity.**
$$\mathcal{R}(\omega) = \frac{V_{s}}{P_{0}} = \frac{\alpha\, \eta\, I_{b}\,R_{0}}{G_{th}\sqrt{1+\omega^{2}\tau_{th}^{2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Reducing $G_{th}$ (long thin support legs) **boosts responsivity** but **increases $\tau_{th}$** (slows response). The design trade-off is captured by the dimensionless figure of merit $F = \alpha/\sqrt{C_{th}\,G_{th}}$ — maximized in materials like VOx and a-Si.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Photon vs. Thermal Detectors (KTU 2024 Compare Table)

| Performance Aspect | Photon Detector (e.g., MCT) | Thermal Detector (e.g., Microbolometer) |
|---|---|---|
| Physical mechanism | Photon → e⁻/hole | Photon → heat → ΔR |
| Spectral sensitivity | **Wavelength-selective** (cutoff by $E_{g}$) | **Broadband / wavelength-independent** |
| Operating temperature | **Cryogenic** (77 K for LWIR MCT) | **Room temperature** (uncooled) |
| Detectivity $D^{\ast}$ | $\sim 10^{11}$ Jones (LWIR, cooled) | $\sim 10^{8}$ to $10^{9}$ Jones (uncooled) |
| Response time | **ns – μs** (fast) | **ms – tens of ms** (slow) |
| Cost & weight | High (cryocooler, vacuum dewar) | Low (no cooler, solid-state) |
| Medical use | Research-grade (very high-end) | **Clinical routine, portable, screening** |
| Power consumption | Watts (cooler) | mW (ROIC only) |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Wien's Law for Human Skin

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Compute the peak emission wavelength of human skin (assume T = 305.15 K).

**Step 1:** Apply Wien's Displacement Law.
$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{b}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m·K}}{305.15\,\text{K}}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate.
$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{305.15} = 9.498 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m} = 9.50\;\mu\text{m}$$

**Step 3:** Conclusion. The peak lies in the **LWIR atmospheric window (8–14 μm)** — the reason medical thermal cameras use germanium or chalcogenide lenses transparent in this band.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Bolometer Resistance Change

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A VOx microbolometer has $R_{0} = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$, TCR $\alpha = -0.025\;\text{K}^{-1}$. The absorbed IR power causes a temperature rise of $\Delta T = 0.05\,\text{K}$ (50 mK). Find the resistance change.

**Step 1:** Apply $R(T) = R_{0}\,(1+\alpha \Delta T)$.
$$R = 50{,}000 \times [1 + (-0.025)(0.05)]$$
$$R = 50{,}000 \times [1 - 0.00125] = 50{,}000 \times 0.99875 = 49{,}937.5\;\Omega$$

**Step 2:** Change in resistance.
$$\Delta R = R - R_{0} = -62.5\;\Omega$$

**Step 3:** Relative change. $\frac{\Delta R}{R_{0}} = -0.125\%$ — a tiny but *measurable* change, made possible by low-noise CMOS readout.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Differentiate between photon detectors and thermal detectors used in infrared imaging. List two medical applications of IR thermography.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Photon Detector | Thermal Detector |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Photon → e⁻–hole pair | Photon → heat → ΔR |
| Spectral response | Wavelength-selective (depends on $E_g$) | Broadband, wavelength-independent |
| Cooling | Requires cryogenic cooling | Operates at room temperature |
| Response time | ns – μs | ms |
| Example | HgCdTe (MCT), InSb | Microbolometer (VOx), Thermopile |

**Two medical applications** *(1 mark)*:
1. **Breast cancer screening** — tumors have higher metabolic rate and altered vascularity → surface temperature rise of 1–3 °C.
2. **Raynaud's phenomenon / peripheral vascular disease** — finger temperature asymmetry and post-cooling rewarming pattern.
   *(Acceptable alternatives: fever screening, burn depth assessment, diabetic foot imaging, surgical flap perfusion monitoring.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Define Temperature Coefficient of Resistance (TCR) for a microbolometer. State its typical value for VOx and a-Si.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **TCR (α)** is defined as the fractional change in resistance per unit change in temperature of the bolometer material:
> $$\alpha = \frac{1}{R}\,\frac{dR}{dT} \quad \text{[K}^{-1}\text{]}$$

It is the primary *figure of merit* governing the sensitivity of a resistive microbolometer — higher |α| ⇒ greater signal for a given temperature rise.

**Typical values:**
- **Vanadium Oxide (VOx):** $\alpha \approx -2$ to $-3$ %/K
- **Amorphous Silicon (a-Si):** $\alpha \approx -2.5$ to $-5$ %/K

A high |α|, low 1/f noise, and low thermal conductance $G_{th}$ are the three pillars of a high-performance microbolometer.
