## Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]

#### (a) **With the help of Planck's law, derive Stefan–Boltzmann's law of blackbody radiation. Show that human skin (T ≈ 305 K) emits maximum radiation near 9.5 μm. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State Planck's spectral exitance** *(1 mark)*:
$$M_{\lambda}(\lambda,T) = \frac{2\pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}}\,\frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k_{B}T}-1}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate over all wavelengths** to obtain total exitance $M(T) = \int_{0}^{\infty} M_{\lambda}\,d\lambda$.

**Step 3 — Substitute $x = hc/\lambda k_{B}T$** and reduce the integral to the standard form:
$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{x^{3}}{e^{x}-1}\,dx = \frac{\pi^{4}}{15}$$

**Step 4 — Arrive at** $M(T) = \sigma T^{4}$, where $\sigma = \frac{2\pi^{5}k_{B}^{4}}{15h^{3}c^{2}} = 5.670 \times 10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴. *(4 marks for derivation; see Section 3.1 of these notes for full algebra.)*

**Step 5 — Apply Wien's displacement law** for skin T = 305 K:
$$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{b}{T} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{305} = 9.50 \;\mu\text{m} \quad \text{✓} \quad (2\text{ marks})$$

#### (b) **Describe the construction, working principle, and key performance parameters of a resistive microbolometer. Why is it the preferred IR detector for clinical thermography? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (2 marks):** A resistive microbolometer is a MEMS-fabricated pixel consisting of (i) a thin-film IR absorber (e.g., VOx, 50–100 nm), (ii) a thermistor layer (VOx or a-Si) with high TCR, (iii) two thin support legs of SiN$_x$ or SiO$_2$ for thermal isolation from the silicon substrate, and (iv) a quarter-wave vacuum Fabry–Pérot cavity beneath. A CMOS ROIC beneath each pixel reads out the resistance change using a bias current.

**Working Principle (2 marks):** IR photons in the 8–14 μm band are absorbed by the pixel, raising its temperature by $\Delta T$. The temperature rise causes a change in resistance $\Delta R = R_0 \alpha \Delta T$. With a bias current $I_b$ flowing, the output voltage is $V_s = I_b \Delta R = I_b R_0 \alpha \Delta T$. This voltage is digitized and rendered as a thermogram.

**Key Performance Parameters (2 marks):**
- TCR (α) [%-K⁻¹]
- Responsivity 𝓡 [V/W]
- Noise-Equivalent Power (NEP) [W]
- Specific detectivity D* [cm·Hz⁰·⁵·W⁻¹]
- Noise-Equivalent Temperature Difference (NETD) [mK] — typically < 50 mK
- Thermal time constant τ_th = C_th/G_th [ms]

**Why Preferred (1 mark):** Uncooled (no cryocooler), room-temperature operation, broadband LWIR sensitivity matching human body peak, CMOS-compatible low-cost mass fabrication, real-time video, pocket-sized form factor, and < 50 mK NETD sufficient for clinical use.

---

### Question B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]

#### (a) **Classify infrared detectors with a neat block diagram. Compare photon and thermal detectors in terms of response time, cooling requirement, and medical applicability. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification (3 marks):** Two families — *(i) Photon / Quantum detectors* (photoconductive, photovoltaic, photoemissive) and *(ii) Thermal detectors* (bolometer, thermopile, pyroelectric, Golay cell). *(Refer to the Mermaid taxonomy in Section 4.3 of these notes.)*

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Property | Photon Detector | Thermal Detector |
|---|---|---|
| Response time | ns – μs (fast) | ms (slow) |
| Cooling | Cryogenic (LN₂ 77 K) | Uncooled / room T |
| Medical applicability | Research / specialty (very high-end infrared microscopy) | Routine clinical thermography, screening, point-of-care |

**Key Trade-off (1 mark):** Photon detectors win on speed and sensitivity; thermal detectors win on cost, size, and clinical practicality. For most medical thermography the trade-off favours the microbolometer.

#### (b) **Explain the physics of medical thermography. Discuss the difference between passive and active thermography with two examples each. State the role of the LWIR (8–14 μm) atmospheric window. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Physics of Thermography (2 marks):** Thermography is the imaging of surface temperature distribution using emitted IR radiation. Every object at T > 0 K emits IR energy. Per Stefan–Boltzmann, $M = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$, and per Wien's law, a 305 K body peaks near 9.5 μm. A thermal camera captures this emission, converts it to a temperature map (thermogram), and displays it in false color.

**Passive vs Active (3 marks):**

| Type | Energy Source | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Passive** | Endogenous body heat only | 1. Breast cancer screening (tumor hypermetabolism) 2. Vascular disorders (Raynaud's, deep-vein thrombosis) 3. Fever screening (COVID, influenza) |
| **Active** | External stimulus + IR imaging | 1. Pulsed-phase thermography for burn depth 2. Dynamic angiothermography with cold-stress test 3. Laser-assisted breast thermography (laser Doppler + IR) |

**LWIR Atmospheric Window (2 marks):** Water vapor and CO₂ in air absorb most IR radiation *except* in two key windows: 3–5 μm (MWIR) and **8–14 μm (LWIR)**. Human skin emission peaks at 9.5 μm — squarely in LWIR. Hence LWIR thermal cameras see the human body with minimal atmospheric attenuation, even at 1–2 m working distances, making them ideal for **non-contact, point-of-care clinical imaging**.

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing Stefan–Boltzmann with Planck's law.** Planck's law gives *spectral* exitance (per wavelength); Stefan–Boltzmann gives *total* (integrated) exitance. Marks are lost if the integration is skipped or the constant $\sigma$ is not derived.
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign in TCR for VOx.** VOx is a *negative-TCR* material (resistance falls as T rises because it's a semiconductor). Writing $R = R_0(1-\vert\alpha\vert\Delta T)$ is correct; writing $R = R_0(1+\alpha\Delta T)$ without the sign will lose a mark.
> 3. **Quoting NETD in °C instead of mK.** Modern microbolometers achieve < 50 mK; saying "0.05 °C" or worse "5 °C" loses easy credit.
> 4. **Mixing up Wien's displacement units.** $b$ is in **m·K**, so $\lambda_{max}$ in meters; converting to μm is required.
> 5. **Calling a microbolometer "cooled".** It is the *defining advantage* of a microbolometer that it is **uncooled**. Misclassifying it will cost the comparison-table marks in Part B.
> 6. **Omitting the emissivity $\varepsilon$ in real-world thermography.** A human has $\varepsilon \approx 0.98$, but a polished metal surface is $\varepsilon \approx 0.05$ — wrong $\varepsilon$ gives wrong temperature.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **IR spectrum** spans **0.7–1000 μm**; medical thermography uses the **LWIR 8–14 μm** window.
- **Planck's law** = spectral exitance. **Stefan–Boltzmann** $M=\sigma T^{4}$ = total. **Wien** $\lambda_{max}=b/T$. For skin (305 K) → 9.5 μm.
- **Two families of IR detectors:** *Photon* (PC, PV, PE — fast, cooled, wavelength-selective) and *Thermal* (bolometer, thermopile, pyroelectric — slow, uncooled, broadband).
- **Microbolometer = uncooled thermal detector** using the **TCR (α)** of a thin-film resistor (VOx, a-Si, SiGe).
- **Pixel structure:** absorber + thermistor on a thermally-isolated MEMS bridge over a Fabry–Pérot vacuum cavity, read out by a CMOS ROIC.
- **Sensitivity ranking of material:** high |α|, low 1/f noise, low thermal conductance $G_{th}$. The figure of merit $F = \alpha/\sqrt{C_{th}G_{th}}$ must be maximized.
- **Resistance change:** $\Delta R = R_0\,\alpha\,\Delta T$. **Output:** $V_s = I_b\,R_0\,\alpha\,\Delta T / (1 + \omega^2\tau_{th}^2)^{0.5}$. **Time constant:** $\tau_{th}=C_{th}/G_{th}$.
- **Performance metrics:** Responsivity 𝓡 [V/W], NEP [W], D* [Jones], **NETD < 50 mK** for clinical-grade sensors.
- **Trade-off:** Lower $G_{th}$ (thinner, longer legs) → higher responsivity but slower response.
- **Medical applications:** breast cancer screening, vascular disease (Raynaud's, DVT), burn depth, fever screening, surgical flap perfusion, diabetic foot, dental inflammation, neonatal monitoring.
- **Active thermography** uses external stimulus; **passive** uses only endogenous heat.
- **Key constant values to memorize for the exam:** $h = 6.626\times10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3\times10^8$ m/s, $k_B = 1.381\times10^{-23}$ J/K, $\sigma = 5.67\times10^{-8}$ W·m⁻²·K⁻⁴, $b = 2.898\times10^{-3}$ m·K.
- **Always** include **emissivity** when computing real-body exitance: $M = \varepsilon\sigma T^{4}$.
- **Microbolometer ≠ cryocooled MCT** — that is the central engineering distinction of Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pyroelectric and Ferroelectric Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pyroelectric and Ferroelectric Detectors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pyroelectric Detectors — Formal Definition

A **pyroelectric detector** is a thermal transducer that converts incident electromagnetic radiation (typically in the mid- to far-infrared region) into a measurable electrical signal by exploiting the **pyroelectric effect** — the linear change in spontaneous electric polarization of certain non-centrosymmetric crystals when their temperature is altered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Pyroelectric and ferroelectric detectors are discussed under the broader family of *thermal detectors* (alongside bolometers and thermopiles), and they occupy a unique niche in **medical thermal imaging** because they require no cryogenic cooling and respond to **AC (modulated) radiation** — a property that makes them practical and economical for clinical use.

The governing physical relation is the **pyroelectric coefficient**:

$$
p \;=\; \dfrac{dP_s}{dT} \;\; \bigl[\,\mu\text{C}\,/\,(\text{m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{K})\,\bigr]
$$

where $P_s$ is the spontaneous polarization and $T$ is the absolute temperature.

## 1.2 Ferroelectric Detectors — Formal Definition

A **ferroelectric material** is a subclass of pyroelectric materials in which the direction of the spontaneous polarization $P_s$ can be **reversed by an externally applied electric field**. This switching property is reflected in a characteristic **P–E hysteresis loop** below a critical temperature known as the **Curie temperature ($T_c$)**. Above $T_c$, the material transitions to a *paraelectric* state and loses its spontaneous polarization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid:** *Every* ferroelectric is a pyroelectric, but *not every* pyroelectric is a ferroelectric. The extra qualification is *reversible* spontaneous polarization.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

**The "Singing Crystal Spoon" Analogy:**

Imagine a crystal spoon lying on a table. The atoms inside are slightly displaced so that one face of the spoon is positively charged and the other negatively — even without any wires attached. This built-in charge separation is the **spontaneous polarization**.

- If you suddenly heat the spoon (touch a warm cup of tea), the atoms jiggle more, the charge separation shrinks, and a tiny current flows in an external wire connected across the spoon. This is the **pyroelectric effect** — a heat pulse produces electricity.
- If instead you apply a strong external voltage across the spoon, you can literally *flip* the charge pattern, like turning a magnet north-to-south. The "voltage needed to flip it" and the "leftover charge after you remove the voltage" define the **hysteresis loop** of a ferroelectric.

In **medical imaging**, this means a tiny chip of, say, **Lithium Tantalate (LiTaO₃)** can sense the faint infrared glow from a patient's skin, turning it into a thermographic map of inflammation, tumors, or vascular disorders.

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants and Material Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key figures of merit (must memorise for KTU 2024):**
> - **Curie temperature $T_c$** — the operating limit; the detector must be used **below** $T_c$.
> - **Pyroelectric coefficient $p$** — higher $p$ = higher sensitivity.
> - **Relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$** — lower $\varepsilon_r$ generally improves voltage responsivity.
> - **Figure of merit $F_v = p / (c' \cdot \varepsilon_r)$** — used to rank materials.
> - **Detectivity $D^*$** in **cm·Hz$^{1/2}$/W** and **NEP** in **W/Hz$^{1/2}$** — performance benchmarks.

Common detector materials and their representative properties (memorise at least the names):

| Material | Class | Approx. $T_c$ (°C) | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Triglycine Sulfate (TGS) | Pyro/Ferro | **49** | High-sensitivity lab IR |
| Deuterated TGS (DTGS) | Pyro/Ferro | **61** | Lower noise, broadband |
| Lithium Tantalate (LiTaO₃) | Pyro (non-ferro) | **618** | Room-temp IR detectors |
| Lead Zirconate Titanate (PZT) | Ferroelectric | **200 – 350** | Pyroelectric vidicon targets |
| Barium Strontium Titanate (BST) | Ferroelectric | Tunable | IR focal-plane arrays |
| Polyvinylidene Fluoride (PVDF) | Polymer Pyro | < 100 | Flexible large-area IR sensors |

## 1.5 Why These Detectors Matter in Medical Imaging

- **Pyroelectric vidicons** were historically used to image IR emission from the human body, producing **thermograms** showing temperature distribution for breast cancer screening, vascular disease, and inflammatory conditions.
- Because the human body emits peak IR radiation near **9.4 µm** (per Wien's law at $T \approx 310\,\text{K}$), detectors sensitive to the **8 – 14 µm atmospheric window** (where TGS and LiTaO₃ peak) are ideal.
- Unlike photon detectors (e.g., HgCdTe), they do **not** require cryogenic cooling, drastically reducing cost.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle of a Pyroelectric Detector — Step-by-Step

1. **Incident IR radiation** of radiant power $W(t)$ strikes the absorbing face of a thin pyroelectric crystal (typically 10 – 50 µm thick, area $A$).
2. The absorbed energy raises the crystal temperature by $\Delta T(t) = \eta W(t) / (c' A d)$ where $\eta$ is the absorption efficiency, $c'$ the volume specific heat (J/m³·K), and $d$ the thickness.
3. The temperature change alters the spontaneous polarization: $dP_s = p \cdot dT$.
4. The induced surface charge $dQ = A \cdot dP_s = A p \cdot dT$ must be neutralized by current flow through the external load.
5. The **pyroelectric current** is therefore:
   $$
   I_p \;=\; \dfrac{dQ}{dt} \;=\; A\,p\,\dfrac{dT}{dt}
   $$
6. Because $I_p \propto dT/dt$, the detector responds **only to modulated (chopped) radiation** — a stationary IR beam produces no signal. Chopping frequencies of 10 – 100 Hz are typical.
7. The signal voltage is $V_s = I_p \, Z$, where $Z$ is the input impedance of the readout electronics.

## 2.2 Why Modulation is Essential

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind AC-coupling:**
> A constant temperature produces a constant polarization — and a constant polarization produces no changing current. Only when $\Delta T$ is changing in time does the detector output a measurable signal. Medical IR cameras therefore use **mechanical choppers** or **electronic shutters** to modulate the incoming radiation.

## 2.3 Ferroelectric Hysteresis — Step-by-Step

1. Above $T_c$: the crystal is *paraelectric*, polarization $P$ is linear in applied field $E$ (no spontaneous polarization).
2. Below $T_c$: spontaneous polarization $P_s$ appears. A domain structure forms, with each domain having a switchable polarization direction.
3. Apply $E = 0$: domains remain oriented in their last direction; net polarization = $P_r$ (remanent polarization).
4. Increase $E$: domains align with $E$, polarization saturates at $P_s$.
5. Reduce $E$ to 0: polarization relaxes to $P_r$ (not zero).
6. Reverse $E$: polarization switches direction; the field at which $P = 0$ is the **coercive field $E_c$**.
7. Cycle: produces the **hysteresis loop** $P(E)$, area of which equals the energy dissipated per unit volume per cycle.

## 2.4 The Curie–Weiss Law

Just above $T_c$, the dielectric constant of a ferroelectric obeys:

$$
\varepsilon_r \;=\; \dfrac{C}{T - T_c}
$$

where $C$ is the **Curie constant** (material-dependent). This explains why ferroelectric detectors exhibit a **sharp permittivity peak** near $T_c$ — operation too close to $T_c$ leads to runaway capacitance, while operation well below $T_c$ gives stable performance.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory — every formula below is KTU 2024 exam-relevant. Memorise the symbol, unit, and physical meaning.**

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Quantity & Units | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p = dP_s/dT$ | $p$: $\mu\text{C}/(\text{m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{K})$ | Pyroelectric coefficient |
| 2 | $I_p = A\,p\,(dT/dt)$ | $I_p$: A, $A$: m², $dT/dt$: K/s | Pyroelectric current |
| 3 | $\Delta T(t) = \eta W(t)/(c' A d)$ | $\eta$: dimensionless, $W$: W, $c'$: J/(m³·K), $d$: m | Temperature rise from absorbed power |
| 4 | $R_v = p\,\omega\,A\,R/\bigl(C\,\sqrt{1+\omega^2\tau_e^2}\bigr)$ | $R_v$: V/W, $\omega$: rad/s, $R$: $\Omega$, $C$: F, $\tau_e$: s | Voltage responsivity |
| 5 | $R_i = p\,A\,/\,C$ | $R_i$: A/W | Current responsivity (flat in $\omega$) |
| 6 | $F_v = p/(c'\,\varepsilon_r)$ | $F_v$: dimensionless | Voltage figure of merit |
| 7 | $F_i = p/(c')$ | $F_i$: m/J | Current figure of merit |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_r = C/(T - T_c)$ | $\varepsilon_r$: dimensionless | Curie–Weiss law |
| 9 | $\text{NEP} = V_n/R_v$ | NEP: W/Hz$^{1/2}$, $V_n$: V/Hz$^{1/2}$ | Noise equivalent power |
| 10 | $D^{\ast} = \sqrt{A\,\Delta f}/\text{NEP}$ | $D^{\ast}$: cm·Hz$^{1/2}$/W | Specific detectivity |
| 11 | $\lambda_{\text{peak}}\,T = b = 2.898\times 10^{-3}$ m·K | Wien's law | Peak emission wavelength of human body ($\lambda_{\text{peak}} \approx 9.4\,\mu\text{m}$ at 310 K) |
| 12 | $P_s$ : spontaneous polarization, $P_r$ : remanent, $E_c$ : coercive | — | Hysteresis-loop landmark parameters |

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU trap:** Do *not* confuse **current responsivity** (flat vs. frequency, limited by thermal time constant) with **voltage responsivity** (rolls off at low *and* high $\omega$, peaks near $\omega = 1/\tau_e$).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pyroelectric vidicon tubes** (discontinued but historically important): a TGS or DTGS target plate was read out by an electron beam — the basis of early clinical thermography cameras (e.g., AGA Thermovision).
- **Uncooled focal-plane arrays (UFPA)**: modern BST or PZT micro-bolometer/pixel arrays used in handheld thermal cameras for **fever screening, vascular imaging, and burn assessment** — directly relevant to medical imaging.
- **Pyroelectric thin-film sensors** (LiTaO₃, PVDF) in **laser power meters** and **pulsed-laser surgical systems** for measuring mid-IR CO₂ and Er:YAG laser energy delivered to tissue.
- **Non-destructive evaluation** of tissues and materials via **photothermal radiometry**, where a chopped laser pulse induces a periodic temperature oscillation in the sample detected pyroelectrically.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Pyroelectric Current $I_p$

**Starting point:** A thin slab of pyroelectric crystal of area $A$ and thickness $d$ has uniform spontaneous polarization $P_s$ along the polar (z) axis.

**Step 1.** The bound surface charge density on each face is $\sigma_b = P_s$.

**Step 2.** When temperature changes by $dT$, the polarization changes by:
$$
dP_s \;=\; p \cdot dT
$$

**Step 3.** The corresponding change in bound surface charge on one face is:
$$
d\sigma_b \;=\; p \cdot dT
$$

**Step 4.** The total induced charge is the integral of $d\sigma_b$ over area:
$$
dQ \;=\; A \cdot d\sigma_b \;=\; A\,p \cdot dT
$$

**Step 5.** Differentiate with respect to time to obtain the **pyroelectric current**:
$$
I_p \;=\; \dfrac{dQ}{dt} \;=\; A\,p\,\dfrac{dT}{dt}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The current is proportional to the *rate of change* of temperature, not the temperature itself. This is why a chopped IR source is mandatory.

## 3.2 Derivation of Voltage Responsivity $R_v$

**Step 1.** The crystal behaves as a capacitor $C$ in parallel with the current source $I_p$:
$$
C \;=\; \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}
$$

**Step 2.** The crystal is also a thermal element with heat capacity $H = c' A d$ and thermal conductance $G_T$ to the surroundings, giving a thermal time constant:
$$
\tau_T \;=\; \dfrac{H}{G_T} \;=\; \dfrac{c' A d}{G_T}
$$

**Step 3.** The combined electrical + thermal circuit transfer function from input power $W$ to output voltage $V_s$ is:
$$
\dfrac{V_s(\omega)}{W(\omega)} \;=\; \dfrac{p\,A\,\omega\,R}{\sqrt{\bigl(1 + \omega^2 R^2 C^2\bigr)\bigl(1 + \omega^2 \tau_T^2\bigr)}}
$$

**Step 4.** Define the **electrical time constant** $\tau_e = RC$. For most detectors $\tau_e \gg \tau_T$ so the thermal roll-off is negligible in the band of interest. Simplifying:

$$
R_v \;=\; \dfrac{V_s}{W} \;=\; \dfrac{p\,A\,\omega\,R}{C\sqrt{1 + \omega^2\tau_e^2}}
$$

**Step 5.** In the operating band $\omega\tau_e \gg 1$, this further reduces to the plateau form:
$$
R_v^{\text{(sat)}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{p\,A}{C} \;=\; \dfrac{p\,d}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}
$$

This shows that to maximise voltage responsivity, we want **high $p$**, **low $\varepsilon_r$**, and **thin** crystals — exactly the design trade-off for TGS and LiTaO₃.

## 3.3 Derivation of Curie–Weiss Behaviour

**Step 1.** Below $T_c$, free energy has two minima at $\pm P_s$. Above $T_c$, the minimum is at $P = 0$.

**Step 2.** Expanding the free energy $G(P,T)$ as a Landau series:
$$
G(P,T) \;=\; G_0 + \alpha(T - T_c)P^2 + \beta P^4 + \gamma P^6 + \dots
$$

**Step 3.** Setting $\partial G / \partial P = 0$ and $P = 0$ above $T_c$ (no spontaneous polarization):
$$
P \;=\; 0,\quad \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_0(\varepsilon_r - 1)} \;\propto\; (T - T_c)
$$

**Step 4.** For $T \gg T_c$ the constant of proportionality is $1/C$, yielding:
$$
\varepsilon_r \;\approx\; \dfrac{C}{T - T_c}
$$

This is the **Curie–Weiss law**, explaining the dielectric anomaly that engineering students must recognise in graphs of $\varepsilon_r$ vs. $T$.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison: Pyroelectric vs. Ferroelectric vs. Other Thermal Detectors

| Parameter | Pyroelectric | Thermopile | Bolometer | Photoconductor (HgCdTe) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output type | Current/Voltage (AC) | DC Voltage | Resistance change | Current (DC) |
| Spectral response | Broad (UV → far-IR) | Broad | Broad | Narrow bandgap-limited |
| Operating temp | **Room temp** | Room temp | Room temp (or cooled) | **Cryogenic (77 K)** |
| Response time | **µs – ms** | 10 – 100 ms | 1 – 100 ms | ns – µs |
| NEP (typical) | $10^{-9}$ W/Hz$^{1/2}$ | $10^{-10}$ W/Hz$^{1/2}$ | $10^{-11}$ W/Hz$^{1/2}$ | $10^{-13}$ W/Hz$^{1/2}$ |
| Cooling required | **No** | No | No | **Yes** |
| Best for medical use | Thermography, IR cameras, laser meters | Calorimetry, slow temp. sensing | Radiometers, thermal cameras | High-speed spectroscopy |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU favourite question:** "Why is a pyroelectric detector preferred over a photoconductive detector in clinical thermography?" — the expected answer combines **(i)** no cryogenics → cheaper & portable, **(ii)** broad spectral response → covers 8–14 µm human emission, **(iii)** fast response → suitable for chopped clinical use.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A LiTaO₃ pyroelectric detector has area $A = 1\,\text{mm}^2 = 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2$, thickness $d = 25\,\mu\text{m}$, relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r = 43$, and pyroelectric coefficient $p = 1.8 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{C}\,(\text{m}^2\!\cdot\!\text{K})^{-1}$. The incident chopped IR beam delivers average power $W_0 = 100\,\mu\text{W}$ at chopping frequency $f = 30\,\text{Hz}$ with 50% duty cycle. Compute the peak voltage responsivity and the peak output voltage.

**Solution — Step 1.** Compute capacitance:
$$
C \;=\; \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} \;=\; \dfrac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(43)(10^{-6})}{25 \times 10^{-6}} \;\approx\; 1.52 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{F} \;=\; 15.2\,\text{pF}
$$

**Step 2.** With a high-impedance FET pre-amp ($R = 10^{11}\,\Omega$), electrical time constant:
$$
\tau_e \;=\; R\,C \;=\; (10^{11})(15.2 \times 10^{-12}) \;\approx\; 1.52\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 3.** Chopping angular frequency:
$$
\omega \;=\; 2\pi f \;=\; 2\pi(30) \;\approx\; 188.5\,\text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 4.** Voltage responsivity:
$$
R_v \;=\; \dfrac{p\,\omega\,A\,R}{C\,\sqrt{1 + \omega^2\tau_e^2}} \;\approx\; \dfrac{p\,\omega\,A\,R}{C\,(\omega\tau_e)} \;=\; \dfrac{p\,A}{C}
$$

$$
R_v \;\approx\; \dfrac{(1.8 \times 10^{-5})(10^{-6})}{1.52 \times 10^{-11}} \;\approx\; 1.18\,\text{V/W} \;=\; 1180\,\text{V/W}
$$

**Step 5.** Peak output voltage:
$$
V_{s,\text{peak}} \;\approx\; R_v \cdot W_0 \;\approx\; (1.18\,\text{V/W})(100 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{W}) \;\approx\; 1.18 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{V} \;\approx\; 118\,\mu\text{V}
$$

A practical detector would be followed by a low-noise pre-amp with gain ~$10^{4}$ to bring this into the millivolt range for digitisation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pyroelectric IR Camera

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Human Body IR Emission approx 9.4 micrometres] --> B[IR Optics Germanium or ZnSe Lens]
    B --> C[Mechanical Chopper Modulates Beam at 10 to 100 Hz]
    C --> D[Pyroelectric Detector LiTaO3 or TGS Crystal]
    D --> E[FET Preamplifier High Input Impedance]
    E --> F[Bandpass Filter Centre Frequency equals Chopper Frequency]
    F --> G[Lock-in Amplifier or Synchronous Detector]
    G --> H[ADC and Digital Processing]
    H --> I[Display Thermographic Image]
    D --> J[Thermal Link to Heat Sink Controls tauT]
    J --> K[Curie Temperature Monitor T less than Tc]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this architecture matters:** The lock-in detection at the chopper frequency rejects ambient 1/f noise and 50/60 Hz mains interference — the same approach is used in clinical **photoplethysmography (PPG)** and **pulse oximetry** lock-in circuits.

## 4.2 Domain Switching and Hysteresis in a Ferroelectric

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Paraelectric: T greater than Tc
    Paraelectric --> Ferroelectric: T drops below Tc
    Ferroelectric --> PoledUp: Strong positive E field applied
    PoledUp --> PoledDown: Reverse E field exceeds Ec
    PoledDown --> PoledUp: Forward E field exceeds Ec
    Ferroelectric --> Paraelectric: Heating above Tc
    note right of Paraelectric
        Random domains
        P equals 0 net
    end note
    note right of PoledUp
        Domains aligned
        P equals Ps
    end note
    note right of PoledDown
        Domains reversed
        P equals negative Ps
    end note
```

## 4.3 Equivalent Circuit Model of the Pyroelectric Detector

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Chopped IR Power W t] --> B[Thermal Block c prime A d, G sub T]
    B --> C[Current Source I sub p equals A p dTdt]
    C --> D[Parallel Capacitor C equals eps0 epsr A over d]
    C --> E[Parallel Loss Resistance R sub loss]
    C --> F[Load Resistor R sub L to FET]
    F --> G[Output Voltage V sub s]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read this circuit like a Thevenin model:** The thermal block converts power to temperature (slow, time constant $\tau_T$). The current source converts temperature to charge. The capacitor and load resistor form the electrical filter with time constant $\tau_e$. The detector's **figure of merit** is determined by the ratio of these two time constants.

## 4.4 Flow of the Pyroelectric Effect Inside the Crystal

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IR Photon Absorbed] --> B[Crystal Lattice Heats Up]
    B --> C[Spontaneous Polarization P sub s Decreases with T]
    C --> D[Bound Surface Charge Decreases]
    D --> E[Free Charge Flows in External Circuit]
    E --> F[Current or Voltage Measured]
    F --> G[Signal proportional to dT over dt]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the pyroelectric effect. Why is modulation of incident radiation necessary for a pyroelectric detector?**

**Model Answer:**

The **pyroelectric effect** is the linear change in the spontaneous electric polarization of certain non-centrosymmetric crystals with temperature, quantified by the pyroelectric coefficient $p = dP_s/dT$.

Modulation (chopping) is essential because the output current $I_p = A\,p\,(dT/dt)$ depends on the **time derivative** of temperature. A steady (DC) radiation flux would raise the crystal to a constant temperature, producing $dT/dt = 0$ and hence zero signal. A chopped beam produces a periodic $dT/dt$, generating an alternating current that can be amplified and detected. **(3 marks — 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for necessity of modulation.)**

### Question 2 (3 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between a pyroelectric material and a ferroelectric material with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Pyroelectric | Ferroelectric |
|---|---|---|
| Spontaneous polarization | Yes, not switchable by E | Yes, **reversible** by applied E |
| Hysteresis loop | No (or trivial) | **Yes, characteristic P–E loop** |
| Above $T_c$ | May still exist (e.g., LiTaO₃) | Becomes paraelectric |
| Example | **Lithium Tantalate (LiTaO₃)** | **Barium Titanate (BaTiO₃)** or TGS |

*Key sentence:* Every ferroelectric is a pyroelectric, but the converse is not true — ferroelectricity adds the *reversibility* of $P_s$. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### Question A (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **voltage responsivity $R_v$** of a pyroelectric detector, clearly defining the electrical and thermal time constants. Explain the role of the figure of merit $F_v$ in material selection. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the operation of a **pyroelectric vidicon tube** used in medical thermography. Discuss why such a detector is suitable for imaging the **8 – 14 µm** infrared emission from the human body. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Derivation outline (7 marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** Crystal as a parallel-plate capacitor: $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$.
2. **(1 mark)** Pyroelectric current source: $I_p = A\,p\,(dT/dt)$.
3. **(1 mark)** Thermal equivalent: $\tau_T = c' A d / G_T$ and $\Delta T(\omega) = \eta W / (G_T\sqrt{1+\omega^2\tau_T^2})$.
4. **(2 marks)** Combining via $V_s = I_p \cdot Z(\omega)$ where $Z = R/(1 + j\omega RC)$:
   $$
   R_v \;=\; \dfrac{V_s}{W} \;=\; \dfrac{p\,\eta\,A\,\omega\,R}{c' A d \, \sqrt{1+\omega^2\tau_T^2}\,\sqrt{1+\omega^2R^2C^2}}
   $$
   and using $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$, the $A$ and $d$ can be eliminated to give:
   $$
   R_v \;=\; \dfrac{p\,\eta\,\omega\,R}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \, c' \sqrt{1+\omega^2\tau_T^2}\,\sqrt{1+\omega^2\tau_e^2}}
   $$
5. **(1 mark)** Define $F_v = p / (c'\,\varepsilon_r)$ — this combines *all* material properties. Maximising $F_v$ maximises $R_v$. Best materials: **TGS** ($F_v$ high), **LiTaO₃** (moderate $F_v$ but $T_c$ high enough for room-temperature use).
6. **(1 mark)** Explain that **PVDF polymer** is poor in $F_v$ but useful for *large-area flexible* sensors despite its lower performance.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Block diagram (3 marks):** [Refer to Section 4.1 — Human body → IR lens → chopper → pyroelectric target → electron-beam readout → video amplifier → display.]

**Operation (2 marks):**
- The **pyroelectric target** (TGS or DTGS) is a thin slice scanned by a low-velocity electron beam.
- Surface charge variations due to the chopped thermal image are read out capacitively as the beam sweeps, producing a video signal.
- Synchronous detection at the chopper frequency recovers the AC thermal image, stored frame-by-frame.

**Suitability for 8 – 14 µm (2 marks):**
- **Wien's law:** $\lambda_{\text{peak}} = b/T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} / 310 \approx 9.4\,\mu\text{m}$ — squarely in the 8–14 µm atmospheric window.
- Pyroelectric response is **wavelength-independent** (thermal detector) and broadband — covers the entire 8–14 µm range, unlike narrow-band photoconductors.
- **No cryogenic cooling** required — important for clinical settings where liquid nitrogen would be impractical.

---

### Question B (14 marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a P–E hysteresis diagram, define **spontaneous polarization $P_s$, remanent polarization $P_r$, and coercive field $E_c$**. Explain why ferroelectric materials must be operated **below** the Curie temperature $T_c$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** State the **Curie–Weiss law** for ferroelectrics. A ferroelectric detector with Curie constant $C = 1.5 \times 10^{5}\,\text{K}$ and $T_c = 49\,^\circ\text{C}$ is operating at $T = 30\,^\circ\text{C}$. Compute the relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$. Comment on the consequences of operating too close to $T_c$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Hysteresis loop (3 marks):** [Refer to Section 4.2; draw a typical "square" hysteresis loop with P on y-axis and E on x-axis, showing saturation regions at $\pm P_s$, remanent points at $\pm P_r$ on the y-axis, and coercive points at $\pm E_c$ on the x-axis.]

**Definitions (3 marks):**
- $P_s$: magnitude of polarization when all domains are aligned by a strong saturating field.
- $P_r$: residual polarization at $E = 0$ after removing the saturating field.
- $E_c$: magnitude of the reverse field required to bring the net polarization to zero.

**Why operate below $T_c$ (1 mark):** Above $T_c$, the spontaneous polarization vanishes (the crystal becomes *paraelectric*), so the hysteresis loop disappears and the material loses its ferroelectric (and consequently its pyroelectric) functionality. Operation must be well below $T_c$ for stable, reproducible response.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Curie–Weiss law (1 mark):**
$$
\varepsilon_r \;=\; \dfrac{C}{T - T_c}
$$

**Computation (3 marks):**
- $T = 30\,^\circ\text{C} = 303.15\,\text{K}$, $T_c = 49\,^\circ\text{C} = 322.15\,\text{K}$.
- $T - T_c = 303.15 - 322.15 = -19.0\,\text{K}$.
- Magnitude: $\varepsilon_r = 1.5 \times 10^{5} / 19.0 \approx 7.89 \times 10^{3}$ (i.e., $\approx 7895$).

> **Note for students:** The Curie–Weiss law formally applies *above* $T_c$. Below $T_c$ the same mathematical form still describes the *inverse susceptibility* trend; we report the *magnitude* as the effective permittivity. Many textbooks simplify and use $|T - T_c|$ — this is acceptable in KTU answers.

**Consequences of operating too close to $T_c$ (3 marks):**
1. **Runaway permittivity** — $\varepsilon_r \to \infty$ as $T \to T_c^{-}$, causing enormous $C$ and hence **collapse of voltage responsivity** $R_v \propto 1/C$.
2. **Domain instability** — thermal fluctuations depole the material; $P_r$ drifts, leading to **non-reproducible** response.
3. **Noise amplification** — high $\varepsilon_r$ increases Johnson noise current, degrading NEP and $D^*$.
4. Practical rule: keep detector temperature at least **10 – 20 K below $T_c$** for stable, low-noise operation — which is why room-temperature IR cameras favour **LiTaO₃ ($T_c \approx 618\,^\circ\text{C}$)** over TGS ($T_c \approx 49\,^\circ\text{C}$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **Sign convention in Curie–Weiss:** Below $T_c$, $T - T_c$ is **negative** — students often forget the absolute value or panic. State explicitly that the *magnitude* of $\varepsilon_r$ is reported.
> 2. **Hysteresis loop drawing:** Always label the axes, the saturation regions, the four intercepts ($\pm P_s$, $\pm P_r$, $\pm E_c$), and the *direction of traversal* (arrows). A loop drawn as a circle will cost 1 mark.
> 3. **Pyroelectric current formula:** Marks lost if students write $I_p = p \cdot dT$ instead of $I_p = A \cdot p \cdot dT/dt$. The factor $A$ and the time derivative are **non-negotiable**.
> 4. **Confusing ferroelectric and ferromagnetic:** They are *not* the same. "Ferro" refers to iron-like hysteresis, not to iron content. Coercive **field** (V/m), not coercive **force** (N).
> 5. **Units of $D^*$:** Always express in **cm·Hz$^{1/2}$/W**, not m·Hz$^{1/2}$/W. The "cm" is a unit convention, not a unit conversion error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — tick before entering the exam hall:**

- [ ] **Pyroelectric effect:** linear change of $P_s$ with $T$, defined by $p = dP_s/dT$.
- [ ] **Pyroelectric current:** $I_p = A p\,dT/dt$ — note the *time derivative*.
- [ ] **Modulation is mandatory** for any practical pyroelectric detector; otherwise $I_p = 0$.
- [ ] **Pyroelectric ≠ Ferroelectric.** Ferroelectric adds *reversible* $P_s$ and a P–E hysteresis loop.
- [ ] **Hysteresis parameters to memorise:** $P_s$ (spontaneous), $P_r$ (remanent), $E_c$ (coercive field).
- [ ] **Curie temperature $T_c$:** detector must be operated well **below** $T_c$ (typically 10 – 20 K margin).
- [ ] **Curie–Weiss law:** $\varepsilon_r = C/(T - T_c)$, valid above $T_c$ (extrapolated form used below).
- [ ] **Voltage responsivity:** $R_v = p\omega A R/(C\sqrt{1+\omega^2\tau_e^2})$; **plateau** value $\approx pA/C = pd/(\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r)$.
- [ ] **Current responsivity:** $R_i = pA/C$ — flat with frequency (within thermal limits).
- [ ] **Figure of merit $F_v = p/(c'\varepsilon_r)$** governs material ranking; high $F_v$ = good IR detector.
- [ ] **Detectivity $D^* = \sqrt{A\Delta f}/\text{NEP}$** in **cm·Hz$^{1/2}$/W** is the industry standard metric.
- [ ] **Common materials:** TGS (high $F_v$, low $T_c$), LiTaO₃ (high $T_c$, room-temp workhorse), PZT (cheap, ceramic), PVDF (polymer, flexible).
- [ ] **Medical applications:** thermography (8–14 µm), fever screening, vascular imaging, breast thermography, laser surgical power meters, photothermal radiometry.
- [ ] **Wien's law** for human body: $\lambda_{\text{peak}} = b/T = 2.898\times 10^{-3}/310 \approx 9.4\,\mu\text{m}$ — useful for justifying detector spectral choice.
- [ ] **No cryogenic cooling** required — major advantage over HgCdTe and InSb photoconductors.
- [ ] **Pyroelectric vidicon** = early electronic thermography camera; replaced today by **microbolometer and pyroelectric FPA** arrays.
- [ ] **Always state operating temperature regime** (room temp vs. cooled) in any detector comparison.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermoelectric Detectors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Thermoelectric Detectors # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) **Thermoelectric Detectors** are a class of thermal transducers that convert incident electromagnetic radiation (primarily in the **Infrared (IR) region**, wavelengths **3 µm to 14 µm**) into a measurable electrical signal. The conversion is achieved indirectly by first absorbing the incident radiation, converting it into **heat energy** that raises the detector element's temperature, and then exploiting temperature-dependent electrical properties (such as the **Seebeck coefficient**, **electrical resistance**, or **spontaneous polarization**) to generate a quantifiable voltage, current, or change in resistance. In the context of **Medical Imaging**, thermoelectric detectors form the fundamental sensing core of **Infrared Thermography** systems and **Pyroelectric Vidicons**, which are used for non-contact temperature mapping of biological tissues to detect inflammation, vascular disorders, and malignant tumors (oncology screening) based on local hyperthermia. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Hook:** "Thermoelectric detectors are *thermal* detectors — they respond to the *power* (or energy) of absorbed radiation, not to its photon count. Therefore, they have a **broad spectral response** (essentially wavelength-independent) and are cooled only when ultra-low noise is required." --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hot Spoon Voltage" Intuition Imagine dipping a **copper spoon** and an **iron spoon** into a hot cup of tea and twisting their free ends together at the top. The two dissimilar metals, where they touch the hot liquid, will produce a tiny voltage across the open ends. The *hotter* the tea, the *larger* the voltage. This is **exactly how a thermocouple works**: two dissimilar conductors joined at a "hot junction" exposed to IR radiation, and a "cold junction" kept at a reference temperature. > A **thermopile** is simply **many such spoons connected in series** (like Christmas lights) — boosting the tiny voltage to a measurable level. A **bolometer**, in contrast, is like a **thermometer whose mercury column is replaced by a resistor** — as the detector heats up, its electrical resistance changes, and we measure that change. A **pyroelectric detector** is like a **self-charging thermal capacitor** — certain crystals (e.g., Lithium Tantalate, **LiTaO₃**) develop a surface charge when warmed, which we can read directly without external bias. --- ## 1.3 Classification Overview Thermoelectric detectors used in medical imaging fall into **four major families**: | Family | Exploited Physical Effect | Typical Application in Medicine | |---|---|---| | **Thermocouple** | Seebeck Effect | Point IR radiometers, ear thermometers | | **Thermopile** | Series-coupled Seebeck Effect | Non-contact clinical thermometers, thermography arrays | | **Bolometer** | Temperature-dependent Resistance | Focal-plane thermal imaging, microwave/radiometric imaging | | **Pyroelectric** | Spontaneous Polarization change | Pyroelectric vidicons, motion-triggered thermal cameras | > [!NOTE] > **Constant for Memorization:** Standard operating temperature for uncooled medical thermal cameras = **Room Temperature (~300 K)**. Sensitivity (NEP) for bolometers improves ~10× when cooled to **77 K (Liquid Nitrogen)**. --- ## 1.4 Why Thermoelectric Detectors Matter in Medical Imaging Biological tissue emits **blackbody-like IR radiation** governed by the Stefan–Boltzmann Law. Pathological tissues (e.g., malignant tumors) exhibit elevated metabolic activity and abnormal vasculature, producing a temperature differential of **0.5 °C to 2 °C** versus surrounding healthy tissue. Thermoelectric detectors are uniquely capable of sensing this **mili-Kelvin level** thermal contrast *without physical contact*, making them indispensable for: - **Breast Cancer Screening (Thermography)** — adjunct to mammography. - **Inflammatory Disease Detection** — arthritis, soft-tissue injuries. - **Peripheral Vascular Disease (PVD)** — Raynaud's syndrome mapping. - **Burn Depth Assessment** in plastic surgery. - **Neonatal Monitoring** — non-contact skin temperature mapping. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Foundational Thermoelectric Effects All thermoelectric detectors ultimately rest on **three reversible thermoelectric phenomena**, originally discovered by Thomas Johann Seebeck, Jean Charles Athanase Peltier, and William Thomson (Lord Kelvin): ### (a) Seebeck Effect (1831) — The Foundation When two dissimilar conductors (A and B) form a closed loop and their two junctions are held at different temperatures (T_hot and T_cold), an **electromotive force (EMF)** appears in the loop. $$V_{ab} = \alpha_{AB} \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{cold}) = \alpha_{AB} \cdot \Delta T$$ where: - $\alpha_{AB} = \alpha_A - \alpha_B$ is the **relative Seebeck coefficient** of the thermocouple pair (units: **V/K** or more commonly **µV/°C**). - For the common **Constantan–Chromel** pair: $\alpha \approx 60$–$70$ µV/°C. - For **Bismuth–Antimony** pair (high-sensitivity): $\alpha \approx 100+$ µV/°C. > [!IMPORTANT] > The Seebeck voltage is the **direct cause of signal generation** in thermocouples and thermopiles. It is independent of the absolute temperature difference; it depends on the *gradient* ΔT. ### (b) Peltier Effect (1834) — Reverse Direction When a current $I$ is forced through a junction of two dissimilar metals, heat is either **absorbed or liberated** at the junction (depending on current direction): $$Q_{Peltier} = \Pi_{AB} \cdot I \cdot t$$ where $\Pi_{AB}$ is the **Peltier coefficient** (V) and $t$ is time. This effect is used in **thermoelectric coolers (TECs)** that actively stabilize thermopile cold-junction temperature, improving baseline stability in medical thermography. ### (c) Thomson Effect (1851) — Homogeneous Gradient Heat is absorbed or liberated along a single conductor carrying current in the presence of a temperature gradient: $$Q_{Thomson} = \tau \cdot I \cdot \Delta T$$ where $\tau$ is the **Thomson coefficient** (V/K). Generally secondary in medical imaging detectors, but relevant in long lead-wire compensation. --- ## 2.2 Bolometer Operating Principle A **bolometer** is a thermal detector consisting of a thin resistive element (absorber + thermometer) thermally isolated from a heat sink via a thermal conductance $G$ (W/K). When IR radiation of power $P$ falls on it, the element's temperature rises by: $$\Delta T = \frac{P}{G} \quad \text{(steady-state)}$$ The resistance change is: $$\Delta R = R_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T$$ where: - $R_0$ = nominal resistance at reference temperature (Ω). - $\beta$ = **Temperature Coefficient of Resistance (TCR)**, defined as $\beta = \frac{1}{R}\frac{dR}{dT}$ (per K). - For metals: $\beta \approx +0.003$ to $+0.005$ /K (positive TCR). - For semiconductors: $\beta \approx -0.03$ to $-0.05$ /K (negative, larger magnitude → higher sensitivity). > **KTU Insight:** Semiconductor bolometers (e.g., amorphous silicon, **VOₓ — Vanadium Oxide**) dominate modern uncooled IR focal-plane arrays (IRFPAs) used in medical thermography cameras (e.g., **FLIR T-Series**). The bolometer forms one arm of a **Wheatstone bridge** for readout. The output voltage is: $$V_{out} = \frac{V_{bias}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R_0} = \frac{V_{bias}}{4} \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T$$ --- ## 2.3 Pyroelectric Detector Principle Certain non-centrosymmetric crystals (e.g., **Triglycine Sulphate — TGS, LiTaO₃, PZT**) possess a **spontaneous electric polarization** $P_s$ that is temperature-dependent. A change in temperature alters $P_s$, which manifests as a **surface charge change** on the crystal faces: $$\Delta Q = A \cdot p \cdot \Delta T$$ where $A$ is the electrode area and $p = dP_s/dT$ is the **pyroelectric coefficient** (C/m²·K). The corresponding current under sinusoidal modulation is: $$i_p = A \cdot p \cdot \frac{dT}{dt} = A \cdot p \cdot \omega \cdot \Delta T$$ > [!NOTE] > Pyroelectric detectors respond only to **changes** in temperature (AC-coupled), not to steady thermal scenes. This is why thermal imaging cameras using pyroelectric vidicons mechanically **chop** (modulate) the incoming IR beam with a rotating paddle wheel. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > **MASTER THIS TABLE — it covers >85% of numerical questions in KTU Module 4.** | # | Parameter / Quantity | Governing Equation | Typical Units | Used In | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Seebeck EMF (single junction) | $V = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | V (µV) | Thermocouple | | 2 | Thermopile output (N junctions in series) | $V_{tp} = N \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | V | Thermopile | | 3 | Bolometer steady-state temperature rise | $\Delta T = P / G$ | K | Bolometer | | 4 | Bolometer resistance change | $\Delta R = R_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T$ | Ω | Bolometer | | 5 | Bolometer bridge output | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_b}{4} \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T$ | V | Bolometer | | 6 | Bolometer responsivity (DC) | $\Re = \dfrac{I_b \cdot \alpha_R \cdot R_0}{G}$ | V/W | Bolometer | | 7 | Bolometer responsivity (AC, freq. f) | $\Re(f) = \dfrac{\Re_0}{\sqrt{1+(2\pi f \tau)^2}}$ | V/W | Bolometer | | 8 | Thermal time constant | $\tau = C / G$ | s | All thermal | | 9 | Johnson (thermal) noise | $v_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ | V/√Hz | Bolometer | | 10 | Noise Equivalent Power | $NEP = v_n / \Re$ | W/√Hz | All | | 11 | Specific detectivity | $D^* = \sqrt{A} / NEP$ | cm·Hz^(1/2)/W | All | | 12 | Pyroelectric current | $i_p = A \cdot p \cdot \omega \cdot \Delta T$ | A | Pyroelectric | | 13 | Stefan–Boltzmann tissue emission | $P = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 A$ | W | Thermography | | 14 | Planck spectral radiance peak | $\lambda_{max} = b / T$ , $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K | m | IR physics | | 15 | Peltier heat | $Q = \Pi \cdot I \cdot t$ | J | TEC coolers | **Universal Physical Constants (memorize):** - Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K - Stefan–Boltzmann: $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴) - Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU answer scripts, **always quote the equation first in symbol form, then substitute numerical values with units, then write the final result with units.** Examiners are trained to look for unit consistency — losing it costs **1–2 marks per sub-question**. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Medical thermography cameras** (e.g., FLIR T1020, Meditherm IR2000) use **640×480 VOₓ microbolometer arrays** with NEP ~ **20 pW/√Hz** and thermal sensitivity < **30 mK**. - **Ear/forehead clinical thermometers** use single-junction **thermopiles** with ±0.1 °C accuracy. - **Pyroelectric vidicons** historically enabled the first portable thermal cameras (1960s–80s), now largely replaced by microbolometers. - **Thermoelectric coolers (TECs)** stabilize detector temperature to **−40 °C**, improving NETD (Noise-Equivalent Temperature Difference) by ~3×. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Seebeck EMF for a Single Thermocouple Couple We consider a closed loop of two dissimilar conductors **A** and **B**, joined at two junctions held at temperatures $T_1$ (hot) and $T_2$ (cold), where $T_1 > T_2$. **Step 1 — Define the absolute Seebeck coefficients.** Each material has an intrinsic (absolute) Seebeck coefficient: - $\alpha_A$ for conductor A - $\alpha_B$ for conductor B These are thermodynamic material properties (functions of $T$) and have units of **V/K** (typically expressed as **µV/°C**). **Step 2 — Express the EMF contribution from each conductor segment.** For a homogeneous conductor with ends at temperatures $T_a$ and $T_b$, the integrated Seebeck EMF along its length is: $$V_A = \int_{T_b}^{T_a} \alpha_A(T)\, dT$$ **Step 3 — Apply superposition around the loop.** Tracing the loop A → B → A through the two junctions: $$V_{loop} = \int_{T_2}^{T_1} \alpha_A(T)\, dT - \int_{T_2}^{T_1} \alpha_B(T)\, dT$$ **Step 4 — Combine integrals.** $$V_{loop} = \int_{T_2}^{T_1} \left[\alpha_A(T) - \alpha_B(T)\right] dT = \int_{T_2}^{T_1} \alpha_{AB}(T)\, dT$$ **Step 5 — For small ΔT, take α as constant.** For a medical IR detector, ΔT is small (mK to a few K). The relative Seebeck coefficient is approximately constant in this range: $$\alpha_{AB}(T) \approx \alpha_{AB} = \text{const.}$$ **Step 6 — Perform the integration.** $$V_{loop} = \alpha_{AB} \int_{T_2}^{T_1} dT = \alpha_{AB} \cdot (T_1 - T_2)$$ **Step 7 — Final Result.** $$\boxed{\,V_{AB} = \alpha_{AB}\cdot \Delta T \quad \text{where } \Delta T = T_{hot} - T_{cold}\,}$$ > **Physical Meaning:** The Seebeck EMF is linear in the temperature difference for small ΔT. This is the foundational equation for **every thermocouple and thermopile used in medical thermography**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Thermopile Output Voltage A **thermopile** is built by connecting $N$ identical thermocouples in series electrically but in parallel thermally — meaning all hot junctions are bonded to the IR absorber, and all cold junctions are bonded to a heat sink. **Step 1 — EMF from a single junction.** From Derivation 1: $V_1 = \alpha_{AB}\cdot \Delta T$ **Step 2 — Series addition of N junctions.** Since the EMFs add in series (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law) when cold junctions are at common potential and hot junctions are at common potential: $$V_{tp} = V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_N$$ **Step 3 — Assume identical junctions.** For matched fabrication: $$V_{tp} = N \cdot \alpha_{AB} \cdot \Delta T$$ **Step 4 — Final Result.** $$\boxed{\,V_{thermopile} = N \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T\,}$$ > **Design Insight:** A typical medical-grade thermopile uses **N = 40 to 100** junctions of **Bi–Sb** ($\alpha \approx 100$ µV/°C) to give ~10 mV output per °C of skin-temperature difference — comfortably readable by a 16-bit ADC. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bolometer Steady-State ΔT and Responsivity **Step 1 — Define thermal circuit.** A bolometer element has: - Heat capacity $C$ (J/K) — for thermal storage. - Thermal conductance to heat sink $G$ (W/K) — for heat loss. This is electrically analogous to an **RC low-pass filter** with thermal "voltage" = temperature, thermal "current" = heat flow. **Step 2 — Energy balance equation.** $$C \frac{d(\Delta T)}{dt} + G \cdot \Delta T = P_{abs}(t)$$ where $P_{abs}(t)$ is the absorbed radiation power (W). **Step 3 — Solve for steady state ($\frac{d(\Delta T)}{dt} = 0$).** $$G \cdot \Delta T = P_{abs} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta T = \frac{P_{abs}}{G}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for time-dependent case (input chopped at angular frequency ω).** Assume $P_{abs}(t) = P_0 e^{j\omega t}$. Then $\Delta T(t) = \Delta T_0 \, e^{j\omega t}$: $$C \cdot j\omega \cdot \Delta T + G \cdot \Delta T = P_0$$ $$\Delta T(\omega) = \frac{P_0}{G + j\omega C} = \frac{P_0/G}{1 + j\omega \tau}$$ where $\tau = C/G$ is the **thermal time constant**. **Step 5 — Magnitude.** $$|\Delta T(\omega)| = \frac{P_0/G}{\sqrt{1+(\omega\tau)^2}}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to resistance change.** $$\Delta R = R_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T = \frac{R_0 \cdot \beta \cdot P_0/G}{\sqrt{1+(\omega\tau)^2}}$$ **Step 7 — Convert to bridge output voltage.** With bias current $I_b$, the bridge output is $V_{out} = I_b \cdot \Delta R$: $$V_{out} = \frac{I_b \cdot R_0 \cdot \beta \cdot P_0/G}{\sqrt{1+(\omega\tau)^2}}$$ **Step 8 — Define responsivity (V/W).** $$\boxed{\,\Re(\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{P_0} = \frac{I_b \cdot R_0 \cdot \beta}{G \sqrt{1+(\omega\tau)^2}}\,}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Note:** Responsivity is *maximum* at DC ($\omega = 0$): $\Re_0 = I_b R_0 \beta / G$. It rolls off at $-3$ dB when $\omega = 1/\tau$, i.e., at **thermal cutoff frequency** $f_c = 1/(2\pi\tau)$. Medical thermal cameras typically operate at **30 Hz** frame rate, requiring $\tau \le 16$ ms. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Noise Equivalent Power (NEP) and Specific Detectivity (D*) **Step 1 — Identify dominant noise source.** For a bolometer, the dominant noise is **Johnson (thermal) noise** of the resistive element: $$v_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R_0 \Delta f}$$ where $\Delta f$ is the measurement bandwidth (Hz). **Step 2 — Define NEP.** NEP is the incident radiation power that produces an RMS signal equal to the RMS noise — i.e., **Signal-to-Noise Ratio = 1**: $$NEP = \frac{v_n}{\Re} = \frac{\sqrt{4 k_B T R_0 \Delta f}}{\Re_0}$$ **Step 3 — Specific Detectivity D\*.** D* normalizes NEP to detector area $A$ and bandwidth, allowing comparison across different detectors: $$\boxed{\,D^* = \frac{\sqrt{A \cdot \Delta f}}{NEP} = \frac{\sqrt{A}}{NEP / \sqrt{\Delta f}}\,}$$ Units: **cm·Hz^(1/2)·W⁻¹** (called "Jones"). **Step 4 — Typical values in medical thermography.** - Uncooled VOₓ microbolometer: $D^* \approx 10^8$ cm·Hz^(1/2)/W. - Cooled (77 K) MCT photovoltaic: $D^* \approx 10^{10}$ cm·Hz^(1/2)/W (used in research-grade thermal imaging, *not* a thermoelectric detector — this is for comparison). --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **[Q]:** A medical thermopile uses **N = 50** junctions of **bismuth–antimony** with Seebeck coefficient $\alpha = 110$ µV/°C. The hot junction is at skin temperature **33.0 °C** and the cold junction reference is at **30.0 °C**. Calculate the open-circuit output voltage. **Solution:** Step 1 — Temperature difference: $$\Delta T = T_{hot} - T_{cold} = 33.0 - 30.0 = 3.0 \text{ °C}$$ Step 2 — Apply thermopile equation: $$V_{tp} = N \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$ Step 3 — Substitute: $$V_{tp} = 50 \times (110 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V/°C}) \times (3.0 \text{ °C})$$ Step 4 — Compute: $$V_{tp} = 50 \times 110 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-6} = 16500 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V}$$ Step 5 — Final answer: $$\boxed{\,V_{tp} = 16.5 \text{ mV}\,}$$ > **Examiner's Marking Logic:** Full marks (2/2) require explicit Step 1 + Step 4 numerical evaluation. Skipping the unit conversion (µV → V) costs 1 mark. --- ## 3.6 Comparative Performance Matrix (Exam Favorite) | Parameter | Thermocouple | Thermopile | Bolometer (VOₓ) | Pyroelectric | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Spectral range** | 0.1–100 µm | 0.1–100 µm | 8–14 µm (with window) | 1–1000 µm | | **Sensitivity (NEP)** | ~1 nW | ~0.1 nW | ~20 pW | ~0.5 nW | | **Response time** | 10–100 ms | 30–100 ms | 10–20 ms | 1 ns – 1 µs | | **Operating Temp.** | 300 K | 300 K | 300 K (or 77 K cooled) | 300 K | | **DC or AC coupled?** | DC | DC | DC | AC only | | **Best medical use** | Spot radiometer | Clinical thermometer | Thermal imager | Motion / chopping camera | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Thermopile-Based Medical Thermometer ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Human Skin Surface
T approx 33 C] --> B[IR Radiation Emission
Stefan Boltzmann Law] B --> C[Optical Lens or
Fresnel Mirror Assembly] C --> D[Thermopile Sensor
N junctions in series] D --> E[Low Noise Preamplifier
INA128 INA129] E --> F[16 bit ADC
ADS1115 or similar] F --> G[Microcontroller Unit
ARM Cortex M0] G --> H[LCD Display Module
Temperature Readout] D --> I[Cold Junction Compensation
NTC Thermistor Network] I --> E G --> J[Thermoelectric Cooler
TEC Controller Optional] J --> D ``` **Functional Notes:** - The **NTC thermistor** measures the cold-junction temperature in real time and feeds back to the preamp for **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC)** — a mandatory step in any medical-grade instrument. - The **TEC** (Peltier cooler) is optional but improves baseline drift in long-duration monitoring. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Microbolometer Focal Plane Array (FPA) for Medical Thermography ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Patient Body
Thermal Scene] --> B[Germanium Optical Lens
f number 1.0] B --> C[Microbolometer FPA
640 x 480 VO x Pixels] C --> D[ROIC Readout Integrated Circuit] D --> E[Column Amplifiers and
Correlated Double Sampling] E --> F[14 bit ADC Array] F --> G[Image Processor
DSP FPGA] G --> H[Non Uniformity Correction NUC] H --> I[False Color Mapping
Ironbow Rainbow] I --> J[Display Monitor] C --> K[TEC Stabilizer
Target 30 C] K --> C G --> L[Alarm Module
Delta T > 1.5 C] ``` --- ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Pyroelectric Vidicon IR Camera (Historical, KTU Theory) ```mermaid flowchart TB A[IR Scene] --> B[Germanium Lens] B --> C[Mechanical Chopper
Rotating Paddle] C --> D[Pyroelectric Target
TGS or LiTaO3] D --> E[Electron Beam Scanning
Vidicon Tube] E --> F[Signal Preamp] F --> G[Bandpass Filter
Centered at Chop Freq] G --> H[Synchronous Detector
Lock in Amplifier] H --> I[Video Display] C -.Reference Clock.-> H ``` --- ## 4.4 Schematic: Equivalent Circuit of a Bolometer (Bridge Readout) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph BR[Wheatstone Bridge] R1[Fixed Resistor R1] --- TOP1[Node A] TOP1 --- Rb[Bolometer Rb] Rb --- BTM1[Node B] BTM1 --- R2[Fixed Resistor R2] R2 --- BTMTOP[Node C] BTMTOP --- R3[Fixed Resistor R3] R3 --- TOP2[Node D] TOP2 --- R4[Fixed Resistor R4] R4 --- BTM1 end VB[Bias Voltage Vb] --> TOP1 VB --> BTMTOP TOP1 --> OPA[Differential Amplifier] BTMTOP --> OPA OPA --> ADC[Output to ADC] ``` **KTU Note:** A properly balanced bridge has $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R_b$ at the reference temperature. Any change $\Delta R$ in $R_b$ produces a differential voltage across the bridge diagonals (Nodes A and C), which is amplified by a precision instrumentation amplifier (e.g., **INA128**). --- ## 4.5 Cross-Classification Mind Map ```mermaid flowchart TB T[Thermoelectric Detectors] --> A[Seebeck Type] T --> B[Bolometric Type] T --> C[Pyroelectric Type] A --> A1[Thermocouple single] A --> A2[Thermopile series array] B --> B1[Metal Bolometer] B --> B2[Semiconductor Bolometer] B --> B3[Superconducting TES] B2 --> B2a[VOx Microbolometer FPA] B2 --> B2b[Amorphous Si Microbolometer] C --> C1[TGS] C --> C2[LiTaO3] C --> C3[PZT Ceramic] ``` > **KTU Quick-Recall Hook:** The examiner loves asking *"Classify thermoelectric detectors with examples."* This tree is your **3-mark short-answer blueprint**. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]** ### **Q1. Define the Seebeck Effect. How is it exploited in a medical thermopile?** (3 marks) **Model Answer:** The **Seebeck Effect** is the phenomenon whereby an electromotive force (EMF) is generated in a closed circuit formed by two dissimilar conductors when their junctions are maintained at different temperatures. The magnitude of this EMF is given by $V = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$, where $\alpha$ is the relative Seebeck coefficient (V/K) and $\Delta T$ is the temperature difference between the junctions. In a **medical thermopile**, the Seebeck effect is exploited by arranging **N identical thermocouples in series**, with all hot junctions bonded to an IR-absorbing membrane (exposed to the patient's skin) and all cold junctions bonded to a heat sink. The output voltage is $V = N\alpha\Delta T$, typically in the range of millivolts per degree Celsius of skin-temperature difference. The thermopile thus serves as the sensing element in non-contact clinical thermometers and infrared thermography cameras used in dermatology and oncology screening. **(3 marks)** --- ### **Q2. Differentiate between a bolometer and a pyroelectric detector.** (3 marks) **Model Answer:** | Feature | Bolometer | Pyroelectric Detector | |---|---|---| | **Exploited property** | Temperature-dependent resistance (TCR) | Temperature-dependent spontaneous polarization | | **Response to steady IR** | Yes (DC coupled) | No (AC coupled — requires chopper) | | **Typical material** | VOₓ, amorphous Si, Ge | TGS, LiTaO₃, PZT | | **Output signal** | Resistance change ΔR (read via bridge) | Surface charge / current | | **Medical use** | Focal plane array thermography | Pyroelectric vidicon, motion sensors | **(3 marks)** — Examiner's marking: 1 mark each for the three distinguishing points + table format. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark ESE Questions (Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **(a) Derive the expression for the open-circuit output voltage of a thermopile consisting of N identical thermocouples in series, each with Seebeck coefficient α, when the hot and cold junctions are at temperatures T_h and T_c respectively. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — State Seebeck's law for a single thermocouple:** For a single junction pair, the EMF produced is: $$V_1 = \alpha \cdot (T_h - T_c) = \alpha \cdot \Delta T \quad \text{...(1)}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law for N series junctions:** Since the N thermocouples are connected in series, their EMFs add algebraically (assuming matched construction and identical temperature differences): $$V_{tp} = V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_N = \sum_{k=1}^{N} V_k \quad \text{...(2)}$$ **Step 3 — Assume identical junctions:** For a uniform thermopile fabricated on a single substrate, all N junctions see the same $\Delta T$ and use the same material pair, so $V_1 = V_2 = \dots = V_N$: $$V_{tp} = N \cdot V_1 \quad \text{...(3)}$$ **Step 4 — Final derived expression:** Substituting (1) into (3): $$\boxed{\,V_{thermopile} = N \cdot \alpha \cdot (T_h - T_c)\,}$$ **Step 5 — Physical interpretation (for full marks):** - $N$ multiplies the weak single-junction EMF (~µV) to a measurable mV-level signal. - $\alpha$ depends on the material pair; **Bi–Sb** gives the highest sensitivity (~110 µV/°C). - The output is linear in $\Delta T$ for small temperature differences. **(7 marks — Examiner's Split:** 2 marks equation statement + 2 marks derivation + 2 marks physical interpretation + 1 mark for final boxed expression.) --- **(b) A thermopile in a medical ear thermometer uses N = 40 junctions of chromel–constantan (α = 65 µV/°C). The tympanic membrane temperature is 36.8 °C and the reference cold-junction temperature is 25.0 °C. Calculate (i) the open-circuit output voltage, and (ii) the output voltage if the number of junctions is increased to 80. Comment on the trade-off. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $N_1 = 40$ junctions - $\alpha = 65 \, \mu V/°C = 65 \times 10^{-6}$ V/°C - $T_h = 36.8$ °C - $T_c = 25.0$ °C **Step (i) — Compute ΔT:** $$\Delta T = T_h - T_c = 36.8 - 25.0 = 11.8 \text{ °C}$$ **Step (ii) — Apply thermopile equation for N = 40:** $$V_{tp,1} = N_1 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 40 \times (65 \times 10^{-6}) \times 11.8$$ $$V_{tp,1} = 40 \times 65 \times 11.8 \times 10^{-6} = 30{,}680 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V}$$ $$\boxed{\,V_{tp,1} = 30.68 \text{ mV}\,}$$ **Step (iii) — Repeat for N = 80:** $$V_{tp,2} = 80 \times (65 \times 10^{-6}) \times 11.8 = 2 \times V_{tp,1}$$ $$\boxed{\,V_{tp,2} = 61.36 \text{ mV}\,}$$ **Step (iv) — Trade-off comment (1 mark):** Doubling N doubles the signal but: - Increases **thermal mass** of the absorber → slower response time (longer τ). - Increases **sensor cost** and **footprint** of the device. - Increases **series electrical resistance** of the thermopile, which can degrade SNR if not impedance-matched. **(7 marks — Examiner's Split:** 2 marks ΔT + 2 marks V₁ + 2 marks V₂ + 1 mark trade-off comment.) --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model] **(a) Explain the working principle of a microbolometer used in medical infrared thermography cameras. Derive an expression for its frequency-dependent responsivity. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Construction (1 mark):** A microbolometer is a MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical System) device consisting of a thin suspended membrane (~0.5 µm thick) of a high-TCR material (typically **Vanadium Oxide — VOₓ**, or amorphous silicon) coated with an **IR-absorbing layer** (e.g., black gold or carbon nanotube composite). The membrane is suspended over a CMOS readout integrated circuit (ROIC) substrate by **two narrow support legs** that provide mechanical support and define the **thermal conductance G** to the substrate heat sink. **Step 2 — Working principle (2 marks):** - IR radiation from the patient (8–14 µm atmospheric window) passes through a germanium lens and falls on the bolometer pixel array. - Each pixel absorbs the radiation; its temperature rises by $\Delta T = P/G$ in steady state. - This temperature rise causes a resistance change $\Delta R = R_0 \beta \Delta T$. - The ROIC reads the resistance change pixel-by-pixel using a bridge or constant-current bias circuit. - A 2D thermal image is reconstructed, with temperature encoded as pixel intensity (or false color). **Step 3 — Derivation of frequency-dependent responsivity (4 marks):** The thermal equivalent circuit gives: $$C \frac{d\Delta T}{dt} + G \cdot \Delta T = P_{abs}(t)$$ For a sinusoidal input $P_{abs}(t) = P_0 e^{j\omega t}$: $$\Delta T(\omega) = \frac{P_0}{G + j\omega C} = \frac{P_0 / G}{1 + j\omega \tau}$$ where $\tau = C/G$ is the thermal time constant. The bridge output voltage is: $$V_{out} = I_b \cdot \Delta R = I_b \cdot R_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T(\omega)$$ $$\Re(\omega) = \frac{V_{out}}{P_0} = \frac{I_b \cdot R_0 \cdot \beta / G}{1 + j\omega \tau}$$ The magnitude: $$\boxed{\,\vert \Re(\omega) \vert = \frac{\Re_0}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega \tau)^2}}\,, \quad \Re_0 = \frac{I_b R_0 \beta}{G}\,}$$ **(7 marks)** --- **(b) A medical thermography camera uses a VOₓ microbolometer with the following specifications: pixel resistance R₀ = 50 kΩ, TCR β = 0.025 /K, bias current I_b = 10 µA, thermal conductance G = 1×10⁻⁷ W/K, and thermal time constant τ = 12 ms. Calculate: (i) the DC responsivity, (ii) the −3 dB cutoff frequency, and (iii) the responsivity at the camera's 30 Hz frame rate. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $R_0 = 50 \times 10^3$ Ω - $\beta = 0.025$ /K - $I_b = 10 \times 10^{-6}$ A - $G = 1 \times 10^{-7}$ W/K - $\tau = 12 \times 10^{-3}$ s - $f = 30$ Hz **Step (i) — DC Responsivity:** $$\Re_0 = \frac{I_b R_0 \beta}{G} = \frac{(10 \times 10^{-6}) \times (50 \times 10^3) \times 0.025}{1 \times 10^{-7}}$$ $$\Re_0 = \frac{10 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 \times 10^3 \times 0.025}{10^{-7}}$$ $$\Re_0 = \frac{12.5 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-7}} = 12.5 \times 10^{4}$$ $$\boxed{\,\Re_0 = 1.25 \times 10^5 \text{ V/W}\,}$$ **Step (ii) — −3 dB Cutoff Frequency:** $$f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi\tau} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 12 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{0.0754}$$ $$\boxed{\,f_c \approx 13.26 \text{ Hz}\,}$$ **Step (iii) — Responsivity at 30 Hz:** $$\Re(30) = \frac{\Re_0}{\sqrt{1 + (2\pi f \tau)^2}} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^5}{\sqrt{1 + (2\pi \times 30 \times 0.012)^2}}$$ $$2\pi f \tau = 2\pi \times 30 \times 0.012 = 2.262$$ $$(2\pi f \tau)^2 = 5.116$$ $$\Re(30) = \frac{1.25 \times 10^5}{\sqrt{1 + 5.116}} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^5}{\sqrt{6.116}} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^5}{2.473}$$ $$\boxed{\,\Re(30) \approx 5.05 \times 10^4 \text{ V/W}\,}$$ **Comment (1 mark):** At 30 Hz, the responsivity has dropped to ~40% of its DC value, indicating that the camera's frame rate is above the thermal cutoff — this affects the SNR and may require **on-chip integration** or **slower scanning**. **(7 marks — Examiner's Split:** 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 for comment.) --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Reasons Students Lose Marks on Thermoelectric Detector Questions:** > 1. **Unit confusion:** Writing Seebeck coefficient in V/K but plugging in ΔT in °C without realizing they are equivalent *only* in magnitude (since K and °C have the same *interval* size). This is acceptable, but be consistent. > 2. **Forgetting N in thermopile equations:** A single-junction answer for a thermopile question loses 2–3 marks immediately. > 3. **Mixing bolometer responsivity formula:** Some students write $\Re_0 = I_b R_0 / G$ forgetting the TCR β term. Always write the **complete** formula on the first line. > 4. **Confusing D\* units:** $D^*$ is in **cm·Hz^(1/2)/W** (Jones), *not* W⁻¹. Examiners deduct 1 mark. > 5. **In derivations, skipping the "why":** Don't just write equations — write a sentence explaining the physics (e.g., "Because the Seebeck coefficient is approximately constant over the small ΔT range relevant to medical imaging, the integral reduces to a simple product"). This is what fetches full marks. > 6. **Forgetting to convert µV to V:** Numerical answers with mismatched units lose 1 mark per sub-question. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist > **Bookmark this section — read it the night before the exam.** - [x] **Thermoelectric detectors** convert IR radiation → heat → electrical signal via one of three physical effects: Seebeck (thermocouple/thermopile), Resistance change (bolometer), or Polarization change (pyroelectric). - [x] **Seebeck EMF (single junction):** $V = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$. α in µV/°C, ΔT in K or °C. - [x] **Thermopile output:** $V = N\alpha\Delta T$ — series N junctions multiply the signal. - [x] **Three thermoelectric effects:** Seebeck (EMF from ΔT), Peltier (heat from current at junction), Thomson (heat along a single conductor with ΔT and current). - [x] **Bolometer steady-state ΔT:** $\Delta T = P/G$. Thermal time constant: $\tau = C/G$. - [x] **Bolometer resistance change:** $\Delta R = R_0 \cdot \beta \cdot \Delta T$ — β is the TCR (~0.003 /K metals, ~0.04 /K semiconductors). - [x] **Bolometer DC responsivity:** $\Re_0 = I_b R_0 \beta / G$. **AC responsivity:** $\Re(\omega) = \Re_0 / \sqrt{1+(\omega\tau)^2}$. - [x] **−3 dB cutoff:** $f_c = 1/(2\pi\tau)$. - [x] **NEP:** $\text{NEP} = v_n / \Re$ where $v_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ (Johnson noise). - [x] **Specific detectivity:** $D^* = \sqrt{A} / \text{NEP}$ — units Jones (cm·Hz^(1/2)/W). - [x] **Pyroelectric current:** $i_p = A \cdot p \cdot \omega \cdot \Delta T$ — AC coupled, requires chopper. - [x] **Tissue emission:** Stefan–Boltzmann: $P = \varepsilon \sigma T^4 A$. Skin emissivity $\varepsilon \approx 0.98$. - [x] **Wien's peak wavelength at 310 K (skin):** $\lambda_{max} = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} / 310 \approx 9.35$ µm — explains the 8–14 µm medical IR window. - [x] **Medical applications:** Breast thermography, PVD screening, burn depth assessment, neonatal monitoring, ear thermometry, inflammatory disease mapping. - [x] **Best medical thermoelectric detector:** **VOₓ microbolometer FPA** for imaging; **Thermopile** for single-point thermometry.

topics.title: Pyroelectric Vidicon Camera - Camera Characterization - Thermographic Image Processing - Clinical Applications of Thermography in Rheumatology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Pyroelectric Vidicon Camera - Camera Characterization - Thermographic Image Processing - Clinical Applications of Thermography in Rheumatology ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Pyroelectric Vidicon (PEV) Camera — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** > A **Pyroelectric Vidicon (PEV) Camera** is a thermal imaging device that combines the **pyroelectric effect** of ferroelectric crystals (most commonly **Triglycine Sulfate, TGS**) with a **vidicon-style electron-beam-scanned pickup tube**, converting incident long-wave infrared (LWIR) radiation (~8–14 µm) into a video signal that displays the spatial temperature distribution of a target. The PEV tube consists of: - A **germanium (Ge) or silicon (Si) input window** transparent to IR - A thin **pyroelectric target** (TGS, LiTaO₃, or DTGS) deposited on a reticulated supporting mesh - A **thermionic cathode** generating an electron beam - **Deflection coils** scanning the electron beam (standard 525-line/625-line TV raster) - A **signal electrode** collecting the displaced charge > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the vidicon was unique:** Unlike cooled semiconductor detectors (e.g., HgCdTe/MCT), the PEV operated at **room temperature** with no cryogenic cooler, making it the first **practical, portable medical thermography system** in the 1970s–1990s before FPA cameras replaced it. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition Imagine a **black-and-white television screen that "sees heat instead of light"**: 1. A doctor's patient walks in with an inflamed knee. The skin over the inflamed joint emits slightly more infrared radiation than the healthy side. 2. This IR radiation **heats up a small crystal** at the front of the camera tube. 3. The crystal, in turn, generates a tiny **electrical charge proportional to its temperature change** — this is the *pyroelectric effect*. 4. A **scanning electron beam** (the same principle as a TV tube) sweeps across this charged crystal, line by line, like a gardener reading a lawn square by square. 5. Wherever the crystal has more charge (hotter spot), the beam "reads" a stronger signal. 6. The signal is reconstructed as a **picture where bright = hot, dark = cool**, and the doctor can literally *see the inflammation*. > **Real-world analogy:** A PEV is to a thermal camera what a **vinyl record player** is to a digital music file — older, mechanical, scanning-based — but the *physical principle* (heat → charge → signal) remains the foundation of all modern thermal imaging. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Thermal radiation peak (skin @ 305 K):** $\lambda_{\max} \approx 9.5 \, \mu m$ (via Wien's law) - **Human skin emissivity:** $\varepsilon \approx 0.98$ (often taken as **0.98–0.99** in medical protocols) - **Pyroelectric coefficient of TGS:** $p \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-8} \, C/(cm^2 \cdot K)$ - **Curie temperature of TGS:** $T_C = 49^\circ C$ (just above body temperature, near ideal) - **Standard thermal sensitivity (NETD):** $\leq 0.2 \, K$ at $30^\circ C$ target - **Working temperature range for rheumatology:** **20–30 °C room** (controlled) --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **IR Photon Absorption (8–14 µm band):** - IR photons from the patient's skin pass through the Ge/Si lens and fall on the pyroelectric target. Photon energy is absorbed as **lattice vibrational energy**, raising the local temperature of the crystal. 2. **Pyroelectric Charge Generation:** - Spontaneous polarization $P_s$ of the ferroelectric material is **temperature-dependent**. A change $\Delta T$ produces a surface charge $\Delta Q$ given by: $$\Delta Q = p \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$ where $p$ = pyroelectric coefficient, $A$ = pixel area. 3. **Charge Reading by Electron Beam:** - The scanning electron beam acts as a **commutating switch**, neutralizing the surface charge at each pixel. The displacement current induced in the signal electrode is proportional to $\Delta Q$. 4. **Video Signal Output:** - The modulated current is amplified and converted to a standard **RS-170 (525-line) or CCIR (625-line)** video signal, displayed on a monitor. 5. **Chopper Modulation (Critical for DC Rejection):** - The PEV is **AC-coupled** by nature — it only responds to *changing* IR flux. A **mechanical chopper** (rotating slotted disc) modulates the incoming IR at ~25–30 Hz, producing a square-wave thermal stimulus so the pyroelectric material sees alternating heating/cooling. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Without the **mechanical chopper**, the PEV would be **blind to static temperature scenes** — this is a frequent 2-mark question. ### 2.2 Camera Characterization — Performance Metrics #### 2.2.1 Noise Equivalent Temperature Difference (NETD) The smallest temperature difference between target and background that produces a signal equal to the RMS noise: $$\text{NETD} = \frac{(S/N)_{\text{noise-floor}} \cdot \Delta T_{\text{calibration}}}{(S/N)_{\text{measured}}}$$ A more rigorous form based on noise voltage: $$\text{NETD} = \frac{V_{\text{noise}}}{R_v \cdot \Delta T}$$ where $R_v$ is the **voltage responsivity** (V/K). For a PEV typical values: $\text{NETD} = 0.1$ to $0.3 \, K$. #### 2.2.2 Minimum Resolvable Temperature Difference (MRTD) Combines thermal sensitivity with spatial resolution: $$\text{MRTD}(f) = \frac{\text{NETD}}{\text{MTF}(f)} \cdot k(f)$$ where $f$ = spatial frequency (cycles/mrad), $\text{MTF}(f)$ = Modulation Transfer Function, $k(f)$ = observer-dependent factor. #### 2.2.3 Minimum Detectable Temperature Difference (MDTD) For a **large target** (low spatial frequency), the MDTD approaches the NETD. #### 2.2.4 Spatial Resolution - **Pixel pitch:** $25 \, \mu m$ typical for TGS targets - **TV lines:** 250–400 TV lines - **Spot size of electron beam:** $\approx 25 \, \mu m$ #### 2.2.5 Thermal Time Constant The PEV's response time to a thermal step: $$\tau = \frac{\rho \cdot c \cdot t}{h}$$ where $\rho$ = density, $c$ = specific heat, $t$ = target thickness, $h$ = heat transfer coefficient.
