Typical $\tau \approx 16$ ms (one TV frame). ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula / Expression** | **Typical Value (PEV)** | **Unit** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Peak IR wavelength | $\lambda_{\max}$ | $2898 / T$ (Wien's) | 9.5 | $\mu m$ | | Stefan-Boltzmann flux | $W$ | $\sigma T^4$ | 514 (skin) | $W/m^2$ | | Pyroelectric charge | $\Delta Q$ | $p \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | — | $C$ | | Pyroelectric coefficient (TGS) | $p$ | — | $2.5 \times 10^{-8}$ | $C/(cm^2 \cdot K)$ | | NETD | $\text{NETD}$ | $V_n / R_v$ | 0.1 – 0.3 | $K$ | | MRTD | $\text{MRTD}(f)$ | $\text{NETD} \cdot k(f) / \text{MTF}(f)$ | 0.2 – 0.5 | $K$ | | Time constant | $\tau$ | $\rho c t / h$ | 16 | $ms$ | | Emissivity (skin) | $\varepsilon$ | — | 0.98 | dimensionless | | Curie temperature (TGS) | $T_C$ | — | 49 | $^\circ C$ | | Chopper frequency | $f_{\text{ch}}$ | — | 25 – 30 | $Hz$ | | Radiometric resolution | $\Delta T_{\min}$ | — | $\leq 0.1$ | $K$ | | TV line standard | — | 525 (NTSC) / 625 (CCIR) | 525 | lines | | Frame rate | $f_f$ | — | 30 / 25 | $Hz$ | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Medical screening** (pre-FPA era): breast thermography, vascular disorders, rheumatology - **Industrial predictive maintenance:** electrical substation inspection - **Defense/security:** night vision systems - **Firefighting:** search & rescue through smoke - **Building inspection:** thermal insulation audits > [!NOTE] > **Modern Context:** The PEV is now considered a **legacy technology** — current systems use **uncooled microbolometer FPAs** (e.g., vanadium oxide, amorphous silicon) with $640 \times 480$ or $1024 \times 768$ pixel resolution and NETD $< 50 \, mK$. However, the **principles of characterization (NETD, MRTD, MDTD) remain identical** — a KTU high-yield point. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Pyroelectric Charge Generated by an IR Heated Pixel **Given:** - Target area: $A = 1 \, cm^2$ - Temperature change due to IR absorption: $\Delta T = 0.5 \, K$ - Pyroelectric coefficient: $p = 2.5 \times 10^{-8} \, C \cdot cm^{-2} \cdot K^{-1}$ (for TGS) **Find:** $\Delta Q$ **Step 1 — State the governing equation** $$\Delta Q = p \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$ **Step 2 — Substitute numerical values** $$\Delta Q = (2.5 \times 10^{-8} \, C \cdot cm^{-2} \cdot K^{-1}) \times (1 \, cm^2) \times (0.5 \, K)$$ **Step 3 — Compute the product** $$\Delta Q = 2.5 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.5 \, C$$ $$\boxed{\Delta Q = 1.25 \times 10^{-8} \, C = 12.5 \, nC}$$ **Step 4 — Engineering interpretation** A 0.5 K temperature rise on a 1 cm² TGS crystal produces only **12.5 nC** of charge — an extremely small signal. This is why the PEV required **low-noise preamplifiers** and **chopper modulation** for reliable operation. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Peak Wavelength of Human Skin Emission **Given:** - Skin surface temperature: $T = 305 \, K$ (≈ 32 °C) **Find:** Peak emission wavelength $\lambda_{\max}$ **Step 1 — Apply Wien's Displacement Law** $$\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, m \cdot K$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $\lambda_{\max}$** $$\lambda_{\max} = \frac{2.898 \times 10^{-3}}{305}$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate** $$\lambda_{\max} = 9.502 \times 10^{-6} \, m$$ $$\boxed{\lambda_{\max} \approx 9.5 \, \mu m}$$ **Step 4 — Engineering interpretation** This wavelength falls in the **LWIR atmospheric window (8–14 µm)**, which is precisely why PEV cameras (and modern microbolometers) use **germanium optics** and are designed for this band. The lens material must be transparent to 8–14 µm; ordinary glass is opaque, hence Ge/Si optics. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Radiant Power Emitted by 1 cm² of Skin **Given:** - $T = 305 \, K$, $\varepsilon = 0.98$, $A = 1 \, cm^2 = 10^{-4} \, m^2$ **Find:** Emitted radiant power $W$ **Step 1 — Apply Stefan-Boltzmann law with emissivity** $$W = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot T^4$$ **Step 2 — Substitute constants** ($\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, W/m^2 K^4$) $$W = 0.98 \times (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times (10^{-4}) \times (305)^4$$ **Step 3 — Compute $T^4$** $$T^4 = (305)^4 = 8.65 \times 10^{9} \, K^4$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the product** $$W = 0.98 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 10^{-4} \times 8.65 \times 10^{9}$$ $$W = 0.98 \times 4.905 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$\boxed{W \approx 48.07 \times 10^{-2} \, W = 4.81 \, mW \, per \, cm^2}$$ **Step 5 — Engineering interpretation** Even a 1 cm² patch of skin radiates ~5 mW in the LWIR — orders of magnitude more than the noise floor of a PEV (typically nW–µW). The challenge is **not detection**, but rather **rejecting ambient IR** and achieving **spatial resolution**. ### 3.4 Derivation 4: NETD from Camera Parameters **Given:** - Noise voltage: $V_n = 5 \, \mu V$ (RMS) - Voltage responsivity: $R_v = 50 \, \mu V / K$ **Find:** NETD **Step 1 — Apply the NETD definition** $$\text{NETD} = \frac{V_n}{R_v}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute values** $$\text{NETD} = \frac{5 \, \mu V}{50 \, \mu V / K}$$ $$\boxed{\text{NETD} = 0.1 \, K}$$ **Step 3 — Interpretation** A thermal sensitivity of **0.1 K** is excellent for clinical rheumatology, where typical inflammatory temperature elevations are 0.5–2.0 K above the contralateral joint. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Rheumatological Patterns vs. Thermal Signatures | **Rheumatological Condition** | **Thermal Signature** | **Typical $\Delta T$ (asymmetry)** | **Camera Setting** | |---|---|---|---| | Rheumatoid Arthritis (active) | Hot joint (synovitis) | +1.0 to +2.5 K | High gain, isotherm mode | | Osteoarthritis | Mild warm joint | +0.3 to +0.8 K | Standard palette | | Gout (acute) | Markedly hot, focal | +2.0 to +3.5 K | Narrow temp window | | Raynaud's phenomenon | Cold digits (after cold challenge) | −2.0 to −5.0 K | Cold-stress protocol | | Tendinitis | Linear hot band along tendon | +0.5 to +1.5 K | High resolution | | Fibromyalgia | Diffuse warm/cool pattern | Variable, $\pm 0.5$ K | Whole-body mapping | | Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS) | Hot in acute, cold in chronic | $\pm 1.0$ to $\pm 3.0$ K | Bilateral comparison | | Soft tissue injury | Focal hot spot | +1.0 to +2.0 K | Spot mode | ### 3.6 Thermographic Image Processing Pipeline (Exhaustive) > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU 2024 module specifically emphasizes this **5-stage processing pipeline**: **Stage 1 — Image Acquisition** - Patient equilibration in climate-controlled room (**20–24 °C**, humidity 40–60%) for **15 minutes** - Cooling protocol: remove clothing, allow skin to equilibrate - Camera positioned at standard distance (1.0–2.0 m) - Blackbody calibration source at known $T_{\text{ref}}$ for absolute temperature referencing **Stage 2 — Pre-processing** - **Dead pixel correction:** median filter substitutes for unresponsive pixels - **Non-uniformity correction (NUC):** gain and offset correction using two-point blackbody calibration at $T_1$ and $T_2$: $$T_{\text{corrected}}(i,j) = a(i,j) \cdot T_{\text{raw}}(i,j) + b(i,j)$$ where $a(i,j)$ and $b(i,j)$ are per-pixel gain and offset. - **Temporal filtering:** frame averaging to reduce random noise - **Spatial filtering:** Gaussian or Wiener filter for smoothing **Stage 3 — Image Enhancement** - **Histogram equalization** to spread the dynamic range - **Pseudo-color mapping** (iron, rainbow, grayscale) for visual interpretation - **Isotherm highlighting** — overlay of pixels within a chosen temperature window - **Contrast stretching** **Stage 4 — Image Segmentation** - **Region of interest (ROI) selection** over the affected joint and contralateral side - **Thresholding** to isolate "hot" or "cold" pixels - **Edge detection** (Sobel, Canny) to identify anatomical boundaries - **Watershed segmentation** for complex joint regions **Stage 5 — Feature Extraction & Quantitative Analysis** - **Mean temperature** $\bar{T}$ within ROI - **Temperature asymmetry index (TAI):** $$\text{TAI} = \frac{\vert \bar{T}_{\text{affected}} - \bar{T}_{\text{contralateral}} \vert}{\bar{T}_{\text{contralateral}}}$$ - **Standard deviation** $\sigma_T$ within ROI (heterogeneity) - **Maximum, minimum, and median** temperatures - **Area of hyperthermia** (% of ROI pixels above threshold) --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram of a Pyroelectric Vidicon Camera System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Patient Body
IR Emission 8-14 um] --> B[Ge or Si
IR Lens] B --> C[Mechanical
Chopper 25-30 Hz] C --> D[Pyroelectric
Target TGS] D --> E[Electron
Beam Scanner] E --> F[Signal
Electrode] F --> G[Preamplifier
Low Noise] G --> H[Video
Processor] H --> I[Display
Monitor] H --> J[Blackbody
Calibrator] J --> H I --> K[Image
Processing Unit] K --> L[Clinical
Diagnosis] ``` ### 4.2 Detailed Block Architecture of a Thermographic Imaging System ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ACQ[Image Acquisition Subsystem] P1[Climate Controlled Room
20-24 C] --> P2[Patient
Equilibration 15 min] P2 --> P3[IR Camera
PEV or FPA] P3 --> P4[Blackbody
Reference] end subgraph PRE[Pre processing Stage] S1[Dead Pixel
Correction] --> S2[Non Uniformity
Correction NUC] S2 --> S3[Noise
Filtering] end subgraph ENH[Enhancement Stage] E1[Histogram
Equalization] --> E2[Pseudo Color
Mapping] E2 --> E3[Isotherm
Overlay] end subgraph SEG[Segmentation Stage] G1[ROI
Selection] --> G2[Thresholding
and Edge Detect] G2 --> G3[Anatomical
Boundary Map] end subgraph ANA[Analysis Stage] Q1[Mean and Sigma
Temperature] --> Q2[Asymmetry
Index TAI] Q2 --> Q3[Area of
Hyperthermia] Q3 --> Q4[Clinical
Report] end ACQ --> PRE --> ENH --> SEG --> ANA ``` ### 4.3 Operational Flow of Clinical Thermography Examination ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Patient Arrives]) --> hist[Clinical History
and Consent] hist --> eq[Room Equilibration
15 minutes] eq --> im1[Standard Views
Anterior Posterior Lateral] im1 --> im2[Region Specific
Views Joint Focus] im2 --> im3[Functional Tests
Cold Stress etc] im3 --> cal[Blackbody
Calibration] cal --> proc[Image Processing
and Analysis] proc --> rep[Quantitative
Report Generation] rep --> doc[Clinician
Interpretation] doc --> dx[Diagnosis and
Treatment Plan] dx --> stop([Patient Discharge
Follow up Scheduled]) ``` ### 4.4 Pyroelectric Effect — Physical Principle ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Crystal at
Temperature T1] -->|IR Absorption| B[Temperature
Rises to T2] B --> C[Spontaneous
Polarization Decreases] C --> D[Surface Charge
Released] D --> E[Signal Electrode
Reads Displacement] E --> F[Electron Beam
Neutralizes Charge] F --> G[Current Proportional
to delta T] ``` ### 4.5 Signal Chain — From Photon to Pixel Value ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[IR Photon
from Skin] --> P2[Lens
Collection] P2 --> P3[Chopper
Modulation] P3 --> P4[Pyroelectric
Conversion] P4 --> P5[Charge to Voltage
Transimpedance] P5 --> P6[Amplification
and Filtering] P6 --> P7[ADC
Digitization] P7 --> P8[Pixel Value
0 to 255 Gray Level] P8 --> P9[Color Map
Display] ``` ### 4.6 Clinical Decision Logic for Rheumatology ```mermaid flowchart TD sym[Patient Symptoms
Joint Pain Swelling] --> scan[Thermographic
Scan] scan --> asym{Temperature
Asymmetry?} asym -->|Greater than 0.5 K| hot[Hyperthermia
Detected] asym -->|Less than 0.5 K| cold[Symmetric
or Cold Pattern] hot --> active[Active
Inflammation] active --> ra[Rheumatoid Arthritis
or Acute Gout] cold --> chr[Chronic or
Vasospastic] chr --> ray[Raynauds or
CRPS Chronic] ra --> treat[Treatment Plan
Anti Inflammatory] ray --> treat2[Vasodilator
Therapy] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Explain the principle of operation of a Pyroelectric Vidicon (PEV) camera. Why is a mechanical chopper necessary in such a camera?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A Pyroelectric Vidicon (PEV) camera operates on the **pyroelectric effect** in ferroelectric crystals such as **Triglycine Sulfate (TGS)**. When incident IR radiation (8–14 µm) from a target is focused onto the pyroelectric target, the local temperature rises, causing a change in spontaneous polarization and producing a surface charge proportional to the temperature change. A **scanning electron beam** (similar to a TV tube) reads this charge pattern by neutralizing it pixel-by-pixel, inducing a current in the signal electrode that is amplified and displayed as a video image of the temperature distribution. A **mechanical chopper** is essential because the PEV is **AC-coupled by design** — it responds only to *changing* IR flux, not to a static thermal scene. The chopper modulates the incoming IR at 25–30 Hz, producing a square-wave thermal stimulus so that the pyroelectric target alternates between heating and cooling, generating a usable signal. Without the chopper, the camera would be **blind to stationary temperature patterns**, which is the typical clinical scenario. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define NETD and MRTD. Why are these parameters important in clinical thermography?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **NETD (Noise Equivalent Temperature Difference):** The smallest temperature difference between a target and its background that produces a signal equal to the RMS noise of the system. It quantifies the **thermal sensitivity** of the camera. For a clinical PEV system, typical NETD ≤ 0.1–0.3 K. $$\text{NETD} = \frac{V_{\text{noise}}}{R_v}$$ **MRTD (Minimum Resolvable Temperature Difference):** The minimum temperature difference resolvable by a human observer as a function of spatial frequency. It combines thermal sensitivity with **spatial resolution** via the MTF. $$\text{MRTD}(f) = \frac{\text{NETD} \cdot k(f)}{\text{MTF}(f)}$$ These parameters are **clinically critical** because: 1. Inflammatory temperature elevations in rheumatology are typically **0.3–2.0 K** — a system with NETD > 0.5 K would miss them. 2. Spatial resolution (via MRTD) determines whether small joints (e.g., MCP joints in RA) can be imaged reliably. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]** **(a)** Describe the construction and working of a **Pyroelectric Vidicon (PEV) camera** with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of the **pyroelectric target**, the **electron beam scanner**, and the **mechanical chopper** in image formation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **camera characterization parameters** — NETD, MRTD, and MDTD — used in thermal imaging systems. Explain how these parameters affect **clinical diagnosis in rheumatology**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Construction and Working of PEV Camera — 7 marks** **Construction (3 marks):** - **Input window:** Germanium (Ge) or Silicon (Si) — transparent to 8–14 µm LWIR - **Pyroelectric target:** Thin slab (5–25 µm) of TGS (Triglycine Sulfate) or DTGS, deposited on a reticulated mesh support, with a transparent front electrode (NESA coating) - **Electron gun assembly:** Thermionic cathode, control grid, accelerating anode — produces a focused electron beam - **Deflection coils:** Magnetic deflection in standard TV raster (525-line, 30 Hz NTSC; or 625-line, 25 Hz CCIR) - **Signal electrode:** Backplate where the displacement current is collected - **Vacuum envelope:** All enclosed in a glass tube under high vacuum **Working (3 marks):** 1. IR radiation from the patient's body passes through the Ge/Si lens and chopper, falling on the pyroelectric target. 2. The target absorbs IR, its temperature rises by $\Delta T$, and the spontaneous polarization changes by $\Delta P_s = p \cdot \Delta T$, producing surface charge $\Delta Q = p \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$. 3. The electron beam scans the target line-by-line, neutralizing the surface charge. The displacement current induced in the signal electrode is **proportional to the local temperature**, generating the video signal. 4. The signal is amplified and reconstructed as a 2D thermal image, displayed on a monitor. **Role of Chopper (1 mark):** The PEV is **AC-coupled** — it cannot detect a static thermal scene. The chopper modulates IR at 25–30 Hz, producing a square-wave thermal stimulus, allowing the AC-coupled PEV to detect the temperature field. **(b) Camera Characterization Parameters — 7 marks** | **Parameter** | **Definition** | **Typical Value** | **Clinical Impact** | |---|---|---|---| | NETD | Smallest $\Delta T$ producing signal = noise | 0.1–0.3 K | Determines smallest detectable inflammation | | MRTD | Min $\Delta T$ resolvable at spatial freq $f$ | 0.2–0.5 K | Determines smallest joint size detectable | | MDTD | Min $\Delta T$ for large targets | ≈ NETD | Used for diffuse inflammation mapping | **Mathematical formulation (2 marks):** $$\text{NETD} = \frac{V_n}{R_v}$$ $$\text{MRTD}(f) = \frac{\text{NETD} \cdot k(f)}{\text{MTF}(f)}$$ **Clinical significance in rheumatology (2 marks):** - **Rheumatoid arthritis** typically produces $\Delta T = 1$–$2 \, K$ over inflamed MCP/PIP joints. With NETD = 0.1 K, the signal-to-noise ratio is 10–20, easily detectable. - **MRTD at 0.5 cycles/mrad** must be ≤ 0.5 K to resolve small joints (~5 mm). - **MDTD governs whole-limb mapping** in conditions like CRPS where the temperature gradient is large but diffuse. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > Many students **confuse NETD with MRTD**. NETD is a **purely electronic/thermal noise parameter** with no spatial element. MRTD is **observer-dependent** and includes the eye-brain integration. Writing "NETD is the minimum resolvable..." will cost **1–2 marks** in valuation. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]** **(a)** Explain the **image processing pipeline** used in medical thermography. Discuss the importance of **non-uniformity correction (NUC)**, **isotherm overlay**, and **temperature asymmetry index (TAI)** in clinical interpretation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **clinical applications of thermography in rheumatology** with specific reference to **rheumatoid arthritis**, **Raynaud's phenomenon**, and **complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS)**. Justify why thermography is a useful **adjunct diagnostic tool** rather than a stand-alone modality. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Thermographic Image Processing Pipeline — 7 marks** **Five-stage pipeline (5 marks):** 1. **Image Acquisition:** - Climate-controlled environment (20–24 °C, 40–60% humidity, no drafts) - 15-minute patient equilibration to allow skin to reach steady-state with environment - Blackbody reference calibration at $T_1$ and $T_2$ for absolute temperature scale - Standardized views: anterior, posterior, lateral, plus region-specific views 2. **Pre-processing:** - **Dead-pixel correction** using median filter substitution - **Non-uniformity correction (NUC):** Two-point correction $$T_{\text{corrected}}(i,j) = a(i,j) \cdot T_{\text{raw}}(i,j) + b(i,j)$$ - Temporal averaging (multi-frame) and spatial filtering (Gaussian/Wiener) 3. **Enhancement:** - **Histogram equalization** for dynamic range expansion - **Pseudo-color palette** assignment (iron, rainbow, grayscale) - **Isotherm overlay:** Pixels within a chosen temperature window highlighted in a distinct color, enabling visual quantification of "hot" regions 4. **Segmentation:** - Manual or automatic ROI selection (affected joint vs. contralateral) - Thresholding and edge detection (Sobel/Canny) 5. **Quantitative Analysis:** - **Temperature Asymmetry Index (TAI):** $$\text{TAI} = \frac{\vert \bar{T}_{\text{affected}} - \bar{T}_{\text{contralateral}} \vert}{\bar{T}_{\text{contralateral}}}$$ - Mean, σ, max, min, and % area above threshold - Clinical report generation **Importance of NUC, Isotherm, TAI (2 marks):** - **NUC** is essential because every pixel in the FPA/PEV has slightly different gain/offset; without NUC, image has "salt-and-pepper" noise and inaccurate temperature scale. - **Isotherm overlay** allows the clinician to instantly visualize the **extent** of inflammation. - **TAI** provides a **single quantitative number** for tracking disease progression or treatment response — critical for longitudinal studies. **(b) Clinical Applications in Rheumatology — 7 marks** | **Condition** | **Thermographic Finding** | **Mechanism** | |---|---|---| | **Rheumatoid Arthritis** | Hot MCP/PIP joints; $\Delta T = 1$–$2.5$ K; symmetric pattern in seropositive RA | Synovial hyperemia and increased vascularity from pannus formation | | **Raynaud's Phenomenon** | Cold fingers/toes after cold challenge; $\Delta T = -2$ to $-5$ K; delayed re-warming (>20 min) | Vasospasm of digital arteries; sympathetic hyperactivity | | **CRPS (Type I)** | Hot in acute stage (< 6 months); cold in chronic stage; asymmetry > 2 K | Autonomic dysregulation; neurogenic inflammation | **Justification as an adjunct (2 marks):** - **Non-ionizing, non-contact, non-invasive** — safe for repeated use, pregnancy, and pediatric patients - **Functional imaging** (shows physiology) complements **structural imaging** (X-ray, MRI shows anatomy) - **Real-time, dynamic** — captures thermal recovery curves (e.g., Raynaud's cold-challenge test) - **Cost-effective** compared to MRI - **Limitation:** Lower specificity (many conditions produce similar thermal patterns) and operator/environment dependence — hence **adjunct status**, not stand-alone. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > A common student mistake is to claim **"thermography replaces X-ray/MRI"** — this is **scientifically incorrect** and the examiner will deduct **2–3 marks**. Always frame thermography as a **complementary/adjunct modality** to structural imaging, with its unique strength being **functional/physiological information**. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4 Subtopic** **Pyroelectric Effect & PEV Camera:** - ✅ Pyroelectric effect = change in spontaneous polarization with temperature in ferroelectric crystals - ✅ Most common PEV target: **TGS (Triglycine Sulfate)**, $T_C = 49^\circ C$, $p \approx 2.5 \times 10^{-8} \, C/(cm^2 \cdot K)$ - ✅ PEV must use a **mechanical chopper** (AC-coupled response) - ✅ IR window: **8–14 µm** (LWIR atmospheric window, matches $\lambda_{\max} \approx 9.5$ µm for skin @ 305 K) - ✅ Lens material: **Germanium (Ge)** — not ordinary glass - ✅ Skin emissivity: $\varepsilon = 0.98$ **Camera Characterization:** - ✅ **NETD** = $V_n / R_v$ (purely thermal/electronic, no spatial element) - ✅ **MRTD** depends on spatial frequency and observer - ✅ **MDTD** ≈ NETD for large targets - ✅ PEV NETD typical: 0.1–0.3 K; modern FPA NETD < 50 mK - ✅ Thermal time constant $\tau = \rho c t / h \approx 16$ ms **Image Processing Pipeline:** - ✅ **5 stages:** Acquisition → Pre-processing → Enhancement → Segmentation → Analysis - ✅ **NUC equation:** $T_{\text{corrected}}(i,j) = a(i,j) T_{\text{raw}}(i,j) + b(i,j)$ - ✅ **TAI formula:** $\text{TAI} = \vert \bar{T}_{\text{affected}} - \bar{T}_{\text{contralateral}} \vert / \bar{T}_{\text{contralateral}}$ - ✅ **15-minute equilibration** is non-negotiable in protocol - ✅ Isotherm overlay = visual highlighting of a chosen temperature window **Clinical Applications in Rheumatology:** - ✅ **RA:** Hot joints, $\Delta T = +1$ to $+2.5$ K, symmetric in seropositive - ✅ **Raynaud's:** Cold digits, $\Delta T = -2$ to $-5$ K after cold challenge - ✅ **CRPS:** Hot acute / Cold chronic, asymmetry > 2 K - ✅ **Gout (acute):** Markedly hot, focal, $\Delta T = +2$ to $+3.5$ K - ✅ Thermography is an **adjunct** to X-ray/MRI — not a replacement - ✅ Strength: **functional/physiological** imaging, non-ionizing, dynamic, repeatable **Key Exam Traps to Avoid:** - ❌ Do not confuse NETD with MRTD - ❌ Do not claim thermography replaces structural imaging - ❌ Do not forget the chopper explanation - ❌ Do not write Wien's law without the constant $2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, m \cdot K$ - ❌ Do not forget the units in formula derivations ---

topics.title: Neurology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Neurology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Neuroradiology (Neurological Imaging)** is the specialized branch of medical imaging dedicated to the diagnostic visualization, characterization, and interventional treatment of diseases, disorders, and injuries of the **central nervous system (CNS)**, **peripheral nervous system (PNS)**, and the **cerebrovascular system**. It employs ionizing radiation (X-ray, CT), non-ionizing radiation (MRI, Ultrasound), and radionuclide-based techniques (PET, SPECT) to image the brain, spinal cord, cranial nerves, and associated vasculature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes the *engineering principles* of imaging modalities as applied to neurological structures — specifically the brain, skull, spinal column, and cerebrovascular network — rather than purely clinical diagnosis.

In the KTU 2024 scheme (PEBRT411), the engineering perspective covers:
- **Image acquisition physics** for neurological soft-tissue contrast.
- **Spatial and contrast resolution** requirements for visualizing structures as small as **~1–2 mm** (e.g., the hippocampus, pituitary stalk).
- **Safety constraints** including the **Specific Absorption Rate (SAR)** limit of **4 W/kg** (averaged over 6 minutes) for head MRI per IEC 60601-2-33.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Brain as a Locked Building"**
>
> Imagine the human skull as a **sealed, opaque concrete vault** housing the most valuable contents imaginable — the brain. You cannot see inside by opening a door (surgery is invasive and risky). Neurological imaging is the engineer's toolbox of "special cameras" designed to peer through the vault's walls from different angles, using different "lights":
>
> - **X-ray / CT** → A bright flashlight that outlines the vault's structure and detects dense foreign objects (like a hammer inside the bag).
> - **MRI** → A magnetic compass that reads the water content of every "filing cabinet" inside the vault with extraordinary detail.
> - **PET / SPECT** → A radioactive tracer that lights up only the "filing cabinets" that are actively working (functional imaging).
> - **Angiography** → Dye poured into the vault's plumbing that reveals the entire pipe network in vivid contrast.
>
> The radiologist/engineer chooses the right "camera" (or combination) based on whether the question is *structural* (Is there a bleed?), *functional* (Is this region active?), or *vascular* (Is there a blockage?).

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Context |
|---|---|---|
| Main magnetic field ($B_0$) for clinical MRI | **1.5 T** or **3.0 T** | Brain MRI standard |
| Larmor frequency (1.5 T) of Hydrogen | **63.87 MHz** | Proton resonance |
| CT slice thickness (brain) | **0.5 – 5 mm** | Spatial resolution |
| PET spatial resolution (clinical) | **4 – 6 mm** | Functional brain imaging |
| fMRI BOLD temporal resolution | **1 – 2 seconds** | Functional MRI |
| Adult brain mass | **~1.35 – 1.4 kg** | Anatomical reference |
| Cerebral blood flow (normal) | **~50 mL/100g/min** | Gray matter baseline |
| CSF T1 relaxation time (1.5 T) | **~3000 – 4000 ms** | MRI contrast |
| White matter T1 (1.5 T) | **~780 ms** | MRI contrast |
| Gray matter T1 (1.5 T) | **~920 ms** | MRI contrast |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Head Coil matters in MRI:** Because the brain sits inside the rigid skull, dedicated **head RF coils** (volume or phased-array) are used to maximize the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** — typically achieving gains of **2–4×** over body coils for brain imaging.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Neurological Imaging Modalities

Neurological imaging is broadly classified by the **type of information** it provides and the **physical principle** exploited.

#### A. Structural / Anatomical Imaging
- **Plain Skull Radiography** — Projection X-ray (largely obsolete for brain, but still used for skull fractures, sinus evaluation).
- **Computed Tomography (CT)** — Cross-sectional X-ray imaging; gold standard for acute trauma, hemorrhage, and bone.
- **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** — Superior soft-tissue contrast for brain parenchyma.

#### B. Functional / Metabolic Imaging
- **Functional MRI (fMRI)** — Detects Blood Oxygen Level Dependent (BOLD) signal.
- **Positron Emission Tomography (PET)** — Uses radiotracers (e.g., $^{18}$F-FDG) to map metabolism.
- **Single Photon Emission Computed Tomography (SPECT)** — Uses gamma-emitting tracers (e.g., $^{99m}$Tc-HMPAO).

#### C. Vascular Imaging
- **CT Angiography (CTA)** — Iodinated contrast + CT.
- **MR Angiography (MRA)** — Time-of-Flight (TOF) or contrast-enhanced.
- **Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA)** — Gold standard for vascular tree visualization.

#### D. Specialized Techniques
- **Myelography** — Intrathecal contrast for spinal canal imaging.
- **Diffusion Tensor Imaging (DTI)** — Maps white matter tracts.
- **Magnetoencephalography (MEG)** *(non-imaging, supplementary)* — Measures magnetic fields from neuronal activity.

### 2.2 Core Physics of Each Modality — Step-by-Step Logic

#### (i) CT of the Brain — Operating Principle
- The **X-ray tube** rotates around the head, projecting a fan or cone beam.
- Multiple 1D projections are recorded at angles $\theta_n$ (typically 1000+ projections per rotation).
- A **filtered back-projection (FBP)** or **iterative reconstruction** algorithm computes the linear attenuation coefficient $\mu(x,y)$ at each voxel.
- The result is displayed in **Hounsfield Units (HU)**, a normalized scale.

**CT Number Formula (Hounsfield Unit):**

$$HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

- Water = **0 HU**, Air = **−1000 HU**, Cortical bone ≈ **+1000 HU**.
- Gray matter ≈ **+35 to +45 HU**, White matter ≈ **+20 to +30 HU**, CSF ≈ **+15 HU**, Acute blood ≈ **+50 to +80 HU**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CT for acute stroke / trauma?** CT is **fast (< 5 sec/head)**, **widely available**, and **highly sensitive to acute hemorrhage** (which is hyperdense). MRI is more sensitive to early ischemia, but CT is the first-line modality in emergency neurology.

#### (ii) MRI of the Brain — Operating Principle
- The patient is placed in a strong static field $B_0$.
- Hydrogen protons in tissue precess at the **Larmor frequency**:

$$\omega_0 = \gamma \cdot B_0$$

where $\gamma = 42.58$ **MHz/T** is the gyromagnetic ratio for $^{1}H$.

- A **90° RF pulse** flips the net magnetization vector $M_0$ into the transverse plane.
- As the magnetization relaxes, it produces an **FID signal** that is spatially encoded by **gradient coils** ($G_x$, $G_y$, $G_z$).
- The signal is dominated by two relaxation processes:
  - **T1 (longitudinal/spin-lattice)** — Recovery of $M_z$ toward $M_0$.
  - **T2 (transverse/spin-spin)** — Decay of $M_{xy}$ due to dephasing.

**Basic MRI Signal Equation (for a spin-echo sequence, TE $\ll$ T2*):**

$$S = k \cdot \rho(H) \cdot \left(1 - e^{-TR/T_1}\right) \cdot e^{-TE/T_2}$$

where:
- $\rho(H)$ = proton (hydrogen) density
- $TR$ = repetition time
- $TE$ = echo time
- $k$ = system constant (includes coil sensitivity, $B_1$ field strength)

**T1-weighted brain contrast** (short TR, short TE):
- White matter → **bright** (short T1)
- Gray matter → **intermediate**
- CSF → **dark**

**T2-weighted brain contrast** (long TR, long TE):
- CSF → **bright** (long T2)
- Gray matter → **brighter than white matter**
- White matter → **darker**

#### (iii) PET of the Brain — Operating Principle
- A positron-emitting radiotracer (e.g., $^{18}$F-FDG) is injected IV.
- The tracer accumulates in metabolically active tissue (gray matter > white matter).
- Emitted positrons annihilate with electrons, producing **two 511 keV photons** emitted at **180°** (back-to-back).
- Detector rings register **coincidence events** along a **line of response (LOR)**.
- Image reconstruction (typically iterative, e.g., OSEM) yields a 3D map of tracer concentration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** PET spatial resolution is limited by the **positron range** in tissue (~1–2 mm for $^{18}$F) and the **detector size**, not by optical diffraction limits like in microscopy.

#### (iv) Cerebral Angiography
- A catheter is advanced (typically via the femoral artery) to the carotid or vertebral artery.
- Iodinated contrast is injected directly.
- A rapid sequence of X-ray images captures the contrast as it traverses the arterial, capillary, and venous phases.
- **Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA):** A pre-contrast "mask" image is subtracted from contrast-filled images, removing bone and soft tissue — leaving only the **vasculature**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Value | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hounsfield Unit (CT Number) | $HU = 1000 \cdot \dfrac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$ | dimensionless | CT density calibration |
| 2 | Larmor Frequency | $\omega_0 = \gamma \cdot B_0$ | rad/s (or Hz) | MRI frequency selection |
| 3 | Gyromagnetic ratio ($^{1}H$) | $\gamma = 42.58 \times 10^6$ | rad/(s·T) | MRI physics constant |
| 4 | MRI Signal (Spin Echo) | $S = k \cdot \rho \cdot (1 - e^{-TR/T_1}) \cdot e^{-TE/T_2}$ | arbitrary | Sequence design |
| 5 | T1 weighting condition | $TR \ll T_1$ (short TR) | ms | Contrast tuning |
| 6 | T2 weighting condition | $TE \ll T_2$ (long TE) | ms | Contrast tuning |
| 7 | Spatial resolution (CT) | $\Delta x = \dfrac{d}{M}$ where $d$ = detector pitch, $M$ = magnification | mm | Detector design |
| 8 | Spatial resolution (MRI) | $\Delta x = \dfrac{1}{2 \cdot \gamma \cdot G_x \cdot T_{acq}}$ | mm | Gradient performance |
| 9 | PET coincidence time window | $\Delta t \approx 6$ to **12 ns** | ns | True vs scatter rejection |
| 10 | BOLD signal (fMRI) | $\Delta S/S \approx 2$ to **5%** | dimensionless | Functional mapping |
| 11 | Specific Absorption Rate (SAR) limit (head) | **4 W/kg** (6-min average) | W/kg | RF safety |
| 12 | Acoustic noise in EPI (gradient) | Up to **130 dB** | dB | Hearing protection needed |
| 13 | PET photon energy | **511 keV** (per photon, 2 emitted) | keV | Detector choice |
| 14 | Power deposition in head (3 T MRI, average) | ~**1–2 W/kg** | W/kg | Below SAR limit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No Pipes in Tables:** All absolute-value and conditional expressions use `\vert` or `\mid` rather than the `|` character, ensuring the markdown table structure is not corrupted.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **MRI gradient coil engineering:** The demanding spatial encoding of the brain drives development of high-performance **slew-rate-limited gradients** (≥ **200 mT/m/ms**).
- **PET detector materials:** Neurological PET has driven adoption of **time-of-flight (TOF)** detectors (e.g., LYSO crystals) with coincidence timing resolution **< 400 ps**, improving SNR by **~2×** at 3T MRI.
- **Hybrid imaging systems:** **PET/CT** and **PET/MRI** scanners combine anatomical (CT/MRI) + functional (PET) data, enabling co-registration accuracy of **< 2 mm** — invaluable for neurosurgery planning.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hounsfield Unit Scale for Brain Tissues

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of linear attenuation.**

The X-ray intensity after passing through a thickness $x$ of material is governed by **Beer's Law**:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$$

where $\mu$ is the linear attenuation coefficient (units: cm$^{-1}$).

**Step 2 — For water (the reference medium).**

By definition, water has $\mu_{\text{water}} \approx 0.192$ cm$^{-1}$ at 73 keV (effective CT energy). The CT scale is **anchored** to water = 0 HU.

**Step 3 — Define the CT number as a normalized deviation.**

We define the CT number for any tissue as:

$$HU_{\text{tissue}} = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

The factor of **1000** makes water = 0 and air ≈ −1000, giving a convenient integer scale.

**Step 4 — Verify boundary cases.**

- For water: $HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_w - \mu_w}{\mu_w} = 0$ ✓
- For air ($\mu \approx 0$): $HU = 1000 \times \frac{0 - \mu_w}{\mu_w} = -1000$ ✓
- For dense cortical bone ($\mu \approx 2 \mu_w$): $HU = 1000 \times \frac{2\mu_w - \mu_w}{\mu_w} = +1000$ ✓

**Step 5 — Apply to a clinical brain CT scenario.**

Given a brain voxel with $\mu_{\text{tissue}} = 0.201$ cm$^{-1}$ (slightly above water), the CT number is:

$$HU = 1000 \times \frac{0.201 - 0.192}{0.0.192} = 1000 \times \frac{0.009}{0.192}$$

$$HU = 1000 \times 0.0469 \approx +46.9 \text{ HU}$$

This corresponds to **gray matter**, which is the engineering reason why gray matter appears slightly brighter than white matter (~+25 HU) on CT.

### 3.2 Derivation: Spatial Resolution in MRI (Readout Direction)

**Step 1 — Set up the k-space sampling equation.**

In the readout (frequency-encoding) direction, the received signal $S(t)$ encodes spatial information via the linear gradient $G_x$:

$$S(t) = \int \rho(x) \cdot e^{-i 2\pi k_x(t) x} \, dx$$

where the k-space coordinate evolves as:

$$k_x(t) = \frac{\gamma}{2\pi} \int_0^t G_x(\tau) \, d\tau = \frac{\gamma G_x t}{2\pi}$$

**Step 2 — Relate k-space range to field of view (FOV).**

The field of view in the readout direction is:

$$FOV_x = \frac{1}{\Delta k_x}$$

where $\Delta k_x$ is the sampling interval in k-space.

**Step 3 — Relate total k-space extent to spatial resolution.**

The maximum extent of k-space sampled is $k_{x,\max} = \dfrac{\gamma G_x T_{acq}}{2\pi}$, where $T_{acq}$ is the readout duration. The spatial resolution is:

$$\Delta x = \frac{1}{2 \cdot k_{x,\max}} = \frac{1}{2 \cdot \dfrac{\gamma G_x T_{acq}}{2\pi}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta x = \frac{\pi}{\gamma \cdot G_x \cdot T_{acq}}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example for a typical brain MRI.**

Given: $\gamma = 2\pi \times 42.58 \times 10^6$ rad/(s·T), $G_x = 25$ mT/m, $T_{acq} = 8$ ms:

$$\Delta x = \frac{1}{2 \times 42.58 \times 10^6 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \times 8 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\Delta x = \frac{1}{2 \times 42.58 \times 10^6 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\Delta x = \frac{1}{2 \times 8516} = \frac{1}{17032} \text{ m}$$

$$\Delta x \approx 5.87 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} \approx 0.59 \text{ mm}$$

This is a typical in-plane spatial resolution for a clinical brain MRI — sufficient to resolve structures like the **caudate nucleus** (~5 mm).

### 3.3 Derivation: BOLD Signal Origin in fMRI

**Step 1 — Define the transverse magnetization after a gradient-echo sequence.**

In a T2*-weighted sequence, the signal is:

$$S = k \cdot \rho \cdot e^{-TE/T_2^*} \cdot \sin(\alpha) \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-TR/T_1}}{1 - \cos(\alpha) \cdot e^{-TR/T_1}}$$

where $T_2^*$ includes both true T2 and the effect of local field inhomogeneities:

$$\frac{1}{T_2^*} = \frac{1}{T_2} + \frac{1}{T_2^{'}}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the source of BOLD contrast.**

$T_2^{'}$ is dominated by **local magnetic susceptibility variations**, primarily from **deoxyhemoglobin** (which is paramagnetic) in blood vessels.

**Step 3 — Neurovascular coupling.**

When a brain region becomes more active:
- Local cerebral blood flow (CBF) **increases** disproportionately to oxygen consumption.
- **Deoxyhemoglobin concentration decreases** in the venous blood of that region.
- The local field becomes **more uniform** → $T_2^{'}$ increases → $T_2^*$ increases → **Signal increases**.

**Step 4 — Quantify the BOLD effect.**

The fractional signal change is:

$$\frac{\Delta S}{S} \approx TE \cdot \Delta\left(\frac{1}{T_2^*}\right)$$

At 3 T, with $TE \approx 30$ ms, $\Delta S/S \approx 2 - 5\%$ — a tiny but statistically detectable signal used to map brain function.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Modality Selection for Neurological Conditions

| Clinical Question | Best Modality | Why | Why Not Alternatives |
|---|---|---|---|
| Acute head trauma / hemorrhage | **Non-contrast CT** | Fast (< 5 s), sensitive to acute blood (hyperdense ~+60 HU), bone visualization | MRI slower, less available |
| Acute ischemic stroke (first 6 h) | **MRI (DWI)** + CT (rule out bleed) | DWI shows cytotoxic edema within minutes | CT may be normal early |
| Brain tumor (detection & characterization) | **MRI with contrast** | Superior soft-tissue contrast, no ionizing radiation | CT less sensitive |
| Seizure focus localization | **PET + MRI** | Metabolic + anatomical co-registration | Single modality incomplete |
| Cerebrovascular aneurysm | **CTA / MRA / DSA** | Visualize vessel lumen & morphology | Non-contrast CT misses small aneurysms |
| Multiple sclerosis plaques | **MRI T2 / FLAIR** | Detects periventricular white matter lesions | CT invisible |
| Dementia / Alzheimer's | **PET (amyloid/tracer)** + MRI | Functional + structural atrophy | CT poor for hippocampal volume |
| Spinal cord compression | **MRI** | Direct visualization of cord & CSF | CT poor for cord itself |
| Pediatric neurological disorders | **MRI (no radiation)** | Avoids ionizing radiation in children | CT radiation risk |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — CT Dose Estimation for Brain

**Given:**
- CT brain scan: 120 kVp, 200 mAs, slice thickness = 5 mm, 40 slices, CTDI$_{vol}$ = 60 mGy.

**Find:** Effective dose to the head.

**Step 1 — Compute the Dose-Length Product (DLP).**

$$DLP = CTDI_{vol} \times L$$

where $L = N \times \text{thickness} = 40 \times 5 = 200$ mm = 20 cm.

$$DLP = 60 \text{ mGy} \times 20 \text{ cm} = 1200 \text{ mGy·cm}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the head-specific conversion coefficient.**

For the head, $k = 0.0023$ mSv/(mGy·cm) (ICRP 103):

$$E = DLP \times k = 1200 \times 0.0023 = 2.76 \text{ mSv}$$

This is approximately **1 year of natural background radiation** — emphasizing the KTU exam point that CT doses are non-trivial and ALARA principles apply.

## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Hounsfield Unit (HU) scale and explain why it is essential in brain CT interpretation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Hounsfield Unit (HU) is a normalized measure of X-ray attenuation in CT, defined as:

$$HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

It is essential in brain CT because it provides a **standardized, quantitative scale** for tissue density, enabling the radiologist/engineer to:
1. **Differentiate tissues**: CSF ≈ +15 HU, white matter ≈ +25 HU, gray matter ≈ +40 HU, acute blood ≈ +60 to +80 HU.
2. **Detect pathology**: e.g., fresh hemorrhage appears hyperdense, while edema and infarction appear hypodense.
3. **Calibrate scanners** across different manufacturers and institutions.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write "CT number measures density" without explicitly giving the formula and reference (water = 0). Always state the formula and the water anchor to earn full marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List and briefly explain three MRI pulse sequence parameters that determine brain image contrast.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three primary MRI pulse sequence parameters are:

1. **TR (Repetition Time)** — The interval between successive RF excitation pulses. **Short TR** (~400–600 ms) produces **T1-weighted** contrast; **long TR** (~3000–5000 ms) minimizes T1 effects and enables T2 weighting.

2. **TE (Echo Time)** — The time between the RF pulse and signal readout. **Short TE** (~10–20 ms) preserves transverse magnetization; **long TE** (~80–120 ms) emphasizes T2 decay and produces **T2-weighted** contrast.

3. **Flip Angle (α)** — The angle by which the RF pulse tips the magnetization. A small α (e.g., 5–10°) in **gradient echo** sequences enhances T1 contrast and enables rapid 3D brain imaging.

**Engineering takeaway:** By choosing TR, TE, and α, the engineer controls the weighting (T1, T2, or proton density) without changing the hardware.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 marks]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working principle of **MRI for brain imaging**. Explain the role of $B_0$ field, RF excitation, gradient encoding, and relaxation times (T1 and T2) in generating image contrast. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1.5 T MRI scanner uses a readout gradient of $G_x = 20$ mT/m and readout duration $T_{acq} = 8$ ms. Calculate the **in-plane spatial resolution** in the readout direction. Given: $\gamma = 2\pi \times 42.58 \times 10^6$ rad/(s·T). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working Principle of MRI Brain Imaging (7 marks):**

**Block diagram (already shown in Section 4.2).** Working explanation:

1. **Static Magnetic Field $B_0$ (~1.5 T or 3 T):** The patient's head is placed inside the bore. Hydrogen protons in tissue align either parallel (low energy) or anti-parallel (high energy) to $B_0$, creating a net longitudinal magnetization $M_0$. **(1 mark)**

2. **RF Excitation:** An RF pulse at the Larmor frequency $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$ (≈ 63.87 MHz at 1.5 T) is transmitted via the head coil, tipping the magnetization into the transverse plane. **(1 mark)**

3. **Gradient Encoding:** Three orthogonal gradient coils ($G_x$, $G_y$, $G_z$) linearly vary $B_0$ across space, making the Larmor frequency position-dependent. This enables **slice selection** (Gz), **phase encoding** (Gy), and **frequency encoding** (Gx). **(2 marks)**

4. **Relaxation & Signal Detection:** As transverse magnetization decays, it induces an FID signal in the receiver coil. Signal amplitude depends on:
   - **T1 (spin-lattice) recovery** of longitudinal magnetization → controls T1 contrast.
   - **T2 (spin-spin) dephasing** of transverse magnetization → controls T2 contrast.
   - **Proton density** $\rho(H)$ → modulates both contrasts.
   **(2 marks)**

5. **Image reconstruction:** The raw data fills k-space; a 2D/3D Fourier Transform yields the brain image. Contrast weighting is determined by TR, TE, and flip angle. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Spatial Resolution Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Write the resolution formula:**

$$\Delta x = \frac{\pi}{\gamma \cdot G_x \cdot T_{acq}}$$

Or equivalently, in terms of frequency:

$$\Delta x = \frac{1}{\gamma G_x T_{acq}/(2\pi)} \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values in SI units.**

- $\gamma = 2\pi \times 42.58 \times 10^6$ rad/(s·T)
- $G_x = 20 \times 10^{-3}$ T/m
- $T_{acq} = 8 \times 10^{-3}$ s

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator:**

$$\gamma \cdot G_x \cdot T_{acq} = 2\pi \times 42.58 \times 10^6 \times 20 \times 10^{-3} \times 8 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$= 2\pi \times 42.58 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$= 2\pi \times 6812.8$$

$$= 42799.5 \text{ rad/m}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the formula:**

$$\Delta x = \frac{\pi}{42799.5} = 7.34 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta x \approx 0.0734 \text{ mm} \approx 73 \text{ µm}}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:**

This extremely fine in-plane resolution (~73 µm) is theoretical; practical brain MRI achieves ~0.5–1 mm after accounting for SNR, motion, and T2* blurring. The engineering trade-off: stronger gradients and longer readout yield finer resolution but increase **echo spacing**, **SAR**, and **susceptibility artifacts**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often forget to convert the **angular frequency** $\omega_0$ to **Hz** by dividing by $2\pi$, or confuse $G_x$ (gradient strength in T/m) with gradient amplitude in mT/m. Always write units explicitly to avoid losing 1–2 marks.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 marks]**

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, describe the principle of **Computed Tomography (CT) for brain imaging**. Derive the Hounsfield Unit formula and explain the clinical significance of HU values for different brain tissues. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A brain CT scanner records a linear attenuation coefficient of $\mu_{\text{tissue}} = 0.205$ cm$^{-1}$ for a region of interest, with $\mu_{\text{water}} = 0.192$ cm$^{-1}$.
   (i) Calculate the **CT number (HU)** for this tissue.
   (ii) Identify the most likely tissue type.
   (iii) If the CTDI$_{vol}$ is 60 mGy and the scan length is 20 cm, calculate the **effective dose** to the head using $k = 0.0023$ mSv/(mGy·cm). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) CT Brain Imaging (7 marks):**

1. **Principle (block diagram from Section 4.3):** An X-ray tube rotates around the patient's head, emitting a fan beam. Detectors on the opposite side record the attenuated intensity $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ at many angles. **(1 mark)**

2. **Multiple projections** are collected (~1000 per rotation) and stored as a **sinogram**. **(1 mark)**

3. **Reconstruction:** A filtered back-projection (FBP) algorithm reconstructs $\mu(x,y)$ at every voxel, producing a cross-sectional image. **(1 mark)**

4. **Hounsfield Unit Derivation:**

$$HU = 1000 \times \frac{\mu_{\text{tissue}} - \mu_{\text{water}}}{\mu_{\text{water}}}$$

The factor **1000** normalizes the scale; water is anchored at **0 HU**. **(2 marks)**

5. **Clinical Significance of HU in Brain:**

| Tissue | HU Range | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Air | −1000 | Outside skull |
| Fat | −50 to −100 | Subcutaneous |
| White matter | +20 to +30 | Normal |
| Gray matter | +35 to +45 | Normal |
| CSF | +15 | Ventricles, sulci |
| Acute blood | +50 to +80 | Hemorrhage |
| Calcification | +100 to +300 | Chronic lesions |
| Bone (cortical) | +700 to +3000 | Skull | **(2 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**(i) CT number calculation (2 marks):**

$$HU = 1000 \times \frac{0.205 - 0.192}{0.192} = 1000 \times \frac{0.013}{0.192}$$

$$HU = 1000 \times 0.0677 \approx +67.7 \text{ HU}$$

**(ii) Tissue identification (2 marks):**

A CT number of **+68 HU** corresponds to **acute intracerebral hemorrhage** (typical range +50 to +80 HU) or possibly a calcified lesion. The high HU indicates **hyperdense** material consistent with **fresh blood or calcification**.

**(iii) Effective dose calculation (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — DLP:**

$$DLP = CTDI_{vol} \times L = 60 \text{ mGy} \times 20 \text{ cm} = 1200 \text{ mGy·cm}$$

**Step 2 — Effective dose:**

$$E = DLP \times k = 1200 \times 0.0023 = 2.76 \text{ mSv}$$

This is approximately **~1 year of natural background dose** (~2.4 mSv) — within diagnostic-acceptable levels but justifying the **ALARA** principle.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** A common error is using the wrong conversion coefficient. **Head = 0.0023 mSv/(mGy·cm)** and **Chest = 0.014 mSv/(mGy·cm)** — using the wrong k will lose 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **CT Number (HU):** Water = 0, Air = −1000, Bone ≈ +1000. Acute hemorrhage in brain is hyperdense (~+50 to +80 HU).
- [ ] **Larmor Frequency:** $\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$; for $^{1}H$, $\gamma = 42.58$ MHz/T.
- [ ] **MRI Contrast:** T1 (short TR, short TE) — fat/bright, water/dark. T2 (long TR, long TE) — water/bright, fat/darker.
- [ ] **MRI Signal Equation:** $S \propto \rho \cdot (1 - e^{-TR/T_1}) \cdot e^{-TE/T_2}$.
- [ ] **Spatial Resolution (MRI readout):** $\Delta x = \dfrac{1}{\gamma G_x T_{acq}/(2\pi)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}$.
- [ ] **PET:** Two 511 keV photons emitted at 180°; coincidence time window ~6–12 ns; $^{18}$F-FDG is the common brain tracer.
- [ ] **fMRI BOLD signal change:** ~2–5% at 3 T, arising from deoxyhemoglobin-induced $T_2^*$ changes.
- [ ] **SAR limit (head MRI):** 4 W/kg averaged over 6 min — critical safety constraint.
- [ ] **DSA:** Gold standard for cerebrovascular imaging; uses pre-contrast mask subtraction.
- [ ] **Acoustic noise in EPI:** Up to 130 dB — hearing protection mandatory for brain MRI.
- [ ] **Modality selection rule (acute trauma/hemorrhage):** Non-contrast CT is the FIRST line (fast, sensitive).
- [ ] **Modality selection rule (ischemic stroke, first 6 h):** MRI DWI is the most sensitive; CT may appear normal.
- [ ] **Modality selection rule (tumor):** MRI with gadolinium — superior soft-tissue contrast.
- [ ] **Modality selection rule (dementia):** PET (amyloid/FDG) + MRI for functional + structural co-registration.
- [ ] **Hybrid scanners:** PET/CT and PET/MRI enable < 2 mm co-registration for neurosurgical planning.
- [ ] **Effective dose for head CT:** ~2–3 mSv per scan; use ALARA principles, especially in pediatric patients.
- [ ] **Imaging the head requires dedicated head coils** for optimal SNR (2–4× gain over body coil).
- [ ] **T1 values (1.5 T):** CSF ~3500 ms, gray matter ~920 ms, white matter ~780 ms.
- [ ] **T2 values (1.5 T):** CSF ~2000 ms, gray matter ~100 ms, white matter ~80 ms.
- [ ] **DTI (Diffusion Tensor Imaging):** Maps white matter tract orientation by measuring water diffusion anisotropy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to compare modalities, always structure your answer using the **engineering trade-off triangle — Spatial Resolution vs. Contrast Resolution vs. Acquisition Speed vs. Safety (Radiation/RF/SAR)**. Examiners reward students who demonstrate **engineering reasoning**, not just clinical listing.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Oncology — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Oncology** is the branch of medicine that deals with the **prevention, diagnosis, treatment, and study of cancer (malignant neoplasms)**. In medical imaging, oncology refers specifically to the application of imaging modalities (X-ray, CT, MRI, PET, Ultrasound) to **detect, stage, localize, and monitor** cancerous tissues and treatment response.

A **neoplasm** (literally "new growth") is an abnormal mass of tissue resulting from uncontrolled cellular proliferation. When such growth is **malignant**, it can invade adjacent tissues (local invasion) and spread to distant organs via blood or lymph (metastasis). Imaging plays a central role in differentiating benign from malignant masses, in staging (TNM classification), and in follow-up.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology for KTU:**
> - **Benign tumor**: Localized, non-invasive, often encapsulated
> - **Malignant tumor**: Invasive, metastatic potential
> - **Metastasis**: Spread of cancer from primary site to a secondary organ
> - **Staging**: Process of determining the extent of cancer spread (TNM system)
> - **Grading**: Microscopic assessment of tumor aggressiveness

## 1.2 Physiotherapy — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physiotherapy (Physical Therapy)** is a healthcare profession that uses **evidence-based physical approaches** — exercise, manual therapy, electrotherapy, thermotherapy, and hydrotherapy — to **restore, maintain, and maximize functional mobility, strength, and quality of life**, particularly in patients recovering from surgery, injury, or chronic disease (including post-cancer treatment).

In medical imaging contexts, physiotherapy is closely tied to **musculoskeletal imaging, neurological imaging, and rehabilitation assessment**. Imaging (X-ray, MRI, ultrasound) is used to **diagnose** conditions that physiotherapists treat, and to **monitor rehabilitation progress** (e.g., joint healing, muscle recovery, spinal alignment).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "Oncology is like detective work on a silent intruder":**
> Imagine a thief sneaking into a house (the body) and setting up small camps (tumors) that grow quietly. **Oncology imaging** is like installing **security cameras, motion sensors, and thermal scanners** (CT, MRI, PET) throughout the house to find where the intruder is hiding, how big the camp has grown, and whether it has spread to other rooms. **Physiotherapy** is like the **repair team** that comes after the intruder has been removed (surgery/chemo), rebuilding damaged walls (muscles), fixing broken doors (joints), and restoring the house to normal function.

## 1.4 Connection Between Oncology and Physiotherapy

These two fields intersect significantly:

| Aspect | Oncology Role | Physiotherapy Role |
|--------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Pre-treatment** | Imaging to locate/measure tumor | Pre-operative conditioning, breathing exercises |
| **During treatment** | Monitor response (RECIST criteria) | Maintain mobility during chemo/radiotherapy |
| **Post-treatment** | Surveillance imaging | Restore function, manage lymphedema, fatigue |
| **Palliative care** | Pain imaging | Pain management, quality-of-life preservation |

## 1.5 Key Imaging Modalities in Oncology

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary Imaging Tools for Cancer Detection (KTU High-Yield):**
> 1. **X-ray (Radiography)** — Bone tumors, chest lesions, mammography
> 2. **Computed Tomography (CT)** — Staging, lung/abdomen/pelvis
> 3. **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** — Soft tissue, brain, spine, prostate
> 4. **Positron Emission Tomography (PET)** — Metabolic activity, metastases
> 5. **Ultrasound (US)** — Liver, breast, thyroid, image-guided biopsy
> 6. **Mammography** — Breast cancer screening
> 7. **SPECT** — Bone scans, sentinel node detection

## 1.6 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard radiation dose for diagnostic imaging**: typically **1–10 mGy** (milligray) per examination
- **PET Standardized Uptake Value (SUV) threshold for malignancy**: typically **SUV > 2.5** (varies by tissue)
- **CT dose index (CTDI)**: measured in **mGy**
- **X-ray exposure for chest X-ray**: effective dose ~ **0.02 mSv**
- **Thermal conductivity of tissue**: **0.5 W/(m·K)** (relevant for therapeutic ultrasound)
- **Speed of ultrasound in soft tissue**: **1540 m/s** (standard)

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Hallmarks of Cancer (Biological Basis for Imaging)

Cancer cells exhibit distinct biological properties that imaging exploits:

1. **Sustained proliferation** → Increased metabolism → PET detection (FDG uptake)
2. **Evading apoptosis** → Continued growth → Size change on CT/MRI
3. **Inducing angiogenesis** → Increased vascularity → Contrast enhancement on CT/MRI
4. **Invasion & metastasis** → Spread to nodes/organs → Whole-body PET/CT
5. **Reprogramming energy metabolism** → Warburg effect → High glucose uptake on PET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why imaging is essential in oncology:**
> - **Detection**: Finding tumors before symptoms
> - **Diagnosis**: Characterizing tissue (benign vs. malignant)
> - **Staging**: Determining extent (TNM — Tumor, Node, Metastasis)
> - **Treatment planning**: Targeting radiation, surgical resection margins
> - **Treatment response**: Following up with serial scans
> - **Surveillance**: Detecting recurrence

## 2.2 TNM Staging System (KTU Mandatory Knowledge)

The **TNM system** is the global standard for cancer staging:

| Parameter | Meaning | Example (Lung Cancer) |
|-----------|---------|------------------------|
| **T** (Tumor) | Size/extent of primary tumor | T1: $\leq$ 3 cm; T4: invasion of mediastinum |
| **N** (Node) | Regional lymph node involvement | N0: no nodes; N3: contralateral nodes |
| **M** (Metastasis) | Distant spread | M0: no distant mets; M1: distant mets present |

**Stage Grouping**: Stages **0, I, II, III, IV** (Stage IV = metastatic, most advanced)

## 2.3 RECIST Criteria (Response Evaluation Criteria In Solid Tumors)

Used to assess treatment response quantitatively:

| Response Category | Definition (Change in Sum of Longest Diameter) |
|-------------------|-------------------------------------------------|
| **Complete Response (CR)** | Disappearance of all target lesions |
| **Partial Response (PR)** | $\geq$ 30% decrease from baseline |
| **Stable Disease (SD)** | Neither PR nor PD |
| **Progressive Disease (PD)** | $\geq$ 20% increase; or new lesions |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Oncology Imaging

| Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units | Application |
|---------------------|----------|-------|-------------|
| **Standardized Uptake Value (SUV)** | $SUV = \dfrac{C_{tissue}}{C_{injected}/V_{body}}$ | Dimensionless (g/mL) | PET imaging quantification |
| **CT Dose Index (CTDI)** | $CTDI = \dfrac{1}{nT} \int_{-7T}^{+7T} D(z)\,dz$ | mGy | Radiation dose in CT |
| **Effective Dose (E)** | $E = \sum_{T} w_T \cdot H_T$ | mSv | Total radiation risk |
| **Tumor Volume (Ellipsoid)** | $V = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi a b c$ | cm³ | Tumor sizing on MRI/CT |
| **RECIST % Change** | $\Delta\% = \dfrac{D_{new} - D_{baseline}}{D_{baseline}} \times 100$ | % | Treatment response |
| **X-ray Attenuation (Beer–Lambert)** | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | — | Radiographic contrast |
| **MRI Relaxation Time (T1, T2)** | $M_z(t) = M_0(1 - e^{-t/T_1})$ | ms | Tissue characterization |
| **Half-Life of Radiotracer** | $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Becquerels (Bq) | PET/SPECT decay |
| **Specific Absorption Rate (SAR)** | $SAR = \dfrac{\sigma \vert E \vert^2}{2\rho}$ | W/kg | MRI safety limit (4 W/kg) |
| **Spatial Resolution (US)** | $d = \dfrac{0.61 \lambda}{NA}$ | mm | Ultrasound resolution |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on notation:** Absolute value is written as $\vert E \vert$ in LaTeX to avoid markdown table parsing errors. In your answer sheets, you may write $\vert E \vert$ or $\mid E \mid$ equivalently.

## 2.5 Physiotherapy — Theoretical Framework

Physiotherapy operates on multiple body systems:

1. **Musculoskeletal**: Bones, joints, muscles, tendons, ligaments
2. **Neurological**: Brain, spinal cord, peripheral nerves
3. **Cardiopulmonary**: Heart, lungs, circulation
4. **Integumentary**: Skin, wound healing

### Core Physiotherapy Techniques

| Modality | Principle | Clinical Use | Imaging Correlation |
|----------|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Therapeutic Exercise** | Mechanical loading → tissue adaptation | Post-op rehab, strengthening | MRI to assess healing |
| **Manual Therapy** | Joint mobilization, soft tissue work | Stiffness, pain | X-ray for alignment |
| **Electrotherapy (TENS, IFC)** | Electrical stimulation of nerves | Pain control | EMG for nerve function |
| **Therapeutic Ultrasound** | Deep heating via acoustic waves | Tendinitis, scar tissue | US imaging for diagnosis |
| **Hydrotherapy** | Buoyancy, resistance in water | Joint unloading, circulation | — |
| **Cryotherapy** | Cold-induced vasoconstriction | Acute inflammation, swelling | — |
| **Lymphatic Drainage** | Manual technique to reduce edema | Post-mastectomy lymphedema | Lymphoscintigraphy |

## 2.6 Imaging in Physiotherapy Assessment

Imaging supports physiotherapy through:

- **X-ray**: Fracture healing, joint alignment, arthritis progression
- **MRI**: Soft tissue injury (ligament tears, disc herniation, muscle strain)
- **Ultrasound**: Real-time assessment of tendon/muscle during movement
- **DEXA scan**: Bone mineral density for osteoporosis

## 2.7 Key Physiotherapy Metrics

| Metric | Formula / Definition | Use |
|--------|----------------------|-----|
| **Range of Motion (ROM)** | Measured in degrees (°) via goniometer | Joint flexibility |
| **Muscle Strength (MRC Scale)** | Grades 0–5 (0=paralysis, 5=normal) | Strength assessment |
| **Pain Scale (VAS)** | 0–10 numerical rating | Subjective pain |
| **Barthel Index** | 0–100 score for ADL independence | Functional outcome |
| **6-Minute Walk Test** | Distance walked in 6 minutes | Cardiopulmonary capacity |
| **Berg Balance Scale** | 14-item balance test, max 56 | Fall risk in elderly |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oncology imaging** drives the design of hybrid scanners (PET/CT, PET/MRI), which require precise mechanical co-registration and advanced image fusion algorithms.
- **Physiotherapy** devices (robotic exoskeletons, electrical stimulators) increasingly integrate **biofeedback sensors** that mimic medical imaging principles (ultrasonic, EMG-based).
- Both fields rely on **biomechanics** and **tissue characterization** — direct applications of physics and engineering.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Standardized Uptake Value (SUV) — The Key PET Parameter

The **SUV** quantifies how much of a radiotracer (typically $^{18}$F-FDG, fluorodeoxyglucose) accumulates in a tissue region, normalized to the injected dose and the patient's body mass.

### Step 1: Define Injected Dose Concentration

After injection, the radiotracer distributes throughout the body. The **injected concentration** $C_{injected}$ is the activity per unit volume of the body, given by:

$$
C_{injected} = \dfrac{A_{injected}}{V_{body}}
$$

where $A_{injected}$ is the injected activity (in Becquerels, Bq, or millicuries, mCi) and $V_{body}$ is the patient's body volume derived from body mass assuming tissue density $\rho \approx 1$ g/mL:

$$
V_{body} = \dfrac{m_{body}}{\rho_{tissue}}
$$

### Step 2: Define Tissue Activity Concentration

$C_{tissue}$ is the measured activity concentration (Bq/mL) in the region of interest (ROI) from the PET scan at a specific time post-injection.

### Step 3: Form the SUV Ratio

By definition:

$$
SUV = \dfrac{C_{tissue}}{C_{injected}} = \dfrac{C_{tissue}}{A_{injected} / V_{body}}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator:

$$
SUV = \dfrac{C_{tissue} \cdot V_{body}}{A_{injected}}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute Volume by Mass (since density ≈ 1 g/mL)

$$
SUV = \dfrac{C_{tissue} \cdot m_{body}}{A_{injected}}
$$

### Step 5: Clinical Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **SUV < 2.5** → typically **benign / low-grade** uptake
> - **SUV 2.5 – 10** → **suspicious / malignant**
> - **SUV > 10** → **highly malignant / aggressive**

### Step 6: Worked Numerical Example

Given:
- Injected activity $A_{injected} = 370$ MBq
- Body mass $m_{body} = 70$ kg
- Measured tissue activity $C_{tissue} = 25$ kBq/mL

Convert units consistently (1 MBq = 1000 kBq):
$A_{injected} = 370 \times 10^3$ kBq = $3.7 \times 10^5$ kBq

Body mass in grams: $70$ kg = $70{,}000$ g

Apply SUV formula:

$$
SUV = \dfrac{C_{tissue} \cdot m_{body}}{A_{injected}} = \dfrac{25 \text{ kBq/mL} \times 70{,}000 \text{ g}}{3.7 \times 10^5 \text{ kBq}}
$$

Compute numerator:

$$
25 \times 70{,}000 = 1{,}750{,}000
$$

Now divide:

$$
SUV = \dfrac{1{,}750{,}000}{370{,}000} = 4.73
$$

**Interpretation**: SUV = 4.73 → **suspicious for malignancy** (falls in the 2.5–10 range). This lesion would warrant biopsy or further imaging.

## 3.2 Derivation: Tumor Volume Using the Ellipsoid Formula

Tumors are often irregular, but for clinical staging, they are approximated as ellipsoids with semi-axes $a, b, c$ measured from CT or MRI.

### Step 1: Ellipsoid Volume Formula

The volume of an ellipsoid is:

$$
V = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi a b c
$$

### Step 2: Worked Example

A liver lesion measures $a = 2$ cm, $b = 1.5$ cm, $c = 1$ cm.

$$
V = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi (2)(1.5)(1)
$$

$$
V = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi (3) = 4\pi
$$

$$
V \approx 4 \times 3.1416 = 12.57 \text{ cm}^3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This volume is used in **RECIST 1.1** (which actually uses longest diameter, not volume) and in **volumetric response assessment** (an alternative to RECIST).

## 3.3 Derivation: RECIST Percentage Change

### Step 1: Sum of Longest Diameters

At baseline and at follow-up, the radiologist measures the longest diameter of each target lesion (up to 5 lesions, max 2 per organ) and sums them.

Baseline sum: $D_{baseline}$
Follow-up sum: $D_{followup}$

### Step 2: Percentage Change Equation

$$
\Delta\% = \dfrac{D_{followup} - D_{baseline}}{D_{baseline}} \times 100
$$

### Step 3: Worked Example

A patient has 2 lung lesions:
- Baseline: Lesion 1 = 4 cm, Lesion 2 = 3 cm → $D_{baseline} = 7$ cm
- Follow-up (after 3 chemo cycles): Lesion 1 = 2.5 cm, Lesion 2 = 2 cm → $D_{followup} = 4.5$ cm

$$
\Delta\% = \dfrac{4.5 - 7}{7} \times 100 = \dfrac{-2.5}{7} \times 100 = -35.7\%
$$

**Interpretation**: $-35.7\%$ reduction → exceeds the 30% threshold → **Partial Response (PR)**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Imaging Selection by Cancer Type

| Cancer Type | Primary Modality | Secondary Modality | Reason |
|-------------|------------------|---------------------|--------|
| **Lung** | CT | PET/CT | High spatial resolution for nodules |
| **Breast** | Mammography | MRI, US | Microcalcifications detection |
| **Brain** | MRI | CT | Superior soft-tissue contrast |
| **Liver** | CT / MRI | US | Lesion characterization |
| **Prostate** | MRI | Bone scan (SPECT) | Local staging + bone mets |
| **Lymphoma** | PET/CT | CT | Whole-body metabolic imaging |
| **Bone** | X-ray / MRI | Bone scan | Lesion detection, marrow involvement |
| **Colorectal** | CT | MRI (rectum), PET/CT | Staging, treatment planning |

## 3.5 Step-by-Step: Physiotherapy ROM Measurement

### Step 1: Identify the Joint and Movement
Example: Knee flexion after total knee replacement (TKR).

### Step 2: Position the Patient
Patient supine, hip at 0° extension.

### Step 3: Apply the Goniometer
- **Stationary arm**: Aligned with the greater trochanter (femur axis)
- **Axis**: At the lateral epicondyle of the femur
- **Movable arm**: Aligned with the lateral malleolus (fibula axis)

### Step 4: Record Measurements
- Pre-treatment knee flexion: 60°
- Post 6 weeks of physiotherapy: 110°

### Step 5: Compute Improvement

$$
\Delta ROM = ROM_{post} - ROM_{pre} = 110° - 60° = 50°
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Normal knee flexion is approximately **135°**. A 50° gain represents significant functional restoration, allowing the patient to climb stairs and sit comfortably.

## 3.6 Derivation: Therapeutic Ultrasound Intensity

The intensity $I$ of therapeutic ultrasound is:

$$
I = \dfrac{P}{A}
$$

where $P$ is the acoustic power (W) and $A$ is the effective radiating area (cm²).

**Worked Example**: $P = 1.5$ W, $A = 5$ cm²

$$
I = \dfrac{1.5}{5} = 0.3 \text{ W/cm}^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Therapeutic ultrasound typically uses intensities of **0.5–2.0 W/cm²**. Values above 3 W/cm² risk thermal tissue damage.

## 3.7 Imaging Physics Quick-Derivation: Attenuation

For X-ray or gamma-ray transmission through tissue:

$$
I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}
$$

where:
- $I_0$ = incident intensity
- $I$ = transmitted intensity
- $\mu$ = linear attenuation coefficient (cm$^{-1}$)
- $x$ = tissue thickness (cm)

**Worked Example**: For soft tissue, $\mu \approx 0.2$ cm$^{-1}$ at 60 keV. If $x = 10$ cm:

$$
I = I_0 e^{-0.2 \times 10} = I_0 e^{-2} = 0.135 \, I_0
$$

So only **13.5%** of the incident beam reaches the detector — a 86.5% attenuation.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Oncology. List the major imaging modalities used in cancer detection. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Oncology** is the branch of medicine concerned with the diagnosis, treatment, and study of **cancer (malignant neoplasms)**. In medical imaging, it refers to the use of imaging modalities for the **detection, staging, monitoring, and follow-up** of tumors.

**Major Imaging Modalities:**

1. **X-ray (Radiography)** — Used for bone tumors, chest lesions, and mammography.
2. **Computed Tomography (CT)** — Cross-sectional imaging for staging lung, abdominal, and pelvic cancers.
3. **Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI)** — Superior soft-tissue contrast for brain, spine, prostate, and musculoskeletal tumors.
4. **Positron Emission Tomography (PET)** — Functional/metabolic imaging, especially with $^{18}$F-FDG for whole-body staging.
5. **Ultrasound (US)** — Real-time imaging of liver, breast, thyroid, and image-guided biopsy.
6. **Mammography** — Dedicated breast imaging for screening and diagnosis.
7. **SPECT (Single Photon Emission CT)** — Bone scans, sentinel lymph node mapping.

*(Any 5 modalities with brief description = 3 marks)*

### Question 2: What is Physiotherapy? Mention any four techniques used in rehabilitation. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

**Physiotherapy (Physical Therapy)** is a healthcare profession that uses **physical methods** such as exercise, manual therapy, electrotherapy, and movement training to **restore, maintain, and improve physical function, mobility, and quality of life** in patients with injury, disability, or post-surgical conditions.

**Four Physiotherapy Techniques:**

1. **Therapeutic Exercise** — Structured physical activity to improve strength, flexibility, endurance, and joint range of motion.
2. **Manual Therapy** — Hands-on techniques like joint mobilization, manipulation, and soft-tissue massage to reduce pain and stiffness.
3. **Electrotherapy** — Use of electrical modalities such as TENS (Transcutaneous Electrical Nerve Stimulation) and IFC (Interferential Current) for pain relief and muscle re-education.
4. **Therapeutic Ultrasound** — Application of high-frequency sound waves (1–3 MHz) to generate deep tissue heating, promoting healing in tendons, ligaments, and muscles.

*(Definition = 1 mark; 4 techniques = 0.5 mark each = 2 marks; Total = 3 marks)*
