```mermaid
    style P fill:#bbdefb
    style P1 fill:#c8e6c9
    style P2 fill:#ffccbc
    style P3 fill:#f0f4c3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEBRT411]**
**"What is the fundamental purpose of nuclear medicine imaging, and how does it differ from conventional radiological imaging?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The fundamental purpose of nuclear medicine imaging is to obtain **functional, metabolic, and molecular information** about organs and tissues by administering a radiopharmaceutical and detecting the gamma rays it emits using an external detector.

Unlike conventional radiological imaging (X-ray, CT, MRI), which primarily displays **anatomical structure**, nuclear medicine visualizes **physiological function** — for example, glucose metabolism, blood perfusion, and receptor density.

The clinical advantage is that **functional changes in disease occur before anatomical changes**, allowing earlier diagnosis. *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for clinical relevance.)*

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBRT411]**
**"Define the tracer principle in nuclear medicine."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The tracer principle states that a **very small quantity** of a radioactive isotope (radiotracer) can be introduced into a biological system and will **follow the same metabolic, chemical, and biological pathways** as the corresponding stable element — but can be **detected externally** due to its characteristic radiation emission.

This principle, formulated by **Georg von Hevesy (Nobel Prize 1943)**, is the conceptual foundation of all nuclear medicine procedures, as it allows *in vivo* visualization and quantification of physiological processes without disturbing them. *(3 marks: 1.5 marks for definition, 1.5 marks for consequence/utility.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern:** Each Module typically offers **two internal-choice questions** of 14 marks. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are independent alternatives — a student need answer only one.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PEBRT411]**

**"Discuss in detail the purpose of nuclear medicine imaging. With suitable examples, explain how it complements anatomical imaging modalities. Derive the expression for effective half-life and compute the same for a radiopharmaceutical with $T_{phys} = 6$ h and $T_{biol} = 3$ h."**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Purpose of Nuclear Medicine Imaging (7 marks):**

Nuclear medicine imaging is a specialized branch of medical imaging whose central purpose is to visualize, characterize, and quantify **physiological and biochemical functions** of organs and tissues. The purpose can be enumerated under five key objectives:

1. **Functional imaging:** To detect metabolic activity such as glucose consumption (using F-18 FDG in PET), bone turnover (Tc-99m MDP), or myocardial perfusion (Tc-99m sestamibi).

2. **Molecular imaging:** To image specific cellular targets — receptors, transporters, enzymes — using radiolabeled ligands (e.g., dopamine transporter imaging with I-123 ioflupane for Parkinson's disease).

3. **Quantitative measurement:** To provide absolute physiological parameters — Standardized Uptake Value (SUV), ejection fraction (EF), glomerular filtration rate (GFR), regional cerebral blood flow (rCBF).

4. **Whole-body screening:** A single whole-body bone scan (Tc-99m MDP) can survey the entire skeleton in ~20 minutes for metastatic lesions.

5. **Image-guided therapy:** Pre-therapy scans (e.g., I-131 whole-body scan in thyroid cancer) determine dosimetry and treatment planning.

**Complementarity with anatomical imaging:** While X-ray, CT, and MRI answer *"Where is the lesion?"* with millimetric spatial resolution, they cannot answer *"Is the lesion metabolically active?"*. Nuclear medicine fills this gap. A typical clinical workflow is:
- **CT/MRI** → localizes the anatomical abnormality.
- **PET/SPECT** → confirms whether the abnormality is metabolically active (benign vs. malignant).
- Combined **PET/CT** or **SPECT/CT** scanners now provide both in a single session.

---

**(b) Derivation of Effective Half-Life (7 marks):**

The activity in the body decreases due to **two simultaneous processes**:
- **Physical decay** (decay constant $\lambda_{phys}$)
- **Biological elimination** (decay constant $\lambda_{biol}$)

The net rate of decrease is:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -(\lambda_{phys} + \lambda_{biol})N = -\lambda_{eff} \, N$$

Solving the differential equation:

$$N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda_{eff} t}$$

Substituting $\lambda = \dfrac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$ for each:

$$\frac{\ln 2}{T_{eff}} = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{phys}} + \frac{\ln 2}{T_{biol}}$$

Dividing by $\ln 2$:

$$\frac{1}{T_{eff}} = \frac{1}{T_{phys}} + \frac{1}{T_{biol}}$$

$$\boxed{T_{eff} = \frac{T_{phys} \cdot T_{biol}}{T_{phys} + T_{biol}}}$$

**Numerical computation** with $T_{phys} = 6$ h, $T_{biol} = 3$ h:

$$T_{eff} = \frac{6 \times 3}{6 + 3} = \frac{18}{9} = 2.0 \text{ hours}$$

> **Conclusion:** The effective half-life is **2.0 hours**, which is significantly shorter than either the physical or biological half-life alone — this is the key metric for patient dosimetry and for scheduling the imaging time post-injection. *(14 marks: 7 for part a, 7 for part b.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2023, PEBRT411]**

**"Explain the clinical purposes served by nuclear medicine imaging in modern healthcare. Compare its diagnostic purpose with that of CT and MRI. With a neat block diagram, describe the workflow of a typical PET imaging procedure."**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Clinical Purposes of Nuclear Medicine Imaging (7 marks):**

Nuclear medicine serves several unique clinical purposes that no other imaging modality can fully replicate:

1. **Oncology (Cancer management):** Staging, restaging, treatment-response assessment, and detection of recurrence. The single most impactful application is **F-18 FDG PET/CT** in lung cancer, lymphoma, melanoma, and colorectal cancer.

2. **Cardiology:** Assessment of myocardial perfusion (Tc-99m sestamibi SPECT), viability, and ejection fraction. Stress-rest MPI is a gold-standard non-invasive test for coronary artery disease.

3. **Neurology:** Evaluation of Parkinson's disease (DaTSCAN), Alzheimer's disease (FDG-PET for hypometabolism), and epilepsy focus localization.

4. **Endocrinology:** Thyroid function imaging (Tc-99m pertechnetate, I-123), parathyroid adenoma localization.

5. **Nephrology & Urology:** Renogram for differential renal function and obstruction (Tc-99m DTPA, MAG3); GFR measurement.

6. **Bone & Joint Disease:** Detection of occult fractures, osteomyelitis, avascular necrosis, and metastases.

**Comparison with CT and MRI:**

| **Attribute** | **CT** | **MRI** | **Nuclear Medicine** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Diagnostic Purpose | Anatomical cross-section | Soft-tissue contrast | **Functional/metabolic** |
| Time of Disease Detection | Late (anatomical change) | Late | **Earliest (molecular)** |
| Quantification | CT numbers (HU) | Signal intensity | **Absolute (SUV, mL/min/100g)** |
| Best For | Bone, lung, acute hemorrhage | Brain, spine, joints | **Cancer, perfusion, receptors** |

---

**(b) Block Diagram of PET Imaging Workflow (7 marks):**

```
Patient Preparation
   → Fasting 4–6 hours
   → Blood glucose check (< 200 mg/dL)
   → IV Injection of F-18 FDG (~370 MBq)
       → Uptake Period (45–60 min in dim, quiet room)
           → Patient positioning in PET/CT scanner
               → CT Scout → CT Acquisition (for attenuation correction)
                   → PET Acquisition (3–5 min per bed position)
                       → Image Reconstruction (OSEM with TOF)
                           → Attenuation + Scatter Correction
                               → Display Fusion Image (CT + PET overlay)
                                   → Clinical Interpretation & SUV Calculation
```

The diagnostic purpose of PET lies in detecting **increased glucose uptake** (high SUV) in malignant tissues, which is a hallmark of cancer metabolism (the **Warburg effect**). A region with SUV $> 2.5$ is generally considered suspicious for malignancy in lung nodules.

> **Conclusion:** The unique diagnostic purpose of nuclear medicine — particularly PET — is to provide **quantitative, whole-body, functional information** that complements the anatomical detail of CT and MRI, leading to more accurate diagnosis, staging, and treatment planning. *(14 marks: 7 + 7.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in the "Purpose" Question:**
> 1. **Vague answer trap:** Writing only *"Nuclear medicine is used to image organs"* — this fetches **0 marks**. You MUST mention **functional/metabolic** and contrast with **anatomical imaging**.
> 2. **Skipping the derivation:** The $T_{eff}$ formula derivation is worth **3 of the 7 marks** in the numerical part. Skipping steps costs heavy deductions.
> 3. **Unit mistakes:** Writing effective half-life in seconds when the question gives it in hours is a guaranteed 0.5-mark cut. Always re-state units.
> 4. **Forgetting the "Why"**: Examiners do not award full marks for *what* nuclear medicine is — they require the *why* (i.e., early disease detection, quantification, whole-body screening, theranostics).
> 5. **Mixing up PET and SPECT**: PET uses 511 keV annihilation photons and a *ring* of detectors; SPECT uses 140 keV gamma photons and a *gamma camera with collimator*. Conflating them is a frequent error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **Save this checklist the night before the exam!**

- ⭐ **Core phrase to memorize:** *"Functional, metabolic, and molecular imaging — the purpose of nuclear medicine."*
- ⭐ **Five pillars of purpose:** Functional imaging, molecular imaging, quantification, whole-body screening, image-guided therapy/theranostics.
- ⭐ **Tracer principle:** Georg von Hevesy, 1943 Nobel Prize — a radioactive atom follows the same biological path as its stable counterpart but is externally detectable.
- ⭐ **Key equation — Effective half-life:** $\dfrac{1}{T_{eff}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{phys}} + \dfrac{1}{T_{biol}}$ → $T_{eff} = \dfrac{T_{phys} \cdot T_{biol}}{T_{phys} + T_{biol}}$.
- ⭐ **Standard photopeak energies:** Tc-99m = **140 keV**, I-123 = **159 keV**, F-18 (PET) = **511 keV**.
- ⭐ **Standard patient doses:** Tc-99m bone scan ≈ **740 MBq**, F-18 FDG PET ≈ **370 MBq**.
- ⭐ **Resolution:** SPECT ≈ **8–10 mm**; PET ≈ **4–6 mm**.
- ⭐ **Always mention clinical examples:** Cancer (FDG-PET), thyroid (I-131), bone (Tc-99m MDP), heart (Tc-99m sestamibi), brain (DaTSCAN).
- ⭐ **Compare/contrast table with CT/MRI is mandatory for full marks** in 14-mark questions.
- ⭐ **Derivation of $T_{eff}$** is the most common numerical sub-part — practice it at least 3 times.
- ⭐ **Use units correctly:** Becquerel (Bq), kiloelectron-volt (keV), millisievert (mSv), megabecquerel (MBq).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Cath Labs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Cath Labs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Catheterization Laboratory (Cath Lab):** A specially equipped, radiation-shielded, sterile interventional radiology/cardiology suite housing a high-output X-ray imaging chain (C-arm, image intensifier/flat-panel detector, generator, image processor) and integrated hemodynamic monitoring systems, designed to perform **minimally invasive fluoroscopically-guided diagnostic and therapeutic vascular procedures** such as coronary angiography, percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (PTCA), stenting, and structural heart interventions.

A **Cath Lab** is the operational hub of modern interventional cardiology and interventional radiology. The classification of Cath Labs (i.e., the "types") is determined by:
1. **Geometric configuration** of the X-ray gantry (single-plane vs. biplane).
2. **Clinical application domain** (cardiac vs. peripheral vs. neurological vs. electrophysiology).
3. **Detector generation** (Image Intensifier (II) tube vs. Flat-Panel Detector (FPD)).
4. **Integration level** with adjacent modalities (CT, MRI, OR, surgical robotics).
5. **Physical deployment** (fixed in-room vs. mobile/transportable).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of a Cath Lab as a **"high-precision X-ray garage"** for blood vessels.

- Imagine you are a **plumber** trying to inspect and unclog a complex network of pipes (the coronary arteries) hidden behind a thick concrete wall (the chest).
- You cannot see the pipes directly, so you insert a tiny fiber-optic camera (the **catheter**), and you shine a powerful flashlight (**X-ray beam**) through the wall to track where the camera is going.
- The **C-arm** is the robotic arm holding your flashlight and camera. It can swing around the patient in multiple directions to get the perfect viewing angle of the blocked pipe.
- The **detector** on the other side of the patient is like a "smart X-ray screen" that turns the invisible X-rays into a real-time moving video on the doctor's monitor.
- Different **types of Cath Labs** are simply specialized versions of this "X-ray garage" — some have one flashlight (single-plane), some have two flashlights (biplane), and some combine the garage with a full surgical operating theater (hybrid OR) in case the plumber needs an open-surgery backup.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Standard X-ray tube voltage for fluoroscopy: **60 – 80 kVp**
- Standard tube current for fluoroscopy: **1 – 5 mA** (low-dose pulse mode)
- Standard tube voltage for cine angiography: **70 – 90 kVp**
- Standard cine frame rate: **15 – 30 frames/second (fps)**
- Standard pulsed fluoroscopy rate: **3.75 / 7.5 / 15 / 30 pulses per second (pps)**
- Minimum floor shielding requirement: **2.5 mm lead equivalent**
- Typical C-arm rotation speeds: **0.5 – 25 degrees/second**
- Detector sizes: **20 cm, 23 cm, 30 cm, 40 cm** field-of-view (FOV)
- Standard source-to-image distance (SID): **90 – 120 cm**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Recall:** Cath Lab imaging is fundamentally a **real-time, dynamic, low-contrast, high-spatial-resolution** application — therefore, the X-ray chain is optimized for **temporal resolution** and **minimal motion blur**, NOT for absolute contrast detectability (unlike CT/MRI).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Architecture of Cath Labs

Cath Lab classification can be viewed as a multi-dimensional matrix. The four primary axes of classification are:

**Axis 1 — Gantry Geometry (X-ray Source Configuration):**
- **Single-Plane Cath Lab:** One C-arm with one X-ray tube and one detector. Used for routine coronary angiography, PTCA, single-vessel stenting.
- **Biplane Cath Lab:** Two synchronized C-arms (typically one frontal A-plane, one lateral B-plane) intersecting at the patient isocenter. Used for complex structural heart disease, congenital heart defects, neurovascular interventions, and 3D electroanatomical mapping.

**Axis 2 — Detector Technology:**
- **Image Intensifier (II) Cath Lab (Legacy):** Uses a vacuum-tube image intensifier coupled to a CCD camera. FOV sizes from 15 to 40 cm. Subject to "veiling glare," geometric distortion (pincushion/S-distortion), and magnetic field interference.
- **Flat-Panel Detector (FPD) Cath Lab (Modern):** Uses amorphous silicon (a-Si) or complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor (CMOS) flat panels. Provides distortion-free, higher dynamic range (~14-bit), and lower dose images. FOV sizes from 20 × 20 cm to 30 × 30 cm (or larger).

**Axis 3 — Clinical Application Specialty:**
- **Cardiac Cath Lab:** Dedicated to coronary and structural heart interventions. Equipped with hemodynamic recording, ECG gating, intravascular ultrasound (IVUS), and optical coherence tomography (OCT) integration.
- **Peripheral Vascular Cath Lab:** Used for peripheral artery disease (PAD), aortic aneurysm repair (EVAR/TEVAR), carotid stenting. Requires larger FOV, longer detector coverage, and often ceiling-mounted C-arms to allow table-side access.
- **Electrophysiology (EP) Lab:** Specialized for arrhythmia mapping and ablation. Requires 3D electroanatomical mapping systems (CARTO, EnSite), extensive RF/cryo-ablation generators, and minimal metal-artifact interference. Often a 2-axis C-arm with rotational angiography capability.
- **Pediatric Cath Lab:** Smaller FOV detectors, lower-dose protocols, dedicated pediatric anesthesia and biplane configuration for congenital heart disease (CHD) imaging.
- **Neuro Cath Lab:** High spatial resolution (sub-millimeter), biplane configuration, 3D rotational angiography, and DICOM-compatible integration with neuronavigation.

**Axis 4 — Integration / Deployment:**
- **Fixed (In-Suite) Cath Lab:** Permanent installation, floor- or ceiling-mounted C-arm, dedicated shielded room.
- **Mobile / Portable Cath Lab:** Compact C-arm systems used in operating rooms, ICUs, or as backup. Lower generator output (~3.5 kW vs. 100 kW).
- **Hybrid Cath Lab (Hybrid Operating Room):** Combines a full surgical operating theater (OR table, surgical lights, anesthesia, laminar airflow) with a fixed Cath Lab imaging system (often ceiling-mounted). Enables combined open-surgical and endovascular procedures (e.g., TAVR + surgical valve replacement).
- **CT-Fusion / MRI-Fusion Cath Lab:** Integrates pre-procedural or intra-procedural CT/MRI data with live fluoroscopy for advanced image guidance.
- **Robotic Cath Lab:** Uses robotic catheter manipulation (e.g., Corindus CorPath GRX, Hansen Magellan) for remote, precision-controlled PCI with reduced operator radiation exposure.

### 2.2 Operational Logic: How a Cath Lab Imaging Chain Works

- **Step 1 — Patient preparation:** Patient is positioned supine on a floating carbon-fiber table (radiolucent, low X-ray attenuation).
- **Step 2 — Arterial access:** Femoral or radial artery is punctured using the Seldinger technique; a guidewire and catheter are advanced to the target vessel.
- **Step 3 — X-ray exposure:** The X-ray generator (typically high-frequency inverter type, 80–100 kW) produces a collimated X-ray beam.
- **Step 4 — Image formation:** X-rays pass through the patient, attenuated differentially by iodine-based contrast media, and are captured by the FPD/II.
- **Step 5 — Image processing:** Real-time digital image processing (recursive filtering, edge enhancement, noise reduction, digital subtraction angiography) is applied.
- **Step 6 — Display & storage:** Images are displayed on in-room LCD monitors at the table side and archived in DICOM format.
- **Step 7 — Hemodynamic overlay:** Vital signs (ECG, blood pressure, SpO₂) are superimposed on the fluoroscopic image.

### 2.3 Why the "Why" Behind Each Configuration

> [!NOTE]
> **Why biplane for neuro and pediatric CHD?** Because obtaining orthogonal views in two projections from a single contrast injection **halves the contrast volume and radiation dose** — critical for children and cerebrovascular patients.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why flat-panel over image intensifier?** FPDs provide **distortion-free rectangular fields of view, better detective quantum efficiency (DQE), lower patient dose, and no veiling glare** — essential for quantitative coronary analysis (QCA) and 3D reconstruction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why hybrid OR for TAVR/structural heart?** Because transcatheter aortic valve replacement may require **immediate surgical bailout** (open-chest conversion) — having the OR ready eliminates patient transfer time during a life-threatening emergency.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Value | Units | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| X-ray photon fluence rate | $\Phi$ | $\Phi = \dfrac{N}{A \cdot t}$ | photons/cm²·s | Determines detector exposure rate |
| Attenuation (Beer-Lambert) | $I$ | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$ | — | Governs image contrast in angiography |
| Linear attenuation coefficient | $\mu$ | $\mu \approx \rho \cdot \sigma$ (mass atten.) | cm⁻¹ | Function of kVp, contrast agent (iodine K-edge: 33.2 keV) |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $SNR$ | $SNR = \dfrac{\bar{S}}{\sigma_N}$ | dimensionless | Directly impacts lesion detectability |
| Detective Quantum Efficiency | $DQE$ | $DQE = \dfrac{(SNR_{out})^2}{(SNR_{in})^2}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | FPDs (≈0.65) > II-CCD (≈0.25) |
| Patient skin dose (entrance) | $ESD$ | $ESD = X \cdot \left(\dfrac{SID}{SED}\right)^2$ | mGy | Limited to <2 Gy cumulative per procedure |
| Fluoroscopic dose rate | $\dot{D}$ | $\dot{D} = \dfrac{V \cdot I \cdot \eta}{SID^2}$ | mGy/min | Typical: 25–100 mGy/min |
| Pulse rate reduction factor | $R$ | $R = \dfrac{\text{continuous pps}}{\text{pulsed pps}}$ | dimensionless | 4 pps reduces dose by ~75% vs. 30 pps |
| Geometric magnification | $M$ | $M = \dfrac{SID}{SOD}$ | dimensionless | Larger SID = lower magnification |
| C-arm rotational speed | $\omega$ | $\omega = \dfrac{\theta_{total}}{t_{rot}}$ | °/s | Standard: 30–60 °/s for 3D angio |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Formula:** For DSA (Digital Subtraction Angiography), the subtracted image is:
$$I_{sub}(x,y) = I_{mask}(x,y) - I_{live}(x,y)$$
> where $I_{mask}$ is the pre-contrast mask image and $I_{live}$ is the post-contrast live image. Misregistration artifacts arise from patient motion between mask and live acquisitions.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Clinical utility:** Cath Labs are the cornerstone of modern cardiology — over **2 million PCIs** are performed annually worldwide, with Cath Lab imaging being the critical enabling technology.
- **Industry application:** Major manufacturers include **Siemens Healthineers (Artis series), Philips (Azurion), GE Healthcare (Innova), Canon Medical (Alphenix), and Shimadzu (Trinias)**.
- **AI integration:** Modern Cath Labs now embed AI-based real-time lesion detection, vessel segmentation, and radiation dose monitoring modules.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Entrance Skin Dose (ESD) in Fluoroscopy

The **Entrance Skin Dose (ESD)** is the cumulative X-ray dose delivered to the patient's skin entry point during a fluoroscopic procedure. This is a critical KTU concept for understanding patient safety.

**Given Quantities:**
- $X$ = X-ray output at the tube housing (mGy/mAs at 1 m)
- $I$ = Tube current (mA)
- $t$ = Total fluoroscopic time (s)
- $SID$ = Source-to-Image Distance (cm)
- $SED$ = Source-to-Entrance-skin Distance (cm)

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1:** The X-ray output at the tube housing (at 1 m reference) is given as $X$ (mGy per mAs). The actual output at the skin surface scales by the inverse-square law.

**Step 2:** The skin dose rate at the skin surface is:

$$
\dot{D}_{skin} = X \cdot I \cdot \left(\frac{100}{SED}\right)^2
$$

Here, the factor 100 cm represents the reference 1 m distance at which $X$ is calibrated, and the inverse-square law $\left(\dfrac{1}{d^2}\right)$ scales it to the actual skin entry distance.

**Step 3:** The cumulative skin dose is obtained by integrating (or, in practice, multiplying) the dose rate by the total fluoroscopic on-time $t$:

$$
ESD = \dot{D}_{skin} \cdot t = X \cdot I \cdot t \cdot \left(\frac{100}{SED}\right)^2
$$

**Step 4:** A more practical form separates $I \cdot t$ into the mAs product, recognizing that pulsed fluoroscopy uses pulse width $\tau$ (s/pulse) and frame rate $f$ (pps):

$$
ESD = X \cdot I \cdot \tau \cdot f \cdot T_{total} \cdot \left(\frac{100}{SED}\right)^2
$$

where $T_{total}$ is the total procedure duration in seconds.

**Step 5:** In modern units, the **kerma-area product (KAP)** or **dose-area product (DAP)** is also commonly reported:

$$
KAP = ESD \cdot A_{field}
$$

where $A_{field}$ is the irradiated field area in cm². KAP has units of Gy·cm² and serves as a more reliable estimator of stochastic risk than ESD alone.

**Result:** The fundamental KTU insight is that **lowering tube current, shortening pulse width, reducing frame rate, and increasing source-to-skin distance** are the four primary engineering levers for reducing patient dose in Cath Labs.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Detective Quantum Efficiency (DQE) Comparison

DQE measures how efficiently a detector converts input X-ray quantum information into output image information. It is the single most important detector quality metric for Cath Lab image chain performance.

**Step 1 — Define input SNR squared:**

$$
(SNR_{in})^2 = \bar{q}
$$

where $\bar{q}$ is the mean number of X-ray quanta incident on the detector per unit area (quantum fluence).

**Step 2 — Measure output SNR squared:**

$$
(SNR_{out})^2 = \frac{(\bar{S})^2}{\sigma_S^2}
$$

where $\bar{S}$ is the mean output signal and $\sigma_S^2$ is the output signal variance (noise power).

**Step 3 — DQE definition:**

$$
DQE(f) = \frac{(SNR_{out}(f))^2}{(SNR_{in}(f))^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Typical DQE values at zero spatial frequency $f = 0$:**

| Detector Type | DQE(0) | Dose Efficiency | Geometric Distortion |
|---|---|---|---|
| II + CCD (Legacy) | 0.20 – 0.30 | Low | Pincushion + S-distortion |
| a-Si FPD (Modern) | 0.55 – 0.70 | High | <1% (negligible) |
| CMOS FPD (Newest) | 0.65 – 0.80 | Highest | Negligible |

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation:**
A DQE of 0.65 means the detector preserves **65% of the input quantum information**, which translates directly to either **lower patient dose at equivalent image quality** OR **higher image quality at equivalent dose**.

**Conclusion:** This is precisely why modern Cath Lab installations have universally migrated from II-CCD to FPD technology — the DQE gain enables dose savings of **30–50%** for equivalent diagnostic image quality.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Cath Lab Types

| Cath Lab Type | Detector | C-Arm Geometry | Typical Procedure | FOV (cm) | Dose Level | Cost (INR, approx.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Single-Plane Cardiac | FPD 20×20 | 1-axis floor/ceiling | Diagnostic angio, PTCA, stenting | 20–25 | Moderate | 4 – 7 Cr |
| Biplane Neuro/Cardiac | FPD 20×20 (×2) | 2-axis | Pediatric CHD, stroke, AVM | 20–25 | High (2 beams) | 8 – 15 Cr |
| Hybrid OR (TAVR) | FPD 30×30 | Floor + ceiling rails | TAVR, EVAR, hybrid valve | 30–40 | High | 15 – 25 Cr |
| Mobile C-Arm (Cios, Ziehm) | FPD 20×20 | Mini C-arm | OR backup, pacemaker | 20–25 | Variable | 1 – 3 Cr |
| Electrophysiology Lab | FPD 20×20 | 1-axis + 3D mapping | RF ablation, cryoablation | 20 | Low | 6 – 10 Cr |
| Robotic Cath Lab | FPD 20×20 | Robotic arm | Robotic PCI | 20 | Very low (operator) | 12 – 20 Cr |
| Peripheral Vascular | FPD 30×30 or 40×40 | Ceiling mounted, long travel | EVAR, TEVAR, PAD | 30–40 | High | 8 – 14 Cr |
| CT-Fusion Cath Lab | FPD + in-room CT | Sliding CT gantry | Complex structural heart | 20 | Very high | 20 – 35 Cr |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Single-Plane Cath Lab

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Patient on radiolucent table] --> AC[Catheter arterial access site]
    AC --> GU[Guidewire and catheter]
    GU --> VS[Target vessel coronary or peripheral]
    XG[High frequency X-ray generator 80 to 100 kW] --> XT[X-ray tube assembly]
    XT --> CB[Collimator and beam shaping filter]
    CB --> P
    P --> D1[Flat panel detector FPD]
    D1 --> IP[Real time image processor DSA recursive filter]
    IP --> M1[In room LCD monitor table side]
    IP --> M2[Control room monitor]
    IP --> PACS[DICOM PACS archive]
    HEM[Hemodynamic recorder ECG IBP SpO2] --> OVL[Overlay and registration unit]
    OVL --> M1
    SH[Lead shielded walls 2.5 mm Pb] --- P
    DO[Door with lead glass viewport] --- SH
    CN[Operator control console] --> XG
    CN --> IP
    CN --> HEM
```

### 4.2 Classification Tree of Cath Labs

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CL[Cath Lab Types]

    CL --> SP[Gantry Geometry]
    SP --> SP1[Single Plane C arm]
    SP --> SP2[Biplane Two C arms]

    CL --> DT[Detector Technology]
    DT --> DT1[Image Intensifier legacy]
    DT --> DT2[Flat Panel Detector modern]

    CL --> CA[Clinical Application]
    CA --> CA1[Cardiac Coronary and structural]
    CA --> CA2[Peripheral Vascular]
    CA --> CA3[Electrophysiology]
    CA --> CA4[Pediatric Congenital Heart]
    CA --> CA5[Neurovascular]
    CA --> CA6[Hybrid OR Structural Heart]

    CL --> IN[Integration Level]
    IN --> IN1[Fixed In suite]
    IN --> IN2[Mobile Portable]
    IN --> IN3[Hybrid OR combined surgical]
    IN --> IN4[CT Fusion Cath Lab]
    IN --> IN5[MRI Fusion Cath Lab]
    IN --> IN6[Robotic Cath Lab]
```

### 4.3 Signal Chain Block Diagram (Fluoroscopic Imaging)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High voltage generator] --> B[X ray tube]
    B --> C[Patient with contrast]
    C --> D[Flat panel detector FPD]
    D --> E[Analog to digital converter]
    E --> F[Digital image processor]
    F --> G[Recursive temporal filter]
    G --> H[Edge enhancement kernel]
    H --> I[DICOM display monitor]
    F --> J[PACS archive]
    F --> K[Quantitative coronary analysis QCA software]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** Define a Cath Lab. List any four types of Cath Labs based on clinical application.

**Model Answer:**
A Cath Lab is a radiation-shielded, sterile interventional suite equipped with an X-ray imaging chain, hemodynamic monitoring systems, and a floating radiolucent table, used for minimally invasive fluoroscopically-guided vascular procedures.

Four types based on clinical application are:
1. **Cardiac Cath Lab** — for coronary angiography, angioplasty, stenting, and structural heart disease.
2. **Electrophysiology (EP) Lab** — for cardiac arrhythmia mapping and RF/cryoablation.
3. **Peripheral Vascular Cath Lab** — for peripheral artery disease, aortic aneurysm (EVAR/TEVAR).
4. **Neuro Cath Lab** — for cerebral angiography, stroke thrombectomy, and AVM embolization.

*(Marks: 1 for definition + 2 for listing with one-line explanation of each type)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** Differentiate between a single-plane and a biplane Cath Lab. Give one clinical situation where a biplane configuration is preferred.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Single-Plane | Biplane |
|---|---|---|
| Number of C-arms | 1 (A-plane) | 2 (A-plane + B-plane) |
| Imaging views per contrast injection | 1 projection | 2 orthogonal projections |
| Contrast volume required | Standard | ~50% lower |
| Radiation dose | Standard | 20–40% lower per procedure |
| Cost | Lower | ~1.5–2× higher |
| Clinical use | Routine PCI, single-vessel stenting | Pediatric CHD, neurovascular, complex structural heart |

**Preferred situation:** In **pediatric congenital heart disease imaging**, where the patient is small, contrast volume is restricted, and orthogonal views are needed from a single injection — the biplane configuration is preferred to minimize both contrast load and radiation dose.

*(Marks: 2 for differentiation table + 1 for clinical situation)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Solve the sub-part you are most confident in. Below are TWO alternative question sets (OR options).

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the block diagram of a modern Flat-Panel Detector (FPD) based Cath Lab imaging chain. Identify each block and describe its function.

**Model Solution:**

A modern FPD-based Cath Lab imaging chain consists of the following sequential blocks:

1. **High-Frequency X-ray Generator (80–100 kW):** Converts 3-phase mains supply to a high-frequency (~50–100 kHz) DC voltage to drive the X-ray tube. Provides stable, reproducible kVp and mA independent of line voltage fluctuations.
2. **X-ray Tube Assembly:** A rotating-anode, dual-focus tube (small + large focal spot) producing a collimated X-ray beam. Mounted on the C-arm.
3. **Collimator and Beam-Shaping Filter:** Restricts the X-ray field to the region of interest (reduces patient dose) and shapes the beam intensity profile (wedge filters compensate for varying body thickness).
4. **Patient with Iodinated Contrast:** The patient is positioned supine; iodine-based contrast media (K-edge = 33.2 keV) is injected to opacify vessels.
5. **Flat-Panel Detector (a-Si or CMOS):** Captures the attenuated X-ray beam. Contains a cesium-iodide (CsI) scintillator layer that converts X-rays to visible light, which is then detected by an a-Si photodiode array. Output is a distortion-free digital image.
6. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** Digitizes the analog detector signal at 14-bit depth.
7. **Real-Time Image Processor:** Applies recursive temporal filtering (frame averaging), spatial edge enhancement, and Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA) for background-free vessel imaging.
8. **Image Display (In-room LCD Monitors):** High-brightness, anti-glare medical-grade monitors mounted on ceiling-suspended booms.
9. **PACS / DICOM Archive:** Long-term storage of all fluoroscopic, cine, and DSA runs in DICOM 3.0 format.
10. **Hemodynamic Overlay System:** ECG, blood pressure, and SpO₂ waveforms superimposed on the fluoroscopic image for synchronized procedural guidance.

*(Each block: 0.5 mark for identification + function; 7 blocks × 1 mark = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the working of a Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA) system as used in Cath Labs. What is a "mask image" and why is misregistration a problem?

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle of DSA:**

DSA is a fluoroscopic technique used in Cath Labs to isolate opacified blood vessels by subtracting the pre-contrast "mask" image from the post-contrast "live" image.

**Step-by-Step Working:**

1. **Mask Image Acquisition ($I_{mask}$):** Before injecting contrast, a series of fluoroscopic frames of the patient in the region of interest (ROI) is acquired. The first or averaged frame is stored as the mask image.
2. **Contrast Injection:** Iodinated contrast medium is injected intra-arterially via the catheter, opacifying the vessels of interest.
3. **Live Image Acquisition ($I_{live}$):** A sequence of fluoroscopic images is acquired during and after contrast injection, at the same projection angle, frame rate, and patient position.
4. **Pixel-wise Subtraction:** For each live frame, the mask image is subtracted on a pixel-by-pixel basis:

$$
I_{sub}(x,y) = I_{live}(x,y) - I_{mask}(x,y)
$$

5. **Result:** Static structures (bones, soft tissue) cancel out, leaving only the contrast-filled vessels visible against a uniform grey background.
6. **Display:** The subtracted image sequence is displayed in real-time, with optional remapping and intensity scaling for optimal visualization.

**Definition of Mask Image:**
A mask image is a pre-contrast fluoroscopic image of the same anatomical region that serves as the reference frame for subtraction. It contains all the static background information (bones, soft tissues) that needs to be removed to isolate the contrast-filled vasculature.

**Problem of Misregistration:**
Misregistration is an artifact caused by **patient motion, respiratory movement, bowel peristalsis, or cardiac pulsation** between the mask and live acquisitions. This movement causes the static anatomy to shift position, so the subtraction does not perfectly cancel the background — resulting in **ghosting artifacts** (white or black halos around moving structures). Solutions include:
- Re-masking (acquiring a new mask during the procedure).
- Pixel shifting (rigid 2D registration algorithms).
- RSM (Respiratory Motion Compensation) algorithms.
- Use of biplane imaging for simultaneous orthogonal masks.

*(Step 1–2: 1.5 marks; Step 3–5: 2.5 marks; Mask definition: 1 mark; Misregistration problem: 2 marks = 7 marks)*

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify Cath Labs in detail based on (i) gantry geometry, (ii) clinical application, and (iii) integration level. Give one example procedure for each major type.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Classification by Gantry Geometry:**

| Type | Description | Example Procedure |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Plane** | One C-arm with one X-ray tube + one detector. The arm can rotate around the patient (LAO/RAO, CRA/CAU angulation). | Routine diagnostic coronary angiography |
| **Biplane** | Two synchronized C-arms (frontal A-plane + lateral B-plane) sharing the same isocenter. | Pediatric congenital heart disease imaging; intracranial AVM embolization |

**(ii) Classification by Clinical Application:**

| Type | Description | Example Procedure |
|---|---|---|
| **Cardiac Cath Lab** | Specialized for coronary and structural heart disease. | Percutaneous Coronary Intervention (PCI) with stenting |
| **Peripheral Vascular Cath Lab** | For non-cardiac vessels; larger FOV (30–40 cm) and ceiling-mounted gantry for table-side access. | Endovascular Aneurysm Repair (EVAR) for abdominal aortic aneurysm |
| **Electrophysiology (EP) Lab** | For arrhythmia mapping and ablation; integrates 3D electroanatomical mapping (CARTO, EnSite). | Atrial fibrillation RF ablation |
| **Pediatric Cath Lab** | Smaller FOV, lower-dose protocols, biplane configuration. | Device closure of atrial septal defect (ASD) in a child |
| **Neuro Cath Lab** | High spatial resolution, biplane, 3D rotational angiography. | Mechanical thrombectomy for acute ischemic stroke |

**(iii) Classification by Integration Level:**

| Type | Description | Example Procedure |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed In-Suite** | Permanent installation; floor- or ceiling-mounted. | Standard PCI |
| **Mobile Cath Lab** | Portable, compact C-arm; lower generator output. | Pacemaker lead placement in a remote OR |
| **Hybrid OR (Hybrid Cath Lab)** | Combines a full surgical operating room with a fixed Cath Lab imaging system. | Transcatheter Aortic Valve Replacement (TAVR) with surgical backup |
| **Robotic Cath Lab** | Robotic catheter manipulation with operator seated at a remote shielded console. | Robotic PCI (CorPath GRX) |
| **CT-Fusion Cath Lab** | Integrates in-room sliding CT gantry with fluoroscopy. | Complex structural heart disease (e.g., LAA closure) |

*(Each table 1.5 marks × 4 = 6 marks; concluding summary statement 1 mark = 7 marks)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare Image Intensifier (II) and Flat-Panel Detector (FPD) Cath Lab systems. Explain why modern Cath Labs prefer FPD over II.

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Image Intensifier (II) | Flat-Panel Detector (FPD) |
|---|---|---|
| Operating principle | Vacuum tube with CsI input phosphor + photocathode + electron optics + output phosphor + CCD camera | Direct (a-Se) or indirect (CsI + a-Si photodiode array) digital conversion |
| Field of View (FOV) | Round, 15–40 cm (mechanically adjustable) | Square/rectangular, 20×20 to 30×30 cm (electronic zoom) |
| Geometric distortion | Pincushion + S-distortion (up to 10% at edges) | <1% (negligible) |
| Veiling glare | Significant (5–15%) | Negligible (<1%) |
| Dynamic range | ~8–10 bit | 14 bit |
| DQE(0) | 0.20 – 0.30 | 0.55 – 0.70 |
| Patient dose (for equivalent IQ) | Higher | 30–50% lower |
| Image uniformity | Non-uniform (vignetting) | Highly uniform |
| Susceptibility to magnetic fields | Yes (degrades in hybrid OR with MRI) | No |
| 3D reconstruction capability | Poor | Excellent (used for 3D angio and cone-beam CT) |
| Lifespan | ~5–7 years | ~10+ years |
| Cost (per detector) | Lower | Higher |

**Why modern Cath Labs prefer FPD over II:**

1. **Superior image quality:** FPDs provide distortion-free, uniform images with no veiling glare, enabling accurate vessel measurement and quantitative coronary analysis (QCA).
2. **Lower patient dose:** Higher DQE means 30–50% less radiation dose for equivalent diagnostic image quality, which is critical for long procedures (e.g., CTO interventions).
3. **Better dynamic range:** 14-bit FPDs can simultaneously image dense bone and contrast-filled vessels in the same frame.
4. **3D capability:** FPDs enable cone-beam CT (3D rotational angiography) for advanced interventions like TAVR planning and LAA closure.
5. **MR compatibility:** FPDs are unaffected by magnetic fields, allowing future integration into MRI-hybrid Cath Labs.
6. **Lower total cost of ownership:** Although upfront cost is higher, the longer lifespan, lower service costs, and dose savings offset the initial investment within 3–5 years.

*(Table: 3 marks; 6 reasons × 0.5 mark + 1 concluding remark = 4 marks; Total 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** "C-arm" with "O-arm" — the C-arm is a semi-circular gantry used in Cath Labs, while the O-arm is a circular gantry used in intraoperative 3D imaging during spine surgery.
> 2. **Do NOT state** that biplane Cath Labs use *double* the radiation dose — they actually *reduce* cumulative dose by halving the number of contrast injections and providing orthogonal views in one acquisition.
> 3. **Do NOT confuse** "flat-panel detector" with "flat-panel TV" — FPDs in Cath Labs are X-ray sensors, not display panels.
> 4. **Do NOT omit** units (Gy, mGy, cm, pps) in numerical answers — KTU evaluators deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 5. **Always specify** the K-edge energy of iodine (33.2 keV) when explaining contrast-enhanced angiography — this is a frequently-tested KTU fact.
> 6. **Avoid vague phrases** like "image is good" — use quantitative descriptors like DQE, SNR, MTF, contrast-to-noise ratio (CNR).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Cath Lab** is a fluoroscopy-guided interventional suite; classification is based on **gantry geometry, detector type, clinical application, and integration level**.
- **Single-plane** systems use one C-arm; **biplane** systems use two synchronized C-arms (frontal + lateral).
- **Image Intensifier (II)** detectors are legacy technology with geometric distortion and lower DQE; **Flat-Panel Detectors (FPD)** are modern, distortion-free, with DQE 0.55–0.70.
- **Biplane Cath Labs** reduce contrast volume by ~50% and cumulative dose by 20–40% — preferred in **pediatric CHD and neurovascular** imaging.
- **Hybrid OR Cath Labs** combine a surgical operating room with fixed fluoroscopy — used for **TAVR, EVAR, and structural heart interventions**.
- **Electrophysiology (EP) Labs** integrate 3D electroanatomical mapping (CARTO, EnSite) for **arrhythmia ablation**.
- **Robotic Cath Labs** (CorPath GRX) reduce operator radiation exposure by >90% and improve catheter precision.
- The **fundamental DSA equation** is $I_{sub}(x,y) = I_{live}(x,y) - I_{mask}(x,y)$; **misregistration** is the key artifact due to patient motion.
- **Standard entrance skin dose (ESD)** in a routine PCI is **0.5 – 2 Gy**; the regulatory maximum is **2 Gy per procedure**.
- **Pulsed fluoroscopy** at 4 pps reduces dose by ~75% compared to 30 pps continuous fluoroscopy.
- **K-edge of iodine** is **33.2 keV** — this is why iodinated contrast is used for vascular opacification in Cath Labs.
- **Source-to-Image Distance (SID)** is typically **90 – 120 cm**; **Source-to-Skin Distance (SED)** should be maximized for dose reduction.
- Major Cath Lab manufacturers: **Siemens (Artis), Philips (Azurion), GE (Innova), Canon (Alphenix), Shimadzu (Trinias)**.
- **Lead shielding** requirement: minimum **2.5 mm lead equivalent** for walls, doors, and viewing windows of a Cath Lab.
- The **table is radiolucent carbon-fiber** to minimize X-ray attenuation and enable unrestricted imaging angles.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CT Angio vs Cath Lab Angio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT411 - PRINCIPLES OF MEDICAL IMAGING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** CT Angio vs Cath Lab Angio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**CT Angiography (CTA)** is a *minimally invasive* vascular imaging technique that combines a high-speed helical (spiral) Computed Tomography scanner with a timed intravenous (IV) bolus of iodinated contrast media to produce high-resolution, three-dimensional reconstructions of arterial and venous anatomy. Image acquisition is based on differential **X-ray attenuation** (in Hounsfield Units, HU) between the contrast-filled vessel lumen and surrounding soft tissue, gated typically to a peak arterial enhancement phase (≈ **250–400 HU** within the aorta).

**Cath Lab Angiography** — more formally known as **Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA)** or *Conventional Catheter Angiography* — is the *gold-standard invasive* vascular imaging modality in which a catheter is navigated (under fluoroscopic roadmap guidance) into a peripheral artery, advanced selectively into the target vessel, and an intra-arterial (IA) injection of iodinated contrast is performed. A pre-contrast *mask image* is digitally subtracted from the contrast-filled image to suppress overlying bone and soft tissue, yielding a clean 2D projection of the vessel lumen.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology — KTU 2024 Syllabus**
> - **CTA** → non-invasive / minimally invasive, *intravenous* contrast, cross-sectional / volumetric data.
> - **DSA (Cath Lab Angio)** → *invasive*, *intra-arterial* catheter, projection (2D summation) data.
> - **Gold Standard** for vascular disease is still DSA, but CTA (and MRA) are first-line diagnostic tools.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The City Tunnel Inspection"**
>
> Imagine you are tasked with inspecting an underground tunnel network of a city to find a blockage or leak.
>
> 🔹 **CT Angiography** is like a **drone-based 3D laser scan of the entire city from above**. You fly the drone (X-ray source + detector) in a helical path around the patient, the drone releases a smoke tracer (IV contrast) into a single city-entry manhole (the antecubital vein), and you get a *complete 3D map* of every tunnel within seconds. You can rotate, slice, and view from any angle — but you only see the *outside* of the tunnels.
>
> 🔹 **Cath Lab Angiography (DSA)** is like a **plumber physically crawling inside the tunnels** with a flashlight and dye. The plumber (catheter) is *inserted directly into the tunnel system* (artery) via a city-entry point (femoral/radial puncture), walks to the exact blockage site, sprays dye, and you watch the dye flow on a single 2D X-ray "movie". The plumber can *also fix the leak on the spot* (perform angioplasty / stenting) — but he can only see the tunnel *he is currently inside* in real time.
>
> **Bottom line:** CTA = *global 3D snapshot from outside*; DSA = *selective 2D live inside view with therapeutic capability*.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Reference Metrics

| Parameter | CTA (Typical) | Cath Lab DSA (Typical) |
|---|---|---|
| Spatial resolution | **0.4 – 0.6 mm** (isotropic voxel) | **0.2 – 0.3 mm** (2D projection) |
| Temporal resolution | **50 – 200 ms** (per rotation) | **1 – 8 ms** (real-time fluoro) |
| Field of View (FOV) | **25 – 50 cm** (3D volume) | **15 – 30 cm** (2D projection) |
| Contrast volume | **60 – 120 mL** (IV, 3–5 mL/s) | **5 – 15 mL per injection** (IA) |
| Contrast concentration | **350 – 370 mg I/mL** | **200 – 300 mg I/mL** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values for KTU Board Exam:**
> - **Pitch factor** in helical CT: typical CTA pitch = **0.9 – 1.2**
> - **Bolus tracking threshold:** trigger at **+100 to +150 HU** rise in ROI
> - **DSA frame rate:** typical **2 – 6 frames/second** (pulsed fluoro)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — CT Angiography (CTA)

### Step-by-step workflow:

1. **Patient preparation & IV access** — 18–20 G cannula placed in the antecubital vein. Test bolus or bolus-tracking ROI placed in the target artery (e.g., descending aorta, pulmonary trunk, carina).
2. **Non-contrast localizer (scout/topogram)** — AP and lateral low-dose projection to define scan range.
3. **Contrast injection** — Power injector delivers **60–120 mL of non-ionic iodinated contrast (350–370 mg I/mL)** at **3–5 mL/s**, followed by a **30–40 mL saline chaser** (to push bolus and reduce streak artifact from SVC).
4. **Bolus tracking / SmartPrep** — Real-time low-dose axial acquisitions at the monitoring level; when ROI HU rises by **+100 to +150 HU** above baseline, diagnostic helical acquisition auto-triggers.
5. **Helical (spiral) acquisition** — X-ray tube rotates continuously (rotation time **0.27 – 0.35 s** for 64/128/256-slice CT) while the table translates at a constant speed.
6. **Reconstruction** — Axial slices are reconstructed at **0.5 – 1.0 mm** thickness with **0.4 – 0.6 mm** isotropic voxels. Multiplanar Reformation (MPR), Maximum Intensity Projection (MIP), and Volume Rendering (VR) are generated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind helical CTA:** Continuous rotation + table motion + slip-ring technology → *volumetric data acquired in a single breath-hold* (typically 4–10 s) → eliminates respiratory misregistration and enables true 3D post-processing.

## 2.2 Operational Principle — Cath Lab Angiography (DSA)

### Step-by-step workflow:

1. **Vascular access** — Under sterile technique and local anesthesia, the **common femoral artery** (or radial / brachial) is punctured using the **Seldinger technique** (needle → guidewire → sheath).
2. **Catheter navigation** — A diagnostic catheter (e.g., **5 Fr pigtail, Simmons, Cobra, Judkins**) is advanced over a guidewire under fluoroscopic roadmap guidance to the target vessel ostium.
3. **Mask image acquisition** — **2 – 5 frames** of the region of interest are acquired *before* contrast injection (with breath-hold instruction) and stored as the **mask**.
4. **Contrast injection** — Power injector delivers iodinated contrast **IA at 3 – 10 mL/s** (typical total **5 – 15 mL per selective run**).
5. **Logarithmic subtraction** — In real time, the system computes:

   $$\text{Display Image} = k \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\text{Mask Image}}{\text{Live Image}}\right)$$

   This digital subtraction *cancels* stationary bone / soft tissue, leaving only the contrast-filled vessel visible.
6. **Cine / run recording** — Acquired at **2 – 6 fps** (or up to 15–30 fps for cardiac) in pulsed-fluoro low-dose mode.
7. **Optional intervention** — If a lesion is found (stenosis, aneurysm, AVM), the same session proceeds to **balloon angioplasty, stenting, coiling, or thrombolysis** — the *therapeutic* advantage of DSA.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $CNR = \dfrac{\vert S_{vessel} - S_{tissue} \vert}{\sigma_{noise}}$ | **Contrast-to-Noise Ratio** — image quality metric. HU units in numerator. | dimensionless |
| 2 | $SNR = \dfrac{S_{ROI}}{\sigma_{noise}}$ | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** — governs image noise perception. | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{Pitch} = \dfrac{\text{Table Feed per rotation}}{N \times T}$ | **Helical CT pitch** (N = detector rows, T = collimated slice thickness). Typical CTA pitch = **0.9 – 1.2**. | dimensionless |
| 4 | $CTDI_{vol} = \dfrac{CTDI_w}{\text{Pitch}}$ | **Volume CT Dose Index** — absorbed dose per slice. | mGy |
| 5 | $DLP = CTDI_{vol} \times L$ | **Dose-Length Product** — L = scan length. | mGy·cm |
| 6 | $E_{effective} = k \cdot DLP$ | **Effective dose** (k = tissue-weighting factor; for chest $k \approx 0.014$). | mSv |
| 7 | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$ | **Beer–Lambert attenuation law** — basis of all X-ray imaging. | dimensionless |
| 8 | $S_{vessel} \propto C_I \cdot \dot{Q} \cdot t_{transit}$ | **Vessel enhancement** depends on iodine concentration, flow rate, and transit time. | HU |
| 9 | $t_{delay} = t_{scan} - t_{peak} + t_{inj}$ | **Bolus timing** — delay between injection start and scan trigger. | s |
| 10 | $DAP = \sum (DAP_i \cdot A_i)$ | **Dose-Area Product** (DSA) — cumulative fluoroscopic + cine dose. | Gy·cm² |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility of these formulas:**
> - Equations 4–6 are used to **compare patient dose** between CTA and DSA for the same clinical task (renal CTA, pulmonary CTA, coronary CTA).
> - Equation 3 governs **scan coverage vs dose trade-off** in helical CTA.
> - Equation 7 is the **physical foundation** of all transmission-based imaging (X-ray, CT, DSA).
> - Equation 10 (DAP) is the **gold-standard dose metric** for interventional fluoroscopy procedures.

## 2.4 Real-World Clinical Utility

| Domain | CTA | Cath Lab DSA |
|---|---|---|
| **Aneurysm screening** | First-line (fast, 3D) | Reserved if intervention planned |
| **Pulmonary embolism** | **CT Pulmonary Angiogram (CTPA)** is the gold standard | Rarely used |
| **Acute stroke / LVO** | Rapid triage, *"code stroke"* CTA | Mechanical thrombectomy (DSA) |
| **Coronary artery disease** | **CCTA** (coronary CTA) with HRCT | Diagnostic + **PCI / stenting** |
| **Peripheral arterial disease** | Runoff CTA | **Endovascular repair (EVAR)** |
| **Trauma (vascular injury)** | Whole-body CTA in < 60 s | Therapeutic embolization |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Logarithmic Subtraction in DSA

The defining mathematical operation of Cath Lab Angiography. We begin with the **Beer–Lambert law** for X-ray transmission through tissue:

$$
I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}
$$

where $I_0$ is the incident X-ray intensity, $I$ is the transmitted intensity, $\mu$ is the linear attenuation coefficient (cm⁻¹), and $x$ is the path length (cm).

**Step 1 — Mask image (pre-contrast):**

The X-ray beam traverses only *stationary* anatomy (bone + soft tissue) before contrast injection. The detector records:

$$
I_{mask} = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu_{tissue} \cdot x_{tissue}}
$$

**Step 2 — Live image (post-contrast):**

Now the same beam additionally traverses the iodine-filled vessel:

$$
I_{live} = I_0 \cdot e^{-(\mu_{tissue} x_{tissue} + \mu_{iodine} x_{vessel})}
$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio:**

$$
\frac{I_{mask}}{I_{live}} = \frac{I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu_{tissue} x_{tissue}}}{I_0 \cdot e^{-(\mu_{tissue} x_{tissue} + \mu_{iodine} x_{vessel})}} = e^{+\mu_{iodine} x_{vessel}}
$$

**Step 4 — Take natural logarithm (or log₁₀, scaled by k):**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{I_{mask}}{I_{live}}\right) = \mu_{iodine} \cdot x_{vessel}
$$

**Step 5 — Display scaling:**

$$
\text{Subtracted Image} = k \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_{mask}}{I_{live}}\right) = \frac{k}{\ln 10} \cdot \mu_{iodine} \cdot x_{vessel}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** The resulting pixel value is *directly proportional* to the *integrated iodine path length* $\mu_{iodine} \cdot x_{vessel}$ — i.e., proportional to vessel thickness and iodine concentration. Stationary tissue is mathematically *eliminated* — this is why DSA is exquisitely sensitive to small vessels.

## 3.2 Derivation: Helical Pitch and Effective Slice Thickness

In CTA, the patient table translates continuously during gantry rotation. Define:

- $T$ = collimated detector row thickness (mm)
- $N$ = number of active detector rows
- $S$ = table feed per gantry rotation (mm/rotation)

**Step 1 — Pitch definition (KTU standard form):**

$$
\text{Pitch} = \frac{S}{N \cdot T}
$$

**Step 2 — Case analysis:**

- **Pitch = 1.0** → contiguous (no gap, no overlap). Standard for most CTA.
- **Pitch < 1.0** → overlapping slices, higher dose, better 3D reconstruction (used in pediatric / small FOV).
- **Pitch > 1.0** → gaps, lower dose, used in extended coverage (e.g., aorta-to-ankle runoff).

**Step 3 — Example computation (typical 64-slice chest CTA):**

Given: $N = 64$, $T = 0.625$ mm, $S = 39.4$ mm/rotation:

$$
\text{Pitch} = \frac{39.4}{64 \times 0.625} = \frac{39.4}{40.0} = 0.985 \approx 1.0
$$

**Step 4 — Patient dose relation:**

$$
CTDI_{vol} = \frac{CTDI_{w}}{\text{Pitch}}
$$

For a fixed $CTDI_w$, *increasing pitch decreases dose* — this is the **CT dose-reduction lever** in helical CTA, but at the cost of slightly broadened slice sensitivity profiles (effective slice thickness increases).

## 3.3 Comprehensive Comparison Matrix (Analytical)

| Comparison Axis | CT Angiography (CTA) | Cath Lab DSA | Winner / KTU Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Invasiveness** | Minimally invasive (IV only) | Invasive (arterial puncture + catheter) | CTA (safer outpatient) |
| **Contrast route** | Intravenous (peripheral) | Intra-arterial (selective) | DSA (better vessel opacification per mL) |
| **Contrast volume per study** | 60 – 120 mL | 5 – 15 mL per run (multiple runs possible) | DSA (less total iodine) |
| **CIN risk** (Contrast-Induced Nephropathy) | Higher (large IV bolus) | Lower (small IA dose) | DSA in CKD patients |
| **2D vs 3D** | 3D volumetric (isotropic) | 2D projection summation | CTA (avoids vessel overlap) |
| **Spatial resolution** | 0.4 – 0.6 mm (voxel) | 0.2 – 0.3 mm (projection) | DSA (higher in-plane) |
| **Temporal resolution** | 50 – 200 ms (one rotation) | 1 – 8 ms (real-time fluoro) | DSA (dynamic flow imaging) |
| **Dynamic flow info** | Limited (single arterial phase) | Excellent (real-time cine) | DSA |
| **Calcified plaque / blooming** | Suffers (blooming artifact) | Less affected | DSA |
| **Patient dose (Effective)** | 5 – 20 mSv (chest CTA) | 3 – 15 mSv (diagnostic run) | Comparable; therapeutic DSA much higher |
| **Dose metric used** | CTDIvol, DLP | DAP, Reference Point Air Kerma | KTU expects both |
| **Stent / metal artifact** | Significant (beam hardening) | Minimal | DSA |
| **Therapeutic capability** | None (diagnostic only) | **Yes** — angioplasty, stent, coil, embolization | DSA (unique strength) |
| **Bedside / portable** | No (requires CT room) | Yes (mobile C-arm possible) | DSA |
| **Operator skill required** | Radiographer + radiologist | Interventionalist (cardiologist / radiologist / neuro-IR) | CTA |
| **Cost** | Moderate (1 CT slot) | High (cath lab OR, staff, devices) | CTA |
| **Complication rate (major)** | < 0.5% (extravasation, allergy) | 1 – 3% (access-site bleed, dissection, stroke) | CTA |
| **Throughput** | High (5 – 10 min/study) | Lower (30 – 90 min/case) | CTA |
| **Indication — trauma** | Whole-body CTA in < 60 s | Selective embolization only | CTA for diagnosis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Take-Away Rule:**
> - **Diagnostic question → choose CTA** (faster, 3D, non-invasive).
> - **Therapeutic intent (dilate, stent, embolize, retrieve clot) → choose DSA**.
> - **CTPA is the gold standard for PE** — must be remembered.

## 3.4 Derivation: Effective Dose Comparison

For an **adult chest CTA** (standard protocol):
- $CTDI_{vol} = 12$ mGy
- Scan length $L = 30$ cm
- Tissue-weighting factor for chest $k = 0.014$ mSv/(mGy·cm)

**Step 1 — Compute DLP:**

$$
DLP = CTDI_{vol} \times L = 12 \times 30 = 360 \text{ mGy·cm}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Effective Dose:**

$$
E_{CTA} = k \cdot DLP = 0.014 \times 360 = 5.04 \text{ mSv}
$$

For a **diagnostic coronary DSA** (e.g., 4 cine runs × 5 s, 15 fps, pulsed 4 mGy/frame at 25 cm FOV):
- Total DAP ≈ **40 Gy·cm²**
- Conversion factor for cardiac fluoro $k_{cardiac} \approx 0.10$ mSv/(Gy·cm²)

$$
E_{DSA} = k_{cardiac} \cdot DAP = 0.10 \times 40 = 4.0 \text{ mSv}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Diagnostic doses are *roughly comparable*; however, an *interventional* DSA (PCI with stenting) easily exceeds **20 – 50 mSv** due to prolonged fluoro time. This is a frequent KTU numerical question.

## 3.5 Selection Algorithm — Which Modality to Choose?

```
STEP 1: Is intervention planned (angioplasty, stent, coil, thrombectomy)?
        ├── YES → Cath Lab DSA (therapeutic + diagnostic)
        └── NO  → Go to Step 2

STEP 2: Is the patient hemodynamically unstable or in a "CODE STROKE/PE"?
        ├── YES → CTA (fastest, whole-body coverage)
        └── NO  → Go to Step 3

STEP 3: Is there severe renal impairment (eGFR < 30)?
        ├── YES → Non-contrast MRA / DSA (low-volume IA contrast)
        └── NO  → CTA is appropriate first-line

STEP 4: Are dense calcifications or surgical clips expected (e.g., peripheral)?
        ├── YES → DSA (less blooming, better luminal evaluation)
        └── NO  → CTA preferred (3D, outpatient)
```
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CTA Workflow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Preparation and IV Cannula 18G] --> B[Non-Contrast Scout Topogram AP and Lateral]
    B --> C[ROI Placement in Target Artery]
    C --> D[Power Injector IV Contrast 3 to 5 mL per s]
    D --> E[Bolus Tracking Monitoring Axial]
    E --> F{ROI HU exceeds 100 to 150 HU threshold}
    F -- No --> E
    F -- Yes --> G[Trigger Helical Spiral Acquisition]
    G --> H[X-ray Tube Continuous Rotation 0.3 s]
    H --> I[Slip Ring Technology Continuous Data]
    I --> J[Raw Projection Data Sinograms]
    J --> K[Filtered Backprojection or Iterative Reconstruction]
    K --> L[Axial Slice Reconstruction 0.5 to 1 mm]
    L --> M[Post-Processing 3D Workstation]
    M --> N1[MPR Multiplanar Reformation]
    M --> N2[MIP Maximum Intensity Projection]
    M --> N3[VR Volume Rendering]
    N1 --> O[Diagnostic Interpretation by Radiologist]
    N2 --> O
    N3 --> O
    O --> P[PACS Archive and Report]
```

## 4.2 Cath Lab DSA Workflow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Patient on Angio Table and Sterile Prep] --> B1[Local Anesthesia Lignocaine 2 percent]
    B1 --> C1[Arterial Puncture Femoral or Radial]
    C1 --> D1[Seldinger Technique Wire and Sheath 5 to 7 Fr]
    D1 --> E1[Guidewire and Catheter Navigation under Fluoro]
    E1 --> F1[Roadmap Imaging at Target Vessel Ostium]
    F1 --> G1[Acquire Mask Frames 2 to 5 frames pre-contrast]
    G1 --> H1[Power Injector IA Contrast 3 to 10 mL per s]
    H1 --> I1[Real-time Logarithmic Subtraction Mask minus Live]
    I1 --> J1[DSA Cine Run 2 to 6 fps pulsed fluoro]
    J1 --> K1{Lesion Detected}
    K1 -- No --> L1[Withdraw Catheter and Hemostasis]
    K1 -- Yes --> M1[Therapeutic Intervention Angioplasty Stent Coil Embolization]
    M1 --> N1[Final Control DSA Run]
    N1 --> L1
    L1 --> O1[Recovery and Post-Procedure Monitoring]
```

## 4.3 Side-by-Side Comparative Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CTANGIO [CT ANGIOGRAPHY CTA]
        direction TB
        CTA1[IV Contrast Peripheral Vein] --> CTA2[Helical CT Volume Acquisition 4 to 10 s]
        CTA2 --> CTA3[3D Volumetric Dataset Isotropic Voxels]
        CTA3 --> CTA4[Diagnostic Read Only No Therapy]
    end
    subgraph CATHLAB [CATH LAB DSA]
        direction TB
        DSA1[IA Contrast via Selective Catheter] --> DSA2[2D Projection Fluoro Cine]
        DSA2 --> DSA3[Logarithmic Subtracted Image Sequence]
        DSA3 --> DSA4{Decision Point}
        DSA4 -- Diagnostic Done --> DSA5[Withdraw and Close]
        DSA4 -- Therapeutic --> DSA6[Angioplasty Stent Coil EVAR Thrombectomy]
    end
    CTANGIO -- Patient referred if intervention needed --> CATHLAB
    CATHLAB -- Pre-procedural planning roadmap --> CTANGIO
```

## 4.4 Modality Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Suspected Vascular Pathology] --> S1{Intervention Planned}
    S1 -- YES --> DSA1[Cath Lab DSA Diagnostic plus Therapeutic]
    S1 -- NO --> S2{Emergent Setting Trauma Stroke PE}
    S2 -- YES --> CTA1[CTA Whole-body or Targeted Fastest]
    S2 -- NO --> S3{Renal Function eGFR}
    S3 -- Less than 30 --> ALT1[Non-contrast MRA or Low-volume DSA]
    S3 -- 30 to 60 --> CTA2[CTA with Hydration Protocol]
    S3 -- Greater than 60 --> CTA3[Standard CTA First-line]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]**

**"Define Digital Subtraction Angiography (DSA). Explain the principle of logarithmic subtraction in 2–3 lines."**

**Model Answer:**

DSA is an invasive fluoroscopic technique in which a pre-contrast *mask image* is digitally subtracted from contrast-filled live images to highlight blood vessels. The mathematical operation is:

$$
\text{Display} = k \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_{mask}}{I_{live}}\right)
$$

This eliminates stationary bone/soft-tissue attenuation, leaving only the iodine-filled vessel visible with pixel value ∝ iodine path length $\mu_I \cdot x_{vessel}$. *(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 formula + 1 physical interpretation.)*

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**"List any FOUR advantages of CT Angiography over Cath Lab DSA as a diagnostic vascular imaging modality."**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Non-invasive / minimally invasive** — only IV access; no arterial puncture → lower complication rate (< 0.5% vs 1–3%).
2. **3D volumetric dataset** — isotropic voxels allow MPR, MIP, VR post-processing; eliminates vessel overlap.
3. **Fast acquisition** — single breath-hold (4–10 s); ideal for trauma, stroke, and PE workup.
4. **Lower cost & higher throughput** — no cath lab OR, no interventionalist; suitable for outpatient screening. *(3 marks: 4 × 0.75 = 3.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **omit units** in CTDI, DLP, DAP, and Effective Dose formulas — *always carry mGy, mGy·cm, Gy·cm², mSv* to the final answer.
> - In DSA derivation questions, students *forget the log base 10 vs natural log* — KTU expects **log₁₀** form for image display.
> - In comparison questions, students write "CTA is better" generically — KTU awards marks only for **specific axis-by-axis justification** (invasiveness, 2D vs 3D, dose metric, therapeutic capability).
> - When asked to "choose a modality," always state **assumptions** (renal function, stability, intervention intent) — otherwise the answer is incomplete.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(Attempt any ONE from this and Question B)*

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]**

**"With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of CT Angiography. State the role of bolus tracking."**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle of CTA (Block diagram — see Section 4.1 above for the complete flow):**

1. **Patient preparation:** A **18–20 G IV cannula** is placed in the antecubital vein.
2. **Non-contrast scout (topogram):** Low-dose AP and lateral projections define scan range and ROI placement.
3. **IV contrast injection:** Power injector delivers **60–120 mL** of non-ionic iodinated contrast (**350–370 mg I/mL**) at **3–5 mL/s** followed by a 30–40 mL saline chaser.
4. **Bolus tracking:** A small ROI is placed in the target artery (e.g., descending aorta). Real-time low-dose axial monitoring is performed; when the ROI HU rises by **+100 to +150 HU** above baseline, the diagnostic helical acquisition is auto-triggered.
5. **Helical acquisition:** The X-ray tube rotates continuously (rotation time **0.27–0.35 s**) while the table translates at a constant speed (pitch **0.9–1.2**). Slip-ring technology allows continuous data acquisition.
6. **Reconstruction:** Raw sinograms are reconstructed into **0.5–1.0 mm axial slices** with isotropic voxels (**0.4–0.6 mm**) using filtered back-projection or iterative reconstruction.
7. **3D post-processing:** MPR, MIP, and Volume-Rendered images are generated on a dedicated workstation.

**Role of Bolus Tracking (additional credit):**
- Synchronizes the **peak arterial enhancement** (250–400 HU) with the **center of the scan volume** → uniform vessel opacification.
- Eliminates guesswork of fixed delay timing → adapts to individual cardiac output, IV patency, and breath-hold variability.
- Reduces contrast waste and repeat scans. *(7 marks: 4 for workflow + 2 for bolus tracking role + 1 for diagram.)*

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**"A 64-slice helical chest CT angiogram is performed with detector row thickness T = 0.625 mm, table feed S = 39.4 mm/rotation, and N = 64 active rows. Calculate (i) pitch factor, (ii) effective dose if CTDIvol = 12 mGy and scan length L = 30 cm. Use k = 0.014 mSv/(mGy·cm)."**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Pitch factor:**

$$
\text{Pitch} = \frac{S}{N \cdot T} = \frac{39.4}{64 \times 0.625} = \frac{39.4}{40.0} = 0.985 \approx 0.99
$$

(Within the standard CTA range of 0.9–1.2.) *(2 marks)*

**(ii) Effective dose:**

**Step 1 — Compute DLP:**

$$
DLP = CTDI_{vol} \times L = 12 \text{ mGy} \times 30 \text{ cm} = 360 \text{ mGy·cm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply tissue-weighting factor:**

$$
E = k \cdot DLP = 0.014 \frac{\text{mSv}}{\text{mGy·cm}} \times 360 \text{ mGy·cm}
$$

$$
E = 5.04 \text{ mSv}
$$

**Conclusion:** The effective dose of this chest CTA is approximately **5 mSv**, which is roughly equivalent to **2 years of natural background radiation** (~ 2.4 mSv/year). *(5 marks: 2 DLP + 2 E + 1 conclusion + units carried throughout.)*

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Alternative — Internal Choice)*

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**

**"Describe the working of Digital Subtraction Angiography with a block diagram. Explain the logarithmic subtraction step mathematically."**

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram (see Section 4.2 above).**

**Working of DSA:**

1. **Vascular access:** Under sterile technique and local anesthesia, the common femoral (or radial) artery is punctured (Seldinger technique) and a 5–7 Fr sheath is placed.
2. **Catheter navigation:** A diagnostic catheter (pigtail, Simmons, Judkins, Cobra) is advanced over a guidewire under fluoroscopic roadmap guidance to the ostium of the target vessel.
3. **Mask image acquisition:** With breath-hold, **2–5 frames** of the region of interest are acquired *before* contrast injection and stored as the **mask**.
4. **IA contrast injection:** Power injector delivers iodinated contrast **IA at 3–10 mL/s** (typical **5–15 mL per selective run**).
5. **Logarithmic subtraction (real-time):** The system computes pixel-wise:

$$
\text{Display} = k \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_{mask}}{I_{live}}\right)
$$

Stationary tissue is mathematically eliminated; remaining image ∝ $\mu_{iodine} \cdot x_{vessel}$.

6. **Cine / run recording:** Acquired at **2–6 fps** in pulsed-fluoro low-dose mode.
7. **Optional intervention:** Balloon angioplasty, stenting, coiling, or thrombolysis in the same session. *(5 marks for workflow + 2 marks for diagram.)*

**Mathematical derivation of subtraction (additional credit):**

- Mask: $I_{mask} = I_0 e^{-\mu_t x_t}$
- Live: $I_{live} = I_0 e^{-(\mu_t x_t + \mu_I x_v)}$
- Ratio: $I_{mask}/I_{live} = e^{+\mu_I x_v}$
- Log: $\log_{10}(I_{mask}/I_{live}) = (\mu_I x_v)/\ln 10$

→ **Result: pixel value ∝ iodine × vessel path length.** *(2 marks for math derivation.)*

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**"Tabulate SIX points of comparison between CT Angiography and Cath Lab Angiography (DSA). In which clinical situation is Cath Lab DSA preferred despite being invasive?"**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (6 points — full 8-point reference in Section 3.3):**

| # | Parameter | CTA | DSA |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Invasiveness | Minimally invasive (IV) | Invasive (arterial puncture) |
| 2 | Contrast route | Intravenous | Intra-arterial (selective) |
| 3 | Data type | 3D volumetric | 2D projection |
| 4 | Spatial resolution | 0.4 – 0.6 mm (voxel) | 0.2 – 0.3 mm (projection) |
| 5 | Temporal resolution | 50 – 200 ms | 1 – 8 ms (real-time) |
| 6 | Therapeutic capability | None (diagnostic only) | Yes — angioplasty, stent, coil, EVAR, thrombectomy |
| 7 | Dose metric | CTDIvol, DLP | DAP, Air Kerma |
| 8 | Complications (major) | < 0.5% | 1 – 3% |

*(5 marks for the table — credit for at least 6 rows with units where relevant.)*

**When is Cath Lab DSA preferred? (2 marks)**

Cath Lab DSA is preferred **whenever a therapeutic intervention is anticipated** in the *same session*, for example:
1. **Acute ischemic stroke with large-vessel occlusion** → mechanical thrombectomy.
2. **Acute MI** → primary PCI with stenting.
3. **Ruptured aneurysm / AVM** → emergency coil embolization.
4. **Critical limb ischemia** → peripheral angioplasty / stenting.
5. **Aortic aneurysm** → EVAR (endovascular aneurysm repair).
6. **Severe vascular trauma with active bleeding** → selective embolization.

In all these, the *therapeutic advantage* of DSA outweighs its invasiveness, and CTA would only serve as a *roadmap* for planning. *(2 marks for clinical justification with example.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Must-Know" for KTU Board Exam**

**Core Definitions:**
- ☐ **CTA** = IV contrast + helical CT → 3D vascular map.
- ☐ **DSA (Cath Lab Angio)** = IA contrast + fluoroscopy + logarithmic subtraction → 2D dynamic vascular image.
- ☐ **Mask image** = pre-contrast baseline; **Subtraction** = cancels bone/soft tissue.
- ☐ **Hounsfield Unit** for contrast-filled aorta = **250–400 HU**.

**Key Numbers (memorize):**
- ☐ Pitch formula: $\text{Pitch} = S / (N \cdot T)$; CTA typical **0.9 – 1.2**.
- ☐ Bolus tracking threshold: **+100 to +150 HU** rise.
- ☐ CTA contrast: **60 – 120 mL IV @ 3 – 5 mL/s**.
- ☐ DSA contrast: **5 – 15 mL per IA run @ 3 – 10 mL/s**.
- ☐ CTA effective dose: **5 – 20 mSv** (chest ~ 5 mSv).
- ☐ DSA effective dose: **3 – 15 mSv** diagnostic; **20 – 50 mSv** interventional.

**Clinical Pearls:**
- ☐ **CTPA = gold standard for Pulmonary Embolism.**
- ☐ **CCTA (coronary CTA)** = first-line for low-to-intermediate CAD risk.
- ☐ **DSA = gold standard when intervention is planned** (PCI, EVAR, thrombectomy, embolization).
- ☐ **DSA preferred in CKD / dialysis patients** (lower contrast volume).
- ☐ **CTA better in trauma, stroke triage, and outpatient screening** (speed + 3D).
- ☐ **DSA dose metric: DAP + Reference Point Air Kerma**; **CTA dose metric: CTDIvol + DLP**.

**Formula Sheet — Top 5 to retain:**
1. $\text{Pitch} = S / (N \cdot T)$
2. $DLP = CTDI_{vol} \times L$
3. $E = k \cdot DLP$
4. $DAP$ (cumulative for DSA)
5. $CNR = \vert S_{vessel} - S_{tissue} \vert / \sigma_{noise}$

**Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
- ❌ Don't confuse **CTDIvol** (CTA) with **DAP** (DSA) — different modalities use different dose metrics.
- ❌ Don't claim "CTA is always better" — DSA is irreplaceable for therapy.
- ❌ Don't forget the **logarithm** in the DSA subtraction equation — KTU *will* ask for derivation.
- ❌ Don't omit **units** in numerical problems (mGy, mGy·cm, Gy·cm², mSv).
- ❌ Don't say "DSA has higher resolution" without specifying *spatial* vs *temporal* — clarify the axis.
